entries.title: Allegories of Hiram Abiff
entries.short_summary: This introductory section outlines the scope of the work "Allegories of Hiram Abiff," which delves into exoteric and esoteric philosophies drawn from Christianity, Rosicrucianism, and ancient Egyptian mystery schools. It emphasizes Freemasonry's traditional method of conveying moral and ethical truths through allegory and symbols, asserting that initiates must engage with both their intellect and their emotions to fully comprehend these teachings.
entries.full_summary:
This introductory section outlines the scope of the work "Allegories of Hiram Abiff," which delves into exoteric and esoteric philosophies drawn from Christianity, Rosicrucianism, and ancient Egyptian mystery schools. It emphasizes Freemasonry's traditional method of conveying moral and ethical truths through allegory and symbols, asserting that initiates must engage with both their intellect and their emotions to fully comprehend these teachings.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0003 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Symbolism of the Master Mason Degree

The Master Mason Degree initiates the candidate in darkness, signifying a state of equilibrium and preparedness for sacred ground. The candidate commits fully to the Fraternity, placing faith in the Three Great Lights, and is introduced to working tools, with one symbolizing Brotherly love. The central Mystery Drama involves the candidate assuming the role of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, forging a link to the Fraternity's inner soul and ancient Mystery Schools. This experience aims for self-realization, and the symbolism itself is ancient, evolving culturally over time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0003 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0007 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Low Twelve

This section explains the ancient symbolism of 'Low Twelve,' contrasting it with 'High Twelve.' It details how the number twelve signifies completion, fulfillment, and human life, drawing connections to the twelve signs of the Zodiac and the twelve edges of a cube. Low Twelve specifically represents midnight, the darkest time of the night, while High Twelve denotes noon.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0007 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Section

This section describes the Third Section of a degree, focusing on hieroglyphical emblems, moral lessons, and extensive details regarding the construction of King Solomon's Temple. It covers the temple's foundation, location on Mount Moriah, construction duration, the absence of tool sounds during building, and the vast workforce involved, including Grand Masters, Masters, Fellow Crafts, and Entered Apprentices. The section also elaborates on the two celebrated pillars placed at the temple's entrance, explaining their symbolic significance as a memorial to the Israelites' deliverance from Egyptian bondage, referencing the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0093 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section outlines the significant duties, responsibilities, and moral obligations of a newly raised Master Mason. It emphasizes maintaining the fraternity's reputation, guiding less informed brethren, upholding ancient landmarks, and exemplifying virtues like morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, all while remaining faithful to one's trust and vows, mirroring the example of Hiram Abiff.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0093 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Circuit

This section, titled "Third Circuit," details a solemn ritualistic exchange among lodge officers and the Chaplain, reflecting on the transient nature of life, the inevitability of death, and the sorrows of mortality. It progresses through lamentations of human frailty and grief, balanced by expressions of faith in God's justice, mercy, and the promise of eternal life and resurrection. The ritual includes prayers for strength, wisdom, and comfort for the bereaved, culminating in a collective affirmation of hope and trust in divine beneficence, often associated with a Lodge of Sorrow.

This section describes a solemn Masonic ceremony, likely a Lodge of Sorrow, where the Master reflects on the inevitability of death and the transient nature of life. The Chaplain reads from 1 Corinthians 15, discussing the resurrection of the dead and the transformation from corruptible to incorruptible bodies. A choir sings from Job 19 about seeing God after death. The Orator is then called upon to recount the virtues of the deceased, with an emphasis on Masonic charity and non-judgment.

This section details the 'Third Circuit' of a Masonic memorial service, which includes scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job, a choir performance, and an oration emphasizing truth and Masonic Charity towards the deceased. The ritual proceeds with the Junior Warden sprinkling corn, the Senior Warden sprinkling wine, and the Master sprinkling perfumed oil on the coffin, each accompanied by symbolic interpretations related to resurrection, spiritual refreshment, and consolation. The ceremony concludes with the Master placing a lambskin apron and holding a white glove on the coffin, symbolizing innocence, purity, and the hope of a welcome reception in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

This section details the ritualistic farewell for a deceased Master Mason, highlighting the symbolism of the white glove as an emblem of Fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, and the sprig of evergreen as an emblem of Faith in the immortality of the soul. It describes the Master's address, the placement of the evergreen on the coffin, a collective farewell by the brethren, and a three-circuit procession around the coffin accompanied by Grand Honors.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pronunciation Key

This section provides a pronunciation key for various words, many of which are common in Masonic ritual and discourse. It includes rules for indicating accented syllables and short vowel sounds, followed by an alphabetical list of words with their phonetic pronunciations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mason.

This section details the duegard and sign of a Master Mason, which alludes to the penalty of the obligation, and the grand hailing sign of distress. It describes the physical actions for each sign, including the accompanying words for the grand hailing sign of distress, "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?" This sign is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The text also outlines the specific gavel raps used by the Master and Wardens to open a Lodge on the First, Second, and Third Degrees, concluding with the Master reciting a passage of Scripture (Psalm 133) about brethren dwelling in unity, followed by the brethren's response.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0051 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Master Mason: Opening, Obligation, Overview

This section introduces the Master Mason degree, focusing on the traditional narrative of Hiram Abiff's death, multiple burials, and resurrections. It highlights the absence of Hiram Abiff's death in external historical records like the Bible or Josephus, emphasizing his significant role as an arbiter and a distinguished figure. The text also describes a Master Mason's Lodge as the 'Sanctum Sanctorum' of King Solomon's Temple and details the ritualistic opening where both points of the compasses are elevated above the square. Candidate preparation for this sublime degree is typically performed by the Junior Deacon and Stewards.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0051 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0067 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a historical account of the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, who was slain by fifteen Fellow Crafts just before the completion of King Solomon's Temple. His death is presented as an instance of exceptional virtue, fortitude, and integrity, with the candidate, Brother Gabe, representing Hiram Abiff in the ritual.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0067 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0077 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge

This section presents the formal charge given to a newly raised Master Mason. It emphasizes the Mason's duty, honor, and gratitude to be faithful to their trust, uphold the dignity of their character, and enforce the Order's tenets. The Master Mason is authorized to guide uninformed brethren, preserve the fraternity's reputation, and cultivate universal benevolence. The charge stresses the importance of preserving ancient landmarks, adhering to established customs, and imitating the example of the 'celebrated artist' (Hiram Abiff) in virtue, honor, and fidelity. It concludes with instructions for the candidate to sign the constitution and by-laws and notes regarding the Master Mason lecture.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0077 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0080 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Third Section.

This section, presented in a Q&A format, concludes the Master Mason's Degree by reviewing key aspects of King Solomon's Temple, including its construction duration, structural supports (columns and pillars of Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty), and the workforce involved. It details the constitution and usual meeting places for Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason lodges. The section then enumerates two classes of emblems specific to the Master Mason Degree, such as the Pot of Incense, Beehive, All-seeing Eye, Anchor and Ark, Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid, Hour-Glass, Scythe, Three Steps, Setting-Maul, Spade, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Finally, it addresses the lost word of a Master Mason, its substitute 'Mah-Hah-Bone' given on the five points of fellowship, and notes that the original word was later found and used in the Royal Arch Degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0080 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Critical Notes on the Third Degree

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing the historical variations in Masonic 'work' and lectures across different U.S. states and Europe. It highlights the influence of Brother John Barney's lectures, derived from the Preston Lectures, and their adoption in the Northwestern states, noting how Barney adapted his teachings to different systems in the Western and Southwestern states. The text also mentions the impact of 'Morganry' in Minnesota and New York, where rituals closely resemble Morgan's disclosures. It asserts that while minor differences exist, the essential points of Masonry are universal. Footnotes offer critical commentary on the Third Degree's nature, describing it as traditionary, historical, and legendary, but also hyperbolical, apocryphal, and fabulous. They include practical details of a ritualistic element involving a sword, discuss the symbolism of worldly possession and the historical connections of Tubal Cain, Jubal, and Vulcan. Detailed descriptions of Master's Lodge regalia and officer jewels are provided, alongside the historical evolution of the 'fatal weapons' used in the Third Degree. The section also references Professor Stuart's critique regarding the linguistic origins of names in the legend and notes variations in the narrative concerning the number of Fellow Crafts searching for Hiram and the placement of the sprig of acacia.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing variations in the Hiramic legend, including the disputed location of the lost body and the names of the ruffians. It clarifies the symbolic use of acacia, distinguishing it from cassia, and explains its significance in Jewish law for marking graves. The text also connects Masonic penalties to ancient Hebrew covenant ceremonies, describing their ritualistic elements. Furthermore, it discusses the allegorical nature of the Master Mason degree, particularly regarding the lost word and the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, cautioning against literal historical interpretations of the legend.

This section critically examines the historical and internal consistency of the Hiramic legend within the Third Degree. It challenges the historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death and burial, citing Dr. Anderson and Josephus, who assert Hiram lived to complete the Temple and returned to Tyre. The text also highlights inconsistencies in ritual narratives, such as Hiram drawing plans in the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the pillars and Middle Chamber were built. Despite these critiques, the section reaffirms the symbolic importance of the legend in teaching the doctrine of resurrection, fidelity, and a future state, representing a Master Mason as one saved from iniquity and raised to faith.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing historical variations in ritual descriptions of the "three principal entrances" to the Temple across different jurisdictions and time periods. It outlines the structure of the Master Mason lecture, covering the mode of raising, historical traditions, and emblem explanations. A significant portion is dedicated to an extensive Christian allegorical interpretation of the Third Degree, linking Hiram Abiff's death and resurrection to the Christian dispensation, the state of religion before Christ, and its restoration by the Saviour. This allegory is further elaborated with specific parallels drawn from the 32nd Degree, connecting Hiram's fate to Christ's condemnation, suffering, and the symbolism of the lost Word, false brethren, and the sprig of cassia. The text also includes a description of the solemn closing ceremony of a Mason's Lodge and an author's note emphasizing the allegorical nature of the Hiramic legend over its literal historical accuracy, citing scriptural evidence. Finally, it briefly defines the role of a Grand Lecturer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: Grips and the Wages Parable

This section details the grips and passwords of a Mark Master Mason, including the pass-grip (JOPPA) and the real grip (SIROC or MARK WELL). It then describes a ritualistic parable concerning a keystone ordered by Hiram Abiff, which was initially rejected by the Overseers but later found in the rubbish. The Right Worshipful Master presents this keystone, reads several scriptural passages about the 'stone which the builders refused,' and invests the candidate with a new name, represented by a circle of letters on the keystone.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0108 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Excellent Master Lecture

This section details the lecture for the Most Excellent Master degree, covering the candidate's admission process, the questions asked, and the required answers. It describes the ritualistic progression through the Lodge, the solemn obligation taken, and the specific signs and grips associated with the degree, including their symbolic meanings. The narrative also touches upon the historical context of the degree, linking it to the completion of King Solomon's Temple, the celebration of the cope-stone, and the subsequent death of Hiram Abiff.

This section describes the dedication of King Solomon's Temple after the mourning of Hiram Abiff, including the lodging of the Tabernacle of Moses and the Holy Ark, and Jehovah's divine presence. It outlines the structure of a perfect Lodge, requiring seven members, and introduces the 'Excellent Master' degree as an intermediate step in a sequence leading to the Royal Arch. The text extensively details the symbolism of numbers, particularly three (linked to the ancient Trinity and Church of Rome practices) and six (prominent in the Most Excellent Master degree's ritual actions and also venerated by the Druids). The section concludes with a description of the keystone placement ceremony by the Most Excellent Master and historical evidence of arches and keystones in ancient Ethiopian and Egyptian architecture predating Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0108 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0125 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Historical Notes

This section details the historical account of a secret underground vault constructed by King Solomon, leading from his private apartment to beneath the Temple's sanctum sanctorum. This vault was divided into nine arches, with the ninth arch designated for grand council meetings and as a repository for copies of sacred items from the sanctum sanctorum. After its completion, the three Grand Masters deposited significant craft items, including the Ark of the Covenant, a pot of manna, Aaron's rod, and the book of the law. Following the assassination of Grand Master Hiram Abiff and the subsequent loss of the Master's Word, the two remaining kings agreed to place the Sacred Word in this secret vault to ensure its preservation and potential future discovery alongside the other treasures.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0125 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Notes and Authorities

This section provides an appendix of notes and authorities, covering various aspects of the Master Mason degree. It details the strong grip of a Master Mason, the three Grand Masonic Pillars (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) and their representation by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. It also describes the construction of the Temple, including the number of columns, pilasters, and workers by degree. The text explains the symbolism of the three steps on the Master's carpet and the nine classes of Master's emblems, specifically focusing on the setting maul, spade, coffin, and sprig of acacia as emblems of morality and immortality. Finally, it includes a historical note on the transmission and abridgment of Masonic lectures by figures like Webb, Preston, Gleason, and J. Browne.

This section, an address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, Grand Master of Vermont, defends the authenticity and lineage of Masonic lectures, specifically those transmitted through T. S. Webb and Benjamin Gleason, asserting their connection to Preston's original lectures. Tucker addresses criticisms regarding his previous statements, clarifying that Webb abridged and rearranged Preston's lectures but retained their substance. He cites his possession of 'key' copies of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason. Tucker also defends the claim that Gleason's lectures were approved by the Grand Lodge of England, referencing Gleason's own published letter from 1843, where Gleason states he acquired complete knowledge of the three degrees' lectures from Webb and successfully communicated them across the United States and Europe, 'according to the ancient landmarks,' without objection. He further supports Gleason's knowledge of Preston by quoting Gleason's 1812 oration, which praises Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements'. The discussion also mentions J. Browne's 1802 'Master Key' as a presumed source for Prestonian lectures.

This section discusses the historical presence and influence of Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and the 'Prestonian system of work and lectures' in the United States, particularly in New England. It highlights Benjamin Gleason's crucial role, alongside Thomas Smith Webb, in introducing and promulgating this system. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard in 1804, employing Gleason to communicate it to lodges under its jurisdiction. Gleason continued as a dedicated lecturer until his death in 1847, remembered as a master teacher of ancient Craft Masonry. The author defends the existence of Prestonian lectures in the US against a critic, emphasizing the importance of factual investigation over mere negation.

This section discusses the history and transmission of the Prestonian system of Masonic ritual. It notes that the system, as revived by Preston and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England, underwent a revision in 1813 for reconciliation purposes, which led to some incongruities. The original Prestonian ritual was brought to the United States around 1803 by two English brethren, one of whom was a pupil of Preston. It was communicated to Thomas Smith Webb and subsequently imparted to Brother Gleason, who then taught it to lodges in Massachusetts. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system, which has since been the recognized Masonic work in the Commonwealth. Despite some modifications and innovations, the ritual has been largely preserved in its original purity, especially in older jurisdictions. The text also highlights the ongoing struggle against corrupting influences from ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications, emphasizing the importance of preserving the ritual's integrity.

This section discusses the nature and importance of Masonic lectures, specifically comparing Preston's lengthy lectures with Webb's abridgment. The author, through the Grand Lecturer's opinion, suggests that Webb's version retains practical value and is more manageable. The text criticizes those who undervalue lectures, labeling proponents of their preservation as 'parrot Masons' and advocating for a 'new era' focused solely on the philosophy, history, and symbolism of Masonry, arguing that these elements are already embedded within the traditional lectures.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
practical_elements: Candidate enters the Lodge of the Master Mason in darkness
Initiate is given full use of every working tool
Candidate partakes of the central Mystery Drama of the Fraternity
Candidate assumes the role of the Grand Master Hiram Abiff
The temple was founded in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, on the second day of the month Zif.
It was located on Mount Moriah, near where Abraham was to offer Isaac and where David appeased the destroying angel.
Construction took over seven years, during which it reportedly did not rain in the daytime.
No sound of axe, hammer, or iron tool was heard during construction.
The temple was supported by 1453 pillars and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
The workforce included 3 Grand Masters, 3300 Masters/Overseers, 80000 Fellow Crafts, and 70000 Entered Apprentices.
Two celebrated pillars were placed in front of the temple's porch, one on the left and one on the right.
Support the dignity of your character on every occasion.
Enforce, by precept and example, obedience to the tenets of the Institution.
Correct the errors and irregularities of your uninformed brethren.
Guard brethren against a breach of fidelity.
Preserve the reputation of the fraternity unsullied.
Practice morality.
Live harmoniously.
Teach equality.
Inculcate universal benevolence.
Afford the best example for the conduct of others less informed.
Carefully preserve the ancient landmarks of the Institution.
Never suffer ancient landmarks to be infringed.
Never countenance a deviation from the established usages and customs of the fraternity.
Support with dignity the character of a Master Mason.
Be true and faithful.
Imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you have this evening represented (Hiram Abiff).
Officers (Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, Chaplain) recite specific lines.
Brethren halt and face inward.
Honors are repeated.
Anthem is chanted by choir or read by Chaplain.
Brethren retain positions during anthem.
Master implores the Father in Heaven.
Chaplain repeats a prayer.
All brethren kneel during prayer.
Brethren respond with "So mote it be."
Officers resume stations and brethren their places after prayer.
Choir chants "The Lord is my shepherd."
Master delivers an address reflecting on mortality and the transient nature of life.
Chaplain reads a lesson from the Holy Scriptures (1 Corinthians 15:35-57) concerning the resurrection of the dead.
Choir sings a passage from Job 19:25-27.
Orator is instructed to recount the virtues and good deeds of the deceased Brother(s).
Assembly gathers to pay honors to fallen Brother(s) and learn lessons from death.
Reading from 1 Corinthians 15:35-57.
Choir singing from Job 19:25-27.
Orator delivers an address.
Junior Warden approaches the coffin and sprinkles a few grains of corn upon it.
One of the Deacons lights an altar light while the Junior Warden speaks.
Senior Warden approaches the coffin and sprinkles a few drops of wine upon it.
One of the Deacons lights another altar light while the Senior Warden speaks.
Master approaches the coffin and sprinkles a few drops of perfumed oil upon it.
Brethren are called up to form around the coffin.
Master holds a lambskin apron (or the apron usually worn by the deceased).
The apron is laid upon the coffin.
Master holds a white glove.
The Master holds a sprig of evergreen.
The sprig of evergreen is laid upon the coffin.
The Master calls up the brethren.
All brethren collectively say a farewell.
A procession performs three circuits around the coffin, moving by the west, north, east, and south.
Grand Honors are given before each circuit, with exclamations omitted.
A reading is performed during the procession.
CAPITAL letters indicate accented syllables
a - short a as in the word bat
i - short i as in the word ill or is
e - short e as in the word ethic
(e) - barely audible short e
u - short u as in the word but
Duegard of a Master: (Fig. 5) is mentioned as a visual representation.
Sign of a Master Mason: Draw the right hand (thumb in) across the stomach as low as the vest, then drop the hand suddenly (Fig. 6).
Grand Hailing Sign of Distress: Raise the hands as represented in Fig. 7 and drop them with spirit, repeated three times.
Words accompanying the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress (in night/dark): "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?"

Gavel raps for opening a Third Degree Lodge: Master gives one rap, Senior Warden one, Junior Warden one. Master gives one rap (second time), responded to by Wardens. Master gives one rap (third time), responded to by Wardens.
Gavel raps for opening a Second Degree Lodge: Two raps only.
Gavel raps for opening a First Degree Lodge: One rap each, first by Master, then Senior Warden, lastly Junior Warden.

Master removes hat and recites Psalm 133: "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! It is like the precious ointment upon the head, that ran down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard; that went down to the skirts of his garments; as the dew of Hermon, and as the dew that descended upon the mountains of Zion: for there the Lord commanded the blessing, even life forever more." Amen!
Brethren respond: "Amen! So mote it be!"
A Master Mason's Lodge is opened with both points of the compasses elevated above the square.
A candidate for the Master Mason Degree is generally prepared by the Junior Deacon and the two Stewards, or other brethren acting as such.
The candidate, Brother Gabe, represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the ritual.
The second section of the degree exemplifies the historical account.
Be faithful to your trust.
Enforce obedience to the tenets of the Order by precept and example.
Correct the errors and irregularities of uninformed brethren.
Recommend obedience and submission to inferiors.
Recommend courtesy and affability to equals.
Recommend kindness and condescension to superiors.
Cultivate universal benevolence.
Afford the best example for the conduct of others less informed by your own regular behavior.
Carefully preserve the ancient landmarks of the order.
Support with dignity the character you now bear.
Imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you this evening represent.
Take your seat in this Lodge as a Master Mason after stepping to the Secretary's desk and signing your name to the constitution and by-laws.
King Solomon's Temple was built in seven years, during which it did not rain in the daytime to avoid obstructing workmen.
The Temple was supported by 1453 columns and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
An Entered Apprentices' Lodge is constituted by seven members: one Master and six Entered Apprentices, meeting on the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
A Fellow Crafts' Lodge is constituted by five members: two Masters and three Fellow Crafts, meeting in the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
A Masters' Lodge is constituted by three Master Masons, meeting in the Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies, of King Solomon's Temple.
The substitute word 'Mah-Hah-Bone' is given on the five points of fellowship and in low breath.
The missing word was found after 470 years and is used in the Royal Arch Degree.
A ritualistic element where a conductor or brother draws a cold sword across the candidate's naked belly, causing a shocking effect.
Description of Master's Lodge jewels: suspended from blue velvet collars, bordered and embroidered with silver, with a blue rosette and silver five-pointed star.
Description of Master's Lodge apron: white, lined and bordered with blue, with an eye on the flap and selections from the Master's carpet on the area.
Description of Master's Lodge scarf: blue silk, trimmed with silver, with a blue rosette at the shoulder and hip, worn from left to right.

Specific jewels for officers: Worshipful Master (square), Past Master (compass opened on a quarter-circle), Senior Warden (level), Junior Warden (plumb), Secretary (cross pens), Treasurer (cross keys), Senior Deacon (square and compass with sun), Junior Deacon (square and compass with quarter moon), Stewards (cornucopia), Masters of Ceremonies (cross swords), Tyler (sabre).

Evolution of 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
The Hebrew covenant ceremony involved cutting a beast in twain and passing between the parts thereof.
The throat of the animal was cut across with one single blow, dividing the windpipe, arteries, and veins without touching any bone.
The breast was torn open and the heart plucked out; any imperfection rendered the body unclean.
The animal was divided into two parts, placed north and south, for parties to the covenant to pass between them from east to west.
The carcass was then left as a prey to voracious animals.
Hiram Abiff's supposed practice of retiring to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Hiram Abiff's alleged division of operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters.
Payment of Apprentices' wages at the pillar of Jachin.
Payment of Masters' wages in the Middle Chamber.
The High Priest's unique privilege of entering the Holy of Holies only once a year, with a string or belt around his waist.

The ritual description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has undergone historical alterations, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.

Variations in the sequence of attacks/entrances exist across jurisdictions: United States (south, west, east), Scotland (east, south, west), and France (originally south, north, east; now west, south, east).
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors in its ritual.

The Master Mason lecture is structured into three sections: the first illustrates the ancient and proper mode of raising a candidate, the second introduces historical traditions and exemplifies Masonic virtue, and the third explains emblems and describes Solomon's Temple construction.

The closing of a Mason's Lodge involves a report, inspection of avenues by the meridian officer, rehearsal of duties for leaders of craftsmen, the Senior Warden ensuring instruction and improvement, an impressive address on duties, and a fervent petition to the Deity.
A Grand Lecturer is elected annually by the Grand Lodge to teach the Ritual of Freemasonry to subordinate Lodges, typically compensated by salary or per lecture.
The pass-grip of a Mark Master Mason is associated with the password JOPPA, referencing the steep ascent from Joppa while conveying timber from Lebanon.

The real grip of a Mark Master Mason is named SIROC or MARK WELL, formed by locking the little fingers of the right hands, turning their backs together, and placing the ends of the thumbs against each other.
The Right Worshipful Master engages the candidate in a dialogue to transition from the pass-grip to the true grip.
The Right Worshipful Master admonishes the candidate never to reveal the words of the grip in any way other than how they were received.
Brethren shuffle their feet to signify a disturbance among the workmen.
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel to summon the three Overseers.
The Right Worshipful Master exhibits the figure of a keystone for inspection.
The Overseers report rejecting a stone because it was 'neither oblong nor square,' lacked a craft mark, and was deemed unfit, subsequently thrown among the rubbish.
The Right Worshipful Master orders an immediate search for the keystone, stating the Temple cannot be finished without it.
Brethren shuffle about the Lodge to find the keystone and bring it to the East.
The Senior Warden takes the keystone and reports its discovery in the Temple's rubbish to the Right Worshipful Master.
Two or three brethren carry the keystone to the Right Worshipful Master.
The Right Worshipful Master receives the keystone and places it upright and plumb on his desk, with its initials facing the Lodge and the candidate.
The Right Worshipful Master gives four raps with the gavel, prompting all to rise (though some Lodges remain seated).

The Right Worshipful Master reads specific scriptural passages (Ps. cxviii. 22, Matt. xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, Luke xx. 17) about the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner, striking the keystone once, twice, thrice, and four times after each passage.
The Right Worshipful Master reads from Revelation xi. 17 about receiving a white stone and a new name.
The candidate steps forward to receive the new name.
The Right Worshipful Master invests the candidate with a new name, described as a circle of letters and the general mark of the Degree.
The Right Worshipful Master points out the initials on the keystone to the candidate.
Candidate is asked 'Who comes here?'

Candidate states they are a worthy brother, duly initiated, passed to Fellow Craft, raised to Master Mason, advanced to Mark Master, and regularly passed the chair, now seeking to be received and acknowledged as a Most Excellent Master.
Candidate confirms the act is of their own free will and accord, that they are worthy, well qualified, have made suitable proficiency, and are properly vouched for.
Candidate states they expect to gain privilege 'By the benefit of the pass'.
The pass word is 'Mark Well'.
Candidate is directed to wait for the Right Worshipful Master's answer.
Right Worshipful Master's answer: 'Let the candidate enter'.
Candidate is conducted six times around the Lodge to the Worshipful Senior Warden in the West.
Candidate is conducted to the Right Worshipful Master in the East.
Candidate is conducted to the Worshipful Senior Warden in the West, who teaches the approach to the altar.
Approach to the altar involves six upright Masonic steps, feet forming a square, body erect.
The Most Excellent Master is made 'In due form'.
Due form involves kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses, body erect.
Candidate takes the solemn oath of a Most Excellent Master.
The duegard sign is given.
The sign of the degree is given.
The sign of admiration is given.
The grip is communicated to a brother.
The name of the grip is 'Rabboni'.
The grip is also called 'The cover grip'.
Solomon dedicated the Temple with solemn prayer and costly sacrifices.
The holy ark was fixed in its proper place between the cherubim.
The grand honors of Masonry involve a thrice-repeated action.
A candidate is prepared with a rope wound six times around their body.
Six distinct knocks are given on the Lodge door, answered by six from within.
The candidate is walked six times around the Lodge, moving with the sun.
More advanced brethren form a procession and march six times around the Lodge, against the course of the sun.
Two pillars, about five feet high, are erected with an arch placed over them.
The Most Excellent Master places the keystone in the arch.
The keystone is driven home with six raps of the gavel.
A pot containing gum camphor or other inflammable matter is used during ceremonies.
Construction of a secret underground vault by Solomon.
Division of the vault into nine separate arches.
Designation of the ninth arch for grand council meetings and as a deposit site.
Deposition of the Ark of the Covenant, a pot of manna, the rod of Aaron, and the book of the law.
Deposition of the Master's Word in the secret vault for preservation.
The strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, is mentioned.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five (5) points of fellowship and heard in a low breath.
The instruction to 'Advance and give it' refers to the word associated with the grip.
The word is 'right'.
Promulgating the Prestonian system of work and lectures.
Communicating the Preston ritual to lodges under Grand Lodge jurisdiction.
Teaching Masonic ritual as a lecturer.
Adoption of a ritual standard by a Grand Lodge.
The Prestonian system of ritual was brought to the United States around 1803.
The ritual was communicated to Webb and then imparted to Gleason.
Brother Gleason was employed to teach the ritual to lodges in Massachusetts.
The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system.
The ritual has been preserved in a remarkable degree of purity in Massachusetts lodges, particularly in the lodge where Gleason was Master in 1809.
The system underwent modifications in its general arrangement and adaptation after its introduction to the US.
Grand Lecturer compared Preston and Gleason Lectures
Preston Lectures are lengthy, estimated to cover nearly one hundred pages of foolscap paper if written out in full
Requirement for Masons to commit lectures in extenso is considered a difficult task
Webb's abridgment and new arrangement of lectures are considered to preserve substantial practical value and use preferable language
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Freemasonry utilizes allegory and symbols as the most effective means to teach moral and ethical truths, suggesting that these methods convey deeper meanings than literal instruction alone.

The entrance in darkness signifies a new, unseen light and a state of equilibrium, preparing the candidate for sacred ground. The exalted working tool symbolizes the spreading of Brotherly love. Participating in the Mystery Drama and assuming Hiram Abiff's role forges a link with the Fraternity's inner soul and archetypal forces, connecting to ancient Mystery Schools. The experience in the 'Holy of Holies' leads to self-realization, where one may momentarily forget their former self and realize their true identity.

The number twelve, in ancient symbolism, denotes completion, derived from the twelve signs of the Zodiac forming a complete circle and the twelve edges of a cube symbolizing the earth. It also signifies the fulfillment of a deed and serves as an emblem of human life. High Twelve corresponds to noon, when the sun is at its zenith, while Low Twelve denotes midnight, representing the blackest time of the night.

The section illustrates hieroglyphical emblems and inculcates moral lessons. The two celebrated pillars at the temple entrance symbolize the happy deliverance of the Israelites from Egyptian bondage and commemorate the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud, which provided light and darkness respectively during the Exodus. Their placement ensured the event was continually before the eyes of those going to and from divine worship.

The instruction to "imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you have this evening represented" directly refers to Hiram Abiff, symbolizing fidelity, integrity, and steadfastness in the face of adversity, even unto death. The Master Mason is expected to embody these virtues in their conduct and adherence to Masonic principles.

The "Third Circuit" symbolizes the journey of life, marked by inevitable suffering, loss, and the ultimate certainty of death. The lamentations represent the human experience of grief and the transient nature of earthly existence ("life is a vapor," "days are passed"). The responses from the Chaplain, particularly the references to a "tree planted by the waters" and "a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens," symbolize enduring faith, divine sustenance, and the promise of immortality and resurrection beyond the grave. The ritual as a whole serves as a memento mori, reminding participants of mortality while simultaneously offering comfort and hope through spiritual belief and the prospect of an afterlife.

Life is presented as 'death’s vestibule' and a 'journey to the grave,' symbolizing the transient nature of earthly existence. The comparison of human life to flowers and grass emphasizes fragility and impermanence. The 'valley of the shadow of death' symbolizes the journey through mortality with divine comfort. The scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the hope of resurrection, transformation from a natural to a spiritual body, and victory over death. The seed analogy in 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the transformation of the body after death into a new, spiritual form. The 'broad mantle of charity' symbolizes Masonic non-judgment and compassion towards the deceased.

The scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job symbolize resurrection, incorruption, transformation from mortal to immortal, and the belief in a Redeemer. The corn of nourishment symbolizes the resurrection of the body, sown in corruption and raised in incorruption, glory, power, and as a spiritual body. The wine of refreshment symbolizes spiritual refreshment, divine grace, blessings, and comfort for those afflicted or distressed. The oil of joy symbolizes comfort, consolation for the bereaved, healing of heart-wounds, the virtue of charity, increased affection, leniency, indulgence, forgiveness, and a joyful passage through death. The lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a Mason's badge, denotes the hope that the departed brother's soul, purified from imperfections, has found a welcome in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

The white glove is an emblem of Fidelity, symbolizing Masonic friendship and brotherly love, and signifies an increased obligation to the living brethren. The sprig of evergreen is an emblem of Masonic Faith in the immortality of the soul, representing the eternal principle in man that survives death and blooms perpetually. It denotes trust that the deceased's immortal spirit has entered a celestial temple and found acceptance with the Supreme Grand Master.

The sign of a Master Mason alludes to the penalty of the obligation of a Master Mason. The grand hailing sign of distress, accompanied by the words "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?", is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a plea for aid in dire circumstances and a connection to the central legend of the degree. The scripture passage (Psalm 133) emphasizes the importance of unity among brethren.

A Master Mason's Lodge is symbolically styled the 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies, of King Solomon's Temple'. The elevation of both points of the compasses above the square during the opening of the Lodge signifies the advanced knowledge and moral principles of the Master Mason degree.

The death of Hiram Abiff symbolizes an instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity that is rarely equaled. His steadfastness in guarding the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death, serves as a profound example.

The instruction to 'imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you this evening represent' directly refers to Hiram Abiff, symbolizing unwavering fidelity, integrity, and adherence to duty even in the face of adversity. The 'ancient landmarks of the order' are symbolic of the foundational, unalterable principles and traditions of Freemasonry, which the Master Mason is charged to preserve.

The three grand columns or pillars supporting the Temple are named Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty. Wisdom represents King Solomon, who conceived the Temple; Strength represents Hiram, King of Tyre, who supported the undertaking; and Beauty represents Hiram Abiff, whose craftsmanship adorned the Temple. The emblems of the Master Mason Degree, divided into two classes, serve as symbolic lessons, though their specific explanations are noted as monitorial. The Sprig of Acacia is a significant emblem. The lost word and its substitute, Mah-Hah-Bone, symbolize a profound truth or secret that was lost and later replaced, with the original word eventually being recovered in a higher degree.

The text notes that an unspecified element denotes 'worldly possession.' It also connects Tubal Cain as an instructor of artificers in brass and iron, relating him to Vulcan, and Jubal as the inventor of music, relating him to Apollo. The sprig of acacia is mentioned as a mark left by either the murderers or recanters in the Hiramic legend.

The Hebrew covenant ceremony, involving the division of an animal, symbolized a solemn binding to a promise, with the implied curse: "Thus let it be done to him, and thus let his body be cut in two, who shall break this covenant." The acacia, used in Jewish law to mark graves for priests to avoid ritual impurity, is symbolically significant in Freemasonry. The Hiramic legend, including the exhumation of the body, is presented as an allegory rather than a literal historical fact, serving to inculcate the doctrine of resurrection and a future state. The Master Mason represents a man saved from iniquity and raised to faith in salvation, embodying the doctrine of love.

The Master Mason represents a man under the doctrine of love, saved from the grave of iniquity, and raised to the faith of salvation. The degree testifies faith in the resurrection of the body and inculcates a practical lesson of prudence and unshrinking fidelity, inspiring hope for a final reward. The legend serves to impress upon the candidate's mind the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state.

The Third Degree is interpreted as an allegory for the Christian dispensation, symbolizing the revelation of salvation, atonement for sin, and the resurrection from the dead, confirmed by Christ's resurrection. The degeneration of Jewish law and the burial of piety and reverence are symbolized by "filth and rubbish" from the Temple. The inability of ancient wisdom to restore religion is represented by those who sought to raise her in vain. The sprig of acacia waving over religion's monument symbolizes hope and immortality, anticipating the Saviour's arrival. Hiram Abiff's death is a symbol for the Messiah's death. The three blows given to Hiram at the three gates of the Temple allude to the three points of condemnation against Christ before Caiaphas, Herod, and Pilate. The three tools (gauge, square, gavel) used for the blows symbolize the blow on Christ's cheek, the flagellation, and the spear (or crown of thorns). Brethren assembled around Hiram's grave represent disciples lamenting Christ's death. The "lost Word" symbolizes the word pronounced on the cross, which the Jews could not comprehend. False brethren are represented by Judas. The sprig of cassia (acacia) represents the cross, said to be made of its wood.

The keystone, initially rejected by the builders for its unconventional shape and lack of a recognizable mark, ultimately becomes the 'head stone of the corner,' symbolizing that true value and importance may be overlooked or misunderstood at first. The scriptural passages reinforce this theme of vindication for the rejected. The 'new name' and the 'mark' on the keystone, along with the reference to the 'white stone' from Revelation, symbolize a unique identity, recognition, and a deeper, personal understanding or achievement within the Mark Master degree, signifying a special reward for those who overcome.

The 'cover grip' symbolizes the duty of Most Excellent Masters to cover the faults and imperfections of brethren with the 'broad mantle of charity and brotherly love', recognizing human frailties. The 'sign of admiration' alludes to the wonder and admiration of ancient brethren upon viewing the interior of King Solomon's magnificent Temple dedicated to the Supreme Being. The word 'Rabboni' signifies 'Good Master' or 'Most Excellent Master', originating from King Solomon's commendation of the master builder at the Temple's completion.

The reiteration of the number three in Masonic ceremonies, particularly the grand honors, is suggested to be in honor and reverence to the ancient Trinity. The number six is highly respected in the Most Excellent Master degree, appearing in various ritualistic actions such as candidate preparation, knocks, circumambulation, and the keystone ceremony, with historical parallels drawn to Druidic veneration. The keystone symbolizes completion, structural integrity, and the essential element of an arch, with its placement being a central act of the Most Excellent Master degree. The cloud of glory filling the Temple signifies Jehovah's divine presence and acceptance.

The secret vault, with its nine arches and connection to the sanctum sanctorum, symbolizes a hidden and sacred repository of divine and masonic knowledge. The deposited items, including the Ark of the Covenant and the Master's Word, represent foundational truths and the enduring nature of sacred principles, even when seemingly lost. It signifies the preservation of essential wisdom for future generations.

The three grand masonic pillars, Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty, are represented by Solomon, King of Israel (wisdom to contrive), Hiram, King of Tyre (strength to support), and Hiram Abiff (beauty to adorn). The three steps on the Master's carpet are emblematic of the three principal stages of human life: youth, manhood, and age. The setting maul, spade, and coffin are striking emblems of mortality. The sprig of acacia symbolizes the imperishable part of man that survives the grave, bearing affinity to the Supreme Intelligence that pervades all nature and never dies.

The text implies that Masonic lectures are a primary vehicle for conveying Masonic symbolism, arguing against the idea that symbolism exists independently or is better understood by dismissing the lectures.
entries.candidate_lesson:
An initiate in Freemasonry must approach its teachings not merely with intellectual understanding but also with emotional and spiritual engagement, integrating both mind and heart to truly grasp the moral and ethical truths presented.

The candidate learns to be fully committed to the Fraternity and to place complete faith in the Three Great Lights. They are taught the importance of Brotherly love, symbolized by a specific working tool. Through the Mystery Drama and assuming the role of Hiram Abiff, the candidate is meant to connect with the inner soul of the Fraternity and ancient traditions, leading to a profound self-realization.

The symbolism of 'Low Twelve' and the number twelve teaches about the cycles of life, the concept of completion and fulfillment in one's journey, and the inevitable passage of time, including the darkest hours, which can be interpreted as a reflection on mortality.

The section emphasizes the importance of peace and good fellowship among workmen, as exemplified by King Solomon's wise organization of the temple builders, which prevented envy, discord, or confusion. It also highlights the significance of divine guidance and deliverance, as symbolized by the temple's pillars, reminding candidates of historical and spiritual liberation.

As a Master Mason, one is bound by duty, honor, and gratitude to be faithful to their trust, uphold the dignity of their character, and enforce obedience to the Institution's tenets. This includes correcting errors of less informed brethren, preserving the fraternity's reputation through morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, and diligently guarding the ancient landmarks. The Master Mason must remain true to their vows and imitate the fidelity of Hiram Abiff.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.

The candidate learns the specific signs and words associated with the Master Mason degree, particularly the solemnity of the obligation and the importance of recognizing and responding to a brother's distress. The ritual of opening the Lodge and the recitation of Psalm 133 reinforce the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love, reminding members of the blessings that come from harmonious fellowship.

The Hiramic legend, central to the Master Mason degree, conveys lessons through the account of Hiram Abiff's death, burials, and resurrections. It emphasizes the importance of fidelity and the pursuit of truth, even in the face of adversity, and introduces themes of mortality and immortality.

The candidate is taught to exemplify virtue, fortitude, and integrity, drawing inspiration from the historical account of Grand Master Hiram Abiff's unwavering commitment to his trust, even when faced with mortal danger.

The candidate is taught the profound responsibilities and moral obligations that come with the Master Mason degree. These include maintaining personal integrity, providing moral leadership within the fraternity, upholding the reputation of Masonry, and diligently preserving its ancient traditions and principles. The lesson emphasizes the importance of setting a virtuous example and demonstrating unwavering fidelity, mirroring the legendary example of Hiram Abiff.

The candidate learns about the historical and structural grandeur of King Solomon's Temple, emphasizing the collaborative effort and diverse skills required for its construction. The pillars of Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty teach the necessity of these virtues in any great undertaking. The constitution and meeting places of the different lodges reinforce the hierarchical structure and progression within Masonry. The emblems provide moral and philosophical instruction, while the narrative of the lost word and its substitute underscores themes of loss, perseverance, and the eventual recovery of truth, encouraging continued pursuit of Masonic knowledge.

To be a 'bright Mason,' one must have knowledge of the alterations made in Masonry since Morgan's exposure. The critical notes on the Third Degree's legendary nature (hyperbolical, apocryphal, fabulous) encourage a deeper, allegorical understanding rather than a literal interpretation of its traditions and history. The mention of 'worldly possession' as a symbolic denotation may serve as a lesson against materialism.

The self-imposed penalties within Freemasonry may be understood in the context of ancient Hebrew covenant practices, emphasizing the profound solemnity and consequences of breaking sacred promises. The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, encouraging candidates to contemplate mortality, strive for ethical maturity, and embrace faith in salvation. The narrative of the Temple builders highlights the importance of fidelity and the severe repercussions of treachery.

The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state, emphasizing prudence and unshrinking fidelity. It inspires hope for a final reward for those who are 'just made perfect' and symbolizes being saved from iniquity and raised to the faith of salvation.

Candidates are taught the ancient and proper mode of being raised to the Master Mason Degree, understanding its historical traditions and the exemplification of Masonic virtue. They learn the meaning of the degree's emblems and the description of Solomon's Temple. The solemnity of lodge closing reinforces the "chief care of a Mason" and the importance of continuous Masonic instruction and improvement. Masons are reminded of their duties as men and Masons in their daily avocations and the importance of piety. The allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend teaches profound lessons about spiritual death and resurrection, the restoration of truth and morality, and fidelity in the face of adversity, drawing parallels to Christian themes of salvation and immortality. The distinction between literal history and allegorical truth is emphasized, guiding the candidate to seek deeper symbolic meaning.

The candidate is taught the importance of discerning true worth and recognizing value in things that may initially appear unconventional or rejected. The narrative of the keystone emphasizes that what is deemed unfit by some may be essential and invaluable. The investment with a 'new name' signifies a personal transformation and a unique identity within the Craft, highlighting individual merit and the reward for perseverance. The admonition regarding the grips instills the importance of fidelity and the proper transmission of Masonic knowledge.

The candidate learns the importance of upholding their solemn obligation, even under the threat of severe penalty. They are taught the virtues of charity and brotherly love, encouraging them to overlook the imperfections of others. The degree also instills a sense of admiration for the grand achievements of the ancient brethren, particularly the construction and dedication of King Solomon's Temple, and the recognition of excellence in craftsmanship.

Masons are encouraged to examine the meaning and original intention behind the ceremonies and practices, rather than merely performing them from habit. The degree emphasizes the completion of the Temple and the importance of the keystone, suggesting themes of perfection and essential elements in one's Masonic journey.

The narrative teaches the profound importance of safeguarding sacred knowledge and the 'Sacred Word,' even in the face of loss or inability to communicate it directly. It conveys that vital truths can be preserved in hidden places, awaiting discovery by those who are worthy and diligent in their search.

Candidates are encouraged to imitate Grand Master Hiram Abiff in his virtuous conduct, unfeigned piety to God, and inflexible fidelity to his trust. This imitation prepares them to welcome death as a kind messenger from the Supreme Grand Master, translating them from an imperfect lodge to a perfect, glorious, and celestial lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the universe.

The candidate should understand that Masonic lectures are a vital source for learning the history, philosophy, and symbolism of Freemasonry, and that their preservation and study are crucial for a complete understanding of the Craft, rather than being dismissed as mere rote memorization.
tradition_notes: Freemasonry traditionally teaches moral and ethical truths by veiling them in allegory and illustrating them with symbols, considering this the most assured method.
The engagement of both the brain/mind and the heart is a traditional expectation for initiates to fully comprehend Masonic teachings.
The legend of the Master Mason Degree is consistent with ancient Mystery Schools, suggesting a communion with archetypal forces that are the foundation of the tradition.

The symbolism encountered in this Degree can be traced back for hundreds, if not thousands, of years, with some elements being almost identical to ancient usage, while others have taken on cultural flavor from successive conveyors.
The symbolism discussed is rooted in ancient traditions, referencing the Zodiac and the cube as sources for the meaning of the number twelve.
Josephus is cited as an informant regarding the temple's construction duration and the absence of rain.
Sacred history is referenced for the detail that no tool sounds were heard during the temple's building.
The placement of the two pillars is presented as a tradition to memorialize the deliverance from Egyptian bondage and the pillars of fire and cloud.
The text refers to "ancient landmarks of the Institution," emphasizing the importance of preserving long-standing Masonic principles and traditions.
It also mentions "established usages and customs of the fraternity," highlighting adherence to traditional practices.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
The grand hailing sign of distress is specifically linked to the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff.
The specific sequence of gavel raps by the Master and Wardens is a traditional method for opening Lodges on different degrees.
The recitation of Psalm 133 and the response "Amen! So mote it be!" are traditional elements of the Lodge opening ceremony.
The traditional account of Hiram Abiff's death, several burials, and resurrections is central to the Master Mason Degree.
Hiram Abiff is described as one of the head workmen at King Solomon's Temple.
Ancient writings inform us that Hiram Abiff was an arbiter between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre.
The tragical death of Hiram Abiff is not recorded in the Bible, the writings of Josephus, or any other ancient writings known outside of Freemasonry.
Hiram Abiff was universally acknowledged as the third most distinguished man then living, and in many respects, the greatest man in the world.
The narrative describes the premeditated death of Hiram Abiff by fifteen Fellow Crafts who sought to obtain the secrets of a Master Mason before the Temple's completion.
The motive of the Fellow Crafts was to gain the ability to travel in foreign countries and receive Master's wages.
The Master Mason lecture is not generally given by the Master to the candidate on the night of his 'raising' due to its length.
The candidate generally receives the Master Mason lecture from brethren who are well posted in the work.
If time permits, the Master and Senior Warden usually go through the first section of the Master Mason lecture before closing the Lodge.
It is a tradition that during the seven years of the Temple's construction, it did not rain in the daytime.
The word 'Mah-Hah-Bone' is presented as the substitute for the lost word in the Master Mason's Degree.
The original lost word was reportedly found after 470 years and is used in the Royal Arch Degree.
The 'work' and lectures taught by Barney are prevalent in the Northwestern States (Michigan, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Iowa) and were adopted by the Baltimore Convention.
Brother John Barney obtained the Preston Lectures from Gleason in Boston, which were approved by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts in 1817.
Barney adapted his lectures to different systems prevailing in the Western and Southwestern States, gaining sanction from respective Grand Masters.
The 'Webb Work,' inculcated by Robert Morris, is generally accepted as being of ancient origin and is expected to be adopted by Grand Lodges throughout the United States.

The 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree have undergone several changes: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
There are variations in the Hiramic legend regarding the number of Fellow Crafts who went in search (fifteen vs. twelve) and whether the sprig of acacia was left by the murderers or recanters.
There are variations in the reported location where the lost body was found: near the seaside (York Masons), Mount Moriah (Americans), Monet Lebanon (French), or near Jerusalem (others).
Accounts differ regarding the brethren's dispersal during the search: some say they dispersed widely (east, west, and south), while others state they kept within hail of each other.

Alternative names for the ruffians are preserved in subsidiary degrees, including GRAVELOT, QUIBO, and AKIROP, and in the Degree of Elect of Fifteen, they are named JUBELA-KURMAVIL (a corruption of Cromwell), JUBELO-GRAVELOT, and JUBELUM-AKIROP.
There are differing traditions on who advised the change to the substitute word: some say King Solomon, while others affirm the three Fellow Crafts adopted it without consulting him.
The Hiramic legend is described as a 'fabulous narrative' used to promote the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, similar to 'spurious initiations'.
Traditions mention instances of treachery, violation of sacred pledges, and crime among Apprentices and Craftsmen during the Temple's construction.
The connection of the 'widow's son' (Hiram Abiff) with the building of the Temple was endeared to the two monarchs, leading to a new arrangement of discipline and a legend to preserve his memory.
It was customary for Hiram Abiff to retire to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Some rituals taught that Hiram Abiff divided operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters, paying them at the pillar of Jachin and in the Middle Chamber.
Traditions state that the Ark of the Covenant had not been removed to the Holy of Holies at the time Hiram supposedly entered it, as this occurred at the dedication.
The description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has evolved over time, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.
Different Masonic jurisdictions (United States, Scotland, France) have varying orders for the "attacks" or "entrances" in the ritual.
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors at all.

The Christian allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend is explicitly stated to be "borne out in the higher Degrees of sublime Masonry," specifically the 32nd Degree (Prince of the Royal Secret) in Continental nomenclature.

The historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death during the Temple's construction is challenged by scriptural references (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) which indicate he was alive at the Temple's completion.
Some Lodges do not require all brethren to rise after the Right Worshipful Master gives four raps, choosing instead to keep their seats.
The degree is founded on Masonic tradition described in the ancient Book of Constitutions.
The Temple was finished in seven years and six months, with the cope-stone celebrated by the fraternity with great joy.
The joy was interrupted by the sudden death of Master Hiram Abiff, who was interred near the Temple.
After mourning Hiram Abiff, the tabernacle of Moses and its holy relics were lodged in the Temple.
The grip originated at the completion of the Temple when King Solomon, pleased with the master builder, exclaimed 'Hail, Rabboni'.
A procession was formed, the ark safely seated, the cap stone placed in the principal arch, and the Lodge closed with solemn invocations to Deity.
It is an established doctrine of the Order that while three form a Lodge, five may hold it, seven only can make it perfect.
The 'Excellent Master' degree is noted as being used by 'transatlantic brethren'.
The practice of thrice-repeated actions and the significance of the number three are compared to the Church of Rome's customs, specifically an Ursuline Convent rule of three knocks.

The veneration of the number six by Druids, including their principal ceremonies on the sixth day of the moon and gathering mistletoe in groups of six, is presented as a historical parallel to Masonic practices.
Historical discoveries in Ethiopia and Egypt show arches with keystones predating King Solomon's Temple, providing ancient context for this architectural element.
This historical account is sourced from 'Fellows's Inquiry into the Origin, History, and Purport of Freemasonry,' pp. 308, 309.

The text includes an extract from an Annual Address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, G. M. of Vermont, discussing the history and transmission of Masonic lectures, specifically noting Webb's abridgment and rearrangement of Preston's lectures.
It mentions that Webb learned and taught Preston's lectures in full, as well as his own abridgment.
Reference is made to a 'copy in key' of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason.
A 'very rare' book by J. Browne, published in London in 1802, called the 'Master Key,' containing lectures in an 'abstruse cypher,' is also noted.
The text discusses the historical transmission and authenticity of Masonic lectures, specifically the lineage from Preston to Webb and Gleason.
It highlights the practice of Grand Lecturers, such as Benjamin Gleason, being commissioned to instruct lodges.
The importance of 'ancient landmarks' in the communication of lectures is emphasized.
Reference is made to historical Masonic publications like 'Free Mason's Monitor' by T. S. Webb, 'Masonic Trestleboard', and J. Browne's 'Master Key'.
The role of Grand Lodges (England, Massachusetts and Maine) in overseeing and potentially authenticating lecture content is noted.
Preston of England's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements' are noted for defining the 'immovable landmarks' of the ancient Society.
Brother Benjamin Gleason, in association with Thomas Smith Webb, introduced the Prestonian system of work and lectures into the Lodges of New England and other sections of the country.
In 1804, the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard of work.
Brother Gleason was exclusively employed by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts during 1804 to communicate the ritual to lodges under its jurisdiction, including what is now the State of Maine.
Benjamin Gleason continued his labors as a lecturer until his death in 1847.
Charles W. Moore, Editor of the Freemasons' Monthly Magazine, acknowledged Benjamin Gleason as a master and teacher in the purest and most perfect Masonic ritual of ancient Craft Masonry.
The 'work' of Masonry, as revived by Preston, was approved and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England near the close of the last century.
The 'union' in 1813 led to a revision of the ritual for reconciliation purposes.
The verbal ritual, as revised by Preston, was introduced to the US around 1803.
The system was received and preserved, especially in older jurisdictions, essentially as it came from the original source of Craft Masonry.
Discussion of the historical lectures by Preston and their abridgment by Webb, highlighting their role in transmitting Masonic knowledge.
Emphasis on the importance of preserving Masonic lectures intact as a repository of history, philosophy, and symbolism.
Reference to the role of a Grand Lecturer in comparing and evaluating different versions of Masonic lectures.
caution_notes: Do not swerve from your duty.
Do not violate your vows.
Do not betray your trust.
Never suffer the ancient landmarks to be infringed.
Never countenance a deviation from the established usages and customs of the fraternity.
Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.
Guard uninformed brethren against a breach of fidelity.
Let no motive make you swerve from your duty, violate your vows, or betray your trust.

The 'work' in Minnesota and New York is 'strongly impregnated with what is called, among Masons, "Morganry"--very similar to the disclosures of Morgan and Richardson,' implying a caution regarding unauthorized disclosures.
The Third Degree's traditions are 'unfortunately hyperbolical; its history apocryphal, and its legends fabulous,' cautioning against literal interpretations.
Professor Stuart endeavored to show that the legend of the Third Degree is an 'imposture' due to the Latin origin of the criminals' names, not Hebrew.
The text notes that the uniformity of Freemasonry on certain subjects is a 'delicate point' and difficult to clarify for brethren, implying a need for discretion regarding ritual variations.
CASSIA is sometimes improperly and ignorantly used for acacia.
The interpolated word (substitute for the lost word) is not a real word in any language, but merely a jumble of letters forming a sound without meaning.

Masons should not literally believe Dr. Anderson's explanation of the exhumation of H.A.B. as a real historical fact, as Hiram Abiff was known to be alive years after the Temple's completion, indicating the story is allegorical.

The historical veracity of the Hiramic legend is questioned, with references to Dr. Anderson and Josephus stating Hiram Abiff lived to complete the Temple and died in Tyre, contradicting the ritual narrative.

Inconsistencies in ritual narratives are highlighted, such as Hiram Abiff's supposed entry into the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the relevant architectural features (pillars, Middle Chamber) were built.
A divergence from uniformity is noted in describing the places where the instruments of Hiram's murder were supposedly used.

The author explicitly states that the tradition of Hiram's death during the Temple's construction is "merely allegorical" and not historically factual, citing scriptural evidence (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) that Hiram was alive at the Temple's completion. This serves as a caution against a literal interpretation of the legend.
The candidate is admonished never to give the words of the grip in any way but that in which he received them.
