The circle of letters, originally Hiram Abiff's mark, is now a general mark of the degree, where each brother places his own private mark.
The traditional price of a brother's mark is a Jewish half shekel of silver, equivalent to one-quarter of a dollar.
The Mark Master degree was founded by Grand Masters Solomon, Hiram King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff.
The degree's purpose is to provide an honorary reward for zeal, fidelity, and attachment to Masonry, and to ensure no worthy brother suffers extreme indigence.
The moral teachings of the chisel and mallet are noted as "Monitorial," indicating they are part of standard Masonic instruction.
The pillars Jachin and Boaz are referenced (see pp. 71 and 83).
The sign of admiration or astonishment is compared to that expressed by the Queen of Sheba upon first viewing Solomon's Temple (see p. 203).
A passage of Scripture from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4 is read by the Right Worshipful Master, describing the fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the Temple after Solomon's prayer.
The Master is identified as the 'high priest of the Chapter'.
The ceremony involves specific readings from Psalms 134 and 23.
The degree is founded upon the ceremonies of the dedication of King Solomon's Temple.
The degree is founded on Masonic tradition described in the ancient Book of Constitutions.
The Temple was finished in seven years and six months, with the cope-stone celebrated by the fraternity with great joy.
The joy was interrupted by the sudden death of Master Hiram Abiff, who was interred near the Temple.
After mourning Hiram Abiff, the tabernacle of Moses and its holy relics were lodged in the Temple.
The grip originated at the completion of the Temple when King Solomon, pleased with the master builder, exclaimed 'Hail, Rabboni'.
A procession was formed, the ark safely seated, the cap stone placed in the principal arch, and the Lodge closed with solemn invocations to Deity.
It is an established doctrine of the Order that while three form a Lodge, five may hold it, seven only can make it perfect.
The 'Excellent Master' degree is noted as being used by 'transatlantic brethren'.
The practice of thrice-repeated actions and the significance of the number three are compared to the Church of Rome's customs, specifically an Ursuline Convent rule of three knocks.

The veneration of the number six by Druids, including their principal ceremonies on the sixth day of the moon and gathering mistletoe in groups of six, is presented as a historical parallel to Masonic practices.
Historical discoveries in Ethiopia and Egypt show arches with keystones predating King Solomon's Temple, providing ancient context for this architectural element.
The Chapter represents a Tabernacle erected by ancient brethren near the ruins of King Solomon's temple.
The three former officers represent ancient brethren who formed the first Most Grand Council at Jerusalem.
The three latter officers represent ancient brethren who brought to light principal secrets buried since the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff until the erection of the second temple.
The veils allude to the four tribes of the children of Israel who bore banners through the wilderness.
The method of identification for the children of Israel involved words and signs given by God to Moses.

The ritual references the progression through previous degrees: Duly initiated (Entered Apprentice), Passed to the Degree of Fellow Craft, Raised to the Sublime Degree of Master Mason, Advanced to the Degree of Mark Master Mason, Regularly passed the Chair (Past Master), Received and acknowledged as Most Excellent Masters.

The historical narrative includes the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, King of Babylon, in the eleventh year of Zedekiah, King of Jerusalem, the seventy-year captivity, and the deliverance by Cyrus, King of Persia.
The narrative connects the participants to the Giblemites, who were skilled stonemasons involved in the construction of King Solomon's Temple.
It references the historical events of the destruction of the First Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, the Babylonian captivity, and the liberation by Cyrus, King of Persia, for the purpose of rebuilding.
The ritual incorporates specific biblical accounts from Exodus regarding Moses' divine commission and the signs given to him.
The explicit mention of "Most Excellent Masters" and "Royal Arch Captain" places this ritual firmly within the Royal Arch degree tradition.

The narrative references a Grand Council decree and the 'children of the captivity,' which are characteristic elements of Royal Arch Masonry, often associated with the rebuilding of the Temple after the Babylonian captivity.
The mention of 'Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, and Scribe' and 'Most Excellent Masters' indicates a degree beyond the Master Mason, specifically within the Royal Arch system.
The use of 'masters of the first, second and third veils' and a 'signet' are specific ritualistic elements found in Royal Arch Masonry.

The identification of the three squares as 'masters' jewels' worn by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff directly links this narrative to the core figures and themes of the Hiramic legend.
This historical account is sourced from 'Fellows's Inquiry into the Origin, History, and Purport of Freemasonry,' pp. 308, 309.
The tradition respecting the payment of the work-men's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple is mentioned, though its authority and calculations are unknown.
Information regarding the Triple Tau is quoted from a popular Masonic journal, not Dr. Oliver's Dictionary, and embodies the sentiments of the fraternity.

The text includes an extract from an Annual Address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, G. M. of Vermont, discussing the history and transmission of Masonic lectures, specifically noting Webb's abridgment and rearrangement of Preston's lectures.
It mentions that Webb learned and taught Preston's lectures in full, as well as his own abridgment.
Reference is made to a 'copy in key' of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason.
A 'very rare' book by J. Browne, published in London in 1802, called the 'Master Key,' containing lectures in an 'abstruse cypher,' is also noted.
The text discusses the historical transmission and authenticity of Masonic lectures, specifically the lineage from Preston to Webb and Gleason.
It highlights the practice of Grand Lecturers, such as Benjamin Gleason, being commissioned to instruct lodges.
The importance of 'ancient landmarks' in the communication of lectures is emphasized.
Reference is made to historical Masonic publications like 'Free Mason's Monitor' by T. S. Webb, 'Masonic Trestleboard', and J. Browne's 'Master Key'.
The role of Grand Lodges (England, Massachusetts and Maine) in overseeing and potentially authenticating lecture content is noted.
Preston of England's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements' are noted for defining the 'immovable landmarks' of the ancient Society.
Brother Benjamin Gleason, in association with Thomas Smith Webb, introduced the Prestonian system of work and lectures into the Lodges of New England and other sections of the country.
In 1804, the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard of work.
Brother Gleason was exclusively employed by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts during 1804 to communicate the ritual to lodges under its jurisdiction, including what is now the State of Maine.
Benjamin Gleason continued his labors as a lecturer until his death in 1847.
Charles W. Moore, Editor of the Freemasons' Monthly Magazine, acknowledged Benjamin Gleason as a master and teacher in the purest and most perfect Masonic ritual of ancient Craft Masonry.
The 'work' of Masonry, as revived by Preston, was approved and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England near the close of the last century.
The 'union' in 1813 led to a revision of the ritual for reconciliation purposes.
The verbal ritual, as revised by Preston, was introduced to the US around 1803.
The system was received and preserved, especially in older jurisdictions, essentially as it came from the original source of Craft Masonry.
Discussion of the historical lectures by Preston and their abridgment by Webb, highlighting their role in transmitting Masonic knowledge.
Emphasis on the importance of preserving Masonic lectures intact as a repository of history, philosophy, and symbolism.
Reference to the role of a Grand Lecturer in comparing and evaluating different versions of Masonic lectures.
caution_notes: The dire consequences of the Ruffians' actions serve as a caution against attempting to obtain knowledge or rewards through illicit or unearned means.
A Clandestine Lodge is one that is not working with a legitimate charter from a Grand Lodge and is considered 'in the dark'.
Only Brethren from RECOGNIZED Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.
The circle is the boundary line beyond which a Mason is never to suffer his passions or prejudices to betray him.
When the service is used in connection with that of the Knight Templar or the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite, all preceding parts of the ritual (before the note) should be omitted.
The Lodge should practice the mode of giving the Funeral Grand Honors before leaving its hall, ensuring each brother pronounces the words audibly.
Singing and holding the open Bible by the Chaplain are optional at the Master's discretion.

The Worshipful Master explicitly warns the candidate of 'amazing trials and dangers', a 'rough and rugged road to travel, beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers', and the possibility of losing his life, stating it 'will not be the first instance of the kind'.
The candidate is not intentionally injured in any Degree of Masonry. The blow is simulated with a buckskin bag, not a real setting-maul.
The fall is safely caught by a canvas held by brethren, ensuring the candidate is unharmed.
King Solomon threatens to deem the twelve Fellow Crafts as murderers and make them suffer if they fail to find the ruffians.
The graphic and severe nature of the self-condemnations uttered by the ruffians, detailing gruesome physical punishments.
The practice of using a large drum or rolling a cannon-ball to represent the execution sounds is not practiced in city Lodges.
The use of a large drum or cannon-ball for sound effects during the ruffians' execution is not practiced in city Lodges.
In some Lodges, the Master does not make the sign of distress first at the body, but only gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body with the thumb inward.
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.

The 'work' in Minnesota and New York is 'strongly impregnated with what is called, among Masons, "Morganry"--very similar to the disclosures of Morgan and Richardson,' implying a caution regarding unauthorized disclosures.
The Third Degree's traditions are 'unfortunately hyperbolical; its history apocryphal, and its legends fabulous,' cautioning against literal interpretations.
Professor Stuart endeavored to show that the legend of the Third Degree is an 'imposture' due to the Latin origin of the criminals' names, not Hebrew.
The text notes that the uniformity of Freemasonry on certain subjects is a 'delicate point' and difficult to clarify for brethren, implying a need for discretion regarding ritual variations.
CASSIA is sometimes improperly and ignorantly used for acacia.
The interpolated word (substitute for the lost word) is not a real word in any language, but merely a jumble of letters forming a sound without meaning.

Masons should not literally believe Dr. Anderson's explanation of the exhumation of H.A.B. as a real historical fact, as Hiram Abiff was known to be alive years after the Temple's completion, indicating the story is allegorical.

The historical veracity of the Hiramic legend is questioned, with references to Dr. Anderson and Josephus stating Hiram Abiff lived to complete the Temple and died in Tyre, contradicting the ritual narrative.

Inconsistencies in ritual narratives are highlighted, such as Hiram Abiff's supposed entry into the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the relevant architectural features (pillars, Middle Chamber) were built.
A divergence from uniformity is noted in describing the places where the instruments of Hiram's murder were supposedly used.

The author explicitly states that the tradition of Hiram's death during the Temple's construction is "merely allegorical" and not historically factual, citing scriptural evidence (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) that Hiram was alive at the Temple's completion. This serves as a caution against a literal interpretation of the legend.
The candidate is admonished never to give the words of the grip in any way but that in which he received them.
An impostor discovered demanding wages not due should have his right hand chopped off.
The penalty for unlawfully divulging any of the secrets of this degree includes suffering the right ear to be smote off.
An additional penalty for an imposter who divulges secrets unlawfully is to have the right hand stricken off.
Candidates are cautioned to be careful not to forfeit the esteem of the brethren through misconduct and inattention to the rules of the Lodge.
The candidate is cautioned against forfeiting the brethren's esteem by misconduct and inattention to the rules.
The penalty of the obligation for this degree involves having one's breast torn open, heart torn out, and exposed to rot upon the dung hill, if the secrets are divulged unlawfully.

Guards were stationed to take 'special pains that none pass or repass, except such as were duly qualified,' indicating a strict requirement for proper authorization and qualification to enter sacred spaces or the presence of principal officers.
The principal secrets of this Degree should be communicated only under a living arch.

The tradition respecting the payment of the work-men's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple may not be accurate, with the probability that it was fabricated in a subsequent age without authenticating documents.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five points of fellowship and heard in a low breath, emphasizing secrecy and proper ritualistic context.
Do not 'pin their faith' upon the mere negations of any writer, whatever may be the strength of his masonic reputation, but rather examine and investigate matters of fact and principle.
The revised Prestonian system after the 1813 union had many incongruities and departures from the original.
Many unwarrantable liberties have been taken with the ritual, and innovations have crept in.
The ritual has had to contend against the corrupting influences of ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications.
Caution against undervaluing Masonic lectures and dismissing them as mere rote memorization.
Warning against attempts to inaugurate a 'new era' that seeks to change or disregard established Masonic history, philosophy, and symbolism as contained in the lectures.

entries.title: Hiram in Scripture
entries.short_summary: This section details scriptural accounts of Hiram, specifically from 2 Chronicles 2:11-14 and 1 Kings 7:13-14, describing his diverse skills in various materials, his parentage as a widow's son from the tribe of Naphtali with a Tyrian father, and his role in building Solomon's Temple. It clarifies that 'Abif' means 'chief' or 'head' and notes the historical reputation of Tyrians and Sidonians as skilled mechanics and artists. The section also mentions Albert Pike's likely incorrect theory attributing Hiram to a solar deity.
entries.full_summary:
This section details scriptural accounts of Hiram, specifically from 2 Chronicles 2:11-14 and 1 Kings 7:13-14, describing his diverse skills in various materials, his parentage as a widow's son from the tribe of Naphtali with a Tyrian father, and his role in building Solomon's Temple. It clarifies that 'Abif' means 'chief' or 'head' and notes the historical reputation of Tyrians and Sidonians as skilled mechanics and artists. The section also mentions Albert Pike's likely incorrect theory attributing Hiram to a solar deity.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: The Lion of the Tribe of Judah

This section explores the symbolism of the lion, particularly as the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah'. It details its historical association with might, royalty, the Hebrew Tribe of Judah, and King Solomon. The text also delves into its medieval symbolism of resurrection, referencing tales of lion cubs being brought to life by their father's breath and roaring lions representing the resurrection on the Last Day. The lion's majestic nature and association with the sun are also noted.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0066 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section congratulates a Brother on being passed to the Second Degree, emphasizing that Freemasonry values internal qualifications over external ones. It highlights the institution's resilience, having survived the destruction of Solomon's magnificent Temple. The text explains that Masonic mysteries are transmitted through attentive listening and faithful preservation, using architectural tools and symbolic emblems to impart wise truths across generations. It concludes by reminding all Masons, from the youngest Entered Apprentice to the Worshipful Master, to humbly bow before the sacred name of Deity.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0066 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pronunciation Key

This section provides a pronunciation key for various words, many of which are common in Masonic ritual and discourse. It includes rules for indicating accented syllables and short vowel sounds, followed by an alphabetical list of words with their phonetic pronunciations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mason.

This section details the duegard and sign of a Master Mason, which alludes to the penalty of the obligation, and the grand hailing sign of distress. It describes the physical actions for each sign, including the accompanying words for the grand hailing sign of distress, "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?" This sign is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The text also outlines the specific gavel raps used by the Master and Wardens to open a Lodge on the First, Second, and Third Degrees, concluding with the Master reciting a passage of Scripture (Psalm 133) about brethren dwelling in unity, followed by the brethren's response.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0051 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Master Mason: Opening, Obligation, Overview

This section introduces the Master Mason degree, focusing on the traditional narrative of Hiram Abiff's death, multiple burials, and resurrections. It highlights the absence of Hiram Abiff's death in external historical records like the Bible or Josephus, emphasizing his significant role as an arbiter and a distinguished figure. The text also describes a Master Mason's Lodge as the 'Sanctum Sanctorum' of King Solomon's Temple and details the ritualistic opening where both points of the compasses are elevated above the square. Candidate preparation for this sublime degree is typically performed by the Junior Deacon and Stewards.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0051 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation for the Third Section Drama

This section details the elaborate preparation for the dramatic climax of the Master Mason degree. It involves specific props, clearing the lodge, dressing the candidate with a Senior Warden's jewel, and a deliberate deception to make him believe the ceremony is complete. The Worshipful Master then reveals that the candidate is not yet a Master Mason and must undergo severe trials to prove his fidelity, emphasizing self-reliance and trust in a higher power. The candidate is hoodwinked, prays, and then embarks on a symbolic journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor who introduces the legend of Hiram Abiff and his daily ritual at King Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0062 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Hiram Legend: Flight and Capture of the Ruffians

This section details the immediate aftermath of Hiram Abiff's death, focusing on the three ruffians' attempt to escape. They are thwarted in their attempt to secure passage on a ship due to lacking a pass from King Solomon, leading them to decide to flee inland. Meanwhile, in the Lodge, the absence of Hiram Abiff is discovered, causing work to cease. King Solomon orders a search, and a roll call of the craftsmen reveals three Fellow Crafts, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, are missing, identifying them as the perpetrators.

This section details the dramatic events following the attack on Hiram Abiff. Twelve Fellow Crafts confess to King Solomon their part in a conspiracy to extort Master Mason secrets from Hiram Abiff, though they recanted their murderous intent. King Solomon pardons them but tasks them with finding the three persistent ruffians, dividing them into search parties. During their search, one party encounters a sea-captain who describes three workmen seeking passage without King Solomon's pass. After reporting this, King Solomon reiterates his command, threatening the twelve with punishment if the ruffians are not found. The Fellow Crafts, expressing their fear of unjust punishment, continue their search and discover a newly broken grave marked by acacia, where the candidate is lying. Overhearing, they hear the distinct self-condemnations of the three ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and the third, expressing remorse and detailing their desired gruesome penalties.

This section details the pursuit, capture, and sentencing of the three ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, who murdered Grand Master Hiram Abiff. Three craftsmen overhear the ruffians' confessions and self-imprecations, leading to their apprehension. They are brought before King Solomon, where they confess their guilt and are sentenced to death according to their own curses. The execution of the ruffians is then ritually represented for the candidate, involving specific actions and sounds in the ante-room, followed by a report to King Solomon and an order to search for Hiram's body.

This section details the ritualistic execution of the three ruffians responsible for the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, followed by the search for his body. King Solomon orders the execution, which is performed with specific ritualistic sounds and actions in the ante-room, representing the dying groans of Jubela and the other two ruffians. Subsequently, twelve Fellow Crafts are dispatched to find Hiram's body and ascertain if any Master's Word or key is present. They locate the mangled body, recognize their limited knowledge as Fellow Crafts regarding the Master's Degree, and discover the jewel of Hiram's office.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0062 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0064 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Funeral Dirge and Prayer

This section details a Masonic funeral dirge and prayer ritual, focusing on the allegorical raising of a deceased Master Mason. The ceremony involves brethren forming a circle around the body, the Master making the grand hailing sign of distress, and a declaration that the Master's word is lost. Attempts by the Junior and Senior Wardens to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips fail due to the body's state of putrefaction. The ritual includes a solemn dirge sung while marching with the sun, and concludes with a prayer recited by the Master, emphasizing themes of mortality and hope for a 'Lodge on High'.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0064 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0065 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Prayer.

This section presents a prayer reflecting on human mortality, the brevity of life, and the need for divine compassion and salvation, explicitly stated to be offered around the grave of Hiram Abiff, the Widow's Son. Following the prayer, the ritual describes the raising of the candidate by the Master using the 'strong grip, or lion's paw, of the tribe of Judah,' also known as the 'real grip of a Master Mason.' The candidate is raised to a standing position on the 'five points of fellowship,' receives the 'grand Masonic word' whispered in their ear, and has the hoodwink removed, symbolizing the reception of light.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0065 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Critical Notes on the Third Degree

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing the historical variations in Masonic 'work' and lectures across different U.S. states and Europe. It highlights the influence of Brother John Barney's lectures, derived from the Preston Lectures, and their adoption in the Northwestern states, noting how Barney adapted his teachings to different systems in the Western and Southwestern states. The text also mentions the impact of 'Morganry' in Minnesota and New York, where rituals closely resemble Morgan's disclosures. It asserts that while minor differences exist, the essential points of Masonry are universal. Footnotes offer critical commentary on the Third Degree's nature, describing it as traditionary, historical, and legendary, but also hyperbolical, apocryphal, and fabulous. They include practical details of a ritualistic element involving a sword, discuss the symbolism of worldly possession and the historical connections of Tubal Cain, Jubal, and Vulcan. Detailed descriptions of Master's Lodge regalia and officer jewels are provided, alongside the historical evolution of the 'fatal weapons' used in the Third Degree. The section also references Professor Stuart's critique regarding the linguistic origins of names in the legend and notes variations in the narrative concerning the number of Fellow Crafts searching for Hiram and the placement of the sprig of acacia.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing variations in the Hiramic legend, including the disputed location of the lost body and the names of the ruffians. It clarifies the symbolic use of acacia, distinguishing it from cassia, and explains its significance in Jewish law for marking graves. The text also connects Masonic penalties to ancient Hebrew covenant ceremonies, describing their ritualistic elements. Furthermore, it discusses the allegorical nature of the Master Mason degree, particularly regarding the lost word and the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, cautioning against literal historical interpretations of the legend.

This section critically examines the historical and internal consistency of the Hiramic legend within the Third Degree. It challenges the historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death and burial, citing Dr. Anderson and Josephus, who assert Hiram lived to complete the Temple and returned to Tyre. The text also highlights inconsistencies in ritual narratives, such as Hiram drawing plans in the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the pillars and Middle Chamber were built. Despite these critiques, the section reaffirms the symbolic importance of the legend in teaching the doctrine of resurrection, fidelity, and a future state, representing a Master Mason as one saved from iniquity and raised to faith.

This section provides critical notes on the Third Degree, detailing historical variations in ritual descriptions of the "three principal entrances" to the Temple across different jurisdictions and time periods. It outlines the structure of the Master Mason lecture, covering the mode of raising, historical traditions, and emblem explanations. A significant portion is dedicated to an extensive Christian allegorical interpretation of the Third Degree, linking Hiram Abiff's death and resurrection to the Christian dispensation, the state of religion before Christ, and its restoration by the Saviour. This allegory is further elaborated with specific parallels drawn from the 32nd Degree, connecting Hiram's fate to Christ's condemnation, suffering, and the symbolism of the lost Word, false brethren, and the sprig of cassia. The text also includes a description of the solemn closing ceremony of a Mason's Lodge and an author's note emphasizing the allegorical nature of the Hiramic legend over its literal historical accuracy, citing scriptural evidence. Finally, it briefly defines the role of a Grand Lecturer.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0083 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master's Song.

This section details the 'Mark Master's Song' and a significant ritualistic drama involving a candidate attempting to receive wages. The song calls Mark Masters to present their work for inspection by the Chief Overseer and Chief Architect, emphasizing the importance of approved work and the right to wages. It references Hiram, the widow's son, and the keystone. The ritual reenacts a scenario where a candidate, after the second verse of the song, attempts to receive a penny from the Senior Warden through a lattice-window, using a specific hand gesture. The candidate is accused of being an 'impostor' for not being able to give the proper token, leading to a dramatic threat of having his hand struck off. The Senior Deacon intercedes, vouching for the candidate as a craftsman and promising to take him to the Right Worshipful Master. The candidate then proves himself a Fellow Craft Mason. The Right Worshipful Master sternly reprimands the candidate for attempting to receive wages without the token, but remits the penalty due to youth and inexperience, ordering the Senior Deacon to teach him how to bring up a regularly wrought stone in the quarries. The reprimand itself is often omitted in modern lodges but is included for historical context. The section concludes with the preparation of the candidate for the degree, including divesting of apparel, money, and valuables, baring the breast, a cable-tow four times around the body, and blindfolding with a hoodwink, followed by four distinct knocks at the door.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0088 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0107 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Excellent Master: Ceremony of Completion

This section details the 'Ceremony of Completion' for the Most Excellent Master degree, celebrating the symbolic completion of the Temple. It includes the singing of an 'Excellent Master's Song' which marks the end of labor and the laying of the cope-stone and keystone. The ritual involves placing the keystone into an arch supported by pillars Jachin and Boaz, secured with six gavel raps. Brethren divest themselves of working tools and regalia, hanging them on the arch, signifying the completion of their work. The Ark is brought forward and placed on the altar with a pot of incense. The ceremony culminates in the acceptance and investiture of worthy candidates as Most Excellent Masters, a sign of admiration or astonishment, a prayer for divine presence, and a reading from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4, followed by a ritual involving fire and repeated declarations of God's mercy. The Master then delivers a charge to the candidate, emphasizing the duty to dispense light and truth and the importance of acquiring knowledge.

This section details the completion and closing ceremonies of the Most Excellent Master degree, including specific ritualistic actions, a charge to the newly admitted candidate, and readings from Psalms. The charge emphasizes the candidate's duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons and to diligently acquire knowledge. The closing ritual involves brethren assembling around the altar, kneeling, joining hands, and performing balancing movements, followed by a lecture explaining the significance of the cap stone and the six distinct knocks for admission.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0107 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0112 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: the Burning Bush and the Captivity

This section details a Royal Arch ritual reenacting two pivotal biblical narratives: Moses' encounter with the Burning Bush at Mount Horeb, where he is instructed to remove his shoes from holy ground, and the Babylonian Captivity. The ritual portrays the destruction of Jerusalem and its Temple due to the transgressions of King Zedekiah and the people, followed by Cyrus's proclamation allowing the return to Jerusalem to rebuild the Second Temple. Candidates are guided through these events, experiencing a simulated siege and captivity before being symbolically liberated.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0112 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0123 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section details the ritualistic preparation and reception of a candidate for the Most Sublime Degree of a Royal Arch Mason. It describes the candidate's attire, the seven distinct knocks at the Chapter door, the required qualifications including previous degrees, and the significance of the pass word 'Rabboni'. The ritual proceeds with reception under a living arch, kneeling at the altar for an obligation, and symbolic representations such as the burning bush and the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, followed by the captivity and deliverance by Cyrus. The journey through 'rough and rugged paths' symbolizing the sojourning of the children of Israel is also described, leading to the first obstruction at the first veil.

This section details the ritualistic progression of "worthy sojourners" through three veils and then to the fourth veil or sanctuary. The narrative identifies the participants as Giblemites, descendants of those who completed the First Temple, endured Babylonian captivity, and are now liberated by Cyrus to assist in rebuilding the house of the Lord without expectation of reward. Admission through each veil is granted by specific words, signs, and explanations, which are derived from biblical accounts of Moses receiving divine signs (rod to serpent, hand to leprosy, water to blood). The journey culminates in an encounter with the Royal Arch Captain at the sanctuary.

This section details a candidate's journey to the Grand Council, where they are examined on their lineage and proficiency. They are tasked with removing rubbish from ancient ruins, leading to a series of significant discoveries. First, a keystone believed to be from King Solomon's Temple is found. Subsequently, after a perilous descent into an arch using a cable tow, three squares identified as masters' jewels of Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff are recovered. A second descent reveals a gold-overlaid trunk with mysterious characters, all promising further important revelations for the Craft.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0123 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0125 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Historical Notes

This section details the historical account of a secret underground vault constructed by King Solomon, leading from his private apartment to beneath the Temple's sanctum sanctorum. This vault was divided into nine arches, with the ninth arch designated for grand council meetings and as a repository for copies of sacred items from the sanctum sanctorum. After its completion, the three Grand Masters deposited significant craft items, including the Ark of the Covenant, a pot of manna, Aaron's rod, and the book of the law. Following the assassination of Grand Master Hiram Abiff and the subsequent loss of the Master's Word, the two remaining kings agreed to place the Sacred Word in this secret vault to ensure its preservation and potential future discovery alongside the other treasures.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0125 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Notes and Authorities

This section provides an appendix of notes and authorities, covering various aspects of the Master Mason degree. It details the strong grip of a Master Mason, the three Grand Masonic Pillars (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) and their representation by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. It also describes the construction of the Temple, including the number of columns, pilasters, and workers by degree. The text explains the symbolism of the three steps on the Master's carpet and the nine classes of Master's emblems, specifically focusing on the setting maul, spade, coffin, and sprig of acacia as emblems of morality and immortality. Finally, it includes a historical note on the transmission and abridgment of Masonic lectures by figures like Webb, Preston, Gleason, and J. Browne.

This section, an address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, Grand Master of Vermont, defends the authenticity and lineage of Masonic lectures, specifically those transmitted through T. S. Webb and Benjamin Gleason, asserting their connection to Preston's original lectures. Tucker addresses criticisms regarding his previous statements, clarifying that Webb abridged and rearranged Preston's lectures but retained their substance. He cites his possession of 'key' copies of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason. Tucker also defends the claim that Gleason's lectures were approved by the Grand Lodge of England, referencing Gleason's own published letter from 1843, where Gleason states he acquired complete knowledge of the three degrees' lectures from Webb and successfully communicated them across the United States and Europe, 'according to the ancient landmarks,' without objection. He further supports Gleason's knowledge of Preston by quoting Gleason's 1812 oration, which praises Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements'. The discussion also mentions J. Browne's 1802 'Master Key' as a presumed source for Prestonian lectures.

This section discusses the historical presence and influence of Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and the 'Prestonian system of work and lectures' in the United States, particularly in New England. It highlights Benjamin Gleason's crucial role, alongside Thomas Smith Webb, in introducing and promulgating this system. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard in 1804, employing Gleason to communicate it to lodges under its jurisdiction. Gleason continued as a dedicated lecturer until his death in 1847, remembered as a master teacher of ancient Craft Masonry. The author defends the existence of Prestonian lectures in the US against a critic, emphasizing the importance of factual investigation over mere negation.

This section discusses the history and transmission of the Prestonian system of Masonic ritual. It notes that the system, as revived by Preston and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England, underwent a revision in 1813 for reconciliation purposes, which led to some incongruities. The original Prestonian ritual was brought to the United States around 1803 by two English brethren, one of whom was a pupil of Preston. It was communicated to Thomas Smith Webb and subsequently imparted to Brother Gleason, who then taught it to lodges in Massachusetts. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system, which has since been the recognized Masonic work in the Commonwealth. Despite some modifications and innovations, the ritual has been largely preserved in its original purity, especially in older jurisdictions. The text also highlights the ongoing struggle against corrupting influences from ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications, emphasizing the importance of preserving the ritual's integrity.

This section discusses the nature and importance of Masonic lectures, specifically comparing Preston's lengthy lectures with Webb's abridgment. The author, through the Grand Lecturer's opinion, suggests that Webb's version retains practical value and is more manageable. The text criticizes those who undervalue lectures, labeling proponents of their preservation as 'parrot Masons' and advocating for a 'new era' focused solely on the philosophy, history, and symbolism of Masonry, arguing that these elements are already embedded within the traditional lectures.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
practical_elements: Hiram King of Tyre sent Huram-Abi to King Solomon.
Huram-Abi was trained to work in gold, silver, bronze, iron, stone, wood, purple, blue, and crimson yarn, and fine linen.
He was experienced in all kinds of engraving and could execute any design.
He was a worker in brass.
He came to King Solomon and performed all his work.
The attentive ear receives the sound from the instructive tongue.
Mysteries of Freemasonry are safely lodged in the repository of faithful breasts.
All Masons, from Entered Apprentice to Worshipful Master, should humbly bow with reverence to Deity.
CAPITAL letters indicate accented syllables
a - short a as in the word bat
i - short i as in the word ill or is
e - short e as in the word ethic
(e) - barely audible short e
u - short u as in the word but
Duegard of a Master: (Fig. 5) is mentioned as a visual representation.
Sign of a Master Mason: Draw the right hand (thumb in) across the stomach as low as the vest, then drop the hand suddenly (Fig. 6).
Grand Hailing Sign of Distress: Raise the hands as represented in Fig. 7 and drop them with spirit, repeated three times.
Words accompanying the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress (in night/dark): "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?"

Gavel raps for opening a Third Degree Lodge: Master gives one rap, Senior Warden one, Junior Warden one. Master gives one rap (second time), responded to by Wardens. Master gives one rap (third time), responded to by Wardens.
Gavel raps for opening a Second Degree Lodge: Two raps only.
Gavel raps for opening a First Degree Lodge: One rap each, first by Master, then Senior Warden, lastly Junior Warden.

Master removes hat and recites Psalm 133: "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! It is like the precious ointment upon the head, that ran down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard; that went down to the skirts of his garments; as the dew of Hermon, and as the dew that descended upon the mountains of Zion: for there the Lord commanded the blessing, even life forever more." Amen!
Brethren respond: "Amen! So mote it be!"
A Master Mason's Lodge is opened with both points of the compasses elevated above the square.
A candidate for the Master Mason Degree is generally prepared by the Junior Deacon and the two Stewards, or other brethren acting as such.
Production of six strong loops and a buckskin bag stuffed with hair.
Removal of altar, lights, and Second Degree pillars.
Candidate dressed with apron (right-hand corner tucked up) and a yoke with a Senior Warden's jewel.
Friends congratulate the candidate upon his Masonic appearance, asking if he is 'through' to mislead him.
Worshipful Master gives one rap with gavel.
Junior Warden gives one rap, steps to desk, and turns small column down.
Senior Warden steps to stand and turns column up.
Brethren and candidate take seats.
Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, and Secretary engage in a deceptive dialogue about closing the Lodge, making the Master Mason sign.
Candidate is called to approach the East.
Conductor (Senior Deacon) leads candidate to the Master's seat in the East.
Conductor takes position behind candidate, holding or concealing a hoodwink.
Worshipful Master questions candidate about his status as a Master Mason, then reveals he is not yet one and must face trials, instructing him to pray for himself.
Candidate kneels, and Conductor ties a hoodwink closely over his eyes.
Candidate prays orally or mentally, signifying completion by saying 'Amen'.
Lodge-room darkened by turning down gaslights or lamps.
Conductor assists candidate to arise and leads him three times around the room, traveling with the sun.
Conductor narrates Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple at high twelve for designs and devotions, then retiring at the south gate.
A small box with a house-plant or dry twig is set near the candidate's head to conceal something.
One intelligent brother stations himself at the ante-room door to act as a sea-captain.
A colloquy occurs between the ruffians and the 'Captain' regarding passage and a pass from King Solomon.
The ruffians return near the body after being denied passage.
The ruffians retire from the body in different directions to flee inland.
Brethren engage in laughter, singing, and exclamations of 'No work to-day' before being called to order.
The Master (styled King Solomon) calls the Lodge to order with a gavel sound.
The Junior Grand Warden (styled J.G.W.) reports no work and Hiram Abiff's absence.
Brethren loudly inquire about Hiram Abiff's whereabouts.
The Grand Secretary (accompanied by brethren) calls the rolls in the ante-room, with brethren responding 'Here!'
Specific names 'Jubela! Jubela!! Jubela!!!', 'Jubelo! Jubelo!! Jubelo!!!', 'Jubelum! Jubelum!! Jubelum!!!' are called out.
The Grand Secretary reports the missing Fellow Crafts to King Solomon.
Twelve Fellow Crafts arrive at the gate with important tidings.
The Warden opens the ante-room door.
Twelve Fellow Crafts enter the Lodge, stamping and scuffling.
They advance before the Master in the East and form across the Lodge.
They make the duegard and sign of a Fellow Craft, which the Master responds to.
One Fellow Craft relates the confession to King Solomon in a clear and distinct voice.
All twelve Fellow Crafts kneel before King Solomon.
King Solomon instructs them to arise, divide into parties (three east, three north, three south, three west), and search for the ruffians.
A brother acts as a sea-captain at the door.
Dialogue occurs between a Craftsman and the Captain regarding strangers.
A Craftsman reports the encounter with the sea-captain to the Master's desk.
King Solomon gives further instructions to divide and travel, with a threat of punishment if the ruffians are not found.
Fellow Crafts separate about the Lodge, conversing about the King's perceived injustice.
One party sits down near the candidate, who is lying on the floor, rolled up in canvas.
One brother pulls up an evergreen (acacia) at the head of the grave (candidate's head).
The voices of Jubela, Jubelo, and a third ruffian are heard from a corner, uttering their self-condemnations.
Three craftsmen stand by the candidate, listening to the ruffians' confessions.
Craftsmen rush forward over benches and chairs to seize the ruffians.
Ruffians are secured and led to the Worshipful Master's seat in the East.
A craftsman reports to King Solomon about pursuing a westerly course, finding a sprig of acacia, and discovering a grave.
Another party reports hearing the ruffians' specific exclamations (imprecations) from rock clefts.
King Solomon interrogates Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, who confess their guilt.
King Solomon sentences the ruffians to be executed outside the court gates, according to their imprecations, in the clefts of the rocks.
Brother Junior Grand Warden is ordered to oversee the execution.
All participants pass out of the Lodge into the ante-room and form a circle.
One person, acting as the principal mover, raises their right foot and hands, makes two false motions, then all bring down their right feet and hands together, producing a sharp noise.
A momentary silence ensues, followed by a groan, representing the death of Jubela.
This sequence is repeated twice more to represent the deaths of Jubelo and Jubelum.
Some Lodges use a large drum or roll a cannon-ball across the ante-room floor to represent the execution sounds.
Brethren return quietly to the Lodge after the execution.
A report is made to King Solomon that his orders regarding the murderers have been executed.
King Solomon orders twelve Fellow Crafts to search for Hiram Abiff's body.
Ruffians pass out of the Lodge into the ante-room and form a circle.

One ruffian acts as the principal mover, raising his right foot and making two false hand motions, followed by a third motion where all bring down right feet and hands together, producing a sharp noise.
A momentary silence ensues, followed by a groan representing the dying Jubela.
This sequence is repeated twice more for the other two ruffians.
Some Lodges use a large drum or roll a cannon-ball across the ante-room floor to create the sound, though this is not practiced in city Lodges.
Brethren return to the Lodge and report to King Solomon that his orders have been executed.
Twelve Fellow Crafts are sent to search for the body of Hiram Abiff.
During the search, dialogue includes: "Well, brothers, can we find where the acacia was pulled up?" and "Yes, this is the place; let us remove the rubbish and dig down here."
The canvas covering the candidate (representing Hiram's body) is lifted.
The Fellow Crafts search about the candidate, feeling his neck.
One brother takes hold of a jewel attached to a yoke about the candidate's neck, exclaiming, "This is the jewel of his office."
Brethren form a circle around the body, with the Master and Wardens at the head.
The Master makes the sign of 'distress' of a Master Mason by raising both hands and arms above the head.
The Master says: 'O Lord my God, I fear the Master's word is forever lost!'
Alternatively, in some Lodges, the Master gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body, thumb inward.
The party marches around the body with the sun, singing a funeral dirge.
An organ or melodeon may be played in a solemn manner.
The Master (as King Solomon) makes the 'grand hailing sign of distress' (possibly twice) accompanied by the exclamation: 'O Lord my God, I fear the Master's word is forever lost!'
The Junior Warden attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice grip, letting his hand slip off, and reports failure due to putrefaction.
The Senior Warden attempts to raise the body using the Fellow Craft's grip, letting his hand slip off, and reports failure due to the flesh cleaving from the bone.
The Master gives the grand hailing sign of distress three times, exclaiming: 'O Lord my God! O Lord my God!! O Lord my God!!! Is there no hope for the widow's son?'
All brethren kneel around the body on one knee.
The Master kneels at the head of the candidate, removes his hat, and recites a prayer.
Brethren kneeling at prayer around the grave of Hiram Abiff.
Brethren rising to their feet after the prayer.
The Master (K. S.) addressing the Senior Warden (S. W.) about raising the body by the strong grip.
The Master stepping to the candidate's feet, bending over, taking the candidate by the real grip of a Master Mason.
The Master placing his right foot against the candidate's right foot and his hand to the candidate's back.
The Master, with assistance, raising the candidate perpendicularly to a standing position.
The Master giving the grand Masonic word on the five points of fellowship.
The canvas being slipped out of the Lodge.
A brother slipping off the hoodwink from the candidate's eyes as the Master whispers the word.
The Master Mason candidate represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the exemplification of the degree.
Hiram Abiff's daily practice involved entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.
Hiram Abiff was struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge across his throat, a square across his breast, and a setting-maul on his forehead.
The ruffians buried Hiram's body in the rubbish of the Temple until low-twelve, then moved it to a grave dug due east and west, six feet perpendicular, west of Mount Moriah.
An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.
The twelve recanting Fellow Crafts appeared before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of their innocence.
Search parties were divided into groups of three, traveling east, west, north, and south.
The Master places his hands in the form of a duegard of a Master Mason, alluding to the manner in which Hiram's body was found.
Attempted raising of the body using the Entered Apprentice grip
Attempted raising of the body using the Fellow Craft grip
Successful raising of the body using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw
Raising on the five points of fellowship
Explanation of the several signs and tokens of the Master Mason Degree, commencing with the first and ending with the grips (referencing Figs. 5, 6, 7, 16, 17 and Note L, Appendix)
A ritualistic element where a conductor or brother draws a cold sword across the candidate's naked belly, causing a shocking effect.
Description of Master's Lodge jewels: suspended from blue velvet collars, bordered and embroidered with silver, with a blue rosette and silver five-pointed star.
Description of Master's Lodge apron: white, lined and bordered with blue, with an eye on the flap and selections from the Master's carpet on the area.
Description of Master's Lodge scarf: blue silk, trimmed with silver, with a blue rosette at the shoulder and hip, worn from left to right.

Specific jewels for officers: Worshipful Master (square), Past Master (compass opened on a quarter-circle), Senior Warden (level), Junior Warden (plumb), Secretary (cross pens), Treasurer (cross keys), Senior Deacon (square and compass with sun), Junior Deacon (square and compass with quarter moon), Stewards (cornucopia), Masters of Ceremonies (cross swords), Tyler (sabre).

Evolution of 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
The Hebrew covenant ceremony involved cutting a beast in twain and passing between the parts thereof.
The throat of the animal was cut across with one single blow, dividing the windpipe, arteries, and veins without touching any bone.
The breast was torn open and the heart plucked out; any imperfection rendered the body unclean.
The animal was divided into two parts, placed north and south, for parties to the covenant to pass between them from east to west.
The carcass was then left as a prey to voracious animals.
Hiram Abiff's supposed practice of retiring to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Hiram Abiff's alleged division of operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters.
Payment of Apprentices' wages at the pillar of Jachin.
Payment of Masters' wages in the Middle Chamber.
The High Priest's unique privilege of entering the Holy of Holies only once a year, with a string or belt around his waist.

The ritual description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has undergone historical alterations, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.

Variations in the sequence of attacks/entrances exist across jurisdictions: United States (south, west, east), Scotland (east, south, west), and France (originally south, north, east; now west, south, east).
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors in its ritual.

The Master Mason lecture is structured into three sections: the first illustrates the ancient and proper mode of raising a candidate, the second introduces historical traditions and exemplifies Masonic virtue, and the third explains emblems and describes Solomon's Temple construction.

The closing of a Mason's Lodge involves a report, inspection of avenues by the meridian officer, rehearsal of duties for leaders of craftsmen, the Senior Warden ensuring instruction and improvement, an impressive address on duties, and a fervent petition to the Deity.
A Grand Lecturer is elected annually by the Grand Lodge to teach the Ritual of Freemasonry to subordinate Lodges, typically compensated by salary or per lecture.
Singing the 'Mark Master's Song' to the tune of 'America'.
Brothers walking up to the Senior Warden after the second verse.
Senior Warden standing behind a lattice-window.
Candidate thrusting right hand, with thumb and two first fingers open, third and little fingers clinched, palm up, through a hole in the window.
Receiving a penny as wages.
Senior Grand Warden seizing the candidate's hand and drawing his arm through to the shoulder.
Exclaiming 'An impostor! an impostor!'
Another person running up with a drawn sword, exclaiming 'Strike off his hand! strike off his hand!'
Senior Deacon interceding for the candidate, stating 'Spare him! spare him! he is not an impostor; I know him to be a craftsman; I have wrought with him in the quarries.'
Candidate withdrawing arm and giving the sign of a Fellow Craft Mason.
Senior Deacon taking candidate to the Master and stating the case.
Right Worshipful Master questioning the candidate about being a Fellow Craft Mason and asking for the sign.
Right Worshipful Master reprimanding the candidate.
Senior Deacon taking candidate by the collar and delivering a severe reprimand (often omitted).
Senior Deacon shaking the candidate severely and hurrying him to the preparation-room.
Junior Deacon divesting candidate of outward apparel, money, and valuables.
Baring the candidate's breast.
Placing a cable-tow four times around the candidate's body.
Blindfolding the candidate with a hoodwink.
Junior Deacon conducting the candidate to the door and giving four distinct knocks (• • • •).
Singing of the 'Excellent Master's Song' celebrating the Temple's completion.
Placement of the keystone into an arch supported by two pillars (Jachin and Boaz), secured with six raps of a gavel by the Most Excellent Master.
Brethren moving around the arch and divesting themselves of jewels, aprons, and sashes, hanging them on the arch.
The Ark, carried by four brethren, is placed on the altar, with a pot of incense placed on top of it.
Brethren halting, facing inward to the altar, and beckoning the candidate to join the ceremonies.
Brethren throwing up hands and rolling eyes upward in a 'sign of admiration or astonishment'.
Brothers joining hands as in opening, while the Right Worshipful Master reads Scripture (2 Chron. vii. 1, 4).
The Master (high priest of the Chapter) kneeling and joining hands with the brethren during the Scripture reading.
Brethren repeating 'For he is good, for his mercy endureth forever' six times in concert, bowing their heads low.
A piece of blazing gum-camphor or other combustible matter thrown into the pot of incense on the altar by a brother.
Members balancing six times, rising, and balancing six more times, then disengaging and taking seats.
One of the brethren throws a piece of blazing gum-camphor or other combustible matter into the pot of incense on the altar.
Brethren kneel around the altar, bowing their heads low toward the floor while repeating 'for his mercy endureth forever' six times.
Members balance six times as in opening, rise, and balance six times more.
Brethren assemble around the altar in a circle, kneeling on the right knee, putting their left arms over, and joining hands.
The Master reads verses from the one hundred and thirty-fourth Psalm.
The Master closes the circle as in opening, followed by balancing six times, rising, balancing six times more, disengaging hands, and giving signs from this Degree downward.
The Lodge is closed as in preceding Degrees.
Psalm twenty-third is read at closing.
Admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is gained by six distinct knocks (• • • • • •).
Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads Exodus 3:1-6 during the first circuit.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Bandages are slipped up from the candidates' eyes in front of the burning bush.
A member personates the Deity behind the bush, calling 'Moses! Moses!'
The Principal Sojourner answers for candidates: 'Here I am.'

The Deity instructs: 'Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground. I am the God of thy fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.'
Candidates are directed to kneel and their faces are covered again.
The Principal Sojourner states: 'And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look upon God.'
Candidates are led three times around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads 2 Chronicles 36:11-20 during the three circuits.
Companions make loud noises (rolling cannon-balls, clashing swords, shouting, groaning, whistling, stamping, throwing down benches) to simulate the siege and destruction of Jerusalem.
Candidates are seized, thrown on the floor, bound hand and foot, and carried into the preparation-room.
Companions shout: 'Hurra for the captives!' several times.
The Captain of the Host opens the door and announces liberty, citing Cyrus's proclamation to build a second Temple.
The Principal Sojourner requests the proclamation to be read.
The Captain of the Host reads Ezra 1:1-3 (Cyrus's proclamation).
The Captain of the Host asks candidates if they are willing to go to Jerusalem.
The Principal Sojourner consults candidates and states willingness, but asks for a pass-word.
The Captain of the Host reads Exodus 3:13-14 (implied, regarding the pass-word).
Candidate divested of outward apparel, in a working posture, hoodwinked, and a cable tow seven times around the body.
Accompanied by two brethren.
Seven distinct knocks at the Chapter door.
Pass word: Rabboni.
Reception under a living arch.
Conducted once around the outer courts of the tabernacle.
Kneeling at the altar to invoke a blessing from Deity.
Kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses for the solemn oath or obligation of a Royal Arch Mason.
Exhibition of the symbol of the burning bush.
Representation of the destruction of the temple.
Journey through rough and rugged paths.
Meeting an obstruction at the first veil.
Identification as "children of the captivity," "descendants of those noble Giblemites," and "Most Excellent Masters."
Stating the purpose: "help, aid, and assist in rebuilding the house of the Lord, without the hope of fee or reward."
Using the pass: "I am that I am, I am hath sent me unto you."
Giving the word of the Master of the first veil: "I am that I am, I am hath sent you unto us, Shem, Ham, and Japheth."
Performing the sign of the first veil, imitating God's command to Moses to cast his rod, which became a serpent.
Explaining the first sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Using the word and sign from the first veil to gain admission to the second veil.
Performing the sign of the second veil, imitating God's command to Moses to put his hand into his bosom, which became leprous.
Explaining the second sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Using the word and sign from the first and second veils to gain admission to the third veil.
Performing the sign of the third veil, imitating God's command to Moses to take water from the river and pour it on dry land, where it became blood.
Explaining the third sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Encountering the Royal Arch Captain at the fourth veil or sanctuary.
Responding to the Royal Arch Captain's challenge: "Three worthy sojourners, who have come up to help, aid, and assist in the rebuilding of the house of the Lord, without the hope of fee or reward."
Being informed of a Grand Council decree restricting entry to true descendants of the twelve tribes of Israel.
Identifying oneself as 'children of the captivity' and 'Most Excellent Masters' to gain admission.
Gaining admission by 'words and signs' from masters of the first, second, and third veils, along with a signet.
Being received by the Captain of the Host and examined for proficiency in the preceding degree.
Undertaking the task of removing rubbish from the northeast part of the ruins.
Being furnished with working tools for the task.
Discovering a keystone resembling the top of an arch with effaced characters.
Descending into the arch using a cable tow wrapped seven times around a companion, with specific signals (swing right to ascend, left to descend).
Discovering three squares identified as masters' jewels during the first descent.
Performing a second descent into the arch, enabled by 'redoubled splendor' of the sun.
Discovering a gold-overlaid trunk with mysterious characters in the eastern-most part of the arch.
Construction of a secret underground vault by Solomon.
Division of the vault into nine separate arches.
Designation of the ninth arch for grand council meetings and as a deposit site.
Deposition of the Ark of the Covenant, a pot of manna, the rod of Aaron, and the book of the law.
Deposition of the Master's Word in the secret vault for preservation.
The strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, is mentioned.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five (5) points of fellowship and heard in a low breath.
The instruction to 'Advance and give it' refers to the word associated with the grip.
The word is 'right'.
Promulgating the Prestonian system of work and lectures.
Communicating the Preston ritual to lodges under Grand Lodge jurisdiction.
Teaching Masonic ritual as a lecturer.
Adoption of a ritual standard by a Grand Lodge.
The Prestonian system of ritual was brought to the United States around 1803.
The ritual was communicated to Webb and then imparted to Gleason.
Brother Gleason was employed to teach the ritual to lodges in Massachusetts.
The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system.
The ritual has been preserved in a remarkable degree of purity in Massachusetts lodges, particularly in the lodge where Gleason was Master in 1809.
The system underwent modifications in its general arrangement and adaptation after its introduction to the US.
Grand Lecturer compared Preston and Gleason Lectures
Preston Lectures are lengthy, estimated to cover nearly one hundred pages of foolscap paper if written out in full
Requirement for Masons to commit lectures in extenso is considered a difficult task
Webb's abridgment and new arrangement of lectures are considered to preserve substantial practical value and use preferable language
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The term 'Abif' means 'chief' or 'head'. Albert Pike, likely incorrectly, associated 'Khurum' (Hiram) with 'Khur-om', 'Her-ra', 'Her-mes', and 'Her-acles', attributing him to be the god of the sun.

The lion symbolizes might, royalty, and the Tribe of Judah. It is a title for King Solomon. In the Middle Ages, it symbolized resurrection, specifically through tales of lion cubs being revived by their father's breath, and roaring lions representing the resurrection of the dead on the Last Day. The lion is also considered the 'king' of beasts and is associated with the sun due to its mane.

The survival of Freemasonry despite the destruction of Solomon's Temple symbolizes the enduring nature and resilience of the institution, suggesting that its true essence is not in physical structures but in its principles and the hearts of its members. Architectural tools and symbolic emblems are used to represent and imprint wise and serious truths, serving as a visual and conceptual language for moral and philosophical instruction.

The sign of a Master Mason alludes to the penalty of the obligation of a Master Mason. The grand hailing sign of distress, accompanied by the words "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?", is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a plea for aid in dire circumstances and a connection to the central legend of the degree. The scripture passage (Psalm 133) emphasizes the importance of unity among brethren.

A Master Mason's Lodge is symbolically styled the 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies, of King Solomon's Temple'. The elevation of both points of the compasses above the square during the opening of the Lodge signifies the advanced knowledge and moral principles of the Master Mason degree.

The deception regarding the completion of the degree symbolizes the unexpected challenges and deeper truths yet to be revealed in the Masonic journey. The Senior Warden's jewel, worn by the candidate, represents a temporary elevation or a test of his understanding of authority and responsibility. The 'rough and rugged road beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers' symbolizes the moral and spiritual dangers and trials one faces in life, requiring unwavering fidelity and trust. The act of praying for oneself emphasizes self-reliance and direct communion with the Deity. The journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor, represents a transition into the profound narrative of Hiram Abiff, symbolizing the search for light and knowledge amidst obscurity, and the emulation of a revered Grand Master's devotion and diligence.

The flight and attempted escape of the ruffians symbolize the immediate consequences of their crime and the futility of evading justice. The discovery of Hiram Abiff's absence and the subsequent cessation of work symbolize the profound disruption and loss of guidance caused by the death of a Master. The roll call and identification of the missing ruffians represent the beginning of the process of discovery and accountability, highlighting that truth will eventually come to light.

The white gloves and aprons worn by the twelve Fellow Crafts symbolize their claimed innocence despite initial complicity, signifying a desire for purity and absolution. The acacia found at the head of the newly broken ground (grave) symbolizes immortality and marks the place of Hiram Abiff's burial. The gruesome self-condemnations of the ruffians (throat cut, tongue torn out; breast torn open, heart plucked out; body severed, bowels burned) symbolize the extreme moral and physical consequences of treachery and murder within the Masonic context, representing the severity of their crime and their own internal judgment. The search for the ruffians represents the pursuit of justice, truth, and the recovery of what was lost. The candidate's position on the floor, rolled in canvas, symbolically represents the body of Hiram Abiff in his grave, awaiting discovery.

The ruffians' detailed imprecations symbolize the self-condemnation and severe consequences awaiting those who commit such heinous acts of treachery. The discovery of the sprig of acacia by the craftsman is a potent symbol of immortality and the eventual discovery of Hiram Abiff's resting place. The ritualistic representation of the ruffians' execution signifies the triumph of justice and the ultimate fate of those who betray their sacred trust, reinforcing the gravity of the Master Mason's obligation.

The execution of the ruffians symbolizes the punishment for treachery and murder, reinforcing the consequences of violating sacred trusts. The search for Hiram Abiff's body by the Fellow Crafts represents the quest for lost knowledge and the Master's Word, highlighting the importance of perseverance and the limitations of incomplete understanding. The discovery of the mangled and putrid body emphasizes mortality and the tragic loss, while the jewel of office signifies Hiram's identity and authority even in death.

The 'lost Master's word' symbolizes the loss of essential knowledge or the inability to restore life and truth through ordinary means. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body signifies that the knowledge and powers of these lesser degrees are insufficient to overcome death or restore what is lost. The repeated grand hailing sign of distress and exclamations convey profound grief, despair, and a plea for divine intervention in the face of mortality. The 'widow's son' is an allegorical reference to Hiram Abiff, central to the Master Mason degree, whose death and the search for his replacement word are key themes. The marching with the sun (circumambulation) represents the journey of life and the natural order.

The prayer emphasizes the transient nature of human life, comparing man to a flower that is cut down or a shadow that flees, and highlights the ultimate reliance on God's compassion and everlasting salvation. The act of raising the candidate by the 'strong grip' or 'lion's paw' symbolizes resurrection from a state of death or darkness to light and knowledge. The 'five points of fellowship' represent the core tenets of brotherly love, relief, truth, fidelity, and justice, signifying the bond and duties among Master Masons. The removal of the hoodwink signifies the candidate's reception of Masonic light and understanding after a period of symbolic darkness.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.

The text notes that an unspecified element denotes 'worldly possession.' It also connects Tubal Cain as an instructor of artificers in brass and iron, relating him to Vulcan, and Jubal as the inventor of music, relating him to Apollo. The sprig of acacia is mentioned as a mark left by either the murderers or recanters in the Hiramic legend.

The Hebrew covenant ceremony, involving the division of an animal, symbolized a solemn binding to a promise, with the implied curse: "Thus let it be done to him, and thus let his body be cut in two, who shall break this covenant." The acacia, used in Jewish law to mark graves for priests to avoid ritual impurity, is symbolically significant in Freemasonry. The Hiramic legend, including the exhumation of the body, is presented as an allegory rather than a literal historical fact, serving to inculcate the doctrine of resurrection and a future state. The Master Mason represents a man saved from iniquity and raised to faith in salvation, embodying the doctrine of love.

The Master Mason represents a man under the doctrine of love, saved from the grave of iniquity, and raised to the faith of salvation.
