The canvas covering the candidate (representing Hiram's body) is lifted.
The Fellow Crafts search about the candidate, feeling his neck.
One brother takes hold of a jewel attached to a yoke about the candidate's neck, exclaiming, "This is the jewel of his office."
The candidate, Brother Gabe, represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the ritual.
The second section of the degree exemplifies the historical account.
The Master Mason candidate represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the exemplification of the degree.
Hiram Abiff's daily practice involved entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.
Hiram Abiff was struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge across his throat, a square across his breast, and a setting-maul on his forehead.
The ruffians buried Hiram's body in the rubbish of the Temple until low-twelve, then moved it to a grave dug due east and west, six feet perpendicular, west of Mount Moriah.
An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.
The twelve recanting Fellow Crafts appeared before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of their innocence.
Search parties were divided into groups of three, traveling east, west, north, and south.
The Master places his hands in the form of a duegard of a Master Mason, alluding to the manner in which Hiram's body was found.
Attempted raising of the body using the Entered Apprentice grip
Attempted raising of the body using the Fellow Craft grip
Successful raising of the body using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw
Raising on the five points of fellowship
Explanation of the several signs and tokens of the Master Mason Degree, commencing with the first and ending with the grips (referencing Figs. 5, 6, 7, 16, 17 and Note L, Appendix)
Candidate kneels in the center of the Lodge to implore blessings of Deity.
Candidate is accosted by three Fellow Crafts who demand Master Mason secrets.
Candidate receives blows with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul.
Candidate is ordered to be returned and reinvested.
Hiram Abiff's usual practice at high twelve: entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three murderers (Jubela, Jubelo, Jubelum) position themselves at the south, west, and east gates.
Hiram Abiff attempts to retire at the south, then west, then east gates, receiving a blow at each.
The body is buried in the rubbish of the Temple until low twelve.
The body is carried a westerly course to a hill west of Mount Moriah and buried six feet perpendicular, with an acacia planted at the head.
King Solomon orders a search for Hiram Abiff.
King Solomon orders the rolls of workmen to be called.
Twelve Fellow Crafts appear before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons.
King Solomon orders the twelve recanted Fellow Crafts to divide into parties and travel east, west, north, and south to search for the murderers.
Absence of designs on the trestle-board as a sign of Hiram's disappearance
Roll-call of workmen to identify missing Craftsmen
Twelve Fellow Crafts clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of innocence
Division of search parties (three east, three west, three north, three south)
Inquiry with a seafaring man near the port of Joppa
Discovery of a grave marked by an acacia
Overhearing of ruffians' exclamations from rock clefts
Seizure, binding, and presentation of ruffians before King Solomon
Execution of ruffians according to their self-imprecations
A ritualistic element where a conductor or brother draws a cold sword across the candidate's naked belly, causing a shocking effect.
Description of Master's Lodge jewels: suspended from blue velvet collars, bordered and embroidered with silver, with a blue rosette and silver five-pointed star.
Description of Master's Lodge apron: white, lined and bordered with blue, with an eye on the flap and selections from the Master's carpet on the area.
Description of Master's Lodge scarf: blue silk, trimmed with silver, with a blue rosette at the shoulder and hip, worn from left to right.

Specific jewels for officers: Worshipful Master (square), Past Master (compass opened on a quarter-circle), Senior Warden (level), Junior Warden (plumb), Secretary (cross pens), Treasurer (cross keys), Senior Deacon (square and compass with sun), Junior Deacon (square and compass with quarter moon), Stewards (cornucopia), Masters of Ceremonies (cross swords), Tyler (sabre).

Evolution of 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
The Hebrew covenant ceremony involved cutting a beast in twain and passing between the parts thereof.
The throat of the animal was cut across with one single blow, dividing the windpipe, arteries, and veins without touching any bone.
The breast was torn open and the heart plucked out; any imperfection rendered the body unclean.
The animal was divided into two parts, placed north and south, for parties to the covenant to pass between them from east to west.
The carcass was then left as a prey to voracious animals.
Hiram Abiff's supposed practice of retiring to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Hiram Abiff's alleged division of operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters.
Payment of Apprentices' wages at the pillar of Jachin.
Payment of Masters' wages in the Middle Chamber.
The High Priest's unique privilege of entering the Holy of Holies only once a year, with a string or belt around his waist.

The ritual description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has undergone historical alterations, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.

Variations in the sequence of attacks/entrances exist across jurisdictions: United States (south, west, east), Scotland (east, south, west), and France (originally south, north, east; now west, south, east).
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors in its ritual.

The Master Mason lecture is structured into three sections: the first illustrates the ancient and proper mode of raising a candidate, the second introduces historical traditions and exemplifies Masonic virtue, and the third explains emblems and describes Solomon's Temple construction.

The closing of a Mason's Lodge involves a report, inspection of avenues by the meridian officer, rehearsal of duties for leaders of craftsmen, the Senior Warden ensuring instruction and improvement, an impressive address on duties, and a fervent petition to the Deity.
A Grand Lecturer is elected annually by the Grand Lodge to teach the Ritual of Freemasonry to subordinate Lodges, typically compensated by salary or per lecture.
Candidate receives a severe reprimand for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages.
Candidate is taken to the quarries to learn how to bring up a regularly wrought stone.
Candidate is divested of outward apparel, money, and valuables.
Candidate's breast is made bare.
A cable-tow is placed four times around the candidate's body.
Candidate is securely blindfolded with a hoodwink.
Junior Deacon conducts candidate to the door and gives four distinct knocks (• • • •).
Senior Deacon answers the alarm with four knocks, responded to by one knock from outside, then returned by Senior Deacon.
Junior Deacon whispers the pass-word JOPPA to the Senior Deacon.
Candidate enters the Lodge.
Senior Deacon approaches candidate with a mallet and engraving chisel.
Senior Deacon places the edge of the chisel against the candidate's breast.
Senior Deacon raises mallet, makes false motions, and gives a violent blow upon the head of the chisel.
Senior Deacon throws down mallet and chisel, takes candidate's left arm, and instructs him to 'Follow me.'
Candidate and Senior Deacon walk four times around the Lodge.
Master, Senior, and Junior Grand Wardens each give one loud rap with their mallet as the candidate passes their stations.
Master reads scripture passages from Psalm cxviii. 22, Matthew xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, and Luke xx. 17.
Candidate arrives at the Junior Warden's post, where four knocks are given, and questions are asked and answered.
Candidate proceeds to the Senior Warden, who gives four raps, and the same questions and answers are exchanged.
Candidate is directed to the Right Worshipful Master in the East, where the same questions are asked and answered.
The Master orders the candidate to be conducted back to the Senior Warden in the West.
The candidate is taught to approach the East by four upright, regular steps, with feet forming a square and body erect at the altar.
The candidate kneels to receive the obligation.
After the obligation, the Right Worshipful Master instructs the candidate to detach their hand and kiss the book four times.
An alarm is made on the outside of the door immediately after the obligation.
The Junior Deacon attends the alarm and returns with a letter for the Right Worshipful Master.
The letter requests a loan of twenty-five dollars, offering the sender's mark as security.
The pass-grip of a Mark Master Mason is associated with the password JOPPA, referencing the steep ascent from Joppa while conveying timber from Lebanon.

The real grip of a Mark Master Mason is named SIROC or MARK WELL, formed by locking the little fingers of the right hands, turning their backs together, and placing the ends of the thumbs against each other.
The Right Worshipful Master engages the candidate in a dialogue to transition from the pass-grip to the true grip.
The Right Worshipful Master admonishes the candidate never to reveal the words of the grip in any way other than how they were received.
Brethren shuffle their feet to signify a disturbance among the workmen.
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel to summon the three Overseers.
The Right Worshipful Master exhibits the figure of a keystone for inspection.
The Overseers report rejecting a stone because it was 'neither oblong nor square,' lacked a craft mark, and was deemed unfit, subsequently thrown among the rubbish.
The Right Worshipful Master orders an immediate search for the keystone, stating the Temple cannot be finished without it.
Brethren shuffle about the Lodge to find the keystone and bring it to the East.
The Senior Warden takes the keystone and reports its discovery in the Temple's rubbish to the Right Worshipful Master.
Two or three brethren carry the keystone to the Right Worshipful Master.
The Right Worshipful Master receives the keystone and places it upright and plumb on his desk, with its initials facing the Lodge and the candidate.
The Right Worshipful Master gives four raps with the gavel, prompting all to rise (though some Lodges remain seated).

The Right Worshipful Master reads specific scriptural passages (Ps. cxviii. 22, Matt. xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, Luke xx. 17) about the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner, striking the keystone once, twice, thrice, and four times after each passage.
The Right Worshipful Master reads from Revelation xi. 17 about receiving a white stone and a new name.
The candidate steps forward to receive the new name.
The Right Worshipful Master invests the candidate with a new name, described as a circle of letters and the general mark of the Degree.
The Right Worshipful Master points out the initials on the keystone to the candidate.
Craftsmen choose and place a mark upon their work for identification.
Craftsmen thrust their right hand into the apartments of the Senior Grand Warden with a copy of their mark in the palm and give a token to receive wages.
Preparation for Mark Master Mason degree involves being deprived of all metals, divested of outward apparel, in a working posture, with a cable-tow four times around the body.
A demand is made at the Lodge door by four distinct knocks.
The candidate gives the pass 'Joppa'.
Reception into the Lodge is on the edge of the engraver's chisel, applied to the naked left breast, and under the impression of the mallet.
The candidate is conducted four times regularly around the Lodge to the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Right Worshipful Master.
The candidate approaches the East by four upright regular Masonic steps, with feet forming a square and body erect.
The obligation of a Mark Master Mason is taken kneeling upon both knees, with both hands covering the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
A sign, called the due guard, is shown by 'chopping off the right ear'.
Kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses for the solemn oath or obligation of a Mark Master Mason.
Sign: Chopping off the right ear (the duegard).
Sign: Chopping off right hand (the sign).
Sign: Carrying the key-stone (the grand hailing sign of distress of a Mark Master Mason).
Sign: Heave over (the principal sign).
Grip: 'Mark Well'.
Placing a brother's own private mark in the center of the general mark of the degree.
Kneeling on bended knees at the altar
Presenting a mark to receive assistance
Receiving a mark and granting assistance
Using the chisel to cut, carve, mark, and engrave work
Using the mallet to knock off excrescences and smooth surfaces
Reciting a prayer at the closing of a Mark Master's Lodge
Candidate is asked 'Who comes here?'

Candidate states they are a worthy brother, duly initiated, passed to Fellow Craft, raised to Master Mason, advanced to Mark Master, and regularly passed the chair, now seeking to be received and acknowledged as a Most Excellent Master.
Candidate confirms the act is of their own free will and accord, that they are worthy, well qualified, have made suitable proficiency, and are properly vouched for.
Candidate states they expect to gain privilege 'By the benefit of the pass'.
The pass word is 'Mark Well'.
Candidate is directed to wait for the Right Worshipful Master's answer.
Right Worshipful Master's answer: 'Let the candidate enter'.
Candidate is conducted six times around the Lodge to the Worshipful Senior Warden in the West.
Candidate is conducted to the Right Worshipful Master in the East.
Candidate is conducted to the Worshipful Senior Warden in the West, who teaches the approach to the altar.
Approach to the altar involves six upright Masonic steps, feet forming a square, body erect.
The Most Excellent Master is made 'In due form'.
Due form involves kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses, body erect.
Candidate takes the solemn oath of a Most Excellent Master.
The duegard sign is given.
The sign of the degree is given.
The sign of admiration is given.
The grip is communicated to a brother.
The name of the grip is 'Rabboni'.
The grip is also called 'The cover grip'.
Solomon dedicated the Temple with solemn prayer and costly sacrifices.
The holy ark was fixed in its proper place between the cherubim.
The grand honors of Masonry involve a thrice-repeated action.
A candidate is prepared with a rope wound six times around their body.
Six distinct knocks are given on the Lodge door, answered by six from within.
The candidate is walked six times around the Lodge, moving with the sun.
More advanced brethren form a procession and march six times around the Lodge, against the course of the sun.
Two pillars, about five feet high, are erected with an arch placed over them.
The Most Excellent Master places the keystone in the arch.
The keystone is driven home with six raps of the gavel.
A pot containing gum camphor or other inflammable matter is used during ceremonies.
Construction of a secret underground vault by Solomon.
Division of the vault into nine separate arches.
Designation of the ninth arch for grand council meetings and as a deposit site.
Deposition of the Ark of the Covenant, a pot of manna, the rod of Aaron, and the book of the law.
Deposition of the Master's Word in the secret vault for preservation.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The elaborate rites of passage in medieval guilds, and by extension early Masonic degrees, symbolized the trust placed in worthy craftsmen and the protection of esoteric knowledge from outsiders and potential accusations of heresy.

The Three Ruffians' attempt to obtain secrets not rightfully theirs symbolizes the endeavor to acquire knowledge of Divine Truth through means other than faithfulness, thereby making the perpetrator a thief and a murderer. They also represent the internal enemies within each individual: ignorance, passions, and attitudes, which Masons are enjoined to control and subdue.

Prudence symbolizes the rational and judicious approach to life, guiding Masons to act with wisdom and discretion in all circumstances, particularly in safeguarding the integrity of the Craft.

Prudence symbolizes the wise application of reason to guide one's life and actions, ensuring both present and future well-being. It represents the careful discernment required to protect the integrity and secrecy of Freemasonry.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.

The sign of a Master Mason alludes to the penalty of the obligation of a Master Mason. The grand hailing sign of distress, accompanied by the words "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?", is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a plea for aid in dire circumstances and a connection to the central legend of the degree. The scripture passage (Psalm 133) emphasizes the importance of unity among brethren.

The act of approaching the East by three upright, regular steps symbolizes the candidate's progression towards greater Masonic knowledge and enlightenment, as the East is considered the 'place of further light'. The upright posture and regular steps represent rectitude, discipline, and a deliberate, structured advancement in Masonic principles.

The deception regarding the completion of the degree symbolizes the unexpected challenges and deeper truths yet to be revealed in the Masonic journey. The Senior Warden's jewel, worn by the candidate, represents a temporary elevation or a test of his understanding of authority and responsibility. The 'rough and rugged road beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers' symbolizes the moral and spiritual dangers and trials one faces in life, requiring unwavering fidelity and trust. The act of praying for oneself emphasizes self-reliance and direct communion with the Deity. The journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor, represents a transition into the profound narrative of Hiram Abiff, symbolizing the search for light and knowledge amidst obscurity, and the emulation of a revered Grand Master's devotion and diligence.

The steadfast refusal of the candidate (representing Hiram Abiff) to reveal the Master Mason's secrets under duress symbolizes fidelity, integrity, and the ultimate sacrifice for one's principles and obligations. The simulated death and fall represent the mortality of man and the dramatic loss of the Master's word, central to the Master Mason degree's allegorical teachings. The ceremony is intended to create lasting impressions rather than cause physical harm.

The lowering of the candidate symbolizes the death of Hiram Abiff. The silence at 'low twelve' emphasizes the secrecy and solemnity of the event. The act of carrying the body around the Lodge three times represents ascending a hill, leading to the burial site. The planting of an acacia at the head of the grave is a significant symbolic act, though its specific meaning is not fully elaborated in this excerpt.

The flight and attempted escape of the ruffians symbolize the immediate consequences of their crime and the futility of evading justice. The discovery of Hiram Abiff's absence and the subsequent cessation of work symbolize the profound disruption and loss of guidance caused by the death of a Master. The roll call and identification of the missing ruffians represent the beginning of the process of discovery and accountability, highlighting that truth will eventually come to light.

The white gloves and aprons worn by the twelve Fellow Crafts symbolize their claimed innocence despite initial complicity, signifying a desire for purity and absolution. The acacia found at the head of the newly broken ground (grave) symbolizes immortality and marks the place of Hiram Abiff's burial. The gruesome self-condemnations of the ruffians (throat cut, tongue torn out; breast torn open, heart plucked out; body severed, bowels burned) symbolize the extreme moral and physical consequences of treachery and murder within the Masonic context, representing the severity of their crime and their own internal judgment. The search for the ruffians represents the pursuit of justice, truth, and the recovery of what was lost. The candidate's position on the floor, rolled in canvas, symbolically represents the body of Hiram Abiff in his grave, awaiting discovery.

The ruffians' detailed imprecations symbolize the self-condemnation and severe consequences awaiting those who commit such heinous acts of treachery. The discovery of the sprig of acacia by the craftsman is a potent symbol of immortality and the eventual discovery of Hiram Abiff's resting place. The ritualistic representation of the ruffians' execution signifies the triumph of justice and the ultimate fate of those who betray their sacred trust, reinforcing the gravity of the Master Mason's obligation.

The execution of the ruffians symbolizes the punishment for treachery and murder, reinforcing the consequences of violating sacred trusts. The search for Hiram Abiff's body by the Fellow Crafts represents the quest for lost knowledge and the Master's Word, highlighting the importance of perseverance and the limitations of incomplete understanding. The discovery of the mangled and putrid body emphasizes mortality and the tragic loss, while the jewel of office signifies Hiram's identity and authority even in death.

The death of Hiram Abiff symbolizes an instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity that is rarely equaled. His steadfastness in guarding the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death, serves as a profound example.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.

The candidate symbolically represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff, who was slain just before the completion of King Solomon's Temple. The unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum represents the Master's Lodge. The demand for Master Mason secrets and Hiram's refusal symbolize fidelity and integrity. The planting of an acacia at the grave signifies remembrance and immortality.

The absence of designs on the trestle-board symbolizes a disruption of order and the loss of a master builder. The white gloves and aprons worn by the Fellow Crafts signify innocence and purity, even after their initial complicity. The acacia tree, discovered at the grave, is a powerful symbol of immortality and the enduring nature of the soul. The ruffians' self-imprecations symbolize the self-destructive nature of guilt and the just, inevitable consequences of treachery and murder, often interpreted as the penalties associated with Masonic obligations.

The text notes that an unspecified element denotes 'worldly possession.' It also connects Tubal Cain as an instructor of artificers in brass and iron, relating him to Vulcan, and Jubal as the inventor of music, relating him to Apollo. The sprig of acacia is mentioned as a mark left by either the murderers or recanters in the Hiramic legend.

The Hebrew covenant ceremony, involving the division of an animal, symbolized a solemn binding to a promise, with the implied curse: "Thus let it be done to him, and thus let his body be cut in two, who shall break this covenant." The acacia, used in Jewish law to mark graves for priests to avoid ritual impurity, is symbolically significant in Freemasonry. The Hiramic legend, including the exhumation of the body, is presented as an allegory rather than a literal historical fact, serving to inculcate the doctrine of resurrection and a future state. The Master Mason represents a man saved from iniquity and raised to faith in salvation, embodying the doctrine of love.

The Master Mason represents a man under the doctrine of love, saved from the grave of iniquity, and raised to the faith of salvation. The degree testifies faith in the resurrection of the body and inculcates a practical lesson of prudence and unshrinking fidelity, inspiring hope for a final reward. The legend serves to impress upon the candidate's mind the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state.

The Third Degree is interpreted as an allegory for the Christian dispensation, symbolizing the revelation of salvation, atonement for sin, and the resurrection from the dead, confirmed by Christ's resurrection. The degeneration of Jewish law and the burial of piety and reverence are symbolized by "filth and rubbish" from the Temple. The inability of ancient wisdom to restore religion is represented by those who sought to raise her in vain. The sprig of acacia waving over religion's monument symbolizes hope and immortality, anticipating the Saviour's arrival. Hiram Abiff's death is a symbol for the Messiah's death. The three blows given to Hiram at the three gates of the Temple allude to the three points of condemnation against Christ before Caiaphas, Herod, and Pilate. The three tools (gauge, square, gavel) used for the blows symbolize the blow on Christ's cheek, the flagellation, and the spear (or crown of thorns). Brethren assembled around Hiram's grave represent disciples lamenting Christ's death. The "lost Word" symbolizes the word pronounced on the cross, which the Jews could not comprehend. False brethren are represented by Judas. The sprig of cassia (acacia) represents the cross, said to be made of its wood.

The reprimand symbolizes the importance of diligence, skill, and integrity in one's work and the consequences of attempting to deceive or impose upon the Craft. The chisel and mallet, used by operative masons to shape stone, are symbolically employed by Free and Accepted Masons to 'hew, cut, carve, and indent the mind,' signifying the moral and intellectual refinement of the candidate. The reception upon the chisel and mallet represents the enduring impression of the Mark Master's lessons. The scripture passages about 'the stone which the builders refused' becoming 'the head stone of the corner' allude to the value of that which is initially rejected or overlooked, and its ultimate importance, a central theme of the Mark Master degree.

The four upright, regular steps forming a square symbolize precision, rectitude, and the candidate's commitment to moral and Masonic principles in their approach to sacred duties. The repeated questioning at various posts signifies the importance of consistency, truthfulness, and the candidate's unwavering affirmation of their commitment. The 'mark' itself is a profound symbol of a personal pledge, a bond of trust, and a form of security or credit within the fraternity, representing a brother's honor and commitment. Its specified value (half a shekel of silver or quarter of a dollar) gives it a tangible, albeit symbolic, economic weight. The severe penalties of having the right ear smitten off and the right hand chopped off symbolize the complete forfeiture of the ability to hear the sacred word and to perform Masonic work, representing the ultimate consequence for an impostor or oath-breaker.

The keystone, initially rejected by the builders for its unconventional shape and lack of a recognizable mark, ultimately becomes the 'head stone of the corner,' symbolizing that true value and importance may be overlooked or misunderstood at first. The scriptural passages reinforce this theme of vindication for the rejected. The 'new name' and the 'mark' on the keystone, along with the reference to the 'white stone' from Revelation, symbolize a unique identity, recognition, and a deeper, personal understanding or achievement within the Mark Master degree, signifying a special reward for those who overcome.

The token given by Fellow Crafts alludes to the way and manner in which they received their wages and serves to distinguish a true craftsman from an impostor. The four distinct knocks allude to the fourth degree of Masonry. The ancient city of Joppa, where materials for the Temple were landed, symbolizes the assistance afforded to workmen and is adopted as a proper pass. The reception on the engraver's chisel and mallet teaches that the moral precepts of the Mark Master Mason degree should make a deep and lasting impression upon the candidate's mind and future conduct. The due guard (chopping off the right ear) alludes to a specific meaning (text incomplete).

The duegard (chopping off the right ear) symbolizes the penalty for unlawfully divulging secrets. The sign (chopping off the right hand) alludes to an additional penalty for an imposter. The grand hailing sign of distress (carrying the key-stone) represents the manner in which brothers carry their work. The principal sign ('Heave over') refers to the principal words of the degree and the rejection of the Key-Stone by the Overseers. The Key-Stone, wrought by Hiram Abiff, symbolizes a crucial piece of work initially unrecognized and rejected, but later found and applied to its intended use, representing perseverance and the eventual recognition of true worth. The white stone alludes to a passage of Scripture about overcoming and receiving a new name. The new name, composed of the initials 'Hiram, Tyrian, Widow's, Son, Sendeth, To, King, Solomon,' serves as the general mark of the degree, where each brother places his private mark, signifying individual identity and commitment.

Kneeling at the altar symbolizes a solemn commitment to assist worthy brothers and not hastily reject their applications, ensuring their basic needs are met. A brother presenting a mark represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a poor but highly skilled and upright craftsman. A brother receiving a mark and granting assistance represents Grand Master Solomon, symbolizing wealth and great liberality. The chisel morally demonstrates the advantage of discipline and education. The mallet morally teaches to correct irregularities and reduce man to a proper level, fostering contentment through quiet deportment and discipline.

The 'cover grip' symbolizes the duty of Most Excellent Masters to cover the faults and imperfections of brethren with the 'broad mantle of charity and brotherly love', recognizing human frailties. The 'sign of admiration' alludes to the wonder and admiration of ancient brethren upon viewing the interior of King Solomon's magnificent Temple dedicated to the Supreme Being. The word 'Rabboni' signifies 'Good Master' or 'Most Excellent Master', originating from King Solomon's commendation of the master builder at the Temple's completion.

The reiteration of the number three in Masonic ceremonies, particularly the grand honors, is suggested to be in honor and reverence to the ancient Trinity. The number six is highly respected in the Most Excellent Master degree, appearing in various ritualistic actions such as candidate preparation, knocks, circumambulation, and the keystone ceremony, with historical parallels drawn to Druidic veneration. The keystone symbolizes completion, structural integrity, and the essential element of an arch, with its placement being a central act of the Most Excellent Master degree. The cloud of glory filling the Temple signifies Jehovah's divine presence and acceptance.

The secret vault, with its nine arches and connection to the sanctum sanctorum, symbolizes a hidden and sacred repository of divine and masonic knowledge. The deposited items, including the Ark of the Covenant and the Master's Word, represent foundational truths and the enduring nature of sacred principles, even when seemingly lost. It signifies the preservation of essential wisdom for future generations.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The historical context of medieval guilds suggests that only worthy individuals could be trusted with the secrets of the order, implying a lesson for candidates about trustworthiness and the responsibility associated with shared knowledge.

The candidate is reminded that rewards, particularly knowledge of Divine Truth, must be earned through faithfulness and diligent effort, rather than being obtained through violence, deceit, or any other devious means. Furthermore, the Ruffians serve as a lesson to recognize and overcome one's own internal adversaries, such as ignorance, uncontrolled passions, and negative attitudes.

It is important for a Mason to understand the concepts of Regularity and Recognition to ensure proper interaction within the Craft and to avoid associating with Clandestine Lodges. This knowledge ensures that one's Masonic activities are conducted within legitimate and recognized jurisdictions.

A Mason must cultivate prudence as a fundamental virtue to guide their conduct, make wise decisions, and protect the sacred trust of Freemasonry by never divulging its secrets, signs, tokens, or words, especially in public or mixed company.

A Mason must cultivate prudence as a core virtue, using reason to guide all decisions and actions. This virtue is crucial for maintaining proper conduct within the Lodge and in the wider world, and especially for safeguarding the secrets of Freemasonry from unauthorized disclosure.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.

The candidate learns the specific signs and words associated with the Master Mason degree, particularly the solemnity of the obligation and the importance of recognizing and responding to a brother's distress. The ritual of opening the Lodge and the recitation of Psalm 133 reinforce the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love, reminding members of the blessings that come from harmonious fellowship.

The candidate learns the precise ritualistic method of approaching the East, which signifies their journey towards understanding and their commitment to the established forms and traditions of Freemasonry. It emphasizes the importance of order, precision, and a respectful demeanor in the pursuit of Masonic light.

The candidate learns that the Masonic journey is not easily completed and requires profound fidelity and endurance. He is taught to trust in a higher power and to rely on his own spiritual strength, as he must pray for himself in the face of impending trials. The introduction to Hiram Abiff's daily devotions and diligent work instills the importance of consistent dedication, moral rectitude, and reverence for the Deity, setting an example for the candidate's own conduct. The experience also teaches humility, as his presumed status as a Master Mason is challenged, and he is reminded of the 'more valuable secrets' yet to be entrusted.

The candidate learns the profound importance of fidelity, integrity, and unwavering adherence to one's obligations, even in the face of extreme threats. The experience emphasizes the sacred trust associated with Masonic secrets and the moral fortitude required to uphold them, symbolizing a commitment to truth and honor unto death.

The candidate experiences the dramatic narrative of Hiram Abiff's murder, emphasizing the tragic consequences of betrayal and the loss of the Master's Word. The ritualistic disposal of the body highlights the secrecy surrounding the event and the ruffians' desperate attempts to conceal their crime. The lesson underscores the importance of fidelity and the profound impact of the loss of a Master.

This segment teaches the candidate about the inevitability of discovery for those who commit wrongful acts, emphasizing that secrets of such magnitude cannot remain hidden. It underscores the importance of order, accountability, and the severe disruption that occurs when a leader or a vital principle is lost. The strict adherence to rules, such as needing a pass, also reinforces the concept of proper authorization and conduct within the Craft.

The narrative emphasizes the severe consequences of violating sacred trusts and obligations, as seen in the fate of the ruffians and the threatened punishment of the twelve Fellow Crafts. It teaches the importance of repentance and seeking pardon, as demonstrated by the twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous intent. The discovery of the grave and the ruffians' confessions highlight the inevitability of truth coming to light and justice being served, even if delayed. The acacia symbolizes the hope of immortality and the enduring spirit, even in the face of death and betrayal.

The candidate learns about the inevitable consequences of crime and treachery, particularly against a virtuous man like Hiram Abiff. The narrative emphasizes the importance of justice and fidelity. The discovery of the sprig of acacia introduces themes of hope, immortality, and the eventual revelation of truth, even in the face of death and loss.

The candidate experiences the dramatic consequences of the ruffians' actions through the ritualistic sounds of their execution. As the representation of Hiram Abiff's body, the candidate undergoes the symbolic search, learning about the loss of the Master's Word and the distinction between the knowledge of Fellow Crafts and Master Masons. This experience underscores the gravity of the Hiramic legend and the profound lessons of fidelity, mortality, and the pursuit of truth within the Master Mason Degree.

The candidate is taught to exemplify virtue, fortitude, and integrity, drawing inspiration from the historical account of Grand Master Hiram Abiff's unwavering commitment to his trust, even when faced with mortal danger.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.

The narrative emphasizes the importance of fidelity to one's obligations and secrets, even unto death. It teaches about the consequences of avarice and betrayal, as seen in the actions of the three Fellow Crafts. The candidate learns about the ultimate sacrifice made for integrity and the pursuit of knowledge, and the eventual discovery and justice for wrongdoing.

The narrative teaches the severe consequences of treachery and the importance of fidelity to one's obligations. It underscores the concept of justice, where actions lead to fitting retribution. The persistent search for the ruffians and the discovery of the grave emphasize the enduring nature of truth and the eventual revelation of hidden deeds. The confession of the twelve Fellow Crafts highlights the possibility of redemption through acknowledging guilt and seeking pardon.

To be a 'bright Mason,' one must have knowledge of the alterations made in Masonry since Morgan's exposure. The critical notes on the Third Degree's legendary nature (hyperbolical, apocryphal, fabulous) encourage a deeper, allegorical understanding rather than a literal interpretation of its traditions and history. The mention of 'worldly possession' as a symbolic denotation may serve as a lesson against materialism.

The self-imposed penalties within Freemasonry may be understood in the context of ancient Hebrew covenant practices, emphasizing the profound solemnity and consequences of breaking sacred promises. The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, encouraging candidates to contemplate mortality, strive for ethical maturity, and embrace faith in salvation. The narrative of the Temple builders highlights the importance of fidelity and the severe repercussions of treachery.

The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state, emphasizing prudence and unshrinking fidelity. It inspires hope for a final reward for those who are 'just made perfect' and symbolizes being saved from iniquity and raised to the faith of salvation.

Candidates are taught the ancient and proper mode of being raised to the Master Mason Degree, understanding its historical traditions and the exemplification of Masonic virtue. They learn the meaning of the degree's emblems and the description of Solomon's Temple. The solemnity of lodge closing reinforces the "chief care of a Mason" and the importance of continuous Masonic instruction and improvement. Masons are reminded of their duties as men and Masons in their daily avocations and the importance of piety. The allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend teaches profound lessons about spiritual death and resurrection, the restoration of truth and morality, and fidelity in the face of adversity, drawing parallels to Christian themes of salvation and immortality. The distinction between literal history and allegorical truth is emphasized, guiding the candidate to seek deeper symbolic meaning.

The candidate is taught the critical importance of performing work with skill and industry, and the ethical principle of not claiming rewards or wages that are not rightfully earned. The reprimand serves as a 'striking lesson' against imposition upon the Craft. The symbolic use of the chisel and mallet emphasizes the continuous process of self-improvement and the shaping of one's character and mind. The scripture readings impart a lesson on the potential for the rejected to become essential, encouraging perseverance and recognizing inherent worth.

The candidate learns the paramount importance of strict secrecy regarding the Mark Master Mason's secrets, disclosing them only to duly qualified brethren after rigorous examination. They are taught the duty to uphold the Constitutions of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter and State Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as well as the by-laws of any Mark Master Lodge. The obligation instills obedience to all regular signs and summonses from fellow Mark Master Masons or Lodges, within the bounds of their cable-tow. A strong ethical lesson is imparted regarding not wronging a brother or the Lodge, even to the smallest value, and preventing such wrongs if possible. The unique rules surrounding the 'mark' teach about personal integrity, the sanctity of a pledge, and the responsibility to honor commitments related to one's mark, including its redemption. The lesson of mutual aid is emphasized through the promise to receive a brother's mark when a favor is requested and to grant it if possible, or to return its specified value if the favor cannot be granted.

The candidate is taught the importance of discerning true worth and recognizing value in things that may initially appear unconventional or rejected. The narrative of the keystone emphasizes that what is deemed unfit by some may be essential and invaluable. The investment with a 'new name' signifies a personal transformation and a unique identity within the Craft, highlighting individual merit and the reward for perseverance. The admonition regarding the grips instills the importance of fidelity and the proper transmission of Masonic knowledge.

The importance of honesty and proper identification in one's work, as demonstrated by the Fellow Craft's mark and token. The severe consequences of imposture. The necessity of being worthy, well qualified, and duly prepared for advancement in Masonry. The significance of historical allusions within Masonic passes. The profound and lasting impact that the moral teachings of the Mark Master Mason degree should have on one's character and actions. The solemnity of Masonic obligations.

The candidate learns the profound importance of the obligation and the severe consequences of betraying Masonic secrets. The allegory of the Key-Stone teaches the value of diligent work, even if it is initially misunderstood or rejected, and the eventual triumph of truth and proper application. The symbolism of the white stone and new name encourages personal transformation and the adoption of a new identity within the fraternity. The practice of a private mark emphasizes individual contribution and accountability within the collective work.

Candidates learn the importance of charity and mutual assistance among brothers, particularly in preventing indigence. They are taught the value of discipline and education, as symbolized by the chisel. They learn the necessity of self-correction, reducing personal irregularities, and cultivating contentment through a quiet and disciplined demeanor, as symbolized by the mallet. The degree emphasizes zeal, fidelity, and attachment to Masonry as qualities worthy of honor and support.

The candidate learns the importance of upholding their solemn obligation, even under the threat of severe penalty. They are taught the virtues of charity and brotherly love, encouraging them to overlook the imperfections of others. The degree also instills a sense of admiration for the grand achievements of the ancient brethren, particularly the construction and dedication of King Solomon's Temple, and the recognition of excellence in craftsmanship.

Masons are encouraged to examine the meaning and original intention behind the ceremonies and practices, rather than merely performing them from habit. The degree emphasizes the completion of the Temple and the importance of the keystone, suggesting themes of perfection and essential elements in one's Masonic journey.

The narrative teaches the profound importance of safeguarding sacred knowledge and the 'Sacred Word,' even in the face of loss or inability to communicate it directly. It conveys that vital truths can be preserved in hidden places, awaiting discovery by those who are worthy and diligent in their search.
tradition_notes: The Hiramic Legend became part of Masonic Traditions between 1723 and 1738, not earlier.
There is no mention of Hiram Abiff in the Old Charges (Regius Poem, Matthew Cooke manuscript, 1632 document).
Anderson's Constitutions of 1723 make no mention of the Hiramic tragedy, but the 1738 edition includes the three ruffians killing the Prince of Architects.
The structure of Masonic degrees resembles medieval craft guild admission requirements: oath, rules, regulations, and penalties.
Masonic Law is based in part on Anderson's The Constitutions of the Free-Masons, originally published in 1723.
Anderson's Constitutions listed commonly accepted rules for Grand Lodges, Lodges, and individual members.

Examples of these rules include: acceptance of Candidates irrespective of personal religious beliefs, the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass displayed upon the Altar at all times, the acceptance of men only, and the Hiramic Legend as an integral part of the Third Degree.
The Second Section recites the historical traditions of the Institution.
The organization of Freemasonry into three Symbolic Degrees is a foundational tradition.
The use of peculiar modes of recognition and specific ceremonial forms are traditional aspects of the degrees.
The preservation of the institution's elements through secrecy is a long-standing tradition.
The grand hailing sign of distress is specifically linked to the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff.
The specific sequence of gavel raps by the Master and Wardens is a traditional method for opening Lodges on different degrees.
The recitation of Psalm 133 and the response "Amen! So mote it be!" are traditional elements of the Lodge opening ceremony.
The custom of dressing the candidate with a rich apron and yoke in some Lodges.
The specific tying of the apron with the right-hand corner tucked up for a Master Mason.
The use of columns by the Wardens to signify refreshment or labor.

The Conductor's narrative introduces Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum at high twelve for drawing designs and offering devotions, then retiring at the south gate of the Temple. This is explicitly noted as the first time the candidate hears about Hiram Abiff.
The three ruffians are traditionally portrayed by the Junior Warden (Jubela), Senior Warden (Jubelo), and Worshipful Master (Jubelum).
The sequence of confrontations occurs at the Junior Warden's station (South), Senior Warden's station (West), and the Master's station (East).
The secrets of a Master Mason are stated to only be given in the presence of Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff (represented by the candidate).
The dialogue between the ruffians and the Master is a traditional part of the Hiramic Legend ritual.
The names of the three ruffians, Jubela, Jubelum, and Jubelo, are traditional within Masonic lore.
The concept of 'low twelve' (midnight) as the time for the secret disposal of the body is a traditional element.
The burial location west of Mount Moriah is a traditional detail in the legend.
The planting of an acacia at the head of the grave is a key traditional symbol in the Master Mason degree.
Some Lodges use a small box with a house-plant or dry twig placed near the candidate's head during the ritual.
The Worshipful Master is styled as King Solomon and the Senior Warden as Junior Grand Warden during this part of the ritual.
The specific division of the twelve Fellow Crafts into four parties (three east, three north, three south, three west) for the search.
The role of the 'sea-captain' as a character in the narrative, providing information about the fleeing ruffians.
The use of white gloves and aprons as a 'token of innocence' by the recanting Fellow Crafts.
The specific names of the ruffians, Jubela and Jubelo, and the implied third ruffian (Jubelum).
The symbolic significance of the acacia as a marker for Hiram Abiff's grave.
Some Lodges use a large drum to represent the execution sounds, while others roll a large cannon-ball across the ante-room floor, letting it strike on a cushion placed against the wall.
Some Lodges use a large drum or roll a cannon-ball across the ante-room floor to simulate the sounds of execution, while others do not, particularly in urban settings.
The narrative describes the premeditated death of Hiram Abiff by fifteen Fellow Crafts who sought to obtain the secrets of a Master Mason before the Temple's completion.
The motive of the Fellow Crafts was to gain the ability to travel in foreign countries and receive Master's wages.
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
The narrative describes a core tradition of the Master Mason degree, recounting the legend of Hiram Abiff's murder and the subsequent events.
The use of specific tools (twenty-four-inch gauge, square, setting-maul) in the ritual is a traditional element.
The specific names of the ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, are traditional within the Hiramic legend.
The detail of the twelve Fellow Crafts confessing their premeditated guilt and being sent on a search is a traditional element.
The acacia tree is a traditional and significant symbol marking Hiram's grave.
The specific self-imprecations uttered by the ruffians are traditional and correspond to the penalties of Masonic obligations.
The 'work' and lectures taught by Barney are prevalent in the Northwestern States (Michigan, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Iowa) and were adopted by the Baltimore Convention.
Brother John Barney obtained the Preston Lectures from Gleason in Boston, which were approved by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts in 1817.
Barney adapted his lectures to different systems prevailing in the Western and Southwestern States, gaining sanction from respective Grand Masters.
The 'Webb Work,' inculcated by Robert Morris, is generally accepted as being of ancient origin and is expected to be adopted by Grand Lodges throughout the United States.

The 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree have undergone several changes: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
There are variations in the Hiramic legend regarding the number of Fellow Crafts who went in search (fifteen vs. twelve) and whether the sprig of acacia was left by the murderers or recanters.
There are variations in the reported location where the lost body was found: near the seaside (York Masons), Mount Moriah (Americans), Monet Lebanon (French), or near Jerusalem (others).
Accounts differ regarding the brethren's dispersal during the search: some say they dispersed widely (east, west, and south), while others state they kept within hail of each other.

Alternative names for the ruffians are preserved in subsidiary degrees, including GRAVELOT, QUIBO, and AKIROP, and in the Degree of Elect of Fifteen, they are named JUBELA-KURMAVIL (a corruption of Cromwell), JUBELO-GRAVELOT, and JUBELUM-AKIROP.
There are differing traditions on who advised the change to the substitute word: some say King Solomon, while others affirm the three Fellow Crafts adopted it without consulting him.
The Hiramic legend is described as a 'fabulous narrative' used to promote the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, similar to 'spurious initiations'.
Traditions mention instances of treachery, violation of sacred pledges, and crime among Apprentices and Craftsmen during the Temple's construction.
The connection of the 'widow's son' (Hiram Abiff) with the building of the Temple was endeared to the two monarchs, leading to a new arrangement of discipline and a legend to preserve his memory.
It was customary for Hiram Abiff to retire to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Some rituals taught that Hiram Abiff divided operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters, paying them at the pillar of Jachin and in the Middle Chamber.
Traditions state that the Ark of the Covenant had not been removed to the Holy of Holies at the time Hiram supposedly entered it, as this occurred at the dedication.
The description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has evolved over time, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.
Different Masonic jurisdictions (United States, Scotland, France) have varying orders for the "attacks" or "entrances" in the ritual.
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors at all.

The Christian allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend is explicitly stated to be "borne out in the higher Degrees of sublime Masonry," specifically the 32nd Degree (Prince of the Royal Secret) in Continental nomenclature.

The historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death during the Temple's construction is challenged by scriptural references (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) which indicate he was alive at the Temple's completion.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to Hiram the Builder.
The obligation is stated to be 'in addition to my former obligations,' indicating a progressive system of degrees.

The obligation explicitly mentions supporting the 'Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America' and the 'Grand Royal Arch Chapter of this State,' placing the Mark Master degree within the American Royal Arch system.
The 'mark' chosen by a brother must be recorded in a 'book of marks.'
The value of a brother's mark, when a favor cannot be granted, is specified as 'half a shekel of silver, or quarter of a dollar,' linking ancient and modern monetary equivalents.
Some Lodges do not require all brethren to rise after the Right Worshipful Master gives four raps, choosing instead to keep their seats.
Masonic tradition informs that the sea-coast at Joppa was so nearly perpendicular it was difficult for workmen to ascend without assistance from above, which was afforded by guards stationed there.
The Mark Master Mason degree builds upon the narrative of Hiram Abiff's death from the preceding degree.
The Key-Stone allegory describes its creation by Hiram Abiff, its rejection by the Junior, Senior, and Master Overseers, and its eventual discovery and application by order of King Solomon.
The degree incorporates a scriptural allusion to a 'white stone' and a 'new name' for those who overcome.
The new name is formed from the initials 'Hiram, Tyrian, Widow's, Son, Sendeth, To, King, Solomon' found on the Key-Stone.
The circle of letters, originally Hiram Abiff's mark, is now a general mark of the degree, where each brother places his own private mark.
The traditional price of a brother's mark is a Jewish half shekel of silver, equivalent to one-quarter of a dollar.
The Mark Master degree was founded by Grand Masters Solomon, Hiram King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff.
The degree's purpose is to provide an honorary reward for zeal, fidelity, and attachment to Masonry, and to ensure no worthy brother suffers extreme indigence.
The moral teachings of the chisel and mallet are noted as "Monitorial," indicating they are part of standard Masonic instruction.
The degree is founded on Masonic tradition described in the ancient Book of Constitutions.
The Temple was finished in seven years and six months, with the cope-stone celebrated by the fraternity with great joy.
The joy was interrupted by the sudden death of Master Hiram Abiff, who was interred near the Temple.
After mourning Hiram Abiff, the tabernacle of Moses and its holy relics were lodged in the Temple.
The grip originated at the completion of the Temple when King Solomon, pleased with the master builder, exclaimed 'Hail, Rabboni'.
A procession was formed, the ark safely seated, the cap stone placed in the principal arch, and the Lodge closed with solemn invocations to Deity.
It is an established doctrine of the Order that while three form a Lodge, five may hold it, seven only can make it perfect.
The 'Excellent Master' degree is noted as being used by 'transatlantic brethren'.
The practice of thrice-repeated actions and the significance of the number three are compared to the Church of Rome's customs, specifically an Ursuline Convent rule of three knocks.

The veneration of the number six by Druids, including their principal ceremonies on the sixth day of the moon and gathering mistletoe in groups of six, is presented as a historical parallel to Masonic practices.
Historical discoveries in Ethiopia and Egypt show arches with keystones predating King Solomon's Temple, providing ancient context for this architectural element.
This historical account is sourced from 'Fellows's Inquiry into the Origin, History, and Purport of Freemasonry,' pp. 308, 309.
caution_notes: Disclosure of secrets to outsiders in medieval craft guilds could lead to extreme consequences.
Intellectual knowledge not conforming to orthodox teachings could lead to accusations of heresy.
The dire consequences of the Ruffians' actions serve as a caution against attempting to obtain knowledge or rewards through illicit or unearned means.
A Clandestine Lodge is one that is not working with a legitimate charter from a Grand Lodge and is considered 'in the dark'.
Only Brethren from RECOGNIZED Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.
Masons must be particularly attentive to prudence in all strange and mixed companies.
Never let fall the least sign, token, or word whereby the secrets of Freemasonry might be unlawfully obtained.
Particular attention should be paid in all strange and mixed companies never to let fall the least sign, token, or word whereby the secrets of Freemasonry might be unlawfully obtained.
No contention should ever exist among members of the society of friends and brothers.

The Worshipful Master explicitly warns the candidate of 'amazing trials and dangers', a 'rough and rugged road to travel, beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers', and the possibility of losing his life, stating it 'will not be the first instance of the kind'.
The candidate is not intentionally injured in any Degree of Masonry. The blow is simulated with a buckskin bag, not a real setting-maul.
The fall is safely caught by a canvas held by brethren, ensuring the candidate is unharmed.
King Solomon threatens to deem the twelve Fellow Crafts as murderers and make them suffer if they fail to find the ruffians.
The graphic and severe nature of the self-condemnations uttered by the ruffians, detailing gruesome physical punishments.
The practice of using a large drum or rolling a cannon-ball to represent the execution sounds is not practiced in city Lodges.
The use of a large drum or cannon-ball for sound effects during the ruffians' execution is not practiced in city Lodges.
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.

The 'work' in Minnesota and New York is 'strongly impregnated with what is called, among Masons, "Morganry"--very similar to the disclosures of Morgan and Richardson,' implying a caution regarding unauthorized disclosures.
The Third Degree's traditions are 'unfortunately hyperbolical; its history apocryphal, and its legends fabulous,' cautioning against literal interpretations.
Professor Stuart endeavored to show that the legend of the Third Degree is an 'imposture' due to the Latin origin of the criminals' names, not Hebrew.
The text notes that the uniformity of Freemasonry on certain subjects is a 'delicate point' and difficult to clarify for brethren, implying a need for discretion regarding ritual variations.
CASSIA is sometimes improperly and ignorantly used for acacia.
The interpolated word (substitute for the lost word) is not a real word in any language, but merely a jumble of letters forming a sound without meaning.

Masons should not literally believe Dr. Anderson's explanation of the exhumation of H.A.B. as a real historical fact, as Hiram Abiff was known to be alive years after the Temple's completion, indicating the story is allegorical.

The historical veracity of the Hiramic legend is questioned, with references to Dr. Anderson and Josephus stating Hiram Abiff lived to complete the Temple and died in Tyre, contradicting the ritual narrative.

Inconsistencies in ritual narratives are highlighted, such as Hiram Abiff's supposed entry into the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the relevant architectural features (pillars, Middle Chamber) were built.
A divergence from uniformity is noted in describing the places where the instruments of Hiram's murder were supposedly used.

The author explicitly states that the tradition of Hiram's death during the Temple's construction is "merely allegorical" and not historically factual, citing scriptural evidence (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) that Hiram was alive at the Temple's completion. This serves as a caution against a literal interpretation of the legend.

I will not give the secrets of a Mark Master Mason to any one of an inferior degree, nor to any other person in the known world, except it be a true and lawful brother, or brethren, of this degree; and not unto him nor unto them whom I shall hear so to be, but unto him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Lodge, or a brother of this Degree, to the value of his wages (or one penny), myself, knowingly, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.

I will not sell, swap, barter, or exchange my mark, which I shall hereafter choose, after it has been recorded in the book of marks, for any other one, unless it be a dead mark, or one of an older date, nor will I pledge it a second time until it is lawfully redeemed from the first pledge.

Binding myself under no less penalty than to have my right ear smitten off, that I may forever be unable to hear the word, and my right hand chopped off, as the penalty of an impostor, if I should ever prove wilfully guilty of violating any part of this my solemn oath, or obligation, of a Mark Master Mason.
The candidate is admonished never to give the words of the grip in any way but that in which he received them.
An impostor discovered demanding wages not due should have his right hand chopped off.
The penalty for unlawfully divulging any of the secrets of this degree includes suffering the right ear to be smote off.
An additional penalty for an imposter who divulges secrets unlawfully is to have the right hand stricken off.
The penalty of the obligation for this degree involves having one's breast torn open, heart torn out, and exposed to rot upon the dung hill, if the secrets are divulged unlawfully.

entries.title: Allegory, Exoteric, and Esoteric: Definitions
entries.short_summary: This section provides definitions for the terms "Allegory," "Exoteric," and "Esoteric," sourced from the Oxford English Dictionary. Allegory is defined as the use of symbols in a story or picture to convey a hidden or ulterior meaning, typically moral or political, or the symbolic representation itself. Exoteric refers to knowledge or doctrines intended for or intelligible to the general public or outer circle of disciples. Esoteric refers to philosophical doctrines or modes of speech designed for, or intelligible only by, an inner circle of advanced or privileged disciples, implying privacy and confidentiality.
entries.full_summary:
This section provides definitions for the terms "Allegory," "Exoteric," and "Esoteric," sourced from the Oxford English Dictionary. Allegory is defined as the use of symbols in a story or picture to convey a hidden or ulterior meaning, typically moral or political, or the symbolic representation itself. Exoteric refers to knowledge or doctrines intended for or intelligible to the general public or outer circle of disciples. Esoteric refers to philosophical doctrines or modes of speech designed for, or intelligible only by, an inner circle of advanced or privileged disciples, implying privacy and confidentiality.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0023 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Section

The Third Section of the ritual elucidates the fundamental nature and principles of Freemasonry, asserting its status as the most ancient and moral science known to humanity. It emphasizes that every figure, character, and emblem within a Lodge possesses a profound moral meaning, serving to strongly instill the practice of virtue in its members.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0023 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0112 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: the Burning Bush and the Captivity

This section details a Royal Arch ritual reenacting two pivotal biblical narratives: Moses' encounter with the Burning Bush at Mount Horeb, where he is instructed to remove his shoes from holy ground, and the Babylonian Captivity. The ritual portrays the destruction of Jerusalem and its Temple due to the transgressions of King Zedekiah and the people, followed by Cyrus's proclamation allowing the return to Jerusalem to rebuild the Second Temple. Candidates are guided through these events, experiencing a simulated siege and captivity before being symbolically liberated.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0112 -->
practical_elements: Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads Exodus 3:1-6 during the first circuit.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Bandages are slipped up from the candidates' eyes in front of the burning bush.
A member personates the Deity behind the bush, calling 'Moses! Moses!'
The Principal Sojourner answers for candidates: 'Here I am.'

The Deity instructs: 'Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground. I am the God of thy fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.'
Candidates are directed to kneel and their faces are covered again.
The Principal Sojourner states: 'And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look upon God.'
Candidates are led three times around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads 2 Chronicles 36:11-20 during the three circuits.
Companions make loud noises (rolling cannon-balls, clashing swords, shouting, groaning, whistling, stamping, throwing down benches) to simulate the siege and destruction of Jerusalem.
Candidates are seized, thrown on the floor, bound hand and foot, and carried into the preparation-room.
Companions shout: 'Hurra for the captives!' several times.
The Captain of the Host opens the door and announces liberty, citing Cyrus's proclamation to build a second Temple.
The Principal Sojourner requests the proclamation to be read.
The Captain of the Host reads Ezra 1:1-3 (Cyrus's proclamation).
The Captain of the Host asks candidates if they are willing to go to Jerusalem.
The Principal Sojourner consults candidates and states willingness, but asks for a pass-word.
The Captain of the Host reads Exodus 3:13-14 (implied, regarding the pass-word).
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Every figure, character, and emblem depicted in a Lodge carries a moral meaning.

The Burning Bush symbolizes the divine presence, holiness, and God's unconsumed power, requiring reverence and humility (removal of shoes). The Babylonian Captivity represents the consequences of transgression, the destruction of the sacred due to human evil, and a period of spiritual bondage. Cyrus's Proclamation signifies divine intervention, redemption, and the opportunity for spiritual rebuilding and the restoration of purpose, akin to rebuilding the Temple. The entire journey symbolizes a spiritual progression through trials towards enlightenment and inner reformation.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns the precise definitions of key terms like "allegory," "exoteric," and "esoteric," which are essential for understanding the nature of Masonic teachings, their symbolic representations, and the distinction between publicly accessible and privately shared knowledge within the fraternity.

Freemasonry is an ancient and moral science. All symbols and elements within the Lodge are designed to teach and inculcate the practice of virtue.

Candidates learn the importance of reverence for the divine and sacred spaces, the severe consequences of disobedience and moral transgression, and the necessity of humility before God. The ritual teaches about the possibility of redemption and spiritual rebuilding after periods of destruction or moral decline, emphasizing the concept of a 'second temple of reformation' within oneself. It also highlights the need for divine knowledge or a 'pass-word' to navigate the spiritual path.
tradition_notes: The Third Section is traditionally associated with explaining the core tenets and moral philosophy of Freemasonry.
The ritual dramatically reenacts biblical narratives from Exodus, 2 Chronicles, and Ezra.
The use of physical representations, such as the burning bush, and sound effects, like those simulating a siege, are integral to the ritual's immersive experience.
The roles of the Principal Sojourner and Captain of the Host are crucial in guiding candidates and narrating the historical and spiritual events.
The opening prayer introduces the concept of 'holiness to the Lord' and 'incense of piety' as continuous spiritual offerings.
A central theme is the rebuilding of a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' has been metaphorically destroyed by sin.

entries.title: The Exoteric Teaching: Virtue, Justice, and Merit
entries.short_summary: The exoteric teaching derived from the Hiram Abiff narrative focuses on virtue, justice, and merit. Hiram exemplifies virtue ethics, demonstrating integrity regardless of consequences, in contrast to consequentialist ethics. The concept of justice is presented as universal, emphasizing that actions have consequences. Additionally, the teaching promotes earning one's achievements and believing in one's merits, establishing these as foundational principles of the Hiram Abiff story.
entries.full_summary:
The exoteric teaching derived from the Hiram Abiff narrative focuses on virtue, justice, and merit. Hiram exemplifies virtue ethics, demonstrating integrity regardless of consequences, in contrast to consequentialist ethics. The concept of justice is presented as universal, emphasizing that actions have consequences. Additionally, the teaching promotes earning one's achievements and believing in one's merits, establishing these as foundational principles of the Hiram Abiff story.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag

The Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America is recited, affirming loyalty to the nation and its foundational principles of unity, liberty, and justice for all.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0011 -->
