### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Section

The Third Section of the ritual elucidates the fundamental nature and principles of Freemasonry, asserting its status as the most ancient and moral science known to humanity. It emphasizes that every figure, character, and emblem within a Lodge possesses a profound moral meaning, serving to strongly instill the practice of virtue in its members.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0023 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Truth

Truth is presented as a divine attribute and the fundamental basis for all virtues. It is identified as the first lesson taught in Masonry, guiding members to regulate their conduct. Adherence to truth fosters sincerity, plain dealing, and mutual prosperity, eliminating hypocrisy and deceit. The text also alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0042 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Justice

This section defines Justice as the standard of right that ensures every individual receives their due without distinction. It highlights Justice as a virtue consistent with divine and human laws, serving as the foundation of civil society. Masons are exhorted to practice Justice invariably, never deviating from its principles, as it is essential for constituting a truly good man.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0042 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

The Second Section of the ritual recounts the Institution's historical traditions and presents an exemplary instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity. It emphasizes the importance of spreading brotherly love and affection, which serves as the cement uniting Masons into a sacred society where the only acceptable contention is a noble emulation to excel in work and agreement.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0084 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Naked Heart

The concept of the 'Naked Heart' signifies that ultimate justice will inevitably confront individuals, as all thoughts, words, and actions, even those concealed from human perception, are eventually revealed and judged.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0084 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Musical Selection

This section details the 'Musical Selection' portion of a Masonic gathering, which includes a Chaplain's prayer invoking divine blessing for the proceedings and for those about to assume leadership roles. Following the prayer, the Marshal delivers an address explaining the significance of observing the Festival of St. John the Evangelist. The Marshal emphasizes that Freemasonry's purpose extends beyond social enjoyment, focusing on duty, reconsecration through solemn promises, and the propagation of core principles. The discourse highlights Freemasonry's historical influence, its role in promoting liberty, equality, fraternity, law, and order, and its teachings on the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, and the importance of loving God and neighbor. It also stresses the Craft's commitment to elevating humanity, stimulating patriotism, encouraging liberal arts and sciences, promoting education, and upholding the dignity of labor, while remaining aloof from specific creeds.

The text explains the purpose of the Craft and announces the upcoming induction of chosen brethren into their respective offices to preside over the Lodge for the next twelve months.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master

This section details the installation ceremony of a Worshipful Master, beginning with the Master's assent to fifteen Ancient Charges and Regulations that outline moral conduct, civic duties, obedience to Masonic law, and lodge governance. The ceremony proceeds with the investiture of the Master with the collar and the presentation of key Masonic implements and documents: the Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, Lodge Charter, Grand Lodge Regulations, By-Laws, and the Holy Writings. Each item is accompanied by its symbolic meaning and practical instructions for the Master's responsibilities in governing the Lodge.

This section describes a procedural sequence during a lodge installation, where Grand Honors are given, the Worshipful Master seats the Lodge, and an appropriate ode is sung, with an exception noted for public installations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0142 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brothers Senior and Junior Wardens

This section addresses the Senior and Junior Wardens, emphasizing their deep understanding of Masonic principles and their expected exemplary conduct. They are tasked with assisting the Master, diffusing light, and imparting knowledge to those under their care. The text highlights their crucial role in succession, ensuring the Craft's continuous instruction, and anticipates their future conduct will earn the applause of their brethren and a good conscience.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0142 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0152 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Charge to the Grand Lecturer

This section outlines the charge to the Grand Lecturer, detailing their duties to instruct the Craft, communicate knowledge, preserve ritual and traditions, safeguard ancient Landmarks, and illustrate the Institution's principles to Subordinate Lodges. It also includes an interpolated charge to a Grand Lodge Secretary, covering responsibilities such as recording proceedings, managing Grand Lodge finances, and conducting official correspondence.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0152 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0162 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Fundamental Principles

This section introduces the concept of fundamental principles within Freemasonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0162 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0170 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Vii. Certain Great Principles

This section outlines the fundamental principles of Freemasonry, emphasizing the universality of the Brotherhood, founded upon established Landmarks. It details the Right of Visitation for Masons in good standing to any Lodge, provided harmony is not disturbed. Masonry is presented as non-sectarian and non-political, teaching primitive truths of religion, obedience to civil law, equality among members, and universal brotherhood. It stresses that all Masons stand upon the level, with the best being defined by their work and ability to agree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0170 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0031 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Charge and Commentary

This section presents an Entered Apprentice Charge and commentary, focusing on the 'points of profession' (Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth) and the 'points of entrance' (Guttural, Pectoral, Manual, and Pedestal). Each point of entrance is explicitly linked to one of the four cardinal virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice, with detailed explanations of their Masonic application and the importance of concealing secrets. It also touches upon how Entered Apprentices served their Master in ancient and modern times, represented by Chalk, Charcoal, and Clay.

This section details the Entered Apprentice's oath, emphasizing the importance of remembering it while kneeling at the altar with the Holy Bible, square, and compasses. It highlights the virtue of Justice as a fundamental Masonic principle, essential for civil society and the conduct of a good man. The charge received in the northeast corner of the Lodge is mentioned, alluding to the Pedestal. The text also explains how Entered Apprentices served their Master in ancient and modern times, characterized by freedom, fervency, and zeal, symbolically represented by chalk, charcoal, and clay.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0031 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0067 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a historical account of the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, who was slain by fifteen Fellow Crafts just before the completion of King Solomon's Temple. His death is presented as an instance of exceptional virtue, fortitude, and integrity, with the candidate, Brother Gabe, representing Hiram Abiff in the ritual.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0067 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0076 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge to the Lodge

This section presents a solemn charge delivered to the Lodge, emphasizing the regulation of lives by justice and virtue. It includes an optional addition by some Masters that reflects on death, eternity, and the role of the Bible in offering hope beyond the grave. The charge instructs Masons to cultivate brotherly love, relief, and truth, learning morality from the square, equality from the level, and rectitude from the plumb. It calls for emulation of Hiram Abiff's integrity and piety, using the evergreen as a symbol of virtue. Masons are urged to spread brotherly love with the trowel, govern actions with the compass, and seek the approbation of the Supreme Grand Warden, anticipating admission into the Celestial Lodge above through a blameless life and reliance on Divine Providence.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0076 -->
practical_elements: Recitation of the Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America
Regulate our conduct by the dictates of truth
Practice sincerity and plain dealing
Promote each other’s prosperity with heart and tongue
Render to every man his just due without distinction.
Invariable practice of every Mason never to deviate from the minutest principles of justice.
Marshal introduces the invocation of Deity before important undertakings.
Chaplain leads a prayer.
All present respond with 'So mote it be' after the prayer.
Marshal delivers an address on the Festival of St. John the Evangelist and Masonic principles.
Observation of the Festival of St. John the Evangelist for social enjoyment, cementing brotherly love, and consecration to duty.
Induction of brethren into their respective offices
Presiding in the Lodge for twelve months
The new Master faces the East.
The Installing Officer addresses the Ancient Charges and Regulations to the Master.
The Master signifies assent to each charge by answering "I do".
The Master is invested with the insignia of office (the collar is placed upon his shoulders).
The Square is presented with its teaching.
The Compasses are presented with their teaching.
The Rule is presented with its teaching.
The Line is presented with its teaching.
The Book of Constitutions is given in charge, with instruction to search and read it in the Lodge.
The Lodge Charter is given in charge, with instruction for careful preservation and transmission to the successor.
The Regulations of the Grand Lodge relating to Subordinate Lodges and the By-Laws for the government of the same are given in charge, with instruction for careful and punctual execution.
The Holy Writings are placed in the Master's hands as a Great Light in Masonry.
The Installing Officer calls up the brethren.

A Mason in good standing has the Right of Visitation to any Lodge, provided it does not disturb the Lodge's work or harmony.
Every Mason is considered equal to every other in the Lodge, standing upon the level.
The four points of entrance are Guttural, Pectoral, Manual, and Pedestal.
The Guttural alludes to the Mason's obligation.
The Pectoral is emblematically represented by a sharp instrument pressing the naked left breast upon first admission.

The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.

An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The narrative of Hiram Abiff symbolically conveys foundational principles of virtue, justice, and merit, illustrating the importance of integrity and ethical conduct in life.

The pledge symbolizes loyalty to the United States, its republican form of government, national unity under God, and the commitment to liberty and justice for all citizens.

Every figure, character, and emblem depicted in a Lodge carries a moral meaning.

Truth is symbolized as a divine attribute and the foundational principle for all virtues, guiding Masonic conduct and fostering integrity within the fraternity.

Justice is described as the "very cement and support of civil society," symbolizing its foundational and binding role in human organization and moral order.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.

The 'Naked Heart' symbolizes the inherent transparency of one's true self and deeds before a higher, ultimate justice. It represents the inability to hide one's inner thoughts and actions from divine or cosmic accountability, emphasizing that all will eventually be brought to light.

The invocation of Deity symbolizes the spiritual foundation of all Masonic endeavors and the reliance on divine guidance. The Festival of St. John the Evangelist, while a social occasion, is consecrated to duty, representing the ongoing commitment to Masonic principles and the renewal of vows by those taking on leadership. The Marshal's address outlines the symbolic and practical influence of Freemasonry, emphasizing its role as a moral and educational force in society, promoting universal brotherhood and adherence to divine and moral law.

The induction of officers signifies the continuity of leadership, the structured operation of the Lodge, and the fulfillment of the Craft's purposes through chosen individuals.

The Square teaches to regulate actions by rule and line, harmonizing conduct with principles of morality and virtue. The Compasses teach to limit desires, enabling one to rise to eminence by merit, live respected, and die regretted. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pressing forward in virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Holy Writings are presented as an inestimable gift from God, the Great Light in Masonry, guiding to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man.

The Wardens' role as examples of good order and regularity symbolizes the importance of leadership by virtue and adherence to Masonic laws. Their duty to diffuse light and impart knowledge represents the continuous flow of wisdom and instruction within the Craft, ensuring its perpetuation and the enlightenment of its members.

The Grand Lecturer's office symbolizes the guardianship of Masonic knowledge, the integrity of ritual, and the enduring nature of the Fraternity's foundational principles. It represents the commitment to enlighten the uninformed and ensure the continuity of ancient traditions and Landmarks, acting as a beacon for the Institution's design and tendency.

The phrase 'All stand upon the level' symbolizes the principle of equality among all Masons within the Lodge, regardless of external status or wealth.

The Guttural, Pectoral, Manual, and Pedestal points of entrance symbolize the four cardinal virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice. Temperance signifies due restraint on passions to avoid excess and protect secrets. Fortitude represents a steady purpose to endure pain or danger, safeguarding entrusted secrets against illegal attacks. Prudence teaches regulation of life by reason, ensuring wise judgment and careful conduct, especially in mixed company, to prevent unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets. Justice is the standard of right, rendering to every man his due, and is the cement of civil society, requiring Masons to never deviate from its principles. Chalk, Charcoal, and Clay represent freedom, fervency, and zeal, respectively, in how Entered Apprentices serve their Master.

The position of the hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses alludes to the Manual. The charge in the northeast corner of the Lodge, with feet forming a right angle, is an allusion to the Pedestal. Chalk symbolizes freedom, leaving a trace with the slightest touch. Charcoal symbolizes fervency, capable of melting obdurate metals when well lit. Clay, or mother earth, symbolizes zeal, in its ability to bring forth.

The death of Hiram Abiff symbolizes an instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity that is rarely equaled. His steadfastness in guarding the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death, serves as a profound example.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.

The plumb-line symbolizes justice, and the square symbolizes virtue, guiding actions and lives. The square also teaches morality, the level teaches equality, and the plumb teaches rectitude of life. The evergreen blooming at the head of the grave symbolizes the place of interment and virtue's ever-blooming loveliness, designating Free and Accepted Masons. The trowel symbolizes spreading the cement of brotherly love and affection. The compass symbolizes circumscribing words and actions, promoting self-governance. The 'Celestial Lodge above' and 'spiritual temple, that house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens' symbolize the afterlife and divine reward for a virtuous Masonic life.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates learn to embody virtue ethics, acting with integrity irrespective of potential consequences. They are taught the universal principle of justice, understanding that actions lead to corresponding outcomes, and the importance of earning accomplishments and having faith in their own merits.

While not a direct Masonic lesson, the pledge reinforces principles of civic duty, patriotism, and adherence to the ideals of liberty and justice, which are compatible with Masonic teachings on good citizenship and moral conduct.

Freemasonry is an ancient and moral science. All symbols and elements within the Lodge are designed to teach and inculcate the practice of virtue.

The primary lesson for a candidate is to be good and true, understanding that truth is the foundation of all virtue and should govern their actions and interactions, leading to sincerity and mutual support.

A Mason must consistently practice Justice, ensuring that all individuals receive their rightful due without prejudice. This virtue is paramount for personal integrity and the stability of society, aligning with both divine and human laws.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.

Candidates are taught that they are ultimately accountable for all their thoughts, words, and actions, regardless of whether these are known to other men. This lesson encourages integrity, moral conduct, and the understanding that justice will prevail.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The Worshipful Master is charged with upholding moral law, being a peaceful and law-abiding citizen, respecting civil authorities, working diligently, living creditably, and acting honorably. They must venerate the Order's rulers, submit to Lodge resolutions, avoid quarrels and excess, be cautious, courteous, and faithful to the Lodge. The Master is to respect true brethren, discountenance imposters, promote the good of society, cultivate social virtues, and propagate Masonic knowledge. They must pay homage to the Grand Master, conform to Grand Lodge edicts, and preserve the Ancient Landmarks. Regular attendance at Grand Lodge committees and communications is required, along with ensuring proper Lodge formation, initiation, and visitor examination. The Master is taught to regulate actions by morality, limit desires, observe duty, pursue virtue, avoid dissimulation, and be guided by divine truth.

The candidate learns that leadership within Masonry requires a profound understanding of its principles, exemplary conduct, and a commitment to teaching and guiding others. It underscores the importance of self-improvement, learning from others' strengths and weaknesses, and being prepared to assume greater responsibilities for the benefit of the fraternity.

While primarily a charge to an officer, the text implicitly teaches the importance of upholding Masonic duties and obligations, the value of continuous learning and instruction, and the necessity of preserving the Fraternity's ancient ritual and Landmarks. It also highlights the expectation for officers to exemplify promptness and integrity in their conduct.

Candidates learn that Freemasonry is a universal brotherhood, transcending specific religions or political affiliations. They are taught the importance of obedience to civil law, the fundamental equality of all members, and the principle of brotherhood towards all people. The lesson emphasizes that true merit lies in one's actions and ability to foster harmony.

An Entered Apprentice must practice Temperance by restraining affections and passions, avoiding excess and vicious habits to protect Masonic secrets. Fortitude is essential to withstand any attempts to extort secrets. Prudence guides conduct in and out of the Lodge, ensuring no sign, token, or word is revealed unlawfully. Justice is a fundamental principle, requiring Masons to render to every man his just due and never deviate from its minutest principles. The candidate is taught to serve with freedom, fervency, and zeal.

The Entered Apprentice must always remember their oath. The virtue of Justice is paramount, serving as the standard of right, ensuring fairness to all, and acting as the foundation of civil society. Masons should never deviate from the principles of justice. Apprentices are taught to serve with freedom, fervency, and zeal, embodying these qualities in their Masonic and daily lives.

The candidate is taught to exemplify virtue, fortitude, and integrity, drawing inspiration from the historical account of Grand Master Hiram Abiff's unwavering commitment to his trust, even when faced with mortal danger.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.

Candidates are taught to regulate their lives by justice and virtue, cultivate brotherly love, relief, and truth, and learn moral lessons from Masonic working tools. They are encouraged to emulate the integrity and piety of Hiram Abiff, maintain virtuous conduct, and prepare for an afterlife in the Celestial Lodge above through a pure and blameless life and reliance on Divine Providence. The charge instills a sense of mortality while offering hope for eternal bliss and divine approbation.
tradition_notes: The principles of virtue, justice, and merit are presented as foundational to the Hiram Abiff story within Masonic tradition.
This is a common civic tradition in the United States, often recited at public gatherings and educational institutions.
The Third Section is traditionally associated with explaining the core tenets and moral philosophy of Freemasonry.
To be good and true is the first lesson taught in Masonry.
The text alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
The Second Section recites the historical traditions of the Institution.
Freemasonry has a tradition of invoking a blessing of Deity before any great or important undertaking.
The Festival of St. John the Evangelist is a traditional observance in Freemasonry, consecrated to duty and promoting social enjoyment and brotherly love.
Freemasonry's origins are described as ancient, with its beginnings not recorded on written pages of history.

The practice of inducting officers to preside over a Lodge for a specific term (e.g., twelve months) is a traditional aspect of Masonic governance and ensures the continuous operation and leadership of the Lodge.
The Master must assent to Ancient Charges and Regulations which point out the duty of the Master of a Lodge.
The Master must hold in veneration the original rulers and patrons of the Order of Freemasonry and their regular successors.
The Master must submit to the awards and resolutions of brethren in Lodge convened, consistent with the constitutions of the Order.
The Master must respect genuine and true brethren and discountenance dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
The Master must conform to every edict of the Grand Lodge or General Assembly of Masons that is not subversive of the principles and groundwork of Masonry.
It is admitted that it is not in the power of any man, or body of men, to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
It is admitted that no new Lodge can be formed without permission of the Grand Lodge.
It is admitted that no countenance ought to be given to any irregular Lodge, or to any person clandestinely initiated therein, as being contrary to the ancient charges of the Order.
It is admitted that no person can be regularly made a Freemason in, or admitted a member of, any regular Lodge, without previous notice, and due inquiry into his character.
It is agreed that no visitors shall be received into your Lodge without due examination, and producing proper vouchers of their having been initiated in a regular Lodge.
Masters have supported these regulations in all ages before.
The practice of giving Grand Honors and singing an ode is part of the lodge installation ceremony.
The address to the Senior and Junior Wardens is a traditional part of an installation ceremony, delivered by the Installing Officer.
preserve our ritual and our traditions in the memory of the Fraternity
ancient Landmarks of the Institution
time-honored Institution
The Right of Visitation is a fundamental tradition allowing Masons in good standing to visit any Lodge.
Some Masters qualify for office by purchasing and amending Masonic exposures like Richardson's or Avery Allyn's.
The lecture presented is used in some Lodges, especially in the Northwestern States.
The text references 'Masonic Monitors, on "Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth."' and 'Webb's Monitor' for further information.
Reference to how Entered Apprentices served their Master in ancient times and how they should in modern times.
The representation of apprentices by Chalk, Charcoal, and Clay is attributed to Webb's Monitor.
The narrative describes the premeditated death of Hiram Abiff by fifteen Fellow Crafts who sought to obtain the secrets of a Master Mason before the Temple's completion.
The motive of the Fellow Crafts was to gain the ability to travel in foreign countries and receive Master's wages.
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
Some Masters add a lecture on the emblems, focusing on the solemn thought of death and the promise of eternity through the Bible.
caution_notes: Hypocrisy and deceit are unknown among us when influenced by the principle of truth.
Masons should never deviate from the minutest principles of Justice.
No contention should ever exist among members of the society of friends and brothers.
Do not be concerned in plots and conspiracies against the government; patiently submit to law and authorities.
Avoid private piques and quarrels, and guard against intemperance and excess.
Discountenance imposters and all dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
It is not in the power of any man or body of men to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
No new Lodge can be formed without Grand Lodge permission; do not countenance irregular Lodges or clandestinely initiated persons.
No person can be regularly made a Freemason or admitted to a regular Lodge without previous notice and due inquiry into character.
No visitors shall be received without due examination and proper vouchers of regular initiation.
The Lodge Charter should never be out of the Master's immediate control and must be duly transmitted to the successor.
Grand Honors are not given during a public installation.
to see that the ancient Landmarks of the Institution are not removed by unskillful hands
Masons are taught to avoid excess or contracting any licentious or vicious habit, the indulgence of which might lead to disclosing valuable secrets.
Fortitude serves as a safeguard against any illegal attack to extort secrets.
Prudence dictates never to let fall the least sign, token, or word in strange and mixed companies, whereby the secrets of Masonry might be unlawfully obtained.
Always remember your oath as an Entered Apprentice to conceal and never reveal secrets.
Secrets of Masonry might be unlawfully obtained.
Always remember your oath as an Entered Apprentice.
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.

entries.title: Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising
entries.short_summary: This section introduces Albert Mackey's list of seventeen different symbolic meanings or interpretations for the raising of Hiram Abiff. It highlights the diversity of perspectives on this central Masonic event, ranging from historical death to various allegories, religious parallels, and even political or philosophical interpretations. The text also notes that three specific meanings—the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and the Osiris legend—will be examined in more detail.
entries.full_summary:
This section introduces Albert Mackey's list of seventeen different symbolic meanings or interpretations for the raising of Hiram Abiff. It highlights the diversity of perspectives on this central Masonic event, ranging from historical death to various allegories, religious parallels, and even political or philosophical interpretations. The text also notes that three specific meanings—the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and the Osiris legend—will be examined in more detail.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Raising of a Candidate

This section clarifies the meaning of being 'raised to the Sublime Degree of Master Mason,' explaining that 'sublime' signifies 'not to be excelled' and positions the Master Mason Degree as the pinnacle of Symbolic Freemasonry. It traces the term 'raised' to operative masonry, where only a few journeymen were elevated to Master Craftsman. Historically, only the presiding officer was a Master Mason, but the fraternity evolved to divide initiation into three degrees: Entered Apprentice, Fellow of the Craft, and Master Mason. Ultimately, being raised means a new member has achieved full membership in a Symbolic Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0013 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0063 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Hiram Legend: Discovery of the Body and the Lost Word

This section details the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body by twelve Fellow Crafts, who report their findings to King Solomon. They confirm the body's identity through the jewel of his office but state they found no Master's word or key. King Solomon then declares the Master's word lost and decrees that the first sign given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body is raised shall serve as the new regulation for Master's Lodges until the true word is found. The Master then performs the sign of distress, saying, "O Lord my God, I fear the Master's word is forever lost!" The section also notes a ritual variation regarding the initial sign made at the body, followed by a solemn march and dirge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0063 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
practical_elements: Twelve Fellow Crafts discover the body of Hiram Abiff.
A jewel of office is taken from the candidate's neck.
Fellow Crafts report "Tidings of the body" to King Solomon.
The jewel of Grand Master Hiram Abiff is presented to King Solomon for identification.
King Solomon instructs twelve Fellow Crafts to assist in raising the body.
King Solomon declares the Master's word lost and establishes a temporary replacement: the first sign given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body is raised.
All form a circle around the body, with the Master and Wardens at the head.
The Master makes the sign of "distress" of a Master Mason by raising both hands and arms above the head (Grand Hailing Sign of Distress, Fig. 7, p. 18).
The Master utters, "O Lord my God, I fear the Master's word is forever lost!"
In some Lodges, the Master gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body with the thumb inward (Sign of a Master Mason, Fig. 6, p. 18).
The party marches around the body with the sun.
A dirge is sung, accompanied by an organ or melodeon if available, in a solemn manner.
The Master Mason candidate represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the exemplification of the degree.
Hiram Abiff's daily practice involved entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.
Hiram Abiff was struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge across his throat, a square across his breast, and a setting-maul on his forehead.
The ruffians buried Hiram's body in the rubbish of the Temple until low-twelve, then moved it to a grave dug due east and west, six feet perpendicular, west of Mount Moriah.
An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.
The twelve recanting Fellow Crafts appeared before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of their innocence.
Search parties were divided into groups of three, traveling east, west, north, and south.
The Master places his hands in the form of a duegard of a Master Mason, alluding to the manner in which Hiram's body was found.
Attempted raising of the body using the Entered Apprentice grip
Attempted raising of the body using the Fellow Craft grip
Successful raising of the body using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw
Raising on the five points of fellowship
Explanation of the several signs and tokens of the Master Mason Degree, commencing with the first and ending with the grips (referencing Figs. 5, 6, 7, 16, 17 and Note L, Appendix)
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The raising of Hiram Abiff is presented as having at least seventeen different symbolic meanings, according to Albert Mackey. These interpretations include: the actual death of Hiram Abiff, the legend of Osiris, an allegory of the setting sun, the expulsion of Adam from Eden, the death of Abel, Noah's entry into the ark, Joseph's mourning for Jacob, an astronomical problem, the death and resurrection of Christ, the persecution of the Templars, the violent death of Charles I, a drama invented by Cromwell, a representation of old age, a drama of regeneration, a savage ceremony of initiation, a memorial to the murder of Thomas Becket, and an invention by the Jacobites. These diverse meanings underscore the rich allegorical potential of the Hiramic legend.

The term 'sublime' in 'Sublime Degree of Master Mason' symbolizes that this degree is the highest and 'not to be excelled' within Symbolic Freemasonry. The term 'raised' symbolically connects to the operative tradition where stonemasons were 'elevated' to Master Craftsman status, signifying a significant advancement and attainment of full membership.

The discovery of Hiram Abiff's body signifies the culmination of the search for the Master Architect, while the declaration of the "lost word" represents the loss of perfect knowledge or the true essence of the Master Mason's degree. The temporary adoption of a new word and sign underscores the idea of substitution and the ongoing quest for truth. The signs of distress and the dirge emphasize the profound grief and the solemnity associated with the loss of a Master and the sacred word.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.
entries.candidate_lesson:
A candidate should understand that the central event of the raising of Hiram Abiff is open to a multitude of interpretations, reflecting its profound allegorical depth. This encourages a broader perspective on Masonic symbolism and the various philosophical, historical, and religious lenses through which the legend can be understood, emphasizing that its meaning is not singular but multifaceted.

The candidate learns that attaining the Master Mason Degree signifies reaching the pinnacle of Symbolic Freemasonry and achieving full membership within a Symbolic Lodge. This elevation carries historical significance rooted in operative masonry's structure of advancement.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the importance of the search for truth and knowledge. The ritualistic actions and declarations impress upon the candidate the gravity of the Hiramic legend and the commitment to preserving Masonic traditions, even in the face of loss, by adopting temporary measures until the true word can be recovered. It also teaches the significance of fidelity and the consequences of betrayal.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.
tradition_notes: Albert Mackey compiled a list of at least seventeen different traditional or proposed meanings for the raising of Hiram Abiff, indicating a long history of diverse interpretations within Masonic thought.
The text specifically mentions the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and the Osiris legend as significant interpretations deserving closer examination.
The term 'raised' originates from operative Masonry, where journeymen were elevated to Master Craftsman.
Historically, only the presiding officer of a Lodge held the status of a Master Mason.
The fraternity's system of initiation evolved to be divided into three parts: Entered Apprentice, Fellow of the Craft, and Master Masons.
In some Lodges, the Master does not make the sign of distress first at the body, but only gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body with the thumb inward.
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
caution_notes: The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.
