### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag

The Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America is recited, affirming loyalty to the nation and its foundational principles of unity, liberty, and justice for all.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0011 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Truth

Truth is presented as a divine attribute and the fundamental basis for all virtues. It is identified as the first lesson taught in Masonry, guiding members to regulate their conduct. Adherence to truth fosters sincerity, plain dealing, and mutual prosperity, eliminating hypocrisy and deceit. The text also alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0042 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Justice

This section defines Justice as the standard of right that ensures every individual receives their due without distinction. It highlights Justice as a virtue consistent with divine and human laws, serving as the foundation of civil society. Masons are exhorted to practice Justice invariably, never deviating from its principles, as it is essential for constituting a truly good man.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0042 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

The Second Section of the ritual recounts the Institution's historical traditions and presents an exemplary instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity. It emphasizes the importance of spreading brotherly love and affection, which serves as the cement uniting Masons into a sacred society where the only acceptable contention is a noble emulation to excel in work and agreement.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0084 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Naked Heart

The concept of the 'Naked Heart' signifies that ultimate justice will inevitably confront individuals, as all thoughts, words, and actions, even those concealed from human perception, are eventually revealed and judged.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0084 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Circuit

This section, titled "Third Circuit," details a solemn ritualistic exchange among lodge officers and the Chaplain, reflecting on the transient nature of life, the inevitability of death, and the sorrows of mortality. It progresses through lamentations of human frailty and grief, balanced by expressions of faith in God's justice, mercy, and the promise of eternal life and resurrection. The ritual includes prayers for strength, wisdom, and comfort for the bereaved, culminating in a collective affirmation of hope and trust in divine beneficence, often associated with a Lodge of Sorrow.

This section describes a solemn Masonic ceremony, likely a Lodge of Sorrow, where the Master reflects on the inevitability of death and the transient nature of life. The Chaplain reads from 1 Corinthians 15, discussing the resurrection of the dead and the transformation from corruptible to incorruptible bodies. A choir sings from Job 19 about seeing God after death. The Orator is then called upon to recount the virtues of the deceased, with an emphasis on Masonic charity and non-judgment.

This section details the 'Third Circuit' of a Masonic memorial service, which includes scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job, a choir performance, and an oration emphasizing truth and Masonic Charity towards the deceased. The ritual proceeds with the Junior Warden sprinkling corn, the Senior Warden sprinkling wine, and the Master sprinkling perfumed oil on the coffin, each accompanied by symbolic interpretations related to resurrection, spiritual refreshment, and consolation. The ceremony concludes with the Master placing a lambskin apron and holding a white glove on the coffin, symbolizing innocence, purity, and the hope of a welcome reception in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

This section details the ritualistic farewell for a deceased Master Mason, highlighting the symbolism of the white glove as an emblem of Fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, and the sprig of evergreen as an emblem of Faith in the immortality of the soul. It describes the Master's address, the placement of the evergreen on the coffin, a collective farewell by the brethren, and a three-circuit procession around the coffin accompanied by Grand Honors.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Musical Selection

This section details the 'Musical Selection' portion of a Masonic gathering, which includes a Chaplain's prayer invoking divine blessing for the proceedings and for those about to assume leadership roles. Following the prayer, the Marshal delivers an address explaining the significance of observing the Festival of St. John the Evangelist. The Marshal emphasizes that Freemasonry's purpose extends beyond social enjoyment, focusing on duty, reconsecration through solemn promises, and the propagation of core principles. The discourse highlights Freemasonry's historical influence, its role in promoting liberty, equality, fraternity, law, and order, and its teachings on the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, and the importance of loving God and neighbor. It also stresses the Craft's commitment to elevating humanity, stimulating patriotism, encouraging liberal arts and sciences, promoting education, and upholding the dignity of labor, while remaining aloof from specific creeds.

The text explains the purpose of the Craft and announces the upcoming induction of chosen brethren into their respective offices to preside over the Lodge for the next twelve months.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0149 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Charge to the Grand Chaplain

The charge to the Grand Chaplain outlines the sacred duties of the office, including leading devotional exercises at Grand Communications and performing functions at public ceremonies. It emphasizes that while Masonry is not a religion, it serves as 'religion’s handmaid,' promoting universal tolerance. The Grand Chaplain is expected to communicate doctrines of morality and virtue to the brethren, and is entrusted with the Holy Bible, the Great Light in Masonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0149 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0067 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a historical account of the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, who was slain by fifteen Fellow Crafts just before the completion of King Solomon's Temple. His death is presented as an instance of exceptional virtue, fortitude, and integrity, with the candidate, Brother Gabe, representing Hiram Abiff in the ritual.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0067 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Hiram Abiff story serves as an allegory, potentially for figures like Christ, Osiris, Noah, Adam, or a son of Cain, or even a real person. Its core symbolic meaning lies in conveying virtue, integrity, and brotherhood. The legend also symbolizes the ability to find a substitute when something truly important is forever lost, allowing for a fulfilling life to continue. The concept of sacrifice is also highlighted, where giving up something precious is not a loss but a passing on to someone else.

The pledge symbolizes loyalty to the United States, its republican form of government, national unity under God, and the commitment to liberty and justice for all citizens.

Truth is symbolized as a divine attribute and the foundational principle for all virtues, guiding Masonic conduct and fostering integrity within the fraternity.

Justice is described as the "very cement and support of civil society," symbolizing its foundational and binding role in human organization and moral order.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.

The 'Naked Heart' symbolizes the inherent transparency of one's true self and deeds before a higher, ultimate justice. It represents the inability to hide one's inner thoughts and actions from divine or cosmic accountability, emphasizing that all will eventually be brought to light.

The "Third Circuit" symbolizes the journey of life, marked by inevitable suffering, loss, and the ultimate certainty of death. The lamentations represent the human experience of grief and the transient nature of earthly existence ("life is a vapor," "days are passed"). The responses from the Chaplain, particularly the references to a "tree planted by the waters" and "a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens," symbolize enduring faith, divine sustenance, and the promise of immortality and resurrection beyond the grave. The ritual as a whole serves as a memento mori, reminding participants of mortality while simultaneously offering comfort and hope through spiritual belief and the prospect of an afterlife.

Life is presented as 'death’s vestibule' and a 'journey to the grave,' symbolizing the transient nature of earthly existence. The comparison of human life to flowers and grass emphasizes fragility and impermanence. The 'valley of the shadow of death' symbolizes the journey through mortality with divine comfort. The scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the hope of resurrection, transformation from a natural to a spiritual body, and victory over death. The seed analogy in 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the transformation of the body after death into a new, spiritual form. The 'broad mantle of charity' symbolizes Masonic non-judgment and compassion towards the deceased.

The scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job symbolize resurrection, incorruption, transformation from mortal to immortal, and the belief in a Redeemer. The corn of nourishment symbolizes the resurrection of the body, sown in corruption and raised in incorruption, glory, power, and as a spiritual body. The wine of refreshment symbolizes spiritual refreshment, divine grace, blessings, and comfort for those afflicted or distressed. The oil of joy symbolizes comfort, consolation for the bereaved, healing of heart-wounds, the virtue of charity, increased affection, leniency, indulgence, forgiveness, and a joyful passage through death. The lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a Mason's badge, denotes the hope that the departed brother's soul, purified from imperfections, has found a welcome in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

The white glove is an emblem of Fidelity, symbolizing Masonic friendship and brotherly love, and signifies an increased obligation to the living brethren. The sprig of evergreen is an emblem of Masonic Faith in the immortality of the soul, representing the eternal principle in man that survives death and blooms perpetually. It denotes trust that the deceased's immortal spirit has entered a celestial temple and found acceptance with the Supreme Grand Master.

The invocation of Deity symbolizes the spiritual foundation of all Masonic endeavors and the reliance on divine guidance. The Festival of St. John the Evangelist, while a social occasion, is consecrated to duty, representing the ongoing commitment to Masonic principles and the renewal of vows by those taking on leadership. The Marshal's address outlines the symbolic and practical influence of Freemasonry, emphasizing its role as a moral and educational force in society, promoting universal brotherhood and adherence to divine and moral law.

The induction of officers signifies the continuity of leadership, the structured operation of the Lodge, and the fulfillment of the Craft's purposes through chosen individuals.

The office of Grand Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual and moral foundation of Masonry. The Holy Bible, entrusted to the Chaplain, represents the Great Light and source of divine guidance. Masonry's role as 'religion's handmaid' signifies its supportive function in moral and spiritual development, distinct from being a religion itself, and underscores its spirit of universal tolerance.

The death of Hiram Abiff symbolizes an instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity that is rarely equaled. His steadfastness in guarding the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death, serves as a profound example.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns the profound importance of virtue, integrity, and brotherhood as fundamental principles. They are taught to seek deeper, esoteric understandings of human motivations and to cultivate perseverance in striving for personal and collective freedom from earthly constraints. A key lesson is the ability to adapt and find substitutes when significant aspects of life are lost. Candidates are also cautioned about potential harm from others, both within and outside the fraternity, driven by jealousy or uncontrolled passions, and are encouraged to serve as ethical models through their pure actions and goodness.

While not a direct Masonic lesson, the pledge reinforces principles of civic duty, patriotism, and adherence to the ideals of liberty and justice, which are compatible with Masonic teachings on good citizenship and moral conduct.

The primary lesson for a candidate is to be good and true, understanding that truth is the foundation of all virtue and should govern their actions and interactions, leading to sincerity and mutual support.

A Mason must consistently practice Justice, ensuring that all individuals receive their rightful due without prejudice. This virtue is paramount for personal integrity and the stability of society, aligning with both divine and human laws.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.

Candidates are taught that they are ultimately accountable for all their thoughts, words, and actions, regardless of whether these are known to other men. This lesson encourages integrity, moral conduct, and the understanding that justice will prevail.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The Grand Chaplain's charge teaches that Masonry, while not a religion, is deeply rooted in moral and virtuous principles, acting as a supportive framework for spiritual growth. It emphasizes the importance of universal tolerance, the communication of ethical doctrines, and the reverence for the Holy Bible as a source of light and guidance. It also highlights the steadfastness and perseverance expected in discharging duties.

The candidate is taught to exemplify virtue, fortitude, and integrity, drawing inspiration from the historical account of Grand Master Hiram Abiff's unwavering commitment to his trust, even when faced with mortal danger.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.
tradition_notes: Esoteric philosophies have been present for millennia and across all cultures and belief systems.
Masons are encouraged to delve deep into the histories and values richly given by those Masons who have come before.
This is a common civic tradition in the United States, often recited at public gatherings and educational institutions.
To be good and true is the first lesson taught in Masonry.
The text alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
The Second Section recites the historical traditions of the Institution.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
Freemasonry has a tradition of invoking a blessing of Deity before any great or important undertaking.
The Festival of St. John the Evangelist is a traditional observance in Freemasonry, consecrated to duty and promoting social enjoyment and brotherly love.
Freemasonry's origins are described as ancient, with its beginnings not recorded on written pages of history.

The practice of inducting officers to preside over a Lodge for a specific term (e.g., twelve months) is a traditional aspect of Masonic governance and ensures the continuous operation and leadership of the Lodge.
The investiture of a Grand Officer with a jewel of office.
The specific duties assigned to the Grand Chaplain, including leading devotional exercises.
The philosophical stance of Masonry as 'religion's handmaid,' promoting universal tolerance.
The Holy Bible being recognized and entrusted as the Great Light in Masonry.
The narrative describes the premeditated death of Hiram Abiff by fifteen Fellow Crafts who sought to obtain the secrets of a Master Mason before the Temple's completion.
The motive of the Fellow Crafts was to gain the ability to travel in foreign countries and receive Master's wages.
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
caution_notes: We are reminded that there are others outside our fraternity, and some from within, who may do us harm out of jealousy or other of their uncontrolled passions.
Hypocrisy and deceit are unknown among us when influenced by the principle of truth.
Masons should never deviate from the minutest principles of Justice.
No contention should ever exist among members of the society of friends and brothers.
Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0022 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Regularity and Recognition in Freemasonry

This section defines and differentiates between 'Regularity' and 'Recognition' in Freemasonry. Regularity is a subjective standard for Grand Lodges, often based on Anderson's Constitutions of 1723, which outlines rules like candidate acceptance, display of the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass, and the integral role of the Hiramic Legend in the Third Degree. Recognition, in contrast, is an objective state of amity between sovereign Grand Lodges, allowing members to visit and hold dual membership. The text also defines a 'Clandestine Lodge' as one operating without a legitimate charter.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0022 -->
practical_elements: Members of recognized Grand Lodges are permitted to visit one another's lodges.
Members of recognized Grand Lodges can, in most cases, hold dual membership across jurisdictional lines.
Only Brethren from recognized Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.
entries.candidate_lesson: It is important for a Mason to understand the concepts of Regularity and Recognition to ensure proper interaction within the Craft and to avoid associating with Clandestine Lodges. This knowledge ensures that one's Masonic activities are conducted within legitimate and recognized jurisdictions.
tradition_notes: Masonic Law is based in part on Anderson's The Constitutions of the Free-Masons, originally published in 1723.
Anderson's Constitutions listed commonly accepted rules for Grand Lodges, Lodges, and individual members.

Examples of these rules include: acceptance of Candidates irrespective of personal religious beliefs, the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass displayed upon the Altar at all times, the acceptance of men only, and the Hiramic Legend as an integral part of the Third Degree.
caution_notes: A Clandestine Lodge is one that is not working with a legitimate charter from a Grand Lodge and is considered 'in the dark'.
Only Brethren from RECOGNIZED Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0003 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Symbolism of the Master Mason Degree

The Master Mason Degree initiates the candidate in darkness, signifying a state of equilibrium and preparedness for sacred ground. The candidate commits fully to the Fraternity, placing faith in the Three Great Lights, and is introduced to working tools, with one symbolizing Brotherly love. The central Mystery Drama involves the candidate assuming the role of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, forging a link to the Fraternity's inner soul and ancient Mystery Schools. This experience aims for self-realization, and the symbolism itself is ancient, evolving culturally over time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0003 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0007 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Low Twelve

This section explains the ancient symbolism of 'Low Twelve,' contrasting it with 'High Twelve.' It details how the number twelve signifies completion, fulfillment, and human life, drawing connections to the twelve signs of the Zodiac and the twelve edges of a cube. Low Twelve specifically represents midnight, the darkest time of the night, while High Twelve denotes noon.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0007 -->
practical_elements: Candidate enters the Lodge of the Master Mason in darkness
Initiate is given full use of every working tool
Candidate partakes of the central Mystery Drama of the Fraternity
Candidate assumes the role of the Grand Master Hiram Abiff
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The entrance in darkness signifies a new, unseen light and a state of equilibrium, preparing the candidate for sacred ground. The exalted working tool symbolizes the spreading of Brotherly love. Participating in the Mystery Drama and assuming Hiram Abiff's role forges a link with the Fraternity's inner soul and archetypal forces, connecting to ancient Mystery Schools. The experience in the 'Holy of Holies' leads to self-realization, where one may momentarily forget their former self and realize their true identity.

The number twelve, in ancient symbolism, denotes completion, derived from the twelve signs of the Zodiac forming a complete circle and the twelve edges of a cube symbolizing the earth. It also signifies the fulfillment of a deed and serves as an emblem of human life. High Twelve corresponds to noon, when the sun is at its zenith, while Low Twelve denotes midnight, representing the blackest time of the night.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns to be fully committed to the Fraternity and to place complete faith in the Three Great Lights. They are taught the importance of Brotherly love, symbolized by a specific working tool. Through the Mystery Drama and assuming the role of Hiram Abiff, the candidate is meant to connect with the inner soul of the Fraternity and ancient traditions, leading to a profound self-realization.

The symbolism of 'Low Twelve' and the number twelve teaches about the cycles of life, the concept of completion and fulfillment in one's journey, and the inevitable passage of time, including the darkest hours, which can be interpreted as a reflection on mortality.
tradition_notes: The legend of the Master Mason Degree is consistent with ancient Mystery Schools, suggesting a communion with archetypal forces that are the foundation of the tradition.

The symbolism encountered in this Degree can be traced back for hundreds, if not thousands, of years, with some elements being almost identical to ancient usage, while others have taken on cultural flavor from successive conveyors.
The symbolism discussed is rooted in ancient traditions, referencing the Zodiac and the cube as sources for the meaning of the number twelve.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Working Tools

This section defines the working tools of a Master Mason, specifically focusing on the Trowel. It explains its practical use by Operative Masons to spread cement for building and its symbolic application for Free and Accepted Masons to spread brotherly love and affection, uniting members into a harmonious society free from contention, encouraging only noble emulation.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0071 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0123 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section details the ritualistic preparation and reception of a candidate for the Most Sublime Degree of a Royal Arch Mason. It describes the candidate's attire, the seven distinct knocks at the Chapter door, the required qualifications including previous degrees, and the significance of the pass word 'Rabboni'. The ritual proceeds with reception under a living arch, kneeling at the altar for an obligation, and symbolic representations such as the burning bush and the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, followed by the captivity and deliverance by Cyrus. The journey through 'rough and rugged paths' symbolizing the sojourning of the children of Israel is also described, leading to the first obstruction at the first veil.

This section details the ritualistic progression of "worthy sojourners" through three veils and then to the fourth veil or sanctuary. The narrative identifies the participants as Giblemites, descendants of those who completed the First Temple, endured Babylonian captivity, and are now liberated by Cyrus to assist in rebuilding the house of the Lord without expectation of reward. Admission through each veil is granted by specific words, signs, and explanations, which are derived from biblical accounts of Moses receiving divine signs (rod to serpent, hand to leprosy, water to blood). The journey culminates in an encounter with the Royal Arch Captain at the sanctuary.

This section details a candidate's journey to the Grand Council, where they are examined on their lineage and proficiency. They are tasked with removing rubbish from ancient ruins, leading to a series of significant discoveries. First, a keystone believed to be from King Solomon's Temple is found. Subsequently, after a perilous descent into an arch using a cable tow, three squares identified as masters' jewels of Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff are recovered. A second descent reveals a gold-overlaid trunk with mysterious characters, all promising further important revelations for the Craft.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0123 -->
practical_elements: The Trowel is used by Operative Masons to spread cement, uniting a building into one common mass.
Candidate divested of outward apparel, in a working posture, hoodwinked, and a cable tow seven times around the body.
Accompanied by two brethren.
Seven distinct knocks at the Chapter door.
Pass word: Rabboni.
Reception under a living arch.
Conducted once around the outer courts of the tabernacle.
Kneeling at the altar to invoke a blessing from Deity.
Kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses for the solemn oath or obligation of a Royal Arch Mason.
Exhibition of the symbol of the burning bush.
Representation of the destruction of the temple.
Journey through rough and rugged paths.
Meeting an obstruction at the first veil.
Identification as "children of the captivity," "descendants of those noble Giblemites," and "Most Excellent Masters."
Stating the purpose: "help, aid, and assist in rebuilding the house of the Lord, without the hope of fee or reward."
Using the pass: "I am that I am, I am hath sent me unto you."
Giving the word of the Master of the first veil: "I am that I am, I am hath sent you unto us, Shem, Ham, and Japheth."
Performing the sign of the first veil, imitating God's command to Moses to cast his rod, which became a serpent.
Explaining the first sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Using the word and sign from the first veil to gain admission to the second veil.
Performing the sign of the second veil, imitating God's command to Moses to put his hand into his bosom, which became leprous.
Explaining the second sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Using the word and sign from the first and second veils to gain admission to the third veil.
Performing the sign of the third veil, imitating God's command to Moses to take water from the river and pour it on dry land, where it became blood.
Explaining the third sign by reciting the biblical passage from Exodus.
Encountering the Royal Arch Captain at the fourth veil or sanctuary.
Responding to the Royal Arch Captain's challenge: "Three worthy sojourners, who have come up to help, aid, and assist in the rebuilding of the house of the Lord, without the hope of fee or reward."
Being informed of a Grand Council decree restricting entry to true descendants of the twelve tribes of Israel.
Identifying oneself as 'children of the captivity' and 'Most Excellent Masters' to gain admission.
Gaining admission by 'words and signs' from masters of the first, second, and third veils, along with a signet.
Being received by the Captain of the Host and examined for proficiency in the preceding degree.
Undertaking the task of removing rubbish from the northeast part of the ruins.
Being furnished with working tools for the task.
Discovering a keystone resembling the top of an arch with effaced characters.
Descending into the arch using a cable tow wrapped seven times around a companion, with specific signals (swing right to ascend, left to descend).
Discovering three squares identified as masters' jewels during the first descent.
Performing a second descent into the arch, enabled by 'redoubled splendor' of the sun.
Discovering a gold-overlaid trunk with mysterious characters in the eastern-most part of the arch.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Trowel is symbolically used by Free and Accepted Masons to spread the cement of brotherly love and affection, which unites them into a sacred band or society of friends and brothers. This symbolic use aims to foster unity and prevent contention, promoting instead a noble emulation of who can best work and best agree.

The seven distinct knocks allude to the seventh Degree of Masonry. The reception under a living arch signifies that the principal secrets of the Royal Arch Degree should be communicated only under a living arch. The symbol of the burning bush impresses that the words and signs are of divine origin and sacred. The rough and rugged paths denote the sojourning of the children of Israel through the wilderness. The pass word 'Rabboni' signifies 'Good Master' or 'Most Excellent Master'.

The progression through the veils symbolizes a journey of testing, purification, and the acquisition of deeper knowledge and understanding. The biblical signs given to Moses (rod to serpent, hand to leprosy, water to blood) represent divine power, authentication of a divine messenger, and the ability to perform miracles to convince unbelievers, signifying God's ultimate authority. The phrase "I am that I am" symbolizes God's self-existence, eternal nature, and supreme authority. The narrative of the Giblemites, captivity, and rebuilding symbolizes resilience, dedication to a sacred task, and the continuity of spiritual and moral construction despite adversity.

The removal of rubbish symbolizes the clearing away of ignorance or obstacles to uncover hidden truths and ancient wisdom. The discovery of the keystone from King Solomon's Temple represents a crucial, foundational element of lost knowledge. The three squares, identified as masters' jewels of ancient Grand Masters, symbolize authority, perfection, and the foundational principles of the Craft. These discoveries collectively signify the ongoing pursuit of knowledge, the recovery of lost secrets, and the importance of perseverance in the search for truth.
entries.candidate_lesson:
As a Master Mason, one is taught to symbolically use the Trowel to cultivate and spread brotherly love and affection among all members, ensuring unity and harmony within the fraternity. The goal is to strive for excellence and agreement, rather than discord.

The candidate is taught the solemnity and divine origin of the Royal Arch secrets, the importance of their obligation, and the historical narrative of the destruction of the Temple, the Babylonian captivity, and the subsequent deliverance, which serves as an allegory for perseverance through trials. The journey through rough and rugged paths emphasizes the challenges and spiritual journey of the children of Israel.

Candidates are taught the importance of perseverance, unwavering faith, and commitment to noble undertakings, even when no material reward is expected. The lessons emphasize the divine origin of authority and the power of God, as demonstrated through the signs given to Moses. The historical narrative of the Giblemites teaches about resilience in the face of adversity, the continuity of sacred work, and the dedication required for the spiritual rebuilding of the 'house of the Lord.' The ritual progression signifies that deeper truths and privileges are earned through demonstrated worthiness, knowledge, and adherence to ritual.

The candidate demonstrates humility, dedication, and courage by expressing willingness to undertake any task, even the most servile or dangerous, to promote a noble and glorious undertaking. This emphasizes the importance of perseverance, self-sacrifice, and unwavering commitment in the pursuit of Masonic ideals and the recovery of lost knowledge.
tradition_notes: The text highlights the traditional distinction between the operative (literal) and speculative (symbolic) uses of Masonic tools, a core aspect of Masonic instruction.

The ritual references the progression through previous degrees: Duly initiated (Entered Apprentice), Passed to the Degree of Fellow Craft, Raised to the Sublime Degree of Master Mason, Advanced to the Degree of Mark Master Mason, Regularly passed the Chair (Past Master), Received and acknowledged as Most Excellent Masters.

The historical narrative includes the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, King of Babylon, in the eleventh year of Zedekiah, King of Jerusalem, the seventy-year captivity, and the deliverance by Cyrus, King of Persia.
The narrative connects the participants to the Giblemites, who were skilled stonemasons involved in the construction of King Solomon's Temple.
It references the historical events of the destruction of the First Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, the Babylonian captivity, and the liberation by Cyrus, King of Persia, for the purpose of rebuilding.
The ritual incorporates specific biblical accounts from Exodus regarding Moses' divine commission and the signs given to him.
The explicit mention of "Most Excellent Masters" and "Royal Arch Captain" places this ritual firmly within the Royal Arch degree tradition.

The narrative references a Grand Council decree and the 'children of the captivity,' which are characteristic elements of Royal Arch Masonry, often associated with the rebuilding of the Temple after the Babylonian captivity.
The mention of 'Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, and Scribe' and 'Most Excellent Masters' indicates a degree beyond the Master Mason, specifically within the Royal Arch system.
The use of 'masters of the first, second and third veils' and a 'signet' are specific ritualistic elements found in Royal Arch Masonry.

The identification of the three squares as 'masters' jewels' worn by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff directly links this narrative to the core figures and themes of the Hiramic legend.
caution_notes: The principal secrets of this Degree should be communicated only under a living arch.

entries.title: Obligation And Law
entries.short_summary: The Master Mason obligation, duties, and landmarks.
entries.full_summary:
<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0017 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Lodge Attendance and Balloting

This section outlines the expectations for Master Masons regarding Lodge attendance, emphasizing loyalty and fellowship over mandatory requirements. It also details the procedures and ethical considerations for balloting on petitions for membership, including who can vote, the use of white balls and black cubes, the importance of examining one's motives, and the guaranteed secrecy of the ballot.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0017 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0056 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Master Mason Obligation

This section details the Master Mason Obligation ceremony, beginning with the candidate kneeling on naked knees with hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses at the altar. The Worshipful Master assures the candidate that the oath does not conflict with duties to God, neighbor, country, or self. The extensive obligation covers vows of secrecy, adherence to Masonic laws and Grand Lodge edicts, aid to distressed Master Masons, protection of a brother's secrets (with exceptions for murder and treason), prohibitions against initiating certain individuals (women, dotage, nonage, atheist, madman, fool), avoiding clandestine Masons, and refraining from harming or defrauding a brother. Specific rules are given for the Grand Masonic word and the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation and the candidate's request for 'further light in Masonry,' followed by the removal of the hoodwink and cable-tow, and the display of the duegard of a Master Mason.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0056 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0090 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: John Jay.

This section describes two methods of instructing a candidate in the Mark Master Mason degree regarding the duty associated with a 'mark' and a request for a loan. The older method involved the Right Worshipful Master asking the candidate to accommodate a Brother Jay with a loan, and the candidate, having no money, would be reminded of his oath to return the mark with its price (half a Jewish shekel or a quarter-dollar). The candidate would then 'discover' a quarter-dollar previously placed in his pocket, leading to a lesson on diligent search before denying a brother's request. The newer, more common method involves the Right Worshipful Master presenting a metal mark and requesting a loan, which the candidate cannot provide. A brother then supplies the candidate with the money, and the Right Worshipful Master administers the same caution. The section concludes with the instruction for the pass-grip and word, and the real grip and word of a Mark Master Mason, noting the pass-grip's origin from assisting someone up a steep ascent.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0090 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0101 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Page 343

A candidate, acting as a presiding Master, attempts to close the Lodge informally amidst apparent confusion. The Retired Master intervenes, reminding the candidate of his obligation not to close a Lodge without giving a lecture. The candidate, realizing his oversight, resigns the chair. The R.W.M. then explains that the entire scenario was a lesson designed to teach the necessity of abstaining from soliciting or accepting any office.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0101 -->
practical_elements: Master Masons should attend Lodge as often as possible.
Master Masons should join in the fellowship that is an important part of Freemasonry.
Only Master Masons in good standing have the right to vote on petitions.
No member present can be excused from balloting on any petition.
No member will be permitted to retire from the Lodge to avoid casting a ballot.
White balls indicate an affirmative, or favorable ballot.
The black cube indicates a negative, or unfavorable ballot.
If no reason to believe otherwise, accept the word of the Investigating Committee and cast a favorable ballot.
Objections to an applicant must be raised before the ballot is taken.
Examine your motives before casting a ballot to ensure justice to the Candidate and Freemasonry.
No Brother should disclose how he voted.
No Brother should inquire into how another Brother voted on a particular Candidate.
Candidate kneels on naked knees.
Candidate places both hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel.
All present assemble around the altar.
Candidate repeats his name and the oath after the Worshipful Master.
Candidate detaches hands and kisses the book.
Conductor prompts the candidate to desire 'Further light in Masonry'.
Conductor takes off the hoodwink.
Conductor removes the cable-tow.
All around the altar place their hands in the position of the duegard of a Master Mason.
Worshipful Master gives one rap with his gavel.
Brethren retire to their seats, leaving the Master, conductor, and candidate at the altar.
Right Worshipful Master (R.W.M.) hands the candidate a mark.
Candidate states he has no money and cannot grant the loan request.
Senior Grand Warden (S.G.W.) offers to accommodate with twenty-five dollars if the mark is left as a pledge.
R.W.M. asks candidate to return the mark.
Candidate returns the mark without the price.
R.W.M. questions the candidate about violating his oath regarding the mark's price (half a Jewish shekel of silver or a fourth of a dollar).
Candidate searches pockets and finds a quarter-dollar (slyly placed by a brother).
Candidate hands the quarter-dollar and the mark to the Master.
R.W.M. administers a caution to the candidate.
In the newer method, R.W.M. presents a metal mark and requests a loan.
Candidate attempts to return the mark, but R.W.M. refuses, citing the oath.
R.W.M. calls attention to the obligation.
A brother provides the candidate with twenty-five cents (the price of the Mark).
Candidate hands the money and mark to the R.W.M.
R.W.M. administers the caution.
R.W.M. takes the candidate by the hand and invests him with the pass-grip and word, and the real grip and word of a Mark Master Mason.
Description of the pass-grip: extending right arms and clasping fingers, as if assisting up a steep ascent.
Brethren proposing to close the Lodge.
Candidate attempting to close the Lodge by rapping with his gavel and declaring it closed.
Retired Master stopping the candidate's attempt to close the Lodge.
Reference to the candidate's obligation regarding closing a Lodge without a lecture.
A brother whispering advice to the candidate to resign the chair.
Candidate abdicating (resigning) the chair.
R.W.M. resuming the chair.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The act of kneeling on naked knees with hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses signifies humility, reverence, and a solemn commitment to the sacred principles and moral guidance of Freemasonry. The 'third time at the altar of Masonry' represents the progression and increasing depth of commitment through the degrees. The request for 'further light in Masonry' symbolizes the candidate's desire for deeper knowledge, understanding, and spiritual enlightenment within the Craft. The severe penalty for violating the obligation underscores the profound seriousness and binding nature of the vows taken.

The 'mark' symbolizes a pledge or a request for aid from a brother. The act of the candidate finding money unexpectedly, or being provided with it, symbolizes that resources or assistance may be available even when one believes they are destitute, and that a brother should make a diligent search before denying aid. The price of the mark (half a Jewish shekel or a quarter-dollar) represents the value of the pledge and the commitment to the obligation. The pass-grip symbolizes mutual assistance and support, particularly in overcoming difficulties or 'steep ascents'.

The 'apparent confusion' and the test of the candidate's memory of his obligation serve as a dramatic and striking method to convey a crucial lesson. It symbolizes the importance of adhering to Masonic obligations and the proper conduct required of a presiding officer, even under pressure. The test also highlights the virtue of humility and the dangers of seeking office.
entries.candidate_lesson:
As a Master Mason, you have an obligation of loyalty to your Lodge, which should induce you to attend regularly and participate in fellowship. When balloting on a petition for membership, you must exercise your right to vote responsibly, ensuring your decision is just to both the candidate and Freemasonry, and always uphold the strict secrecy of the ballot, neither revealing your vote nor asking about others'.

The candidate learns the profound and binding nature of the Master Mason's obligation, emphasizing strict adherence to secrecy, fidelity, and mutual aid among brethren. It instills the importance of upholding Masonic laws, supporting the Grand Lodge, and exercising discernment in recognizing true and lawful Masons. The obligation teaches ethical conduct, including protecting a brother's family and property, avoiding harm or fraud, and maintaining moral integrity. It also highlights the continuous pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment within Masonry.

The primary lesson for the candidate is the importance of diligently searching for means to assist a worthy brother before denying a request. It teaches that one should not hastily refuse aid, as unforeseen resources or assistance from others might be available. It also reinforces the solemnity of the obligation taken, particularly concerning the duties associated with a brother's mark.

The primary lesson conveyed is the necessity of at all times abstaining from soliciting or accepting any office. Additionally, it implicitly teaches the importance of remembering and adhering to one's obligations, specifically that a presiding Master must not close a Lodge without giving a lecture or some part thereof.
tradition_notes: Ancient Lodges had mandatory attendance requirements and penalties for non-attendance.
The Right to Secrecy of the Ballot is guaranteed by Masonic law and custom, allowing each member perfect freedom in balloting.
The candidate kneels on naked knees and places both hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses during the obligation.
The Master assures the candidate that the oath does not conflict with duties to God, neighbor, country, or self.
Brethren assemble around the altar when the Master gives three raps with his gavel during the oath.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to the holy Sts. John.
The candidate requests 'further light in Masonry' after taking the obligation.
The hoodwink and cable-tow are removed after the obligation.
The duegard of a Master Mason is displayed by those around the altar after the obligation.
A specific, severe penalty is invoked for knowingly violating the Master Mason's obligation.

The text notes a change in ritual practice: 'The above is a true description of the manner in which the candidate was formerly taught his duty as a Mark Master Mason. In these latter days, however, very few Masters countenance this method of instruction, and it is therefore almost entirely discarded. The plan now generally adopted is as follows:--'
The pass-grip is said to have originated from the fact that the banks of the river at a certain location required assistance to ascend.
The tradition that a Lodge, when presided over by a Master, should not be closed without giving a lecture or some part thereof, as part of the Master's obligation.
caution_notes: No member present can be excused from balloting on any petition before the Lodge.
No member will be permitted to retire from the Lodge to avoid casting his ballot.
If you have an objection to an applicant, the time to raise that objection is before the ballot is taken.
No Brother should disclose how he voted.
No Brother should inquire into how another Brother voted on a particular Candidate.
The obligation does not interfere with the duty owed to God, neighbor, country, or self.

Secrets of the Master Masons' Degree are not to be revealed to any person except a true and lawful brother of this Degree, or in a regularly constituted Lodge of Master Masons, and only after strict trial, due examination, or lawful information.
Do not aid or be present at the initiation, passing, or raising of a woman, an old man in his dotage, a young man in his nonage, an atheist, a madman, or a fool, knowing them to be such.

Do not sit in a Lodge of clandestine-made Masons, nor converse on Masonry with a clandestine-made Mason, nor one who has been expelled or suspended from a Lodge while under that sentence, knowing them to be such.
Do not cheat, wrong, nor defraud a Master Mason's Lodge, nor a brother of this Degree, knowingly, nor supplant him in any laudable undertakings, but give due and timely notice to ward off danger.
Do not knowingly strike a brother Master Mason or do him personal violence in anger, except in the necessary defense of family or property.
Do not have illegal carnal intercourse with a Master Mason's wife, mother, sister, or daughter, nor suffer the same to be done by others if in power to prevent.
Do not give the Grand Masonic word in any other manner or form than that in which it is received, and then in a low breath.

Do not give the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress except in case of the most imminent danger, in a just and lawful Lodge, or for the benefit of instruction. If seen or heard from a worthy brother in distress, fly to his relief if there is a greater probability of saving his life than losing one's own.

The Right Worshipful Master cautions the candidate: 'should you ever hereafter have a mark presented you by a worthy brother, asking a favor, before you deny him make diligent search, and be quite sure of your inability to serve him; perhaps you will then find, as in the present instance, that some unknown person has befriended you, and you are really in a better situation than you thought yourself.'
A caution against forgetting one's obligation, particularly concerning the proper procedure for closing a Lodge.
A caution against improperly closing a Lodge without fulfilling the required lecture.
A caution against soliciting or accepting any office.

### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Masonic Glossary — Master Mason

This section provides a glossary of terms relevant to the Master Mason degree, defining words such as Approbation, Brute creation, Cleaves, Dotage, Emulation, Hecatomb, Injunction, Seraph, Speculative, Sublime, and Zeal.

entries.symbolic_meaning: Terms like 'Seraph' and 'Sublime' carry significant spiritual and moral connotations, representing divine orders and exalted states of being or thought. 'Brute creation' can symbolize the unrefined state of humanity, while 'Dotage' may allude to themes of mortality and the transient nature of physical existence, which are relevant to the Master Mason degree.
entries.candidate_lesson: The candidate learns key vocabulary and concepts that enrich their understanding of the Master Mason degree, its allegories, and the broader philosophical principles of Freemasonry. Understanding these terms is crucial for comprehending the deeper lessons and language of the Craft.
tradition_notes: The term 'Hecatomb' refers to an ancient Greek tradition of a public sacrifice of 100 oxen to the gods, typically in thanks for a great discovery, event, or victory.

entries.title: Significance of the Master Mason Degree
entries.short_summary: The Master Mason Degree is presented as the culmination of the Symbolic Lodge, representing the candidate's symbolic balancing of inner natures, purification of physical nature, and development of mental faculties. It is an ideal state of self-improvement and spiritual readiness. The text emphasizes that Masons should continuously review their progress and strive for this exalted status, not merely taking the degrees halfheartedly. The term 'Sublime Degree' is defined as inspiring awe and wonder, and metaphorically as a process of sublimation where a substance rises and reforms at a higher level, symbolizing the removal of obstacles to the soul's communion with the Supreme Light.
entries.full_summary:
The Master Mason Degree is presented as the culmination of the Symbolic Lodge, representing the candidate's symbolic balancing of inner natures, purification of physical nature, and development of mental faculties. It is an ideal state of self-improvement and spiritual readiness. The text emphasizes that Masons should continuously review their progress and strive for this exalted status, not merely taking the degrees halfheartedly. The term 'Sublime Degree' is defined as inspiring awe and wonder, and metaphorically as a process of sublimation where a substance rises and reforms at a higher level, symbolizing the removal of obstacles to the soul's communion with the Supreme Light.

It asks that the sublime principles of Freemasonry may subdue discordant passions, harmonize and enrich the hearts of members with love and goodness, allowing the lodge to reflect divine order and beauty.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0010 -->
