The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.

The seven distinct knocks allude to the seventh Degree of Masonry. The reception under a living arch signifies that the principal secrets of the Royal Arch Degree should be communicated only under a living arch. The symbol of the burning bush impresses that the words and signs are of divine origin and sacred. The rough and rugged paths denote the sojourning of the children of Israel through the wilderness. The pass word 'Rabboni' signifies 'Good Master' or 'Most Excellent Master'.

The progression through the veils symbolizes a journey of testing, purification, and the acquisition of deeper knowledge and understanding. The biblical signs given to Moses (rod to serpent, hand to leprosy, water to blood) represent divine power, authentication of a divine messenger, and the ability to perform miracles to convince unbelievers, signifying God's ultimate authority. The phrase "I am that I am" symbolizes God's self-existence, eternal nature, and supreme authority. The narrative of the Giblemites, captivity, and rebuilding symbolizes resilience, dedication to a sacred task, and the continuity of spiritual and moral construction despite adversity.

The removal of rubbish symbolizes the clearing away of ignorance or obstacles to uncover hidden truths and ancient wisdom. The discovery of the keystone from King Solomon's Temple represents a crucial, foundational element of lost knowledge. The three squares, identified as masters' jewels of ancient Grand Masters, symbolize authority, perfection, and the foundational principles of the Craft. These discoveries collectively signify the ongoing pursuit of knowledge, the recovery of lost secrets, and the importance of perseverance in the search for truth.

The penal sign symbolizes the obligation and serves as a reminder of Adam's fall, the penalty of death, and the judgment of God upon the disobedient, likening it to the severing of the head from the body by human justice. Vaults are noted as being used for secret purposes.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The Master Mason Degree is the pinnacle of symbolic learning, requiring continuous self-improvement and a serious commitment to personal progress. Candidates should strive to balance their inner natures, purify their physical being, and sharpen their mental faculties. The designation of Master Mason is an exalted status to be deferred to and pursued as an ideal, not merely a title to be acquired without genuine effort or understanding.

Candidates learn that Freemasonry is founded upon a belief in a Supreme Being and that all lodge proceedings are undertaken with a request for divine blessing and guidance. They are taught the importance of internal harmony, moral self-improvement, and the application of Masonic principles to their hearts and actions.

The Master Mason degree represents the highest point of Craft Masonry, preparing the candidate for deeper contemplation of life, death, and immortality.

As a Master Mason, one is bound by duty, honor, and gratitude to be faithful to their trust, uphold the dignity of their character, and enforce obedience to the Institution's tenets. This includes correcting errors of less informed brethren, preserving the fraternity's reputation through morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, and diligently guarding the ancient landmarks. The Master Mason must remain true to their vows and imitate the fidelity of Hiram Abiff.

Brethren are admonished to seriously consider the uncertainty of human life and the certainty of death, and to prepare for eternity. They are urged to maintain the dignified character of their profession, to evince faith through moral conduct, to hold bright hope for future mysteries, and to practice boundless charity. The lesson also includes the importance of faithfully discharging duties to God, neighbor, and self, and extending Masonic charity to the foibles of deceased brethren.

Masons are admonished to remember their mortality and the uncertainty of life, recognizing the transient value of worldly pursuits. The ceremony encourages them to earnestly obey divine laws, avoid dissensions, hatreds, and revenges, and to labor to do good to their fellow-men, aspiring to a spiritual existence beyond this life. It also teaches confidence in God's mercy and provides consolation for those grieving, emphasizing patience and resignation to divine will.

The candidate learns about the solemn and specific Masonic customs and regulations concerning the death of a brother, emphasizing the importance of adhering to ancient usage and Grand Lodge decisions regarding funeral services and the structured conduct of a Lodge of Sorrow. It underscores the fraternity's commitment to honoring its deceased members through prescribed rituals.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The Worshipful Master is charged with upholding moral law, being a peaceful and law-abiding citizen, respecting civil authorities, working diligently, living creditably, and acting honorably. They must venerate the Order's rulers, submit to Lodge resolutions, avoid quarrels and excess, be cautious, courteous, and faithful to the Lodge. The Master is to respect true brethren, discountenance imposters, promote the good of society, cultivate social virtues, and propagate Masonic knowledge. They must pay homage to the Grand Master, conform to Grand Lodge edicts, and preserve the Ancient Landmarks. Regular attendance at Grand Lodge committees and communications is required, along with ensuring proper Lodge formation, initiation, and visitor examination. The Master is taught to regulate actions by morality, limit desires, observe duty, pursue virtue, avoid dissimulation, and be guided by divine truth.

The importance of financial integrity, accountability, and trust within the Masonic Fraternity. It teaches that officers are chosen based on their proven fidelity and discretion, and their commitment to the organization's well-being.

Candidates learn the importance of character and integrity through the rigorous inquiry process for petitioners. The progression through the degrees, marked by being 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' teaches a journey of continuous self-improvement and increasing knowledge. The caution regarding giving the correct sign emphasizes the importance of attentiveness, respect for the degree at which the Lodge is working, and adherence to proper protocol.

The Hiramic legend, central to the Master Mason degree, conveys lessons through the account of Hiram Abiff's death, burials, and resurrections. It emphasizes the importance of fidelity and the pursuit of truth, even in the face of adversity, and introduces themes of mortality and immortality.

The candidate learns that advancement in Freemasonry is not automatic but requires personal free will, proven worthiness, proper preparation, and demonstrated proficiency in preceding degrees. It emphasizes the importance of merit, the structured progression within the Craft, and the fraternal support of being vouched for by a brother.

The candidate learns the profound and binding nature of the Master Mason's obligation, emphasizing strict adherence to secrecy, fidelity, and mutual aid among brethren. It instills the importance of upholding Masonic laws, supporting the Grand Lodge, and exercising discernment in recognizing true and lawful Masons. The obligation teaches ethical conduct, including protecting a brother's family and property, avoiding harm or fraud, and maintaining moral integrity. It also highlights the continuous pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment within Masonry.

The candidate learns the profound solemnity and binding nature of the Master Mason's obligation, including the duty to aid a worthy brother in distress. They are taught the symbolic significance of the compasses and square in relation to friendship, morality, and brotherly love. The candidate also learns the specific signs, grips, and words necessary for recognition as a Master Mason, and the proper way to wear the Master Mason's apron.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the importance of the search for truth and knowledge. The ritualistic actions and declarations impress upon the candidate the gravity of the Hiramic legend and the commitment to preserving Masonic traditions, even in the face of loss, by adopting temporary measures until the true word can be recovered. It also teaches the significance of fidelity and the consequences of betrayal.

This ritual imparts lessons on the inevitability of mortality and the limitations of human power and knowledge in the face of death. It emphasizes the profound loss associated with the Master's word and the allegorical death of Hiram Abiff. The ceremony encourages contemplation of the afterlife and the hope for a spiritual 'Lodge on High,' reinforcing faith and the promise of immortality beyond earthly existence. It also highlights the distinct knowledge and significance of the Master Mason degree compared to the preceding degrees.

The candidate is taught to exemplify virtue, fortitude, and integrity, drawing inspiration from the historical account of Grand Master Hiram Abiff's unwavering commitment to his trust, even when faced with mortal danger.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.

The candidate is taught the profound responsibilities and moral obligations that come with the Master Mason degree. These include maintaining personal integrity, providing moral leadership within the fraternity, upholding the reputation of Masonry, and diligently preserving its ancient traditions and principles. The lesson emphasizes the importance of setting a virtuous example and demonstrating unwavering fidelity, mirroring the legendary example of Hiram Abiff.

The closing ceremony reinforces the importance of brotherly love, moral and social virtues, and the expectation for every brother to conduct himself as a man and a Mason. It also highlights the duty to support oneself and family, and to contribute to the relief of worthy distressed Master Masons, their widows, and orphans, emphasizing the practical application of Masonic principles in daily life.

To be a 'bright Mason,' one must have knowledge of the alterations made in Masonry since Morgan's exposure. The critical notes on the Third Degree's legendary nature (hyperbolical, apocryphal, fabulous) encourage a deeper, allegorical understanding rather than a literal interpretation of its traditions and history. The mention of 'worldly possession' as a symbolic denotation may serve as a lesson against materialism.

The self-imposed penalties within Freemasonry may be understood in the context of ancient Hebrew covenant practices, emphasizing the profound solemnity and consequences of breaking sacred promises. The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, encouraging candidates to contemplate mortality, strive for ethical maturity, and embrace faith in salvation. The narrative of the Temple builders highlights the importance of fidelity and the severe repercussions of treachery.

The Master Mason degree teaches the doctrine of a resurrection and a future state, emphasizing prudence and unshrinking fidelity. It inspires hope for a final reward for those who are 'just made perfect' and symbolizes being saved from iniquity and raised to the faith of salvation.

Candidates are taught the ancient and proper mode of being raised to the Master Mason Degree, understanding its historical traditions and the exemplification of Masonic virtue. They learn the meaning of the degree's emblems and the description of Solomon's Temple. The solemnity of lodge closing reinforces the "chief care of a Mason" and the importance of continuous Masonic instruction and improvement. Masons are reminded of their duties as men and Masons in their daily avocations and the importance of piety. The allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend teaches profound lessons about spiritual death and resurrection, the restoration of truth and morality, and fidelity in the face of adversity, drawing parallels to Christian themes of salvation and immortality. The distinction between literal history and allegorical truth is emphasized, guiding the candidate to seek deeper symbolic meaning.

The candidate's admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is a recognition of their Masonic abilities and an encouragement to maintain that esteem through good conduct and adherence to rules. A primary duty of a Most Excellent Master is to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, which necessitates an accurate and comprehensive acquaintance with the lectures of all degrees. The candidate is urged to apply double diligence to acquire knowledge and information to properly discharge their duties and preserve the unsullied title of Most Excellent Master.

The candidate's admission is a testament to the brethren's good opinion of their Masonic abilities, which should not be forfeited by misconduct. As a Most Excellent Master, the candidate has a great duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, requiring an accurate acquaintance with the lectures of each degree. Diligence in acquiring knowledge is essential to properly discharge duties and preserve the title of Most Excellent Master.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.

The candidate is taught the solemnity and divine origin of the Royal Arch secrets, the importance of their obligation, and the historical narrative of the destruction of the Temple, the Babylonian captivity, and the subsequent deliverance, which serves as an allegory for perseverance through trials. The journey through rough and rugged paths emphasizes the challenges and spiritual journey of the children of Israel.

Candidates are taught the importance of perseverance, unwavering faith, and commitment to noble undertakings, even when no material reward is expected. The lessons emphasize the divine origin of authority and the power of God, as demonstrated through the signs given to Moses. The historical narrative of the Giblemites teaches about resilience in the face of adversity, the continuity of sacred work, and the dedication required for the spiritual rebuilding of the 'house of the Lord.' The ritual progression signifies that deeper truths and privileges are earned through demonstrated worthiness, knowledge, and adherence to ritual.

The candidate demonstrates humility, dedication, and courage by expressing willingness to undertake any task, even the most servile or dangerous, to promote a noble and glorious undertaking. This emphasizes the importance of perseverance, self-sacrifice, and unwavering commitment in the pursuit of Masonic ideals and the recovery of lost knowledge.

Every candidate must pass through the twelve 'Original Points' for their initiation to be considered legal. A true and faithful brother is obligated to respond to the sign of distress, offering assistance and protection to a brother in need. Masons are taught to uphold their obligations by not writing down the work, preserving the oral tradition.
tradition_notes: The Master Mason Degree is considered the 'crown of the Symbolic Lodge'.
'Raised to the Sublime Degree' is the appropriate terminology for receiving this degree.
This prayer is a traditional element of opening a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing the spiritual foundation of the fraternity.
The text refers to "ancient landmarks of the Institution," emphasizing the importance of preserving long-standing Masonic principles and traditions.
It also mentions "established usages and customs of the fraternity," highlighting adherence to traditional practices.
The Master removes his hat at the place of interment.
Officers wear their jewels during the grave service.
Brethren wear Aprons during the grave service.
The lamb-skin or White Apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The sprig of Acacia is placed upon the coffin only by the Three Principal officers conducting the service.
The Funeral Grand Honors are a specific ritualistic sequence of gestures and spoken words performed by all Masons present at a funeral service.
The ceremony includes specific prayers and blessings invoking the 'heavenly Father' and the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah'.
Ancient Masonic usage dictates that the funeral service at the grave is only performed at the time of burial.
A decision by Grand Master Barkley in 1875, reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge in 1883, established the ruling on the timing of funeral services at the grave.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
Freemasonry has a tradition of invoking a blessing of Deity before any great or important undertaking.
The Festival of St. John the Evangelist is a traditional observance in Freemasonry, consecrated to duty and promoting social enjoyment and brotherly love.
Freemasonry's origins are described as ancient, with its beginnings not recorded on written pages of history.

The practice of inducting officers to preside over a Lodge for a specific term (e.g., twelve months) is a traditional aspect of Masonic governance and ensures the continuous operation and leadership of the Lodge.
The Master must assent to Ancient Charges and Regulations which point out the duty of the Master of a Lodge.
The Master must hold in veneration the original rulers and patrons of the Order of Freemasonry and their regular successors.
The Master must submit to the awards and resolutions of brethren in Lodge convened, consistent with the constitutions of the Order.
The Master must respect genuine and true brethren and discountenance dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
The Master must conform to every edict of the Grand Lodge or General Assembly of Masons that is not subversive of the principles and groundwork of Masonry.
It is admitted that it is not in the power of any man, or body of men, to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
It is admitted that no new Lodge can be formed without permission of the Grand Lodge.
It is admitted that no countenance ought to be given to any irregular Lodge, or to any person clandestinely initiated therein, as being contrary to the ancient charges of the Order.
It is admitted that no person can be regularly made a Freemason in, or admitted a member of, any regular Lodge, without previous notice, and due inquiry into his character.
It is agreed that no visitors shall be received into your Lodge without due examination, and producing proper vouchers of their having been initiated in a regular Lodge.
Masters have supported these regulations in all ages before.
The practice of giving Grand Honors and singing an ode is part of the lodge installation ceremony.
The investiture with a jewel of office is a traditional practice for Masonic officers.
The selection of officers emphasizes qualities such as fidelity and discretion.
All business relative to Masonry is traditionally conducted at a 'regular' meeting, specifically within the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are permitted to be present at such business meetings.
Grand Lodges typically make arrangements for the regulation of their subordinate Lodges.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge', a privilege not extended to other officers.
A 'Blue Lodge' is defined as a Lodge of Master Masons where only the three Craft degrees (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) are conferred.
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons traditionally consists of five officers, with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
A Masters' Lodge requires three Master Masons to constitute it by Masonic Law, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.

The three degrees are symbolically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), referencing King Solomon's Temple.
The traditional account of Hiram Abiff's death, several burials, and resurrections is central to the Master Mason Degree.
Hiram Abiff is described as one of the head workmen at King Solomon's Temple.
Ancient writings inform us that Hiram Abiff was an arbiter between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre.
The tragical death of Hiram Abiff is not recorded in the Bible, the writings of Josephus, or any other ancient writings known outside of Freemasonry.
Hiram Abiff was universally acknowledged as the third most distinguished man then living, and in many respects, the greatest man in the world.
The text references 'Note J, Appendix' regarding the knocking sequence, suggesting a traditional or specific procedural detail that would be elaborated upon in the appendix.
The candidate kneels on naked knees and places both hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses during the obligation.
The Master assures the candidate that the oath does not conflict with duties to God, neighbor, country, or self.
Brethren assemble around the altar when the Master gives three raps with his gavel during the oath.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to the holy Sts. John.
The candidate requests 'further light in Masonry' after taking the obligation.
The hoodwink and cable-tow are removed after the obligation.
The duegard of a Master Mason is displayed by those around the altar after the obligation.
A specific, severe penalty is invoked for knowingly violating the Master Mason's obligation.
Some Lodges use 'step, duegard, and sign' when approaching from the east.
Lodges differ in their mode of work regarding the candidate's perambulation; some pass the candidate around the Lodge once, giving the Master's sign to the Junior and Senior Wardens.
The Master usually instructs the candidate on how to make the signs before rising from the altar after the obligation.
In some Lodges, the Master does not make the sign of distress first at the body, but only gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body with the thumb inward.
The prayer recited by the Master at the grave is stated to be found in all Masonic Monitors.
There is a variation in the initial sign given by the Master at the body in some Lodges.
The narrative describes the premeditated death of Hiram Abiff by fifteen Fellow Crafts who sought to obtain the secrets of a Master Mason before the Temple's completion.
The motive of the Fellow Crafts was to gain the ability to travel in foreign countries and receive Master's wages.
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
The Master Mason lecture is not generally given by the Master to the candidate on the night of his 'raising' due to its length.
The candidate generally receives the Master Mason lecture from brethren who are well posted in the work.
If time permits, the Master and Senior Warden usually go through the first section of the Master Mason lecture before closing the Lodge.
Some Lodges use specific variations in the Senior Warden's response, such as adding 'in the west' after 'Nothing, Worshipful'.
Some Lodges use specific questions and answers regarding the search for 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) both at opening and closing.
The questions and answers about the Master's word are also occasionally used by some Masters in the lecture.
Some Lodges sing a specific closing song, 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon'.
The 'work' and lectures taught by Barney are prevalent in the Northwestern States (Michigan, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Iowa) and were adopted by the Baltimore Convention.
Brother John Barney obtained the Preston Lectures from Gleason in Boston, which were approved by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts in 1817.
Barney adapted his lectures to different systems prevailing in the Western and Southwestern States, gaining sanction from respective Grand Masters.
The 'Webb Work,' inculcated by Robert Morris, is generally accepted as being of ancient origin and is expected to be adopted by Grand Lodges throughout the United States.

The 'fatal weapons' in the Third Degree have undergone several changes: from 'setting-tool, setting maul, and setting-beetle' (1717) to 'twenty-four-inch gauge, square, and gavel', then 'setting-tool, square, and rule', and currently 'plumb-rule, square, and strong or heavy maul'.
There are variations in the Hiramic legend regarding the number of Fellow Crafts who went in search (fifteen vs. twelve) and whether the sprig of acacia was left by the murderers or recanters.
There are variations in the reported location where the lost body was found: near the seaside (York Masons), Mount Moriah (Americans), Monet Lebanon (French), or near Jerusalem (others).
Accounts differ regarding the brethren's dispersal during the search: some say they dispersed widely (east, west, and south), while others state they kept within hail of each other.

Alternative names for the ruffians are preserved in subsidiary degrees, including GRAVELOT, QUIBO, and AKIROP, and in the Degree of Elect of Fifteen, they are named JUBELA-KURMAVIL (a corruption of Cromwell), JUBELO-GRAVELOT, and JUBELUM-AKIROP.
There are differing traditions on who advised the change to the substitute word: some say King Solomon, while others affirm the three Fellow Crafts adopted it without consulting him.
The Hiramic legend is described as a 'fabulous narrative' used to promote the doctrine of resurrection and a future state, similar to 'spurious initiations'.
Traditions mention instances of treachery, violation of sacred pledges, and crime among Apprentices and Craftsmen during the Temple's construction.
The connection of the 'widow's son' (Hiram Abiff) with the building of the Temple was endeared to the two monarchs, leading to a new arrangement of discipline and a legend to preserve his memory.
It was customary for Hiram Abiff to retire to the Most Holy Place at high twelve to draw plans and offer orisons.
Some rituals taught that Hiram Abiff divided operatives into Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Masters, paying them at the pillar of Jachin and in the Middle Chamber.
Traditions state that the Ark of the Covenant had not been removed to the Holy of Holies at the time Hiram supposedly entered it, as this occurred at the dedication.
The description of "the three principal entrances to the Temple" has evolved over time, from "east, west and south doors" to "north, south, and east entrances" by the Union in 1813.
Different Masonic jurisdictions (United States, Scotland, France) have varying orders for the "attacks" or "entrances" in the ritual.
Adonhiramite Masonry, used in France around 1787, did not mention the doors at all.

The Christian allegorical interpretation of the Hiramic legend is explicitly stated to be "borne out in the higher Degrees of sublime Masonry," specifically the 32nd Degree (Prince of the Royal Secret) in Continental nomenclature.

The historical accuracy of Hiram Abiff's death during the Temple's construction is challenged by scriptural references (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) which indicate he was alive at the Temple's completion.
The pillars Jachin and Boaz are referenced (see pp. 71 and 83).
The sign of admiration or astonishment is compared to that expressed by the Queen of Sheba upon first viewing Solomon's Temple (see p. 203).
A passage of Scripture from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4 is read by the Right Worshipful Master, describing the fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the Temple after Solomon's prayer.
The Master is identified as the 'high priest of the Chapter'.
The ceremony involves specific readings from Psalms 134 and 23.
The degree is founded upon the ceremonies of the dedication of King Solomon's Temple.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.

The ritual references the progression through previous degrees: Duly initiated (Entered Apprentice), Passed to the Degree of Fellow Craft, Raised to the Sublime Degree of Master Mason, Advanced to the Degree of Mark Master Mason, Regularly passed the Chair (Past Master), Received and acknowledged as Most Excellent Masters.

The historical narrative includes the destruction of the Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, King of Babylon, in the eleventh year of Zedekiah, King of Jerusalem, the seventy-year captivity, and the deliverance by Cyrus, King of Persia.
The narrative connects the participants to the Giblemites, who were skilled stonemasons involved in the construction of King Solomon's Temple.
It references the historical events of the destruction of the First Temple by Nebuchadnezzar, the Babylonian captivity, and the liberation by Cyrus, King of Persia, for the purpose of rebuilding.
The ritual incorporates specific biblical accounts from Exodus regarding Moses' divine commission and the signs given to him.
The explicit mention of "Most Excellent Masters" and "Royal Arch Captain" places this ritual firmly within the Royal Arch degree tradition.

The narrative references a Grand Council decree and the 'children of the captivity,' which are characteristic elements of Royal Arch Masonry, often associated with the rebuilding of the Temple after the Babylonian captivity.
The mention of 'Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, and Scribe' and 'Most Excellent Masters' indicates a degree beyond the Master Mason, specifically within the Royal Arch system.
The use of 'masters of the first, second and third veils' and a 'signet' are specific ritualistic elements found in Royal Arch Masonry.

The identification of the three squares as 'masters' jewels' worn by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff directly links this narrative to the core figures and themes of the Hiramic legend.
Ancient Masonry recognized twelve original points as the basis of the entire system.
The penal sign applies to both the Entered Apprentice and Royal Arch Mason's 'Duegard'.

The tradition regarding the payment of workmen's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple may not be historically accurate and could have been fabricated in a subsequent age without authenticating documents.
caution_notes: Candidates should not take the Degrees halfheartedly and consider themselves a Master Mason without genuine effort and understanding.
Do not swerve from your duty.
Do not violate your vows.
Do not betray your trust.
Never suffer the ancient landmarks to be infringed.
Never countenance a deviation from the established usages and customs of the fraternity.
When the service is used in connection with that of the Knight Templar or the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite, all preceding parts of the ritual (before the note) should be omitted.
The Lodge should practice the mode of giving the Funeral Grand Honors before leaving its hall, ensuring each brother pronounces the words audibly.
Singing and holding the open Bible by the Chaplain are optional at the Master's discretion.

Reading the funeral service at the grave of a deceased brother is only warranted at the time of burial; any modern practice otherwise is considered an innovation, as per Grand Master Barkley's decision (1875), reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge (1883).
Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.
Do not be concerned in plots and conspiracies against the government; patiently submit to law and authorities.
Avoid private piques and quarrels, and guard against intemperance and excess.
Discountenance imposters and all dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
It is not in the power of any man or body of men to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
No new Lodge can be formed without Grand Lodge permission; do not countenance irregular Lodges or clandestinely initiated persons.
No person can be regularly made a Freemason or admitted to a regular Lodge without previous notice and due inquiry into character.
No visitors shall be received without due examination and proper vouchers of regular initiation.
The Lodge Charter should never be out of the Master's immediate control and must be duly transmitted to the successor.
Grand Honors are not given during a public installation.

Brethren arriving late must be informed by the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is at work to prevent them from giving the wrong sign to the Worshipful Master, especially if members of a lower degree are present.
The obligation does not interfere with the duty owed to God, neighbor, country, or self.

Secrets of the Master Masons' Degree are not to be revealed to any person except a true and lawful brother of this Degree, or in a regularly constituted Lodge of Master Masons, and only after strict trial, due examination, or lawful information.
Do not aid or be present at the initiation, passing, or raising of a woman, an old man in his dotage, a young man in his nonage, an atheist, a madman, or a fool, knowing them to be such.

Do not sit in a Lodge of clandestine-made Masons, nor converse on Masonry with a clandestine-made Mason, nor one who has been expelled or suspended from a Lodge while under that sentence, knowing them to be such.
Do not cheat, wrong, nor defraud a Master Mason's Lodge, nor a brother of this Degree, knowingly, nor supplant him in any laudable undertakings, but give due and timely notice to ward off danger.
Do not knowingly strike a brother Master Mason or do him personal violence in anger, except in the necessary defense of family or property.
Do not have illegal carnal intercourse with a Master Mason's wife, mother, sister, or daughter, nor suffer the same to be done by others if in power to prevent.
Do not give the Grand Masonic word in any other manner or form than that in which it is received, and then in a low breath.

Do not give the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress except in case of the most imminent danger, in a just and lawful Lodge, or for the benefit of instruction. If seen or heard from a worthy brother in distress, fly to his relief if there is a greater probability of saving his life than losing one's own.
The Sign of Distress is to be given only in cases of most imminent danger, in a just and lawful Lodge, or for instruction.
A brother is obligated to fly to the relief of a worthy brother in distress if there is a greater probability of saving his life than losing one's own.
In some Lodges, the Master does not make the sign of distress first at the body, but only gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body with the thumb inward.
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.
Guard uninformed brethren against a breach of fidelity.
Let no motive make you swerve from your duty, violate your vows, or betray your trust.

The 'work' in Minnesota and New York is 'strongly impregnated with what is called, among Masons, "Morganry"--very similar to the disclosures of Morgan and Richardson,' implying a caution regarding unauthorized disclosures.
The Third Degree's traditions are 'unfortunately hyperbolical; its history apocryphal, and its legends fabulous,' cautioning against literal interpretations.
Professor Stuart endeavored to show that the legend of the Third Degree is an 'imposture' due to the Latin origin of the criminals' names, not Hebrew.
The text notes that the uniformity of Freemasonry on certain subjects is a 'delicate point' and difficult to clarify for brethren, implying a need for discretion regarding ritual variations.
CASSIA is sometimes improperly and ignorantly used for acacia.
The interpolated word (substitute for the lost word) is not a real word in any language, but merely a jumble of letters forming a sound without meaning.

Masons should not literally believe Dr. Anderson's explanation of the exhumation of H.A.B. as a real historical fact, as Hiram Abiff was known to be alive years after the Temple's completion, indicating the story is allegorical.

The historical veracity of the Hiramic legend is questioned, with references to Dr. Anderson and Josephus stating Hiram Abiff lived to complete the Temple and died in Tyre, contradicting the ritual narrative.

Inconsistencies in ritual narratives are highlighted, such as Hiram Abiff's supposed entry into the Holy of Holies before its completion or accessibility, and the division of operatives and payment of wages before the relevant architectural features (pillars, Middle Chamber) were built.
A divergence from uniformity is noted in describing the places where the instruments of Hiram's murder were supposedly used.

The author explicitly states that the tradition of Hiram's death during the Temple's construction is "merely allegorical" and not historically factual, citing scriptural evidence (1 Kings vii. 40, 2 Chron. iv. 11) that Hiram was alive at the Temple's completion. This serves as a caution against a literal interpretation of the legend.
Candidates are cautioned to be careful not to forfeit the esteem of the brethren through misconduct and inattention to the rules of the Lodge.
The candidate is cautioned against forfeiting the brethren's esteem by misconduct and inattention to the rules.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.
The principal secrets of this Degree should be communicated only under a living arch.
Writing the Masonic work is considered a violation of obligations and would lead to a general uprising of the craft.
A General Grand Lodge attempting to authorize written work for uniformity would be a foolish sanction by the fraternity.

entries.source_heading: Significance of the Master Mason Degree

entries.title: The Wages of a Master Mason
entries.short_summary: This section distinguishes between the physical wages of ancient Operative Masons, traditionally Corn, Wine, and Oil, and the internal, moral, and intangible wages earned by Speculative Master Masons. These speculative wages include love, friendship, respect, opportunity, happy labor, and association, which are earned through moral effort and are acknowledged by the Senior Warden's declaration in the Lodge opening.
entries.full_summary:
This section distinguishes between the physical wages of ancient Operative Masons, traditionally Corn, Wine, and Oil, and the internal, moral, and intangible wages earned by Speculative Master Masons. These speculative wages include love, friendship, respect, opportunity, happy labor, and association, which are earned through moral effort and are acknowledged by the Senior Warden's declaration in the Lodge opening.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

This section introduces the Fellow Craft candidate to the concepts of Operative and Speculative Masonry, defining each and emphasizing the moral and religious aspects of Speculative Masonry, including the contemplation of creation and homage to Deity. It also details the historical practice of ancient brethren working in both forms and consecrating the seventh day for rest and contemplation. The section concludes with a biblical description of the two pillars of brass, their dimensions, and features like chapiters, network, and pomegranates, as they would appear on the passage to the Middle Chamber.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: First Section

This section introduces the core tenets of the Institution, stating that they are contained within the points of the compasses. These tenets are identified as Friendship, Morality, and Brotherly Love.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Circuit

This section, titled "Third Circuit," details a solemn ritualistic exchange among lodge officers and the Chaplain, reflecting on the transient nature of life, the inevitability of death, and the sorrows of mortality. It progresses through lamentations of human frailty and grief, balanced by expressions of faith in God's justice, mercy, and the promise of eternal life and resurrection. The ritual includes prayers for strength, wisdom, and comfort for the bereaved, culminating in a collective affirmation of hope and trust in divine beneficence, often associated with a Lodge of Sorrow.

This section describes a solemn Masonic ceremony, likely a Lodge of Sorrow, where the Master reflects on the inevitability of death and the transient nature of life. The Chaplain reads from 1 Corinthians 15, discussing the resurrection of the dead and the transformation from corruptible to incorruptible bodies. A choir sings from Job 19 about seeing God after death. The Orator is then called upon to recount the virtues of the deceased, with an emphasis on Masonic charity and non-judgment.

This section details the 'Third Circuit' of a Masonic memorial service, which includes scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job, a choir performance, and an oration emphasizing truth and Masonic Charity towards the deceased. The ritual proceeds with the Junior Warden sprinkling corn, the Senior Warden sprinkling wine, and the Master sprinkling perfumed oil on the coffin, each accompanied by symbolic interpretations related to resurrection, spiritual refreshment, and consolation. The ceremony concludes with the Master placing a lambskin apron and holding a white glove on the coffin, symbolizing innocence, purity, and the hope of a welcome reception in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

This section details the ritualistic farewell for a deceased Master Mason, highlighting the symbolism of the white glove as an emblem of Fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, and the sprig of evergreen as an emblem of Faith in the immortality of the soul. It describes the Master's address, the placement of the evergreen on the coffin, a collective farewell by the brethren, and a three-circuit procession around the coffin accompanied by Grand Honors.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0133 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Secretary

The Installing Officer instructs the newly elected Lodge Secretary on their duties, which include observing and recording lodge proceedings, managing lodge funds by receiving money and paying it to the Treasurer, and performing these duties with fidelity to earn the respect of the brethren.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0133 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0149 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Charge to the Grand Chaplain

The charge to the Grand Chaplain outlines the sacred duties of the office, including leading devotional exercises at Grand Communications and performing functions at public ceremonies. It emphasizes that while Masonry is not a religion, it serves as 'religion’s handmaid,' promoting universal tolerance. The Grand Chaplain is expected to communicate doctrines of morality and virtue to the brethren, and is entrusted with the Holy Bible, the Great Light in Masonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0149 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0175 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Proclamation

This section describes a formal proclamation made by the Grand Master to an assembly of brethren and friends during a ceremony, likely a cornerstone laying. The proclamation affirms the Masons' adherence to the laws of their country, their fear of God as the Great Architect, their commitment to benefiting brethren, and practicing universal benevolence. Following this, the Grand Master delivers the Square, Level, Plumb, and the building plan to the Architect, entrusting them with the superintendence and direction of the work, expressing confidence in their skill to ensure the building rises in order, harmony, and beauty, fulfilling its purpose to the credit and honor of all involved.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0175 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pronunciation Key

This section provides a pronunciation key for various words, many of which are common in Masonic ritual and discourse. It includes rules for indicating accented syllables and short vowel sounds, followed by an alphabetical list of words with their phonetic pronunciations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mason.

This section details the duegard and sign of a Master Mason, which alludes to the penalty of the obligation, and the grand hailing sign of distress. It describes the physical actions for each sign, including the accompanying words for the grand hailing sign of distress, "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?" This sign is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The text also outlines the specific gavel raps used by the Master and Wardens to open a Lodge on the First, Second, and Third Degrees, concluding with the Master reciting a passage of Scripture (Psalm 133) about brethren dwelling in unity, followed by the brethren's response.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0041 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft's Apron

This section details the preparation and initial steps of the Fellow Craft degree, focusing on the candidate's reception. It describes the conductor leading the candidate before the altar, the exchange of the duegard and sign, and the subsequent arrangement of the Lodge for the second reception. Key elements include the placement of two large pillars and fifteen pieces of painted board representing three, five, and seven steps, leading to the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple. The candidate is then dressed with a Fellow Craft's apron, and the conductor explains the ascent through the winding stairs to receive instructions on wages and jewels. The section concludes with an introduction to Operative and Speculative Masonry, defining their respective applications and moral lessons.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0041 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Degree: Closing Notes

This section concludes the Fellow Craft Degree, offering detailed explanations and historical context through footnotes. It explores the historical assertion of St. John's connection to the Essenes as a secret society conveying moral truths through symbols. The text describes a ritualistic gesture involving a rake across the breast, emphasizing the penalty of the obligation and symbolizing fortitude against adversity and the importance of fidelity to secrets. It introduces the Jewish Cabala's rule of Transposition, applying it to Hebrew words like ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) to signify moral and physical light. A comprehensive explanation of 'Shibboleth' is provided, detailing its dual meanings (ear of grain, stream of water) and its historical use by the Gileadites to identify Ephraimites based on speech peculiarities. Finally, it outlines the Fellow Craft Lecture, covering the passing ceremony, the distinction between operative and speculative Masonry, the study of liberal arts and sciences, and the legend of wages paid in the Middle Chamber, guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0079 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section details the symbolic representation of the candidate as Grand Master Hiram Abiff within the Master Mason degree. It recounts the narrative of Hiram Abiff's assassination by three Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) at the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple. The motive for the murder was to extort the secrets of a Master Mason. The narrative describes the sequence of events, including the three blows with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, the subsequent burial of the body, and the discovery of Hiram's absence by King Solomon, leading to the identification of the missing Craftsmen and the recantation of twelve conspirators.

This section details the discovery of Hiram Abiff's absence, marked by no designs on the trestle-board. King Solomon orders a search, leading to the identification of three missing Craftsmen: Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, suspected of his murder. Twelve Fellow Crafts, having recanted their initial murderous designs, confess their guilt and are tasked with finding the ruffians. After an initial search near the port of Joppa yields no results, a second, more urgent search is commanded. During this search, one brother discovers a grave marked by an acacia. Concurrently, another party apprehends the ruffians, who are overheard uttering self-imprecations. They are brought before King Solomon, convicted, and executed according to their own curses.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0079 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0081 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Closing the Lodge.

This section details the ritualistic closing of a Masonic Lodge, noting its similarity across all three degrees. It outlines a sequence of questions and responses among the Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Secretary, and Junior Deacon, covering topics such as the state of the Lodge, approval of minutes, the process of tyling, and the stations and duties of officers. The ceremony concludes with a prayer and an optional closing song, emphasizing brotherly love and moral conduct.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0081 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0087 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: the Rejected Keystone

This section details a ritualistic sequence within the Mark Master degree where a candidate presents a keystone for inspection by the Junior, Senior, and Master Overseers. Despite its unique appearance, the keystone is repeatedly rejected for not being 'square work' and lacking a craftsman's mark. The Overseers ultimately agree to cast it aside as rubbish. The scene concludes with the workmen becoming impatient for their wages, leading to a song and a procession to receive payment.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0087 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master Reception and Obligation

This section details the reception and obligation ceremony for the Mark Master Degree. It begins with a severe reprimand given to the candidate for presenting unfit work and claiming undeserved wages, a practice noted as often omitted in modern lodges. The candidate is then prepared by being divested of valuables, blindfolded, and having a cable-tow placed around his body. After an alarm at the door, the candidate, identified as a Master Mason seeking further light, is admitted using the pass-word JOPPA. The Senior Deacon then symbolically receives the candidate upon the edge of an indenting chisel and under the pressure of a mallet, explaining their use for 'indenting the mind.' The ceremony concludes with a perambulation around the Lodge, during which the Master reads scripture passages concerning 'the stone which the builders refused/rejected' becoming 'the head stone of the corner.'

This section details the reception and obligation of a Mark Master Mason. The candidate undergoes a ritualistic perambulation, answering questions at the posts of the Junior and Senior Wardens, and the Right Worshipful Master. The core of the section is the extensive obligation, which includes promises of secrecy, adherence to Masonic law, obedience to summonses, and specific rules regarding the use, exchange, and redemption of a personal 'mark.' It also outlines severe penalties for violating the oath and concludes with a practical demonstration of how a brother's mark can be used to request financial aid.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0089 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: Grips and the Wages Parable

This section details the grips and passwords of a Mark Master Mason, including the pass-grip (JOPPA) and the real grip (SIROC or MARK WELL). It then describes a ritualistic parable concerning a keystone ordered by Hiram Abiff, which was initially rejected by the Overseers but later found in the rubbish. The Right Worshipful Master presents this keystone, reads several scriptural passages about the 'stone which the builders refused,' and invests the candidate with a new name, represented by a circle of letters on the keystone.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0091 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Exaltation Ceremony

The Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony details the admission process for candidates seeking further light in Masonry. After an alarm at the Chapter door, candidates, who have previously attained the degrees of Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master, confirm their free will, preparation, and proficiency. They present a pass word, RABBONI, before being admitted under a 'Living Arch' formed by companions. This arch involves physical prostration and difficulty in passage. Candidates then kneel at the altar for their seventh obligation, a solemn oath covering secrecy, protection of companions, non-revelation of ineffable characters or the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word (except under specific conditions), avoidance of clandestine chapters, and strict adherence to procedural requirements such as exalting exactly three candidates at a time and requiring nine Royal Arch Masons to open a Chapter.

This section details the extensive obligation of a Royal Arch Mason, covering secrecy, prerequisites for candidates (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master), rules for exaltation (three candidates at a time, nine Masons for opening a Chapter), conduct towards fellow Companions (no speaking evil, apprising of danger, preference in employment, assistance in difficulty, keeping secrets, aiding the indigent), and adherence to constitutional laws and by-laws. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation. Following the obligation, the ritual describes the candidate kissing the book seven times and the Principal Sojourner's address, emphasizing the importance of the Royal Arch secrets and the necessity of trials through 'rough and rugged ways' to test fidelity. The candidates are then led around the Chapter and kneel for a prayer to the 'Supreme and inscrutable Architect of universal Nature'.

This section describes a prayer offered during the Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony, emphasizing gratitude for human reason, capacity for improvement, and the institution of Freemasonry. It highlights new obligations of virtue and holiness, reverence for God's name, and the diligent search for divine authority in nature. The prayer concludes with a plea for devotion, love, and charity, and for heavenly grace to assist in rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' was destroyed by sin. Following the prayer, the Principal Sojourner leads candidates around the Chapter, reading the first six verses of Exodus chapter 3, which describe Moses encountering the burning bush on Mount Horeb. The candidates are then brought before a representation of the burning bush, and their blindfolds are removed.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0127 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Original Points and Commentary

This appendix section details several foundational Masonic concepts, including the twelve 'Original Points' essential for legal initiation, the symbolic meaning of the penal sign linked to Adam's fall and divine judgment, and the specific phrases used to describe a candidate's progression through various degrees (initiated, passed, raised, etc.). It also explains the 'Sign of Distress' as a call for fraternal assistance and discusses the traditional Masonic stance against written work to maintain uniformity, emphasizing oral transmission. Finally, it touches upon the historical use of vaults for secret purposes and questions the historical accuracy of the tradition regarding workmen's wages at Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0127 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0128 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Triple Tau.

This section discusses the tradition concerning the payment of workmen's wages during the construction of Solomon's Temple, noting that its historical accuracy and documentation are questionable, suggesting it may be a later fabrication. It also includes a footnote clarifying that information about the Triple Tau (not detailed in the text itself) is sourced from a popular Masonic journal, reflecting the fraternity's sentiments, rather than Dr. Oliver's Dictionary.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0128 -->

Duegard of a Master: (Fig. 5) is mentioned as a visual representation.
Sign of a Master Mason: Draw the right hand (thumb in) across the stomach as low as the vest, then drop the hand suddenly (Fig. 6).
Grand Hailing Sign of Distress: Raise the hands as represented in Fig. 7 and drop them with spirit, repeated three times.
Words accompanying the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress (in night/dark): "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?"

Gavel raps for opening a Third Degree Lodge: Master gives one rap, Senior Warden one, Junior Warden one. Master gives one rap (second time), responded to by Wardens. Master gives one rap (third time), responded to by Wardens.
Gavel raps for opening a Second Degree Lodge: Two raps only.
Gavel raps for opening a First Degree Lodge: One rap each, first by Master, then Senior Warden, lastly Junior Warden.

Master removes hat and recites Psalm 133: "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! It is like the precious ointment upon the head, that ran down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard; that went down to the skirts of his garments; as the dew of Hermon, and as the dew that descended upon the mountains of Zion: for there the Lord commanded the blessing, even life forever more." Amen!
Brethren respond: "Amen! So mote it be!"
Conductor leads candidate to the center of the Lodge before the altar.
Conductor makes the duegard and sign of a Fellow Craft, responded to by the Master.
Candidate and conductor retire to the ante-room.
Lodge is arranged for the second reception, including two large pillars (6.5 to 7 feet high, 5 feet apart) placed near the door.
Fifteen pieces of painted board are arranged on the carpet to represent three, five, and seven steps/stairs (some Lodges use real steps).
Candidate is dressed with a white apron, flap turned down, as worn by Fellow Crafts.
Conductor opens the Lodge-door and leads the candidate by the left arm through the door in front of the pillars.
Conductor explains the ascent through a porch by a flight of winding stairs to the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
Gives candidate a rake across his breast with the hand to draw attention to the penalty.
Workmen's wages were paid daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly in their respective Lodges.
Wages were paid in the Middle Chamber when the Temple was nearly completed.
The foot of the winding staircase to the Middle Chamber was guarded by the Junior Warden, and the summit by the Senior Warden of a Fellow Crafts' Lodge.
The Master Mason candidate represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the exemplification of the degree.
Hiram Abiff's daily practice involved entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.
Hiram Abiff was struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge across his throat, a square across his breast, and a setting-maul on his forehead.
The ruffians buried Hiram's body in the rubbish of the Temple until low-twelve, then moved it to a grave dug due east and west, six feet perpendicular, west of Mount Moriah.
An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.
The twelve recanting Fellow Crafts appeared before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of their innocence.
Search parties were divided into groups of three, traveling east, west, north, and south.
The Master places his hands in the form of a duegard of a Master Mason, alluding to the manner in which Hiram's body was found.

Candidate kneels in the center of the Lodge to implore blessings of Deity.
Candidate is accosted by three Fellow Crafts who demand Master Mason secrets.
Candidate receives blows with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul.

Hiram Abiff's usual practice at high twelve: entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three murderers (Jubela, Jubelo, Jubelum) position themselves at the south, west, and east gates.
Hiram Abiff attempts to retire at the south, then west, then east gates, receiving a blow at each.

The body is carried a westerly course to a hill west of Mount Moriah and buried six feet perpendicular, with an acacia planted at the head.

The Worshipful Master initiates the closing by asking the Senior Warden if there is anything further before closing the Lodge.
The Senior Warden rises, gives the sign of a Master Mason (if on that degree), and responds.
