Minutes of the communication are read by the Secretary and approved if no objections, indicated by one rap from the Worshipful Master.
The Junior Deacon rises upon the Worshipful Master's rap and is questioned about the Lodge being duly tyled.
The Junior Deacon makes the sign, confirms the Lodge is tyled, and is instructed to inform the Tyler to tyle accordingly.

The Junior Deacon reports the Lodge is tyled by a brother of the degree within the outer door, whose duty is to keep off cowans and eavesdroppers and allow passage only to the qualified with the Worshipful Master's permission.
The Worshipful Master gives one rap, and the Senior Warden rises to answer questions about becoming a Master Mason, including traveling for wages and relieving distressed Masons.
Optional questions and answers are used by some Lodges regarding traveling from west to east in search of 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) and finding a substitute.
The Worshipful Master gives two raps, and all officers rise to confirm their stations and duties, often referring to the opening ceremony.
The Worshipful Master gives three raps, and all in the Lodge rise, with the Master rising first, as the Senior Warden explains the Master's duty to open and govern the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master orders the Lodge closed until the next communication, instructing the Senior Warden to communicate this to the Junior Warden, who then informs the brethren.
Brethren perform the signs, which are the same as at opening.
The Master gives one rap, responded to by the Senior Warden, then the Junior Warden, and repeated three times each.
The Worshipful Master questions how Masons should meet, act, and part, with responses 'On the Level,' 'On the Plumb,' and 'On the Square.'

The Master removes his hat and recites a closing prayer: 'May the blessing of Heaven rest upon us, and all regular Masons; may brotherly love prevail, and every moral and social virtue cement us. Amen.'
The brethren respond: 'So mote it be.'
Some Lodges sing a closing song, such as 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon.'

Senior Deacon places the edge of the chisel against the candidate's breast.
Senior Deacon raises mallet, makes false motions, and gives a violent blow upon the head of the chisel.
Senior Deacon throws down mallet and chisel, takes candidate's left arm, and instructs him to 'Follow me.'
Candidate and Senior Deacon walk four times around the Lodge.
Master, Senior, and Junior Grand Wardens each give one loud rap with their mallet as the candidate passes their stations.
Master reads scripture passages from Psalm cxviii. 22, Matthew xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, and Luke xx. 17.
Candidate arrives at the Junior Warden's post, where four knocks are given, and questions are asked and answered.
Candidate proceeds to the Senior Warden, who gives four raps, and the same questions and answers are exchanged.
Candidate is directed to the Right Worshipful Master in the East, where the same questions are asked and answered.
The Master orders the candidate to be conducted back to the Senior Warden in the West.
The candidate is taught to approach the East by four upright, regular steps, with feet forming a square and body erect at the altar.
The candidate kneels to receive the obligation.
After the obligation, the Right Worshipful Master instructs the candidate to detach their hand and kiss the book four times.
An alarm is made on the outside of the door immediately after the obligation.
The Junior Deacon attends the alarm and returns with a letter for the Right Worshipful Master.
The letter requests a loan of twenty-five dollars, offering the sender's mark as security.
The pass-grip of a Mark Master Mason is associated with the password JOPPA, referencing the steep ascent from Joppa while conveying timber from Lebanon.

The real grip of a Mark Master Mason is named SIROC or MARK WELL, formed by locking the little fingers of the right hands, turning their backs together, and placing the ends of the thumbs against each other.
The Right Worshipful Master engages the candidate in a dialogue to transition from the pass-grip to the true grip.
The Right Worshipful Master admonishes the candidate never to reveal the words of the grip in any way other than how they were received.
Brethren shuffle their feet to signify a disturbance among the workmen.
The Right Worshipful Master gives two raps with his gavel to summon the three Overseers.
The Right Worshipful Master exhibits the figure of a keystone for inspection.
The Overseers report rejecting a stone because it was 'neither oblong nor square,' lacked a craft mark, and was deemed unfit, subsequently thrown among the rubbish.
The Right Worshipful Master orders an immediate search for the keystone, stating the Temple cannot be finished without it.
Brethren shuffle about the Lodge to find the keystone and bring it to the East.
The Senior Warden takes the keystone and reports its discovery in the Temple's rubbish to the Right Worshipful Master.
Two or three brethren carry the keystone to the Right Worshipful Master.
The Right Worshipful Master receives the keystone and places it upright and plumb on his desk, with its initials facing the Lodge and the candidate.
The Right Worshipful Master gives four raps with the gavel, prompting all to rise (though some Lodges remain seated).

The Right Worshipful Master reads specific scriptural passages (Ps. cxviii. 22, Matt. xxi. 42, Mark xii. 10, Luke xx. 17) about the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner, striking the keystone once, twice, thrice, and four times after each passage.
The Right Worshipful Master reads from Revelation xi. 17 about receiving a white stone and a new name.
The candidate steps forward to receive the new name.
The Right Worshipful Master invests the candidate with a new name, described as a circle of letters and the general mark of the Degree.
The Right Worshipful Master points out the initials on the keystone to the candidate.
Seven distinct knocks are given at the Chapter door.
The Captain of the Host raps nine times, answered by the Principal Sojourner with three times three, then one rap, which the Captain answers with one.
The Principal Sojourner whispers the word RABBONI as a pass.
Candidates are led by a rope upon entry.
Companions form a 'Living Arch' by creating two lines facing each other and locking fingers.
As candidates pass under the Living Arch, companions place knuckles on their necks and backs, kneading them and prostrating candidates on the floor.
Candidates kneel at the altar to receive the obligation.
The obligation is the seventh taken by the candidate in their Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken only in the presence of three Royal Arch Masons (including the speaker), by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltation of candidates must be exactly three at one time.
A Chapter of Royal Arch Masons requires nine Royal Arch Masons to form or open.
Obligation taken in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, candidate making one.
Secrets received by 'three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
Candidate must answer and obey all due signs and summonses within the length of their cable-tow.
Candidate kisses the book seven times.
Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter.
Candidates kneel for a prayer read by the Principal Sojourner.
A prayer is recited during the ceremony.
Candidates are led once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads the first six verses of the third chapter of Exodus.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Candidates are directed to halt in front of the burning bush representation.
Bandages (blindfolds) are removed from the candidates' eyes by the Principal Sojourner.

The twelve 'Original Points' are essential forms and ceremonies for legal reception into the Order: opening, preparing, reporting, entering, prayer, circumambulation, advancing, obligation, intrusted, invested, placed, closing.
The penal sign marks the obligation.
A candidate is 'initiated' for the first Degree, 'passed' for the second, 'raised' for the third.
A candidate is 'congratulated' (advanced) for the Mark Degree.
One who has 'passed the chair' is said to have 'presided'.
A Most Excellent Master is 'acknowledged and received'.
A Royal Arch Mason is 'exalted'.
The sign of distress, in conjunction with a few words, is used by a brother in difficulty or danger to claim assistance and protection from other brethren.
Masonic mysteries are communicated orally to candidates, not written.
The tradition involves the payment of work-men's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Corn, Wine, and Oil symbolize the physical sustenance and well-being provided to Operative Masons for their manual labor. For Speculative Masons, wages symbolize internal, moral, and intangible rewards such as love, friendship, respect, opportunity, happy labor, and association, representing the fruits of their ethical and fraternal conduct.

Operative Masonry refers to the proper application of architectural rules for structure, figure, strength, beauty, proportion, and correspondence. Speculative Masonry teaches to subdue passions, act upon the square, maintain a good report, secrecy, and charity. It is interwoven with religion, obliging Masons to pay rational homage to Deity, contemplate the glorious works of creation, and adore the divine Creator. The seventh day of rest is consecrated for contemplation of creation and adoration of the Creator.

The compasses symbolize the containment and embodiment of the fundamental tenets of Freemasonry: Friendship, Morality, and Brotherly Love. These tenets are presented as foundational principles of the Craft.

The "Third Circuit" symbolizes the journey of life, marked by inevitable suffering, loss, and the ultimate certainty of death. The lamentations represent the human experience of grief and the transient nature of earthly existence ("life is a vapor," "days are passed"). The responses from the Chaplain, particularly the references to a "tree planted by the waters" and "a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens," symbolize enduring faith, divine sustenance, and the promise of immortality and resurrection beyond the grave. The ritual as a whole serves as a memento mori, reminding participants of mortality while simultaneously offering comfort and hope through spiritual belief and the prospect of an afterlife.

Life is presented as 'death’s vestibule' and a 'journey to the grave,' symbolizing the transient nature of earthly existence. The comparison of human life to flowers and grass emphasizes fragility and impermanence. The 'valley of the shadow of death' symbolizes the journey through mortality with divine comfort. The scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the hope of resurrection, transformation from a natural to a spiritual body, and victory over death. The seed analogy in 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the transformation of the body after death into a new, spiritual form. The 'broad mantle of charity' symbolizes Masonic non-judgment and compassion towards the deceased.

The scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job symbolize resurrection, incorruption, transformation from mortal to immortal, and the belief in a Redeemer. The corn of nourishment symbolizes the resurrection of the body, sown in corruption and raised in incorruption, glory, power, and as a spiritual body. The wine of refreshment symbolizes spiritual refreshment, divine grace, blessings, and comfort for those afflicted or distressed. The oil of joy symbolizes comfort, consolation for the bereaved, healing of heart-wounds, the virtue of charity, increased affection, leniency, indulgence, forgiveness, and a joyful passage through death. The lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a Mason's badge, denotes the hope that the departed brother's soul, purified from imperfections, has found a welcome in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

The white glove is an emblem of Fidelity, symbolizing Masonic friendship and brotherly love, and signifies an increased obligation to the living brethren. The sprig of evergreen is an emblem of Masonic Faith in the immortality of the soul, representing the eternal principle in man that survives death and blooms perpetually. It denotes trust that the deceased's immortal spirit has entered a celestial temple and found acceptance with the Supreme Grand Master.

The emphasis on 'fidelity' in discharging duties symbolizes the integrity and trustworthiness expected of a Masonic officer.

The office of Grand Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual and moral foundation of Masonry. The Holy Bible, entrusted to the Chaplain, represents the Great Light and source of divine guidance. Masonry's role as 'religion's handmaid' signifies its supportive function in moral and spiritual development, distinct from being a religion itself, and underscores its spirit of universal tolerance.

The proclamation symbolizes the public declaration of Masonic principles, including loyalty to country, reverence for the Great Architect, and the practice of benevolence. The handing over of the working tools (Square, Level, Plumb) to the Architect by the Grand Master symbolizes the trust placed in skilled leadership and the importance of proper design, execution, and moral rectitude in all undertakings, particularly in the construction of a symbolic or literal temple. It signifies the transition from planning to execution, guided by Masonic virtues.

The sign of a Master Mason alludes to the penalty of the obligation of a Master Mason. The grand hailing sign of distress, accompanied by the words "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?", is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a plea for aid in dire circumstances and a connection to the central legend of the degree. The scripture passage (Psalm 133) emphasizes the importance of unity among brethren.

Operative Masonry symbolizes the practical application of architectural rules to create figure, strength, and beauty in structures, providing shelter and demonstrating human wisdom, science, and industry. Speculative Masonry symbolizes the moral and ethical lessons derived from these principles, teaching candidates to subdue passions, act upon the square, maintain a good report, keep secrecy, and practice charity. The ascent through winding stairs to the Middle Chamber represents a journey of learning and progression to receive further instruction and understanding.

Secret societies like the Essenes conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures. The breast, as the abode of fortitude, teaches the candidate to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity with patience rather than betraying secrets. Hebrew words ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) are symbolically interpreted to mean fortifying oneself with higher sciences and preparing to receive moral light. Shibboleth signifies both an ear of grain and a stream of water, with its symbolic interpretation increasing interest as one advances through the 'field of corn by the river-side'. The payment of wages without fear or scruple symbolizes the employers' entitlement to unlimited confidence.

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.

The candidate symbolically represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff, who was slain just before the completion of King Solomon's Temple. The unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum represents the Master's Lodge. The demand for Master Mason secrets and Hiram's refusal symbolize fidelity and integrity. The planting of an acacia at the grave signifies remembrance and immortality.

The absence of designs on the trestle-board symbolizes a disruption of order and the loss of a master builder. The white gloves and aprons worn by the Fellow Crafts signify innocence and purity, even after their initial complicity. The acacia tree, discovered at the grave, is a powerful symbol of immortality and the enduring nature of the soul. The ruffians' self-imprecations symbolize the self-destructive nature of guilt and the just, inevitable consequences of treachery and murder, often interpreted as the penalties associated with Masonic obligations.

The ritual of closing the Lodge includes symbolic elements such as the Senior Warden's journey from west to east in search of 'that which was lost,' referring to the Master's word, and finding a substitute. The principles of meeting 'On the Level,' acting 'On the Plumb,' and parting 'On the Square' symbolize equality, uprightness, and rectitude in Masonic conduct. The Worshipful Master's station in the East, like the rising sun, symbolizes his role in governing and instructing the Craft.

The rejected keystone, despite its 'singular form and beauty,' symbolizes work that does not conform to established Masonic standards or lacks proper identification. Its rejection and casting aside as rubbish emphasize the importance of precision, adherence to rules, and accountability for one's craft. The subsequent impatience for wages and the song about the 'Sabbath' highlight the completion of labor and the expectation of just reward, contrasting with the fate of the non-conforming work.

The reprimand symbolizes the importance of diligence, skill, and integrity in one's work and the consequences of attempting to deceive or impose upon the Craft. The chisel and mallet, used by operative masons to shape stone, are symbolically employed by Free and Accepted Masons to 'hew, cut, carve, and indent the mind,' signifying the moral and intellectual refinement of the candidate. The reception upon the chisel and mallet represents the enduring impression of the Mark Master's lessons. The scripture passages about 'the stone which the builders refused' becoming 'the head stone of the corner' allude to the value of that which is initially rejected or overlooked, and its ultimate importance, a central theme of the Mark Master degree.

The four upright, regular steps forming a square symbolize precision, rectitude, and the candidate's commitment to moral and Masonic principles in their approach to sacred duties. The repeated questioning at various posts signifies the importance of consistency, truthfulness, and the candidate's unwavering affirmation of their commitment. The 'mark' itself is a profound symbol of a personal pledge, a bond of trust, and a form of security or credit within the fraternity, representing a brother's honor and commitment. Its specified value (half a shekel of silver or quarter of a dollar) gives it a tangible, albeit symbolic, economic weight. The severe penalties of having the right ear smitten off and the right hand chopped off symbolize the complete forfeiture of the ability to hear the sacred word and to perform Masonic work, representing the ultimate consequence for an impostor or oath-breaker.

The keystone, initially rejected by the builders for its unconventional shape and lack of a recognizable mark, ultimately becomes the 'head stone of the corner,' symbolizing that true value and importance may be overlooked or misunderstood at first. The scriptural passages reinforce this theme of vindication for the rejected. The 'new name' and the 'mark' on the keystone, along with the reference to the 'white stone' from Revelation, symbolize a unique identity, recognition, and a deeper, personal understanding or achievement within the Mark Master degree, signifying a special reward for those who overcome.

The 'Living Arch' symbolizes the difficult and humbling path to exaltation, requiring perseverance and submission. The physical prostration and kneading represent the trials and purification necessary for advancement. Kneeling for the seventh time at the altar signifies the increasing solemnity and binding nature of the obligations as one progresses through Masonic degrees. The phrase 'he that humbleth himself shall be exalted' directly links the physical act of stooping low under the arch to the spiritual lesson of humility leading to higher understanding. The journey through darkness and crooked paths made straight ('I will bring the blind by a way they know not...') suggests divine guidance and enlightenment provided through the Masonic path.

The 'rough and rugged ways' and 'many trials' symbolize the challenges and tests of fidelity a candidate must undergo to prove their worthiness for the deeper secrets of the Royal Arch Degree. The 'stupendous arch of heaven' mentioned in the prayer symbolizes the divine architectural power of the Supreme Architect of universal Nature.

The prayer symbolizes a deep adoration for divine perfection, gratitude for the gifts of reason and improvement, and a commitment to virtue and holiness. The 'second temple of reformation' represents the spiritual rebuilding of the individual's character after the loss of original purity. The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.

The penal sign symbolizes the obligation and serves as a reminder of Adam's fall, the penalty of death, and the judgment of God upon the disobedient, likening it to the severing of the head from the body by human justice. Vaults are noted as being used for secret purposes.
entries.candidate_lesson:
A Master Mason learns that their true 'wages' are not material but are internal and moral, earned through their dedication to speculative labor, ethical conduct, and fraternal principles. These intangible rewards are a consequence of their personal growth and contributions within the Craft.

The candidate learns the distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry, understanding that modern Freemasonry is primarily Speculative, focusing on moral and spiritual development. They are taught the importance of self-governance, ethical conduct (acting upon the square, good report, secrecy, charity), and a deep reverence for the Creator and His creation, including the practice of setting aside time for rest and contemplation.

The candidate is taught the three primary tenets of the Institution: Friendship, Morality, and Brotherly Love, which are presented as essential principles to guide their conduct and understanding.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.

A candidate learns the importance of diligence, integrity, and faithful execution of responsibilities, particularly in positions of trust within the Lodge, which earns the esteem and applause of fellow Masons.

The Grand Chaplain's charge teaches that Masonry, while not a religion, is deeply rooted in moral and virtuous principles, acting as a supportive framework for spiritual growth. It emphasizes the importance of universal tolerance, the communication of ethical doctrines, and the reverence for the Holy Bible as a source of light and guidance. It also highlights the steadfastness and perseverance expected in discharging duties.

The candidate learns about the public-facing virtues of Freemasonry, including loyalty, piety, and universal benevolence. They also learn the importance of skill, capacity, and diligent conduct in leadership roles, as exemplified by the Architect entrusted with the building project. The emphasis on order, harmony, beauty, and strength in construction serves as an allegory for the moral and spiritual building of character.

The candidate learns the specific signs and words associated with the Master Mason degree, particularly the solemnity of the obligation and the importance of recognizing and responding to a brother's distress. The ritual of opening the Lodge and the recitation of Psalm 133 reinforce the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love, reminding members of the blessings that come from harmonious fellowship.

The candidate learns about the progression within the Fellow Craft degree, the significance of the Middle Chamber as a place of instruction, and the fundamental distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry. They are taught that Operative Masonry relates to the practical craft of building, while Speculative Masonry applies these principles to moral and ethical self-improvement, emphasizing virtues like subduing passions, acting justly, maintaining good reputation, secrecy, and charity.

Candidates are taught to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity, distress, pain, or disappointment with patience. This fortitude prevents the bitter stings of remorse that result from betraying secrets entrusted under solemn obligation. The neophyte should prepare to receive moral light through higher sciences. Candidates are directed to an attentive study of the liberal arts and sciences. The legend of wages paid without fear or scruple teaches the importance of unlimited confidence in employers.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.

The narrative emphasizes the importance of fidelity to one's obligations and secrets, even unto death. It teaches about the consequences of avarice and betrayal, as seen in the actions of the three Fellow Crafts. The candidate learns about the ultimate sacrifice made for integrity and the pursuit of knowledge, and the eventual discovery and justice for wrongdoing.

The narrative teaches the severe consequences of treachery and the importance of fidelity to one's obligations. It underscores the concept of justice, where actions lead to fitting retribution. The persistent search for the ruffians and the discovery of the grave emphasize the enduring nature of truth and the eventual revelation of hidden deeds. The confession of the twelve Fellow Crafts highlights the possibility of redemption through acknowledging guilt and seeking pardon.

The closing ceremony reinforces the importance of brotherly love, moral and social virtues, and the expectation for every brother to conduct himself as a man and a Mason. It also highlights the duty to support oneself and family, and to contribute to the relief of worthy distressed Master Masons, their widows, and orphans, emphasizing the practical application of Masonic principles in daily life.

The candidate learns the critical importance of presenting work that is 'good work, true work, and square work,' adhering strictly to the craft's authorized standards and bearing the proper mark of a craftsman. It teaches about the consequences of attempting to pass off unauthorized or improperly marked work, and the necessity of personal integrity and accountability in all endeavors.

The candidate is taught the critical importance of performing work with skill and industry, and the ethical principle of not claiming rewards or wages that are not rightfully earned. The reprimand serves as a 'striking lesson' against imposition upon the Craft. The symbolic use of the chisel and mallet emphasizes the continuous process of self-improvement and the shaping of one's character and mind. The scripture readings impart a lesson on the potential for the rejected to become essential, encouraging perseverance and recognizing inherent worth.

The candidate learns the paramount importance of strict secrecy regarding the Mark Master Mason's secrets, disclosing them only to duly qualified brethren after rigorous examination. They are taught the duty to uphold the Constitutions of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter and State Grand Royal Arch Chapter, as well as the by-laws of any Mark Master Lodge. The obligation instills obedience to all regular signs and summonses from fellow Mark Master Masons or Lodges, within the bounds of their cable-tow. A strong ethical lesson is imparted regarding not wronging a brother or the Lodge, even to the smallest value, and preventing such wrongs if possible. The unique rules surrounding the 'mark' teach about personal integrity, the sanctity of a pledge, and the responsibility to honor commitments related to one's mark, including its redemption. The lesson of mutual aid is emphasized through the promise to receive a brother's mark when a favor is requested and to grant it if possible, or to return its specified value if the favor cannot be granted.

The candidate is taught the importance of discerning true worth and recognizing value in things that may initially appear unconventional or rejected. The narrative of the keystone emphasizes that what is deemed unfit by some may be essential and invaluable. The investment with a 'new name' signifies a personal transformation and a unique identity within the Craft, highlighting individual merit and the reward for perseverance. The admonition regarding the grips instills the importance of fidelity and the proper transmission of Masonic knowledge.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.

Every candidate must pass through the twelve 'Original Points' for their initiation to be considered legal. A true and faithful brother is obligated to respond to the sign of distress, offering assistance and protection to a brother in need. Masons are taught to uphold their obligations by not writing down the work, preserving the oral tradition.
tradition_notes: Ancient Operative Brethren performed manual labor and received physical wages of Corn, Wine, and Oil for their physical welfare.
The concept of wages evolved from physical sustenance for Operative Masons to moral and internal rewards for Speculative Masons.
Freemasonry is considered under two denominations: Operative and Speculative.
Ancient brethren wrought in both Operative and Speculative Masonry.
Modern Masons work in Speculative Masonry only.
Ancient brethren consecrated the seventh day as a day of rest, following God's creation pattern.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
The formal instruction and investment with the badge of office for an elected Lodge officer is a traditional part of Masonic installation ceremonies.
The investiture of a Grand Officer with a jewel of office.
The specific duties assigned to the Grand Chaplain, including leading devotional exercises.
The philosophical stance of Masonry as 'religion's handmaid,' promoting universal tolerance.
The Holy Bible being recognized and entrusted as the Great Light in Masonry.

This text describes a traditional Masonic ceremony, likely a cornerstone laying, which involves a public proclamation and the symbolic presentation of working tools to the master builder or architect.
The mention of 'concealed from the eyes of men' suggests the esoteric nature of some Masonic knowledge, even within a public ceremony.
The grand hailing sign of distress is specifically linked to the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff.
The specific sequence of gavel raps by the Master and Wardens is a traditional method for opening Lodges on different degrees.
The recitation of Psalm 133 and the response "Amen! So mote it be!" are traditional elements of the Lodge opening ceremony.
Some Lodges, especially in large cities, employ real steps for the ascent, while most country Lodges use painted boards to represent the steps.
Calmet asserts that St. John was an Essene, a secret society of his day that conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures, which may be termed Freemasonry under another name.
The Jewish Cabala includes a rule called Transposition, used to find meaning in anagrammatically formed words.
Brother Rosenberg, a Jewish Mason, used Transposition to interpret ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) in Hebrew, relating them to receiving higher sciences and moral light.
SHIBBOLETH in Hebrew has two significations: an ear of grain and a stream of water.
The historical account of the Ephraimites and Gileadites illustrates how the word 'Shibboleth' was used as a test, exploiting a peculiarity in the Ephraimites' speech organs.

The Fellow Crafts' Degree Lecture's first section recapitulates the ceremonies of passing a candidate, and the second section discusses the ancient division into operative and speculative Masons and the liberal arts and sciences.

According to Masonic legends, workmen's wages were paid in their respective Lodges, and later in the Middle Chamber, which was accessible by a winding staircase guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
The narrative describes a core tradition of the Master Mason degree, recounting the legend of Hiram Abiff's murder and the subsequent events.
The use of specific tools (twenty-four-inch gauge, square, setting-maul) in the ritual is a traditional element.
The specific names of the ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, are traditional within the Hiramic legend.
The detail of the twelve Fellow Crafts confessing their premeditated guilt and being sent on a search is a traditional element.
The acacia tree is a traditional and significant symbol marking Hiram's grave.
The specific self-imprecations uttered by the ruffians are traditional and correspond to the penalties of Masonic obligations.
Some Lodges use specific variations in the Senior Warden's response, such as adding 'in the west' after 'Nothing, Worshipful'.
Some Lodges use specific questions and answers regarding the search for 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) both at opening and closing.
The questions and answers about the Master's word are also occasionally used by some Masters in the lecture.
Some Lodges sing a specific closing song, 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon'.
The ritual involves a specific sequence of inspection by three Overseers (Junior, Senior, Master) at different 'gates' (stations).
The 'heave-over' sign is a specific ritualistic gesture associated with casting aside the keystone.
The timing of the disturbance and the call for wages is traditionally set at 'the sixth hour of the sixth day of the week,' signifying the end of the work week.
The singing of a hymn and a procession for wages are traditional elements concluding this part of the ritual.
The severe reprimand ordered to be given to the candidate is omitted in most Lodges at the present day.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to Hiram the Builder.
The obligation is stated to be 'in addition to my former obligations,' indicating a progressive system of degrees.

The obligation explicitly mentions supporting the 'Constitution of the General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America' and the 'Grand Royal Arch Chapter of this State,' placing the Mark Master degree within the American Royal Arch system.
The 'mark' chosen by a brother must be recorded in a 'book of marks.'
The value of a brother's mark, when a favor cannot be granted, is specified as 'half a shekel of silver, or quarter of a dollar,' linking ancient and modern monetary equivalents.
Some Lodges do not require all brethren to rise after the Right Worshipful Master gives four raps, choosing instead to keep their seats.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.
Ancient Masonry recognized twelve original points as the basis of the entire system.
The penal sign applies to both the Entered Apprentice and Royal Arch Mason's 'Duegard'.

The tradition regarding the payment of workmen's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple may not be historically accurate and could have been fabricated in a subsequent age without authenticating documents.
The tradition respecting the payment of the work-men's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple is mentioned, though its authority and calculations are unknown.
Information regarding the Triple Tau is quoted from a popular Masonic journal, not Dr. Oliver's Dictionary, and embodies the sentiments of the fraternity.
caution_notes: The intangible wages of a Master Mason are not automatically granted; they must be earned through moral labor and dedication.
Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.
A rake across the breast is given to draw the candidate's attention to the penalty.
Betrayal of secrets entrusted on the faith of a solemn obligation will inevitably result in the bitter stings of remorse.
The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.

The Master Overseer's stern look and questioning ('Why do you bring another man's work to impose upon the Overseers?') serve as a caution against deception, presenting work that is not one's own, or attempting to circumvent established inspection processes.

I will not give the secrets of a Mark Master Mason to any one of an inferior degree, nor to any other person in the known world, except it be a true and lawful brother, or brethren, of this degree; and not unto him nor unto them whom I shall hear so to be, but unto him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Lodge, or a brother of this Degree, to the value of his wages (or one penny), myself, knowingly, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.

I will not sell, swap, barter, or exchange my mark, which I shall hereafter choose, after it has been recorded in the book of marks, for any other one, unless it be a dead mark, or one of an older date, nor will I pledge it a second time until it is lawfully redeemed from the first pledge.

Binding myself under no less penalty than to have my right ear smitten off, that I may forever be unable to hear the word, and my right hand chopped off, as the penalty of an impostor, if I should ever prove wilfully guilty of violating any part of this my solemn oath, or obligation, of a Mark Master Mason.
The candidate is admonished never to give the words of the grip in any way but that in which he received them.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.
Writing the Masonic work is considered a violation of obligations and would lead to a general uprising of the craft.
A General Grand Lodge attempting to authorize written work for uniformity would be a foolish sanction by the fraternity.

The tradition respecting the payment of the work-men's wages at the building of Solomon's Temple may not be accurate, with the probability that it was fabricated in a subsequent age without authenticating documents.

entries.title: The Setting Maul
entries.short_summary: The Setting Maul is described as a wooden instrument used by operative masons to firmly set polished stone into a wall. Symbolically, it has been recognized as a symbol of destruction since prehistoric times and appears frequently in mythology.
entries.full_summary:
The Setting Maul is described as a wooden instrument used by operative masons to firmly set polished stone into a wall. Symbolically, it has been recognized as a symbol of destruction since prehistoric times and appears frequently in mythology.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pronunciation Key

This section provides a pronunciation key for various words, many of which are common in Masonic ritual and discourse. It includes rules for indicating accented syllables and short vowel sounds, followed by an alphabetical list of words with their phonetic pronunciations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0095 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section details the system for identifying approved work and paying wages to Fellow Crafts, including the use of a personal mark and a specific token, and the penalty for impostors. It then describes the preparation, reception, and obligation for advancement to the Mark Master Mason degree, including the significance of the pass 'Joppa' and the symbolic reception on the engraver's chisel and mallet, emphasizing the deep impression moral precepts should make.

This section details the obligation, signs, and a significant allegory of the Mark Master Mason degree. It covers the candidate's oath, the specific penalties associated with divulging secrets, and various signs including the duegard, the sign, the grand hailing sign of distress, and the principal sign. A central narrative involves the rejection and eventual discovery of the Key-Stone, its connection to Grand Master Hiram Abiff, and its symbolic meaning. The section also explains the grip, the name 'Mark Well,' the symbolism of a white stone and a new name from Scripture, and the initials found on the Key-Stone. It concludes with the concept of a brother's private mark and its traditional value.

This section details the Mark Master Mason degree, explaining the significance of kneeling at the altar, the degree's founding by Grand Masters Solomon, Hiram King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff, and its purpose to honor worthy brothers and prevent indigence. It clarifies that a brother presenting a mark represents Hiram Abiff, while one granting assistance represents King Solomon. The working tools, the chisel and mallet, are described with their operative uses and moral teachings, emphasizing discipline, education, correcting irregularities, and achieving contentment. The section concludes with a prayer for the closing of a Mark Master's Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0095 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0112 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: the Burning Bush and the Captivity

This section details a Royal Arch ritual reenacting two pivotal biblical narratives: Moses' encounter with the Burning Bush at Mount Horeb, where he is instructed to remove his shoes from holy ground, and the Babylonian Captivity. The ritual portrays the destruction of Jerusalem and its Temple due to the transgressions of King Zedekiah and the people, followed by Cyrus's proclamation allowing the return to Jerusalem to rebuild the Second Temple. Candidates are guided through these events, experiencing a simulated siege and captivity before being symbolically liberated.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0112 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Appendix: Notes and Authorities

This section provides an appendix of notes and authorities, covering various aspects of the Master Mason degree. It details the strong grip of a Master Mason, the three Grand Masonic Pillars (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) and their representation by Solomon, King of Israel, Hiram, King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff. It also describes the construction of the Temple, including the number of columns, pilasters, and workers by degree. The text explains the symbolism of the three steps on the Master's carpet and the nine classes of Master's emblems, specifically focusing on the setting maul, spade, coffin, and sprig of acacia as emblems of morality and immortality. Finally, it includes a historical note on the transmission and abridgment of Masonic lectures by figures like Webb, Preston, Gleason, and J. Browne.

This section, an address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, Grand Master of Vermont, defends the authenticity and lineage of Masonic lectures, specifically those transmitted through T. S. Webb and Benjamin Gleason, asserting their connection to Preston's original lectures. Tucker addresses criticisms regarding his previous statements, clarifying that Webb abridged and rearranged Preston's lectures but retained their substance. He cites his possession of 'key' copies of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason. Tucker also defends the claim that Gleason's lectures were approved by the Grand Lodge of England, referencing Gleason's own published letter from 1843, where Gleason states he acquired complete knowledge of the three degrees' lectures from Webb and successfully communicated them across the United States and Europe, 'according to the ancient landmarks,' without objection. He further supports Gleason's knowledge of Preston by quoting Gleason's 1812 oration, which praises Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements'. The discussion also mentions J. Browne's 1802 'Master Key' as a presumed source for Prestonian lectures.

This section discusses the historical presence and influence of Preston's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and the 'Prestonian system of work and lectures' in the United States, particularly in New England. It highlights Benjamin Gleason's crucial role, alongside Thomas Smith Webb, in introducing and promulgating this system. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard in 1804, employing Gleason to communicate it to lodges under its jurisdiction. Gleason continued as a dedicated lecturer until his death in 1847, remembered as a master teacher of ancient Craft Masonry. The author defends the existence of Prestonian lectures in the US against a critic, emphasizing the importance of factual investigation over mere negation.

This section discusses the history and transmission of the Prestonian system of Masonic ritual. It notes that the system, as revived by Preston and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England, underwent a revision in 1813 for reconciliation purposes, which led to some incongruities. The original Prestonian ritual was brought to the United States around 1803 by two English brethren, one of whom was a pupil of Preston. It was communicated to Thomas Smith Webb and subsequently imparted to Brother Gleason, who then taught it to lodges in Massachusetts. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system, which has since been the recognized Masonic work in the Commonwealth. Despite some modifications and innovations, the ritual has been largely preserved in its original purity, especially in older jurisdictions. The text also highlights the ongoing struggle against corrupting influences from ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications, emphasizing the importance of preserving the ritual's integrity.

This section discusses the nature and importance of Masonic lectures, specifically comparing Preston's lengthy lectures with Webb's abridgment. The author, through the Grand Lecturer's opinion, suggests that Webb's version retains practical value and is more manageable. The text criticizes those who undervalue lectures, labeling proponents of their preservation as 'parrot Masons' and advocating for a 'new era' focused solely on the philosophy, history, and symbolism of Masonry, arguing that these elements are already embedded within the traditional lectures.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0130 -->
practical_elements: A wooden instrument used by operative masons.
Used to set polished stone firmly into a wall.
CAPITAL letters indicate accented syllables
a - short a as in the word bat
i - short i as in the word ill or is
e - short e as in the word ethic
(e) - barely audible short e
u - short u as in the word but
Craftsmen choose and place a mark upon their work for identification.
Craftsmen thrust their right hand into the apartments of the Senior Grand Warden with a copy of their mark in the palm and give a token to receive wages.
Preparation for Mark Master Mason degree involves being deprived of all metals, divested of outward apparel, in a working posture, with a cable-tow four times around the body.
A demand is made at the Lodge door by four distinct knocks.
The candidate gives the pass 'Joppa'.
Reception into the Lodge is on the edge of the engraver's chisel, applied to the naked left breast, and under the impression of the mallet.
The candidate is conducted four times regularly around the Lodge to the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Right Worshipful Master.
The candidate approaches the East by four upright regular Masonic steps, with feet forming a square and body erect.
The obligation of a Mark Master Mason is taken kneeling upon both knees, with both hands covering the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
A sign, called the due guard, is shown by 'chopping off the right ear'.
Kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses for the solemn oath or obligation of a Mark Master Mason.
Sign: Chopping off the right ear (the duegard).
Sign: Chopping off right hand (the sign).
Sign: Carrying the key-stone (the grand hailing sign of distress of a Mark Master Mason).
Sign: Heave over (the principal sign).
Grip: 'Mark Well'.
Placing a brother's own private mark in the center of the general mark of the degree.
Kneeling on bended knees at the altar
Presenting a mark to receive assistance
Receiving a mark and granting assistance
Using the chisel to cut, carve, mark, and engrave work
Using the mallet to knock off excrescences and smooth surfaces
Reciting a prayer at the closing of a Mark Master's Lodge
Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads Exodus 3:1-6 during the first circuit.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Bandages are slipped up from the candidates' eyes in front of the burning bush.
A member personates the Deity behind the bush, calling 'Moses! Moses!'
The Principal Sojourner answers for candidates: 'Here I am.'

The Deity instructs: 'Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground. I am the God of thy fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.'
Candidates are directed to kneel and their faces are covered again.
The Principal Sojourner states: 'And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look upon God.'
Candidates are led three times around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads 2 Chronicles 36:11-20 during the three circuits.
Companions make loud noises (rolling cannon-balls, clashing swords, shouting, groaning, whistling, stamping, throwing down benches) to simulate the siege and destruction of Jerusalem.
Candidates are seized, thrown on the floor, bound hand and foot, and carried into the preparation-room.
Companions shout: 'Hurra for the captives!' several times.
The Captain of the Host opens the door and announces liberty, citing Cyrus's proclamation to build a second Temple.
The Principal Sojourner requests the proclamation to be read.
The Captain of the Host reads Ezra 1:1-3 (Cyrus's proclamation).
The Captain of the Host asks candidates if they are willing to go to Jerusalem.
The Principal Sojourner consults candidates and states willingness, but asks for a pass-word.
The Captain of the Host reads Exodus 3:13-14 (implied, regarding the pass-word).
The strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, is mentioned.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five (5) points of fellowship and heard in a low breath.
The instruction to 'Advance and give it' refers to the word associated with the grip.
The word is 'right'.
Promulgating the Prestonian system of work and lectures.
Communicating the Preston ritual to lodges under Grand Lodge jurisdiction.
Teaching Masonic ritual as a lecturer.
Adoption of a ritual standard by a Grand Lodge.
The Prestonian system of ritual was brought to the United States around 1803.
The ritual was communicated to Webb and then imparted to Gleason.
Brother Gleason was employed to teach the ritual to lodges in Massachusetts.
The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts approved and adopted this system.
The ritual has been preserved in a remarkable degree of purity in Massachusetts lodges, particularly in the lodge where Gleason was Master in 1809.
The system underwent modifications in its general arrangement and adaptation after its introduction to the US.
Grand Lecturer compared Preston and Gleason Lectures
Preston Lectures are lengthy, estimated to cover nearly one hundred pages of foolscap paper if written out in full
Requirement for Masons to commit lectures in extenso is considered a difficult task
Webb's abridgment and new arrangement of lectures are considered to preserve substantial practical value and use preferable language
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Maul is a symbol of destruction, a meaning attributed to it since prehistoric times and frequently depicted in mythology.

The token given by Fellow Crafts alludes to the way and manner in which they received their wages and serves to distinguish a true craftsman from an impostor. The four distinct knocks allude to the fourth degree of Masonry. The ancient city of Joppa, where materials for the Temple were landed, symbolizes the assistance afforded to workmen and is adopted as a proper pass. The reception on the engraver's chisel and mallet teaches that the moral precepts of the Mark Master Mason degree should make a deep and lasting impression upon the candidate's mind and future conduct. The due guard (chopping off the right ear) alludes to a specific meaning (text incomplete).

The duegard (chopping off the right ear) symbolizes the penalty for unlawfully divulging secrets. The sign (chopping off the right hand) alludes to an additional penalty for an imposter. The grand hailing sign of distress (carrying the key-stone) represents the manner in which brothers carry their work. The principal sign ('Heave over') refers to the principal words of the degree and the rejection of the Key-Stone by the Overseers. The Key-Stone, wrought by Hiram Abiff, symbolizes a crucial piece of work initially unrecognized and rejected, but later found and applied to its intended use, representing perseverance and the eventual recognition of true worth. The white stone alludes to a passage of Scripture about overcoming and receiving a new name. The new name, composed of the initials 'Hiram, Tyrian, Widow's, Son, Sendeth, To, King, Solomon,' serves as the general mark of the degree, where each brother places his private mark, signifying individual identity and commitment.

Kneeling at the altar symbolizes a solemn commitment to assist worthy brothers and not hastily reject their applications, ensuring their basic needs are met. A brother presenting a mark represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a poor but highly skilled and upright craftsman. A brother receiving a mark and granting assistance represents Grand Master Solomon, symbolizing wealth and great liberality. The chisel morally demonstrates the advantage of discipline and education. The mallet morally teaches to correct irregularities and reduce man to a proper level, fostering contentment through quiet deportment and discipline.

The Burning Bush symbolizes the divine presence, holiness, and God's unconsumed power, requiring reverence and humility (removal of shoes). The Babylonian Captivity represents the consequences of transgression, the destruction of the sacred due to human evil, and a period of spiritual bondage. Cyrus's Proclamation signifies divine intervention, redemption, and the opportunity for spiritual rebuilding and the restoration of purpose, akin to rebuilding the Temple. The entire journey symbolizes a spiritual progression through trials towards enlightenment and inner reformation.

The three grand masonic pillars, Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty, are represented by Solomon, King of Israel (wisdom to contrive), Hiram, King of Tyre (strength to support), and Hiram Abiff (beauty to adorn). The three steps on the Master's carpet are emblematic of the three principal stages of human life: youth, manhood, and age. The setting maul, spade, and coffin are striking emblems of mortality. The sprig of acacia symbolizes the imperishable part of man that survives the grave, bearing affinity to the Supreme Intelligence that pervades all nature and never dies.

The text implies that Masonic lectures are a primary vehicle for conveying Masonic symbolism, arguing against the idea that symbolism exists independently or is better understood by dismissing the lectures.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The importance of honesty and proper identification in one's work, as demonstrated by the Fellow Craft's mark and token. The severe consequences of imposture. The necessity of being worthy, well qualified, and duly prepared for advancement in Masonry. The significance of historical allusions within Masonic passes. The profound and lasting impact that the moral teachings of the Mark Master Mason degree should have on one's character and actions. The solemnity of Masonic obligations.

The candidate learns the profound importance of the obligation and the severe consequences of betraying Masonic secrets. The allegory of the Key-Stone teaches the value of diligent work, even if it is initially misunderstood or rejected, and the eventual triumph of truth and proper application. The symbolism of the white stone and new name encourages personal transformation and the adoption of a new identity within the fraternity. The practice of a private mark emphasizes individual contribution and accountability within the collective work.

Candidates learn the importance of charity and mutual assistance among brothers, particularly in preventing indigence. They are taught the value of discipline and education, as symbolized by the chisel. They learn the necessity of self-correction, reducing personal irregularities, and cultivating contentment through a quiet and disciplined demeanor, as symbolized by the mallet. The degree emphasizes zeal, fidelity, and attachment to Masonry as qualities worthy of honor and support.

Candidates learn the importance of reverence for the divine and sacred spaces, the severe consequences of disobedience and moral transgression, and the necessity of humility before God. The ritual teaches about the possibility of redemption and spiritual rebuilding after periods of destruction or moral decline, emphasizing the concept of a 'second temple of reformation' within oneself. It also highlights the need for divine knowledge or a 'pass-word' to navigate the spiritual path.

Candidates are encouraged to imitate Grand Master Hiram Abiff in his virtuous conduct, unfeigned piety to God, and inflexible fidelity to his trust. This imitation prepares them to welcome death as a kind messenger from the Supreme Grand Master, translating them from an imperfect lodge to a perfect, glorious, and celestial lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the universe.

The candidate should understand that Masonic lectures are a vital source for learning the history, philosophy, and symbolism of Freemasonry, and that their preservation and study are crucial for a complete understanding of the Craft, rather than being dismissed as mere rote memorization.
tradition_notes: Masonic tradition informs that the sea-coast at Joppa was so nearly perpendicular it was difficult for workmen to ascend without assistance from above, which was afforded by guards stationed there.
The Mark Master Mason degree builds upon the narrative of Hiram Abiff's death from the preceding degree.
The Key-Stone allegory describes its creation by Hiram Abiff, its rejection by the Junior, Senior, and Master Overseers, and its eventual discovery and application by order of King Solomon.
The degree incorporates a scriptural allusion to a 'white stone' and a 'new name' for those who overcome.
The new name is formed from the initials 'Hiram, Tyrian, Widow's, Son, Sendeth, To, King, Solomon' found on the Key-Stone.
The circle of letters, originally Hiram Abiff's mark, is now a general mark of the degree, where each brother places his own private mark.
The traditional price of a brother's mark is a Jewish half shekel of silver, equivalent to one-quarter of a dollar.
The Mark Master degree was founded by Grand Masters Solomon, Hiram King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff.
The degree's purpose is to provide an honorary reward for zeal, fidelity, and attachment to Masonry, and to ensure no worthy brother suffers extreme indigence.
The moral teachings of the chisel and mallet are noted as "Monitorial," indicating they are part of standard Masonic instruction.
The ritual dramatically reenacts biblical narratives from Exodus, 2 Chronicles, and Ezra.
The use of physical representations, such as the burning bush, and sound effects, like those simulating a siege, are integral to the ritual's immersive experience.
The roles of the Principal Sojourner and Captain of the Host are crucial in guiding candidates and narrating the historical and spiritual events.
The opening prayer introduces the concept of 'holiness to the Lord' and 'incense of piety' as continuous spiritual offerings.
A central theme is the rebuilding of a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' has been metaphorically destroyed by sin.

The text includes an extract from an Annual Address by M. W. P. Al. Tucker, G. M. of Vermont, discussing the history and transmission of Masonic lectures, specifically noting Webb's abridgment and rearrangement of Preston's lectures.
It mentions that Webb learned and taught Preston's lectures in full, as well as his own abridgment.
Reference is made to a 'copy in key' of both Webb's abridgment and Preston's full lectures, believed to be true manuscripts as taught by Gleason.
A 'very rare' book by J. Browne, published in London in 1802, called the 'Master Key,' containing lectures in an 'abstruse cypher,' is also noted.
The text discusses the historical transmission and authenticity of Masonic lectures, specifically the lineage from Preston to Webb and Gleason.
It highlights the practice of Grand Lecturers, such as Benjamin Gleason, being commissioned to instruct lodges.
The importance of 'ancient landmarks' in the communication of lectures is emphasized.
Reference is made to historical Masonic publications like 'Free Mason's Monitor' by T. S. Webb, 'Masonic Trestleboard', and J. Browne's 'Master Key'.
The role of Grand Lodges (England, Massachusetts and Maine) in overseeing and potentially authenticating lecture content is noted.
Preston of England's 'Illustrations of Masonry' and his 'estimable system of improvements' are noted for defining the 'immovable landmarks' of the ancient Society.
Brother Benjamin Gleason, in association with Thomas Smith Webb, introduced the Prestonian system of work and lectures into the Lodges of New England and other sections of the country.
In 1804, the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts adopted the Preston ritual as its standard of work.
Brother Gleason was exclusively employed by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts during 1804 to communicate the ritual to lodges under its jurisdiction, including what is now the State of Maine.
Benjamin Gleason continued his labors as a lecturer until his death in 1847.
Charles W. Moore, Editor of the Freemasons' Monthly Magazine, acknowledged Benjamin Gleason as a master and teacher in the purest and most perfect Masonic ritual of ancient Craft Masonry.
The 'work' of Masonry, as revived by Preston, was approved and sanctioned by the Grand Lodge of England near the close of the last century.
The 'union' in 1813 led to a revision of the ritual for reconciliation purposes.
The verbal ritual, as revised by Preston, was introduced to the US around 1803.
The system was received and preserved, especially in older jurisdictions, essentially as it came from the original source of Craft Masonry.
Discussion of the historical lectures by Preston and their abridgment by Webb, highlighting their role in transmitting Masonic knowledge.
Emphasis on the importance of preserving Masonic lectures intact as a repository of history, philosophy, and symbolism.
Reference to the role of a Grand Lecturer in comparing and evaluating different versions of Masonic lectures.
caution_notes: An impostor discovered demanding wages not due should have his right hand chopped off.
The penalty for unlawfully divulging any of the secrets of this degree includes suffering the right ear to be smote off.
An additional penalty for an imposter who divulges secrets unlawfully is to have the right hand stricken off.
The name associated with the strong grip cannot be given except on the five points of fellowship and heard in a low breath, emphasizing secrecy and proper ritualistic context.
Do not 'pin their faith' upon the mere negations of any writer, whatever may be the strength of his masonic reputation, but rather examine and investigate matters of fact and principle.
The revised Prestonian system after the 1813 union had many incongruities and departures from the original.
Many unwarrantable liberties have been taken with the ritual, and innovations have crept in.
The ritual has had to contend against the corrupting influences of ignorant itinerant lecturers and spurious publications.
Caution against undervaluing Masonic lectures and dismissing them as mere rote memorization.
Warning against attempts to inaugurate a 'new era' that seeks to change or disregard established Masonic history, philosophy, and symbolism as contained in the lectures.

entries.title: The Rights of a Master Mason
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the three fundamental rights of a Master Mason: Masonic Relief, Masonic Visitation, and Masonic Burial. It details the conditions, procedures, and limitations associated with each right, emphasizing the voluntary nature of relief, the requirements for visitation, and the family's role in requesting a Masonic funeral service.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the three fundamental rights of a Master Mason: Masonic Relief, Masonic Visitation, and Masonic Burial. It details the conditions, procedures, and limitations associated with each right, emphasizing the voluntary nature of relief, the requirements for visitation, and the family's role in requesting a Masonic funeral service.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0066 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section congratulates a Brother on being passed to the Second Degree, emphasizing that Freemasonry values internal qualifications over external ones. It highlights the institution's resilience, having survived the destruction of Solomon's magnificent Temple. The text explains that Masonic mysteries are transmitted through attentive listening and faithful preservation, using architectural tools and symbolic emblems to impart wise truths across generations. It concludes by reminding all Masons, from the youngest Entered Apprentice to the Worshipful Master, to humbly bow before the sacred name of Deity.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0066 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Section

This section describes the Third Section of a degree, focusing on hieroglyphical emblems, moral lessons, and extensive details regarding the construction of King Solomon's Temple.
