It covers the temple's foundation, location on Mount Moriah, construction duration, the absence of tool sounds during building, and the vast workforce involved, including Grand Masters, Masters, Fellow Crafts, and Entered Apprentices. The section also elaborates on the two celebrated pillars placed at the temple's entrance, explaining their symbolic significance as a memorial to the Israelites' deliverance from Egyptian bondage, referencing the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0094 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Burial Service

This section introduces the concept of a Masonic Burial Service.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0094 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0098 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Burial Service

This section outlines the regulations and procedures for a Masonic Burial Service. Only Master Masons in good standing are entitled to a Masonic funeral, either by their own request or that of their family. Entered Apprentices and Fellow Crafts are not eligible. The Master of the Lodge is responsible for summoning the Lodge, which opens on the Third Degree, proceeds to the funeral and interment, and then returns to the hall to close the Lodge. The entire ceremony must be directed by the deceased's Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0098 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0099 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Ceremonies at the Grave

This section details the Masonic funeral ceremony conducted at the grave site. It outlines the positioning of officers and brethren, the attire worn, and the sequence of the service, which includes a prayer offered by the Master, Chaplain, or a designated Brother, followed by a solemn exhortation from the Master. The ceremony emphasizes the contemplation of mortality, the uncertainty of human life, and the importance of preparing for eternity through faith, hope, and charity.

This section details the Masonic funeral ceremony conducted at the grave, including the symbolic deposit of the lamb-skin apron and evergreen. It describes the ritual of the Funeral Grand Honors, complete with specific gestures and spoken words, and concludes with prayers and blessings offered by the Master or Chaplain, emphasizing themes of mortality, immortality, and divine mercy.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0099 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0104 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: By the Grand Lodge for Use in This Jurisdiction

This section details the regulations and procedures for Masonic funeral services and Lodges of Sorrow within a specific jurisdiction. It clarifies that reading a funeral service at the grave is only permissible at the time of burial, with other modern practices deemed an innovation based on Grand Lodge decisions from 1875 and 1883. It further outlines that a Lodge of Sorrow should be held when a traditional funeral ceremony at interment is impractical, describing the ceremonial setup including black draping, specific placement of a coffin, and the use of extinguished altar lights, whether in a lodge room or a church.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0104 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0108 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: By the Grand Lodge for Use in This Jurisdiction

This section outlines regulations and procedures for Masonic funeral services and Lodges of Sorrow within a specific jurisdiction. It clarifies that reading a funeral service at the grave, except at the time of burial, is an innovation not supported by ancient Masonic usage. It details the appropriate timing for a Lodge of Sorrow, suggesting it be held when a usual funeral ceremony at interment is impracticable. Furthermore, it provides specific instructions for conducting a Lodge of Sorrow, including room draping, coffin placement, altar lights, procession order, dress code for brethren (black clothes, white gloves, aprons), and the role of music and a guard of honor.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0108 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0109 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: First Circuit

This section describes a ritualistic dialogue among the Master, Wardens, Deacons, and Chaplain, reflecting on human mortality, the transient nature of life, and the inevitability of death. The dialogue emphasizes that life is fleeting, full of disappointments, and that earthly achievements are temporary. The ritual culminates in a procession halting, with brethren forming a circle around a coffin, performing the Funeral Grand Honors, and exclaiming 'Alas! alas! alas!'
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0109 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Circuit

This section, titled "Third Circuit," details a solemn ritualistic exchange among lodge officers and the Chaplain, reflecting on the transient nature of life, the inevitability of death, and the sorrows of mortality. It progresses through lamentations of human frailty and grief, balanced by expressions of faith in God's justice, mercy, and the promise of eternal life and resurrection. The ritual includes prayers for strength, wisdom, and comfort for the bereaved, culminating in a collective affirmation of hope and trust in divine beneficence, often associated with a Lodge of Sorrow.

This section describes a solemn Masonic ceremony, likely a Lodge of Sorrow, where the Master reflects on the inevitability of death and the transient nature of life. The Chaplain reads from 1 Corinthians 15, discussing the resurrection of the dead and the transformation from corruptible to incorruptible bodies. A choir sings from Job 19 about seeing God after death. The Orator is then called upon to recount the virtues of the deceased, with an emphasis on Masonic charity and non-judgment.

This section details the 'Third Circuit' of a Masonic memorial service, which includes scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job, a choir performance, and an oration emphasizing truth and Masonic Charity towards the deceased. The ritual proceeds with the Junior Warden sprinkling corn, the Senior Warden sprinkling wine, and the Master sprinkling perfumed oil on the coffin, each accompanied by symbolic interpretations related to resurrection, spiritual refreshment, and consolation. The ceremony concludes with the Master placing a lambskin apron and holding a white glove on the coffin, symbolizing innocence, purity, and the hope of a welcome reception in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

This section details the ritualistic farewell for a deceased Master Mason, highlighting the symbolism of the white glove as an emblem of Fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, and the sprig of evergreen as an emblem of Faith in the immortality of the soul. It describes the Master's address, the placement of the evergreen on the coffin, a collective farewell by the brethren, and a three-circuit procession around the coffin accompanied by Grand Honors.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0170 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Vii. Certain Great Principles

This section outlines the fundamental principles of Freemasonry, emphasizing the universality of the Brotherhood, founded upon established Landmarks. It details the Right of Visitation for Masons in good standing to any Lodge, provided harmony is not disturbed. Masonry is presented as non-sectarian and non-political, teaching primitive truths of religion, obedience to civil law, equality among members, and universal brotherhood. It stresses that all Masons stand upon the level, with the best being defined by their work and ability to agree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0170 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0177 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Proclamation

This section details a public proclamation made by a Masonic authority, typically the Grand Master, during a ceremony such as the laying of a foundation stone. The proclamation asserts that Masons are lawful, fear God as the Great Architect, practice universal benevolence, and possess lawful and honorable secrets. It highlights the enduring and respected nature of the Fraternity and concludes by declaring the foundation stone to be properly laid according to ancient Masonic customs, verified by the square, level, and plumb.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0177 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0189 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master & the Lodge

This section details the essential qualifications and extensive duties of a Worshipful Master, emphasizing moral uprightness, leadership, and the preservation of lodge harmony. It outlines responsibilities including directing lodge activities, ensuring adherence to Grand Lodge laws, fostering communication, promoting Masonic education, managing finances, and maintaining proper behavior within the lodge. The Master's authority to exclude visitors or members and accountability to the Grand Lodge are also highlighted.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0189 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0006 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Masons.

This section outlines the physical layout of a Masonic Lodge, including ritualistic stops, rooms, doors, and the stations of various officers. It details the procedural aspects of conducting lodge business, emphasizing that all significant Masonic business, such as balloting for candidates and receiving petitions, is conducted in the Master Mason Degree. It describes the process of character inquiry by a committee and the balloting procedure. The text also clarifies the progression through the degrees, using terms like 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' and specifies attendance restrictions based on degree. It defines a 'Blue Lodge' and describes the symbolic designations of the three Craft degrees as the Ground Floor, Middle Chamber, and Sanctum Sanctorum, relating them to King Solomon's Temple. Finally, it provides a practical guide for brethren entering a Lodge already at work, including the Tyler's role and the specific knocking signals for each degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0006 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0035 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation for the Third Section Drama

This section details the elaborate preparation for the dramatic climax of the Master Mason degree. It involves specific props, clearing the lodge, dressing the candidate with a Senior Warden's jewel, and a deliberate deception to make him believe the ceremony is complete. The Worshipful Master then reveals that the candidate is not yet a Master Mason and must undergo severe trials to prove his fidelity, emphasizing self-reliance and trust in a higher power. The candidate is hoodwinked, prays, and then embarks on a symbolic journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor who introduces the legend of Hiram Abiff and his daily ritual at King Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0064 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Funeral Dirge and Prayer

This section details a Masonic funeral dirge and prayer ritual, focusing on the allegorical raising of a deceased Master Mason. The ceremony involves brethren forming a circle around the body, the Master making the grand hailing sign of distress, and a declaration that the Master's word is lost. Attempts by the Junior and Senior Wardens to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips fail due to the body's state of putrefaction. The ritual includes a solemn dirge sung while marching with the sun, and concludes with a prayer recited by the Master, emphasizing themes of mortality and hope for a 'Lodge on High'.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0064 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0079 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section details the symbolic representation of the candidate as Grand Master Hiram Abiff within the Master Mason degree. It recounts the narrative of Hiram Abiff's assassination by three Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) at the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple. The motive for the murder was to extort the secrets of a Master Mason. The narrative describes the sequence of events, including the three blows with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, the subsequent burial of the body, and the discovery of Hiram's absence by King Solomon, leading to the identification of the missing Craftsmen and the recantation of twelve conspirators.

This section details the discovery of Hiram Abiff's absence, marked by no designs on the trestle-board. King Solomon orders a search, leading to the identification of three missing Craftsmen: Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, suspected of his murder. Twelve Fellow Crafts, having recanted their initial murderous designs, confess their guilt and are tasked with finding the ruffians. After an initial search near the port of Joppa yields no results, a second, more urgent search is commanded. During this search, one brother discovers a grave marked by an acacia. Concurrently, another party apprehends the ruffians, who are overheard uttering self-imprecations. They are brought before King Solomon, convicted, and executed according to their own curses.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0079 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Exaltation Ceremony

The Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony details the admission process for candidates seeking further light in Masonry. After an alarm at the Chapter door, candidates, who have previously attained the degrees of Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master, confirm their free will, preparation, and proficiency. They present a pass word, RABBONI, before being admitted under a 'Living Arch' formed by companions. This arch involves physical prostration and difficulty in passage. Candidates then kneel at the altar for their seventh obligation, a solemn oath covering secrecy, protection of companions, non-revelation of ineffable characters or the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word (except under specific conditions), avoidance of clandestine chapters, and strict adherence to procedural requirements such as exalting exactly three candidates at a time and requiring nine Royal Arch Masons to open a Chapter.

This section details the extensive obligation of a Royal Arch Mason, covering secrecy, prerequisites for candidates (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master), rules for exaltation (three candidates at a time, nine Masons for opening a Chapter), conduct towards fellow Companions (no speaking evil, apprising of danger, preference in employment, assistance in difficulty, keeping secrets, aiding the indigent), and adherence to constitutional laws and by-laws. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation. Following the obligation, the ritual describes the candidate kissing the book seven times and the Principal Sojourner's address, emphasizing the importance of the Royal Arch secrets and the necessity of trials through 'rough and rugged ways' to test fidelity. The candidates are then led around the Chapter and kneel for a prayer to the 'Supreme and inscrutable Architect of universal Nature'.

This section describes a prayer offered during the Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony, emphasizing gratitude for human reason, capacity for improvement, and the institution of Freemasonry. It highlights new obligations of virtue and holiness, reverence for God's name, and the diligent search for divine authority in nature. The prayer concludes with a plea for devotion, love, and charity, and for heavenly grace to assist in rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' was destroyed by sin. Following the prayer, the Principal Sojourner leads candidates around the Chapter, reading the first six verses of Exodus chapter 3, which describe Moses encountering the burning bush on Mount Horeb. The candidates are then brought before a representation of the burning bush, and their blindfolds are removed.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
practical_elements: Masonic Relief can be applied for by any Master Mason to his own Lodge or an individual Master Mason.
The individual asked for relief has the right to determine the worthiness of the request and if aid can be granted without material injury to his family.
Relief is a voluntary function of both the Lodge and the individual.
If a Lodge cannot provide relief, it can apply to the Grand Lodge Charitable Foundation on the Master Mason's behalf.
Masonic Visitation requires proving oneself to be a Mason in good standing.
A Master Mason can sit in another Lodge if no member objects.
To attend another Lodge in Ohio, a current dues card and photo ID should be presented.
Admission to another Lodge can also be gained by avouchment from a Brother who has previously sat in Lodge with the visitor.
The Masonic Funeral Service is conducted only at the request of a Master Mason in good standing or a member of his immediate family.
The choice for a Masonic Funeral Service belongs to the family, not the Lodge.
Entered Apprentices and Fellow Crafts may receive a Masonic Funeral with the permission of the Grand Master.
The Masonic Funeral Service can be held in a house of worship, Lodge room, funeral home, or cemetery.
The attentive ear receives the sound from the instructive tongue.
Mysteries of Freemasonry are safely lodged in the repository of faithful breasts.
All Masons, from Entered Apprentice to Worshipful Master, should humbly bow with reverence to Deity.
The temple was founded in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, on the second day of the month Zif.
It was located on Mount Moriah, near where Abraham was to offer Isaac and where David appeased the destroying angel.
Construction took over seven years, during which it reportedly did not rain in the daytime.
No sound of axe, hammer, or iron tool was heard during construction.
The temple was supported by 1453 pillars and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
The workforce included 3 Grand Masters, 3300 Masters/Overseers, 80000 Fellow Crafts, and 70000 Entered Apprentices.
Two celebrated pillars were placed in front of the temple's porch, one on the left and one on the right.
Only Master Masons in good standing are entitled to a Masonic funeral.
A Masonic burial requires a request from the deceased or their family, communicated to the Master of the Lodge.
Entered Apprentices and Fellow Crafts are not entitled to a Masonic funeral.
Foreigners and sojourners who are known to be Master Masons in good standing should be given funeral honors.
The Master of the Lodge, upon being informed of the funeral time, summons the Lodge.
Members have a duty to attend the burial of a brother.
The Master may invite other Lodges, but the ceremony must be under the direction of the deceased's Lodge.
The Lodge is opened on the Third Degree in Masonry in due form.
The Lodge proceeds to the funeral and interment locations.
The Lodge is not called from labor to refreshment during the service.
After interment, the Lodge returns to its hall and is closed in due form.
The funeral director places the casket.
The minister concludes their services.
The Master, hat removed, takes position at the head of the grave.
The Chaplain is on the Master's right.
The Senior Warden is at the foot of the grave.
The Junior Warden is at the center of the grave, to the left of the Master.
Officers wear their jewels.
Brethren gather behind the Master or in a convenient place, wearing Aprons.
The Lodge service begins with a prayer.
The prayer is offered by the Master, Chaplain, or a designated Brother.
All respond with 'So mote it be' after the prayer.
The Master delivers an exhortation.
The Master presents and deposits the lamb-skin or White Apron upon the coffin.
The Master holds and deposits the evergreen in the grave.
Only the Three Principal officers conducting the service place the sprig of Acacia upon the coffin, each saying, "Farewell my Brother."
All Masons present join in giving the Funeral Grand Honors.

Funeral Grand Honors involve crossing both arms on the breast (left uppermost, palms touching shoulders), raising them above the head (palms inward, looking upward), and then extending them toward the ground with the head bowed.

During the Funeral Grand Honors, brethren audibly pronounce: "We cherish his memory here" (arms crossed), "We commend his spirit to God, who gave it" (hands above head), and "And consign his body to the earth, whence it came" (hands extended to ground).
The Lodge should practice the mode of giving the Funeral Grand Honors before leaving its hall, with each brother required to pronounce the words audibly.
The Chaplain or Senior Warden recites a passage from scripture: "Despise not thou the chastening of the Lord..."
The Master calls for devout prayer to the heavenly Father.
The Master or Chaplain leads a prayer acknowledging the passing of the brother, asking for divine assistance, and seeking comfort for the bereaved.
All Masons respond with "So mote it be" after the prayer and the Master's final blessing.
The Master pronounces a final blessing: "The Lord bless thee, and keep thee..."
Singing and holding the open Bible by the Chaplain are optional at the Master's discretion.
A funeral service at the grave of a deceased brother is warranted only at the time of burial.
A Lodge of Sorrow should be held within a reasonable time after a brother's death, but only if performing usual funeral ceremonies at the time of interment is impracticable.
The Lodge of Sorrow service may be performed either in public or in private.
The Lodge should be opened in a convenient place, and members should march in procession to the room where ceremonies are to be performed.
The ceremony room should be appropriately draped in black, with the altar and stations also covered with black emblems of mourning.
A coffin, placed upon an elevation of convenient height, should stand between the altar and the Senior Warden’s station.
The three altar lights should be in position but extinguished.
If the service is performed in a church, the altar lights should be placed on the east, south, and west of the coffin.
In a church, the coffin should stand at right angles with the aisles.
The Holy Bible, Square and Compasses should be placed upon a pedestal, at a convenient distance from the foot of the coffin.
Funeral service at grave only at time of burial; modern practice is an innovation.
Lodge of Sorrow to be held within a reasonable time after death if usual funeral ceremonies at interment are impracticable.
Service may be performed in public or private.
Lodge should be opened in a convenient place.
Brethren march in procession to the ceremony room.
Ceremony room should be appropriately draped in black.
Altar and stations to be covered with black emblem of mourning.
A coffin, placed on an elevation, should stand between the altar and Senior Warden's station.
Three altar lights should be in position but extinguished.
If in a church, altar lights placed east, south, and west of the coffin, which stands at right angles with aisles.
Holy Bible, Square, and Compasses placed on a pedestal near the foot of the coffin.
Decorations may vary according to taste and circumstances.
Guard of honor, composed of brethren of equal Masonic office with the deceased, to sit on each side of the coffin.
Organ and choir should be present if possible.
Apron of the deceased and a pair of white gloves placed near the coffin, out of sight but convenient for use.
Brethren should wear black clothes, white gloves, and aprons.
Ceremonies begin with an organ voluntary, timed to conclude as the procession enters.
Brethren enter slowly in formation, passing around the room three times, keeping the center to the right.
Master begins reading as the head of the procession enters and his voice can be heard.
Dialogue between Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain.
Procession halts.
Brethren form a circle around a coffin, facing inward.
Master stands at the head of the coffin.
Brethren give the Funeral Grand Honors once.
All exclaim: 'Alas! alas! alas!'
Officers (Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, Chaplain) recite specific lines.
Brethren halt and face inward.
Honors are repeated.
Anthem is chanted by choir or read by Chaplain.
Brethren retain positions during anthem.
Master implores the Father in Heaven.
Chaplain repeats a prayer.
All brethren kneel during prayer.
Brethren respond with "So mote it be."
Officers resume stations and brethren their places after prayer.
Choir chants "The Lord is my shepherd."
Master delivers an address reflecting on mortality and the transient nature of life.
Chaplain reads a lesson from the Holy Scriptures (1 Corinthians 15:35-57) concerning the resurrection of the dead.
Choir sings a passage from Job 19:25-27.
Orator is instructed to recount the virtues and good deeds of the deceased Brother(s).
Assembly gathers to pay honors to fallen Brother(s) and learn lessons from death.
Reading from 1 Corinthians 15:35-57.
Choir singing from Job 19:25-27.
Orator delivers an address.
Junior Warden approaches the coffin and sprinkles a few grains of corn upon it.
One of the Deacons lights an altar light while the Junior Warden speaks.
Senior Warden approaches the coffin and sprinkles a few drops of wine upon it.
One of the Deacons lights another altar light while the Senior Warden speaks.
Master approaches the coffin and sprinkles a few drops of perfumed oil upon it.
Brethren are called up to form around the coffin.
Master holds a lambskin apron (or the apron usually worn by the deceased).
The apron is laid upon the coffin.
Master holds a white glove.
The Master holds a sprig of evergreen.
The sprig of evergreen is laid upon the coffin.
The Master calls up the brethren.
All brethren collectively say a farewell.
A procession performs three circuits around the coffin, moving by the west, north, east, and south.
Grand Honors are given before each circuit, with exclamations omitted.
A reading is performed during the procession.
A Mason in good standing has the Right of Visitation to any Lodge, provided it does not disturb the Lodge's work or harmony.
Every Mason is considered equal to every other in the Lodge, standing upon the level.
Brethren and friends assemble to behold the ceremony.
A proclamation is made by the Grand Master or his authority.
A foundation stone is laid for a building.
The foundation stone is verified as square, level, and plumb, true and trusty.
A benediction is pronounced by the Grand Chaplain.
The program may be interspersed with music or arranged by the Grand Lodge.
A man must be declared "worthy and well qualified" before being made a Mason, and similarly for aspiring to the office of Worshipful Master.
A Worshipful Master must be moral and upright before God, of good repute, strictly obey the moral law, have served the Lodge well, and be a true example of Masonic spirit.
The Worshipful Master should be a leader of men with respect and confidence to direct Lodge activities.
The most important duty of a Worshipful Master is to preserve the harmony of his Lodge.
The Worshipful Master may, in the exercise of his judgment, exclude any visitor or member from participating in a meeting or appearing in public with the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master is responsible to the Grand Lodge for any abuse of power.
The Worshipful Master must provide the Lodge with direction towards established goals consistent with the Lodge Mission.
The Worshipful Master must be punctual and prepared for all Masonic activities.
The Worshipful Master must exercise firm but kind control over all Lodge activities.
The Worshipful Master must follow the Order of Business (referencing the Blue Lodge Text-Book).
The Worshipful Master must preside over and conduct all Masonic business in an orderly, courteous, and harmonious manner, in strict conformity to the Laws, Regulations, and Edicts of the Grand Lodge.
The Worshipful Master must be well-studied in The Williams Digest of Laws, which includes the Constitution, Rules, and General Laws of the Grand Lodge of Mississippi, Free and Accepted Masons.
The Worshipful Master must ensure proper and effective communication with all Lodge members, family members, Entered Apprentices, Fellowcrafts, and candidates regarding appropriate Lodge activities.
The Worshipful Master must ensure Lodge members do not lack in Masonic education or proficiency in the adopted ritual, planning and announcing schedules for practice and degree work.
The Worshipful Master must develop and implement an annual program and budget plan for the Lodge with assistance from Lodge officers and membership.
The Worshipful Master must ensure all Lodge committees are properly governed and facilitated, appointing and assigning Lodge duties to capable and well-qualified leaders within the lodge.
The Worshipful Master must be in possession or show definite inclination to possess the adopted work and lectures assigned to the Worshipful Master.
The Worshipful Master must enforce an atmosphere for proper Lodge behavior and membership expectations.
Candidate prays.
First stop, Second stop, Third stop in the ritual.
Room where candidates are prepared.
Ante-room where members enter the lodge.
Hall.
Doors.
Door through which candidates are admitted into the lodge.
Door through which members enter.
Altar.
Officers: Treasurer, Secretary, Senior Deacon, Worshipful Master, Junior Warden, Stewards (17 and 18), Senior Warden, Junior Deacon, Tyler.
All business relative to Masonry is done at a 'regular' meeting, specifically in the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are allowed to be present at business meetings.
Balloting for candidates, receiving petitions, and committee reports are generally done on a 'regular' meeting.
A petition for degrees is received at a 'regular' meeting.
The Master appoints a committee of three to inquire into the applicant's character.
The committee reports good or bad at the next regular meeting.
A ballot is held after the committee's report.
No black balls mean the candidate is elected; one or more black balls mean rejection.
No business is done in an Entered Apprentices' Lodge except to initiate a candidate to the First Degree.
No business is done in a Fellow Crafts' Lodge except to pass a Fellow Craft from the first to the second degree.
A candidate initiated to the First Degree is styled 'entered'.
A candidate who has taken the Second Degree is styled 'passed'.
A candidate who has taken the Third Degree is styled 'raised' to the sublime Degree of a Master Mason.
No one is allowed to be present in any degree of Masonry unless they are of that same degree or higher.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge'.
No other officer wears a hat in a 'Blue Lodge'.
A 'Blue Lodge' is a Lodge of Master Masons where only three degrees are conferred: Entered Apprentice (1st), Fellow Craft (2d), Master Mason (3d).
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons consists of five officers (Worshipful Master, Senior and Junior Wardens, Senior and Junior Deacons), with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
Three Master Masons are the requisite number to constitute a Masters' Lodge, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.
All Lodges meet in one room, alike furnished, for conferring different degrees.
The Tyler's duty is to inform brethren on what degree the Lodge is at work, especially late arrivals.
Brethren arriving late come to the ante-room, where the Tyler is stationed with a sword.
Late brethren inquire of the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is opened, put on an apron, and request entry.
The Tyler gives one rap (•) if the Lodge is opened on the First Degree.
The Tyler gives two raps (• •) if the Lodge is opened on the Second Degree.
The Tyler gives three raps (• • •) if the Lodge is opened on the Third Degree.
The Junior Deacon reports the alarm at the inner door to the Worshipful Master.
Putting off the covering of the feet as a sign of reverence, paralleling Eastern customs.
Candidate avows belief and trust in God as the sole basis for admission; refusal leads to rejection.
Chief officer expresses satisfaction upon a candidate's declaration of trust in God.
If more than one candidate is initiated at once, they are required to take hold of each other's arms.
Up to five candidates can be initiated as Entered Apprentices at once.
Up to five candidates can be 'passed' to the Second Degree at once.
Only one candidate at a time can be 'raised' to the Master's Degree.
Small Lodges may only manage one candidate at a time conveniently.
In some Lodges, at the words 'throat cut across,' a member or conductor draws their hand across the candidate's throat, or sometimes draws a sword.
The Master, assisted by the Senior Warden, lays the chief corner-stone of a fabric.
Lodge labors are conducted to produce an exciting spirit of emulation.
The Worshipful Master personally requests each individual to give an opinion on a specific doctrine or ceremony from the Chair.
Production of six strong loops and a buckskin bag stuffed with hair.
Removal of altar, lights, and Second Degree pillars.
Candidate dressed with apron (right-hand corner tucked up) and a yoke with a Senior Warden's jewel.
Friends congratulate the candidate upon his Masonic appearance, asking if he is 'through' to mislead him.
Worshipful Master gives one rap with gavel.
Junior Warden gives one rap, steps to desk, and turns small column down.
Senior Warden steps to stand and turns column up.
Brethren and candidate take seats.
Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, and Secretary engage in a deceptive dialogue about closing the Lodge, making the Master Mason sign.
Candidate is called to approach the East.
Conductor (Senior Deacon) leads candidate to the Master's seat in the East.
Conductor takes position behind candidate, holding or concealing a hoodwink.
Worshipful Master questions candidate about his status as a Master Mason, then reveals he is not yet one and must face trials, instructing him to pray for himself.
Candidate kneels, and Conductor ties a hoodwink closely over his eyes.
Candidate prays orally or mentally, signifying completion by saying 'Amen'.
Lodge-room darkened by turning down gaslights or lamps.
Conductor assists candidate to arise and leads him three times around the room, traveling with the sun.
Conductor narrates Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple at high twelve for designs and devotions, then retiring at the south gate.
Brethren form a circle around the body, with the Master and Wardens at the head.
The Master makes the sign of 'distress' of a Master Mason by raising both hands and arms above the head.
The Master says: 'O Lord my God, I fear the Master's word is forever lost!'
Alternatively, in some Lodges, the Master gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body, thumb inward.
The party marches around the body with the sun, singing a funeral dirge.
An organ or melodeon may be played in a solemn manner.
The Master (as King Solomon) makes the 'grand hailing sign of distress' (possibly twice) accompanied by the exclamation: 'O Lord my God, I fear the Master's word is forever lost!'
The Junior Warden attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice grip, letting his hand slip off, and reports failure due to putrefaction.
The Senior Warden attempts to raise the body using the Fellow Craft's grip, letting his hand slip off, and reports failure due to the flesh cleaving from the bone.
The Master gives the grand hailing sign of distress three times, exclaiming: 'O Lord my God! O Lord my God!! O Lord my God!!! Is there no hope for the widow's son?'
All brethren kneel around the body on one knee.
The Master kneels at the head of the candidate, removes his hat, and recites a prayer.
Candidate kneels in the center of the Lodge to implore blessings of Deity.
Candidate is accosted by three Fellow Crafts who demand Master Mason secrets.
Candidate receives blows with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul.
Candidate is ordered to be returned and reinvested.
Hiram Abiff's usual practice at high twelve: entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three murderers (Jubela, Jubelo, Jubelum) position themselves at the south, west, and east gates.
Hiram Abiff attempts to retire at the south, then west, then east gates, receiving a blow at each.
The body is buried in the rubbish of the Temple until low twelve.
The body is carried a westerly course to a hill west of Mount Moriah and buried six feet perpendicular, with an acacia planted at the head.
King Solomon orders a search for Hiram Abiff.
King Solomon orders the rolls of workmen to be called.
Twelve Fellow Crafts appear before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons.
King Solomon orders the twelve recanted Fellow Crafts to divide into parties and travel east, west, north, and south to search for the murderers.
Absence of designs on the trestle-board as a sign of Hiram's disappearance
Roll-call of workmen to identify missing Craftsmen
Twelve Fellow Crafts clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of innocence
Division of search parties (three east, three west, three north, three south)
Inquiry with a seafaring man near the port of Joppa
Discovery of a grave marked by an acacia
Overhearing of ruffians' exclamations from rock clefts
Seizure, binding, and presentation of ruffians before King Solomon
Execution of ruffians according to their self-imprecations
Seven distinct knocks are given at the Chapter door.
The Captain of the Host raps nine times, answered by the Principal Sojourner with three times three, then one rap, which the Captain answers with one.
The Principal Sojourner whispers the word RABBONI as a pass.
Candidates are led by a rope upon entry.
Companions form a 'Living Arch' by creating two lines facing each other and locking fingers.
As candidates pass under the Living Arch, companions place knuckles on their necks and backs, kneading them and prostrating candidates on the floor.
Candidates kneel at the altar to receive the obligation.
The obligation is the seventh taken by the candidate in their Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken only in the presence of three Royal Arch Masons (including the speaker), by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltation of candidates must be exactly three at one time.
A Chapter of Royal Arch Masons requires nine Royal Arch Masons to form or open.
Obligation taken in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, candidate making one.
Secrets received by 'three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
Candidate must answer and obey all due signs and summonses within the length of their cable-tow.
Candidate kisses the book seven times.
Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter.
Candidates kneel for a prayer read by the Principal Sojourner.
A prayer is recited during the ceremony.
Candidates are led once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads the first six verses of the third chapter of Exodus.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Candidates are directed to halt in front of the burning bush representation.
Bandages (blindfolds) are removed from the candidates' eyes by the Principal Sojourner.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The survival of Freemasonry despite the destruction of Solomon's Temple symbolizes the enduring nature and resilience of the institution, suggesting that its true essence is not in physical structures but in its principles and the hearts of its members. Architectural tools and symbolic emblems are used to represent and imprint wise and serious truths, serving as a visual and conceptual language for moral and philosophical instruction.

The section illustrates hieroglyphical emblems and inculcates moral lessons. The two celebrated pillars at the temple entrance symbolize the happy deliverance of the Israelites from Egyptian bondage and commemorate the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud, which provided light and darkness respectively during the Exodus. Their placement ensured the event was continually before the eyes of those going to and from divine worship.

The ceremony symbolizes the dissolution of the 'earthly tabernacle' and the summoning of the spirit to the 'land where our fathers have gone before us.' It highlights the 'narrow house appointed for all living' as a reminder of the immutable certainty of death and the vanity of human pursuits. The 'cradle and the coffin stand very near to each other' signifies the immediate onset of dying upon birth. The exhortation encourages contemplation of mortality and the hope of eternal life and 'hidden manna' for those who live righteously.

The lamb-skin or White Apron symbolizes innocence, the badge of a Mason, and serves as a reminder of the universal dominion of Death. The mattock, coffin, and grave admonish Masons of their mortality and the eventual return of their bodies to dust. The evergreen is an emblem of faith in the immortality of the soul, reminding Masons of an immortal part that survives the grave and the hope for eternal spring through God's mercy and the merits of the Lion of the Tribe of Judah, leading to the resurrection of bodies as incorruptible as souls.

The use of black draping for the room, altar, and stations serves as an emblem of mourning. The extinguished altar lights symbolize the absence of life or light, fitting for a ceremony commemorating a deceased brother.

Black drapes covering the room, altar, and stations serve as an emblem of mourning, signifying respect and sorrow for the deceased brother.

The dialogue and actions symbolize the fragility and brevity of human life, the certainty of death, and the transient nature of earthly achievements. The 'houses of clay' represent the human body, whose foundation is dust. The coffin and funeral honors represent the ultimate end of mortal existence and prompt contemplation of immortality, a central theme in the Master Mason degree.

The "Third Circuit" symbolizes the journey of life, marked by inevitable suffering, loss, and the ultimate certainty of death. The lamentations represent the human experience of grief and the transient nature of earthly existence ("life is a vapor," "days are passed"). The responses from the Chaplain, particularly the references to a "tree planted by the waters" and "a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens," symbolize enduring faith, divine sustenance, and the promise of immortality and resurrection beyond the grave. The ritual as a whole serves as a memento mori, reminding participants of mortality while simultaneously offering comfort and hope through spiritual belief and the prospect of an afterlife.

Life is presented as 'death’s vestibule' and a 'journey to the grave,' symbolizing the transient nature of earthly existence. The comparison of human life to flowers and grass emphasizes fragility and impermanence. The 'valley of the shadow of death' symbolizes the journey through mortality with divine comfort. The scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the hope of resurrection, transformation from a natural to a spiritual body, and victory over death. The seed analogy in 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the transformation of the body after death into a new, spiritual form. The 'broad mantle of charity' symbolizes Masonic non-judgment and compassion towards the deceased.

The scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job symbolize resurrection, incorruption, transformation from mortal to immortal, and the belief in a Redeemer. The corn of nourishment symbolizes the resurrection of the body, sown in corruption and raised in incorruption, glory, power, and as a spiritual body. The wine of refreshment symbolizes spiritual refreshment, divine grace, blessings, and comfort for those afflicted or distressed. The oil of joy symbolizes comfort, consolation for the bereaved, healing of heart-wounds, the virtue of charity, increased affection, leniency, indulgence, forgiveness, and a joyful passage through death. The lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a Mason's badge, denotes the hope that the departed brother's soul, purified from imperfections, has found a welcome in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

The white glove is an emblem of Fidelity, symbolizing Masonic friendship and brotherly love, and signifies an increased obligation to the living brethren. The sprig of evergreen is an emblem of Masonic Faith in the immortality of the soul, representing the eternal principle in man that survives death and blooms perpetually. It denotes trust that the deceased's immortal spirit has entered a celestial temple and found acceptance with the Supreme Grand Master.

The phrase 'All stand upon the level' symbolizes the principle of equality among all Masons within the Lodge, regardless of external status or wealth.

The proclamation emphasizes that Masonic secrets are lawful, honorable, and inviolable, entrusted to Masons. The longevity and esteemed membership of the Fraternity symbolize its inherent goodness and honor. The verification of the foundation stone as 'square, level and plumb, true and trusty' symbolizes the integrity, precision, and adherence to foundational principles within Masonry, both in physical construction and moral conduct. God is referred to as the 'Great Architect of all things'.

The Fellow Craft Lodge is symbolically styled 'the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple,' representing a stage of intellectual and moral advancement. The Master Masons' Lodge is symbolically called 'the Sanctum Sanctorum, or, Holy of Holies of King Solomon's Temple,' signifying the most sacred and complete state of Masonic knowledge and authority. The three degrees are masonically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), representing a progression through different levels of understanding and spiritual significance within the Temple.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.

The deception regarding the completion of the degree symbolizes the unexpected challenges and deeper truths yet to be revealed in the Masonic journey. The Senior Warden's jewel, worn by the candidate, represents a temporary elevation or a test of his understanding of authority and responsibility. The 'rough and rugged road beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers' symbolizes the moral and spiritual dangers and trials one faces in life, requiring unwavering fidelity and trust. The act of praying for oneself emphasizes self-reliance and direct communion with the Deity. The journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor, represents a transition into the profound narrative of Hiram Abiff, symbolizing the search for light and knowledge amidst obscurity, and the emulation of a revered Grand Master's devotion and diligence.

The 'lost Master's word' symbolizes the loss of essential knowledge or the inability to restore life and truth through ordinary means. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body signifies that the knowledge and powers of these lesser degrees are insufficient to overcome death or restore what is lost. The repeated grand hailing sign of distress and exclamations convey profound grief, despair, and a plea for divine intervention in the face of mortality. The 'widow's son' is an allegorical reference to Hiram Abiff, central to the Master Mason degree, whose death and the search for his replacement word are key themes. The marching with the sun (circumambulation) represents the journey of life and the natural order.

The candidate symbolically represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff, who was slain just before the completion of King Solomon's Temple. The unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum represents the Master's Lodge. The demand for Master Mason secrets and Hiram's refusal symbolize fidelity and integrity. The planting of an acacia at the grave signifies remembrance and immortality.

The absence of designs on the trestle-board symbolizes a disruption of order and the loss of a master builder. The white gloves and aprons worn by the Fellow Crafts signify innocence and purity, even after their initial complicity. The acacia tree, discovered at the grave, is a powerful symbol of immortality and the enduring nature of the soul. The ruffians' self-imprecations symbolize the self-destructive nature of guilt and the just, inevitable consequences of treachery and murder, often interpreted as the penalties associated with Masonic obligations.

The 'Living Arch' symbolizes the difficult and humbling path to exaltation, requiring perseverance and submission. The physical prostration and kneading represent the trials and purification necessary for advancement. Kneeling for the seventh time at the altar signifies the increasing solemnity and binding nature of the obligations as one progresses through Masonic degrees. The phrase 'he that humbleth himself shall be exalted' directly links the physical act of stooping low under the arch to the spiritual lesson of humility leading to higher understanding. The journey through darkness and crooked paths made straight ('I will bring the blind by a way they know not...') suggests divine guidance and enlightenment provided through the Masonic path.

The 'rough and rugged ways' and 'many trials' symbolize the challenges and tests of fidelity a candidate must undergo to prove their worthiness for the deeper secrets of the Royal Arch Degree. The 'stupendous arch of heaven' mentioned in the prayer symbolizes the divine architectural power of the Supreme Architect of universal Nature.

The prayer symbolizes a deep adoration for divine perfection, gratitude for the gifts of reason and improvement, and a commitment to virtue and holiness. The 'second temple of reformation' represents the spiritual rebuilding of the individual's character after the loss of original purity. The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Master Masons learn about the fraternal support available to them through Masonic Relief, the importance of maintaining good standing for the privilege of Masonic Visitation, and the solemn tradition of a Masonic Funeral Service that respects personal religious beliefs while honoring a Brother's life. These rights underscore the principles of charity, fraternity, and respect within the Craft.

The candidate is taught that true worth in Freemasonry comes from internal character and moral qualifications, not outward appearances or worldly status. They learn the importance of diligently receiving and faithfully preserving Masonic knowledge and mysteries. The lesson also instills a profound sense of reverence and humility towards the Deity, a principle that unites all Masons regardless of their degree or station.

The section emphasizes the importance of peace and good fellowship among workmen, as exemplified by King Solomon's wise organization of the temple builders, which prevented envy, discord, or confusion. It also highlights the significance of divine guidance and deliverance, as symbolized by the temple's pillars, reminding candidates of historical and spiritual liberation.

Brethren are admonished to seriously consider the uncertainty of human life and the certainty of death, and to prepare for eternity. They are urged to maintain the dignified character of their profession, to evince faith through moral conduct, to hold bright hope for future mysteries, and to practice boundless charity. The lesson also includes the importance of faithfully discharging duties to God, neighbor, and self, and extending Masonic charity to the foibles of deceased brethren.

Masons are admonished to remember their mortality and the uncertainty of life, recognizing the transient value of worldly pursuits. The ceremony encourages them to earnestly obey divine laws, avoid dissensions, hatreds, and revenges, and to labor to do good to their fellow-men, aspiring to a spiritual existence beyond this life. It also teaches confidence in God's mercy and provides consolation for those grieving, emphasizing patience and resignation to divine will.

The candidate learns about the solemn and specific Masonic customs and regulations concerning the death of a brother, emphasizing the importance of adhering to ancient usage and Grand Lodge decisions regarding funeral services and the structured conduct of a Lodge of Sorrow. It underscores the fraternity's commitment to honoring its deceased members through prescribed rituals.

The lesson conveyed is to contemplate mortality, recognize the fleeting nature of life and worldly possessions, and understand the spiritual journey beyond earthly existence. It encourages reflection on the purpose of life in the face of inevitable death and the understanding that others will eventually 'reap the fruits of our toil and care'.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.

Candidates learn that Freemasonry is a universal brotherhood, transcending specific religions or political affiliations. They are taught the importance of obedience to civil law, the fundamental equality of all members, and the principle of brotherhood towards all people. The lesson emphasizes that true merit lies in one's actions and ability to foster harmony.

Candidates learn that Freemasonry is a lawful and honorable institution, whose members are devoted to God and country, and committed to universal benevolence. They are taught that the Craft possesses ancient, unrevealed secrets that are held in trust. The ceremony of laying a foundation stone demonstrates the importance of precision, integrity, and adherence to ancient customs in all Masonic endeavors.

The qualities and duties of a Worshipful Master serve as an example of the high standards and leadership expected within Freemasonry, implicitly guiding members towards personal and communal responsibility. It highlights the importance of moral conduct, dedication, and adherence to Masonic principles for those who aspire to lead and for all members in their conduct within the Lodge.

Candidates learn the importance of character and integrity through the rigorous inquiry process for petitioners. The progression through the degrees, marked by being 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' teaches a journey of continuous self-improvement and increasing knowledge. The caution regarding giving the correct sign emphasizes the importance of attentiveness, respect for the degree at which the Lodge is working, and adherence to proper protocol.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.

The candidate learns that the Masonic journey is not easily completed and requires profound fidelity and endurance. He is taught to trust in a higher power and to rely on his own spiritual strength, as he must pray for himself in the face of impending trials. The introduction to Hiram Abiff's daily devotions and diligent work instills the importance of consistent dedication, moral rectitude, and reverence for the Deity, setting an example for the candidate's own conduct. The experience also teaches humility, as his presumed status as a Master Mason is challenged, and he is reminded of the 'more valuable secrets' yet to be entrusted.

This ritual imparts lessons on the inevitability of mortality and the limitations of human power and knowledge in the face of death. It emphasizes the profound loss associated with the Master's word and the allegorical death of Hiram Abiff. The ceremony encourages contemplation of the afterlife and the hope for a spiritual 'Lodge on High,' reinforcing faith and the promise of immortality beyond earthly existence. It also highlights the distinct knowledge and significance of the Master Mason degree compared to the preceding degrees.

The narrative emphasizes the importance of fidelity to one's obligations and secrets, even unto death. It teaches about the consequences of avarice and betrayal, as seen in the actions of the three Fellow Crafts. The candidate learns about the ultimate sacrifice made for integrity and the pursuit of knowledge, and the eventual discovery and justice for wrongdoing.

The narrative teaches the severe consequences of treachery and the importance of fidelity to one's obligations. It underscores the concept of justice, where actions lead to fitting retribution. The persistent search for the ruffians and the discovery of the grave emphasize the enduring nature of truth and the eventual revelation of hidden deeds. The confession of the twelve Fellow Crafts highlights the possibility of redemption through acknowledging guilt and seeking pardon.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.
tradition_notes: Masonic Relief is a voluntary function of both the Lodge and the individual.
Masonic Visitation is considered one of the greatest privileges of being a Master Mason.
The Masonic Funeral Service is described as a beautiful and solemn ceremony that does not conflict with a man's personal religious beliefs.
The text references the historical and legendary destruction of Solomon's Temple, a significant event in Masonic tradition, and the subsequent survival of Freemasonry.

It describes the traditional method of transmitting Masonic mysteries verbally ('attentive ear receives the sound from the instructive tongue') and through memory ('safely lodged in the repository of faithful breasts').
Josephus is cited as an informant regarding the temple's construction duration and the absence of rain.
Sacred history is referenced for the detail that no tool sounds were heard during the temple's building.
The placement of the two pillars is presented as a tradition to memorialize the deliverance from Egyptian bondage and the pillars of fire and cloud.
The burial service was arranged by Frederic Speed, P. G. M., and adopted by the Grand Lodge for use in this jurisdiction, revised by the Board of Custodians of the Work in 2003.
The Grand Lodge has decided that the Master has the power to summon a Lodge for a funeral, making attendance a duty for members.
The Master removes his hat at the place of interment.
Officers wear their jewels during the grave service.
Brethren wear Aprons during the grave service.
The lamb-skin or White Apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The sprig of Acacia is placed upon the coffin only by the Three Principal officers conducting the service.
The Funeral Grand Honors are a specific ritualistic sequence of gestures and spoken words performed by all Masons present at a funeral service.
The ceremony includes specific prayers and blessings invoking the 'heavenly Father' and the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah'.
Ancient Masonic usage dictates that the funeral service at the grave is only performed at the time of burial.
A decision by Grand Master Barkley in 1875, reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge in 1883, established the ruling on the timing of funeral services at the grave.
Decision of Grand Master Barkley in 1875, reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge in 1883, regarding funeral service at the grave.
The 'First Circuit' refers to a specific ritualistic movement and dialogue within the degree ceremony.
The 'Funeral Grand Honors' are a traditional Masonic gesture performed during specific solemn occasions, particularly those related to death or remembrance.
The exclamation 'Alas! alas! alas!' is a traditional ritualistic expression of sorrow and lamentation.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
The Right of Visitation is a fundamental tradition allowing Masons in good standing to visit any Lodge.
Masons are true to the laws of their country.
Masons profess to fear God, the Great Architect of all things.
Masons practice universal benevolence to all mankind.
The Craft has existed for many centuries, indicating its enduring nature and honorable calling.
Ceremonies are conducted in accordance with the ancient customs of Freemasonry.
It is a Masonic tradition that a man must be declared 'worthy and well qualified' before being made a Mason.
The Grand Lodge of Mississippi operates under 'The Williams Digest of Laws, being the Constitution, Rules and General Laws of the Grand Lodge of Mississippi, Free and Accepted Masons'.
All business relative to Masonry is traditionally conducted at a 'regular' meeting, specifically within the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are permitted to be present at such business meetings.
Grand Lodges typically make arrangements for the regulation of their subordinate Lodges.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge', a privilege not extended to other officers.
A 'Blue Lodge' is defined as a Lodge of Master Masons where only the three Craft degrees (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) are conferred.
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons traditionally consists of five officers, with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
A Masters' Lodge requires three Master Masons to constitute it by Masonic Law, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.

The three degrees are symbolically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), referencing King Solomon's Temple.

The reverence indicated by putting off the covering of the feet is still prevalent in the East among Christians, Moslems, or pagans, where it is considered highly irreverent to enter a sacred place with feet covered.
Masonic traditions do not support the belief that figures like Adam, Nimrod, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Hiram, St. John the Evangelist, or St. John the Baptist belonged to the Masonic Order.
The custom of dressing the candidate with a rich apron and yoke in some Lodges.
The specific tying of the apron with the right-hand corner tucked up for a Master Mason.
The use of columns by the Wardens to signify refreshment or labor.

The Conductor's narrative introduces Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum at high twelve for drawing designs and offering devotions, then retiring at the south gate of the Temple. This is explicitly noted as the first time the candidate hears about Hiram Abiff.
The prayer recited by the Master at the grave is stated to be found in all Masonic Monitors.
There is a variation in the initial sign given by the Master at the body in some Lodges.
The narrative describes a core tradition of the Master Mason degree, recounting the legend of Hiram Abiff's murder and the subsequent events.
The use of specific tools (twenty-four-inch gauge, square, setting-maul) in the ritual is a traditional element.
The specific names of the ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, are traditional within the Hiramic legend.
The detail of the twelve Fellow Crafts confessing their premeditated guilt and being sent on a search is a traditional element.
The acacia tree is a traditional and significant symbol marking Hiram's grave.
The specific self-imprecations uttered by the ruffians are traditional and correspond to the penalties of Masonic obligations.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.
caution_notes: For Masonic Relief, the individual asked has the right to determine the worthiness of the request and whether aid can be granted without material injury to his family.
For Masonic Visitation, you must prove yourself to be a Mason in good standing, and any member of the Lodge you are visiting has the right to object to your sitting in the Lodge.
The Masonic Funeral Service is conducted only at the request of a Master Mason in good standing or some member of his immediate family; the choice belongs to the family, not to the Lodge.
Only Master Masons in good standing are entitled to a Masonic funeral.
No Master Mason can receive Masonic burial unless it is at his own request or that of his family.
Entered Apprentices and Fellow Crafts are not entitled to a Masonic funeral.
The Lodge is not called from labor to refreshment during the funeral service.
When the service is used in connection with that of the Knight Templar or the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite, all preceding parts of the ritual (before the note) should be omitted.
The Lodge should practice the mode of giving the Funeral Grand Honors before leaving its hall, ensuring each brother pronounces the words audibly.
Singing and holding the open Bible by the Chaplain are optional at the Master's discretion.

Reading the funeral service at the grave of a deceased brother is only warranted at the time of burial; any modern practice otherwise is considered an innovation, as per Grand Master Barkley's decision (1875), reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge (1883).
There is no law nor ancient Masonic usage that warrants reading a funeral service at the grave of a deceased brother, except at the time of burial; modern practice is an innovation.
Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.
Masonic secrets are concealed from the eyes of men, may not be revealed, and which no man has discovered.
The Master is responsible to the Grand Lodge for any abuse of power, as in all other cases.

Brethren arriving late must be informed by the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is at work to prevent them from giving the wrong sign to the Worshipful Master, especially if members of a lower degree are present.
It is unwise to assert more than can be proven and to argue against probability regarding historical figures being Freemasons.

The story of Hipparchus illustrates the terrible consequences of violating engagements, leading to expulsion, social death, and denial of burial rites, serving as a warning against breaches of truth and fidelity.
Disobedience or violation of Masonic engagements, particularly regarding temperance and decorum, carries a penalty.

The Worshipful Master explicitly warns the candidate of 'amazing trials and dangers', a 'rough and rugged road to travel, beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers', and the possibility of losing his life, stating it 'will not be the first instance of the kind'.
In some Lodges, the Master does not make the sign of distress first at the body, but only gives the sign of a Master Mason by drawing the right hand across the body with the thumb inward.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
