I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.

entries.source_heading: The Rights of a Master Mason

entries.title: The Responsibilities of a Master Mason
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the core responsibilities of a Master Mason, emphasizing the preservation of the Fraternity's reputation through leading a good life and upholding personal conduct. It highlights brotherhood as a cornerstone, advocating for respect, consideration, and non-judgmental support towards fellow Masons, encouraging actions that exemplify brotherly love.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the core responsibilities of a Master Mason, emphasizing the preservation of the Fraternity's reputation through leading a good life and upholding personal conduct. It highlights brotherhood as a cornerstone, advocating for respect, consideration, and non-judgmental support towards fellow Masons, encouraging actions that exemplify brotherly love.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0033 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Our Ancient Brethren

This section details the historical and contemporary dedications of Masonic Lodges, noting that ancient brethren dedicated their lodges to King Solomon, while modern lodges are dedicated to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist. It describes the significant emblem of a point within a circle, bordered by two perpendicular parallel lines representing the Saints John, with the Holy Scriptures resting on top. The symbolism of each component is explained: the point signifies an individual brother, the circle represents a moral boundary against passions and prejudices, and the parallel lines symbolize moral rectitude and the summer and winter solstices. The Holy Scriptures denote divine origin and inspiration. The text also states that lodges are traditionally situated East and West, mirroring King Solomon's Temple, and concludes by listing the principal tenets of Masonry: Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0033 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0034 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brotherly Love

Brotherly Love is a fundamental Masonic principle that teaches Masons to view all humanity as one family, created by a single Almighty Parent, who should aid, support, and protect one another. This principle enables Freemasonry to unite individuals from diverse countries, sects, and opinions, fostering true friendship among those who might otherwise remain distant.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0034 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Truth

Truth is presented as a divine attribute and the fundamental basis for all virtues. It is identified as the first lesson taught in Masonry, guiding members to regulate their conduct. Adherence to truth fosters sincerity, plain dealing, and mutual prosperity, eliminating hypocrisy and deceit. The text also alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0036 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0039 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Prudence

Prudence is defined as the habit of regulating one's life and actions according to reason, enabling wise judgment and determination for present and future happiness. It is a characteristic every Mason should possess, guiding their conduct both within the Lodge and in the wider world. Masons are specifically cautioned to exercise prudence in mixed company to prevent the unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets, signs, tokens, or words.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0039 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section, addressed to a newly initiated Brother A. B., congratulates him on joining the ancient and honorable fraternity of Freemasonry. It outlines the fundamental duties of a Mason towards God, his neighbor, and himself, emphasizing reverence, acting upon the square, and avoiding intemperance. It also details civic responsibilities, proper outward demeanor, the importance of lodge attendance without neglecting vocations, and the caution against arguing with those who ridicule the Institution. The text encourages seeking further Masonic knowledge from well-informed brethren and stresses the importance of carefully vetting potential candidates. Finally, it charges the new Mason to keep the mysteries of the Institution sacred and inviolable.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: First Section

This section introduces the core tenets of the Institution, stating that they are contained within the points of the compasses. These tenets are identified as Friendship, Morality, and Brotherly Love.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

The Second Section of the ritual recounts the Institution's historical traditions and presents an exemplary instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity. It emphasizes the importance of spreading brotherly love and affection, which serves as the cement uniting Masons into a sacred society where the only acceptable contention is a noble emulation to excel in work and agreement.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0093 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section outlines the significant duties, responsibilities, and moral obligations of a newly raised Master Mason. It emphasizes maintaining the fraternity's reputation, guiding less informed brethren, upholding ancient landmarks, and exemplifying virtues like morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, all while remaining faithful to one's trust and vows, mirroring the example of Hiram Abiff.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0093 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0104 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: By the Grand Lodge for Use in This Jurisdiction

This section details the regulations and procedures for Masonic funeral services and Lodges of Sorrow within a specific jurisdiction. It clarifies that reading a funeral service at the grave is only permissible at the time of burial, with other modern practices deemed an innovation based on Grand Lodge decisions from 1875 and 1883. It further outlines that a Lodge of Sorrow should be held when a traditional funeral ceremony at interment is impractical, describing the ceremonial setup including black draping, specific placement of a coffin, and the use of extinguished altar lights, whether in a lodge room or a church.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0104 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0108 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: By the Grand Lodge for Use in This Jurisdiction

This section outlines regulations and procedures for Masonic funeral services and Lodges of Sorrow within a specific jurisdiction. It clarifies that reading a funeral service at the grave, except at the time of burial, is an innovation not supported by ancient Masonic usage. It details the appropriate timing for a Lodge of Sorrow, suggesting it be held when a usual funeral ceremony at interment is impracticable. Furthermore, it provides specific instructions for conducting a Lodge of Sorrow, including room draping, coffin placement, altar lights, procession order, dress code for brethren (black clothes, white gloves, aprons), and the role of music and a guard of honor.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0108 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Circuit

This section, titled "Third Circuit," details a solemn ritualistic exchange among lodge officers and the Chaplain, reflecting on the transient nature of life, the inevitability of death, and the sorrows of mortality. It progresses through lamentations of human frailty and grief, balanced by expressions of faith in God's justice, mercy, and the promise of eternal life and resurrection. The ritual includes prayers for strength, wisdom, and comfort for the bereaved, culminating in a collective affirmation of hope and trust in divine beneficence, often associated with a Lodge of Sorrow.

This section describes a solemn Masonic ceremony, likely a Lodge of Sorrow, where the Master reflects on the inevitability of death and the transient nature of life. The Chaplain reads from 1 Corinthians 15, discussing the resurrection of the dead and the transformation from corruptible to incorruptible bodies. A choir sings from Job 19 about seeing God after death. The Orator is then called upon to recount the virtues of the deceased, with an emphasis on Masonic charity and non-judgment.

This section details the 'Third Circuit' of a Masonic memorial service, which includes scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job, a choir performance, and an oration emphasizing truth and Masonic Charity towards the deceased. The ritual proceeds with the Junior Warden sprinkling corn, the Senior Warden sprinkling wine, and the Master sprinkling perfumed oil on the coffin, each accompanied by symbolic interpretations related to resurrection, spiritual refreshment, and consolation. The ceremony concludes with the Master placing a lambskin apron and holding a white glove on the coffin, symbolizing innocence, purity, and the hope of a welcome reception in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

This section details the ritualistic farewell for a deceased Master Mason, highlighting the symbolism of the white glove as an emblem of Fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, and the sprig of evergreen as an emblem of Faith in the immortality of the soul. It describes the Master's address, the placement of the evergreen on the coffin, a collective farewell by the brethren, and a three-circuit procession around the coffin accompanied by Grand Honors.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Musical Selection

This section details the 'Musical Selection' portion of a Masonic gathering, which includes a Chaplain's prayer invoking divine blessing for the proceedings and for those about to assume leadership roles. Following the prayer, the Marshal delivers an address explaining the significance of observing the Festival of St. John the Evangelist. The Marshal emphasizes that Freemasonry's purpose extends beyond social enjoyment, focusing on duty, reconsecration through solemn promises, and the propagation of core principles. The discourse highlights Freemasonry's historical influence, its role in promoting liberty, equality, fraternity, law, and order, and its teachings on the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, and the importance of loving God and neighbor. It also stresses the Craft's commitment to elevating humanity, stimulating patriotism, encouraging liberal arts and sciences, promoting education, and upholding the dignity of labor, while remaining aloof from specific creeds.

The text explains the purpose of the Craft and announces the upcoming induction of chosen brethren into their respective offices to preside over the Lodge for the next twelve months.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master

This section details the installation ceremony of a Worshipful Master, beginning with the Master's assent to fifteen Ancient Charges and Regulations that outline moral conduct, civic duties, obedience to Masonic law, and lodge governance. The ceremony proceeds with the investiture of the Master with the collar and the presentation of key Masonic implements and documents: the Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, Lodge Charter, Grand Lodge Regulations, By-Laws, and the Holy Writings. Each item is accompanied by its symbolic meaning and practical instructions for the Master's responsibilities in governing the Lodge.

This section describes a procedural sequence during a lodge installation, where Grand Honors are given, the Worshipful Master seats the Lodge, and an appropriate ode is sung, with an exception noted for public installations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0142 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brothers Senior and Junior Wardens

This section addresses the Senior and Junior Wardens, emphasizing their deep understanding of Masonic principles and their expected exemplary conduct. They are tasked with assisting the Master, diffusing light, and imparting knowledge to those under their care. The text highlights their crucial role in succession, ensuring the Craft's continuous instruction, and anticipates their future conduct will earn the applause of their brethren and a good conscience.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0142 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0150 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Charge to the Grand Treasurer

The charge to the Grand Treasurer outlines the duties of the office, which include receiving and recording all moneys from the Grand Secretary and disbursing them under the order of the Grand Master and consent of the Grand Lodge. The charge emphasizes that the election to this office signifies the brethren's high opinion of the officer's fidelity and discretion, and their expectation that regard for the Fraternity will ensure faithful duty.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0150 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0175 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Proclamation

This section describes a formal proclamation made by the Grand Master to an assembly of brethren and friends during a ceremony, likely a cornerstone laying. The proclamation affirms the Masons' adherence to the laws of their country, their fear of God as the Great Architect, their commitment to benefiting brethren, and practicing universal benevolence. Following this, the Grand Master delivers the Square, Level, Plumb, and the building plan to the Architect, entrusting them with the superintendence and direction of the work, expressing confidence in their skill to ensure the building rises in order, harmony, and beauty, fulfilling its purpose to the credit and honor of all involved.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0175 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0184 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Challenge of Leadership

The text discusses the critical role of the Worshipful Master in maintaining high brethren interest in the Lodge. It emphasizes that a Lodge cannot thrive solely on degree conferrals, as ritual is only a vehicle for Masonic lessons. To engage members, especially those with limited time for ritual perfection, meetings must offer diverse content including history, landmarks, regulations, symbols, fraternalism, and companionship. The Master is advised to hold regular meetings with officers to discuss challenges, priorities, and foster collaborative decision-making, recognizing that leadership is a shared responsibility for the growth and prosperity of Freemasonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0184 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0185 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: General Duties & Responsibilities

This section introduces the general duties and responsibilities expected within Freemasonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0185 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0189 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master & the Lodge

This section details the essential qualifications and extensive duties of a Worshipful Master, emphasizing moral uprightness, leadership, and the preservation of lodge harmony. It outlines responsibilities including directing lodge activities, ensuring adherence to Grand Lodge laws, fostering communication, promoting Masonic education, managing finances, and maintaining proper behavior within the lodge. The Master's authority to exclude visitors or members and accountability to the Grand Lodge are also highlighted.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0189 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0192 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Senior Warden

The Senior Warden is the second principal officer of the Lodge, responsible for assisting the Worshipful Master, promoting harmony among brethren, and reconciling differences. He is expected to prepare himself for the Master's station by planning the Lodge's program for his future term and must reflect the principles of the fraternity in his own life. In the absence of the Worshipful Master, he serves as the senior principal officer at all Lodge communications and activities.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0192 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0006 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Masons.

This section outlines the physical layout of a Masonic Lodge, including ritualistic stops, rooms, doors, and the stations of various officers. It details the procedural aspects of conducting lodge business, emphasizing that all significant Masonic business, such as balloting for candidates and receiving petitions, is conducted in the Master Mason Degree. It describes the process of character inquiry by a committee and the balloting procedure. The text also clarifies the progression through the degrees, using terms like 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' and specifies attendance restrictions based on degree. It defines a 'Blue Lodge' and describes the symbolic designations of the three Craft degrees as the Ground Floor, Middle Chamber, and Sanctum Sanctorum, relating them to King Solomon's Temple. Finally, it provides a practical guide for brethren entering a Lodge already at work, including the Tyler's role and the specific knocking signals for each degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0006 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0056 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Master Mason Obligation

This section details the Master Mason Obligation ceremony, beginning with the candidate kneeling on naked knees with hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses at the altar. The Worshipful Master assures the candidate that the oath does not conflict with duties to God, neighbor, country, or self. The extensive obligation covers vows of secrecy, adherence to Masonic laws and Grand Lodge edicts, aid to distressed Master Masons, protection of a brother's secrets (with exceptions for murder and treason), prohibitions against initiating certain individuals (women, dotage, nonage, atheist, madman, fool), avoiding clandestine Masons, and refraining from harming or defrauding a brother. Specific rules are given for the Grand Masonic word and the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation and the candidate's request for 'further light in Masonry,' followed by the removal of the hoodwink and cable-tow, and the display of the duegard of a Master Mason.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0056 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Preparation for the Third Section Drama

This section details the elaborate preparation for the dramatic climax of the Master Mason degree. It involves specific props, clearing the lodge, dressing the candidate with a Senior Warden's jewel, and a deliberate deception to make him believe the ceremony is complete. The Worshipful Master then reveals that the candidate is not yet a Master Mason and must undergo severe trials to prove his fidelity, emphasizing self-reliance and trust in a higher power. The candidate is hoodwinked, prays, and then embarks on a symbolic journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor who introduces the legend of Hiram Abiff and his daily ritual at King Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0060 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0076 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge to the Lodge

This section presents a solemn charge delivered to the Lodge, emphasizing the regulation of lives by justice and virtue. It includes an optional addition by some Masters that reflects on death, eternity, and the role of the Bible in offering hope beyond the grave. The charge instructs Masons to cultivate brotherly love, relief, and truth, learning morality from the square, equality from the level, and rectitude from the plumb. It calls for emulation of Hiram Abiff's integrity and piety, using the evergreen as a symbol of virtue. Masons are urged to spread brotherly love with the trowel, govern actions with the compass, and seek the approbation of the Supreme Grand Warden, anticipating admission into the Celestial Lodge above through a blameless life and reliance on Divine Providence.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0076 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0077 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Charge

This section presents the formal charge given to a newly raised Master Mason. It emphasizes the Mason's duty, honor, and gratitude to be faithful to their trust, uphold the dignity of their character, and enforce the Order's tenets. The Master Mason is authorized to guide uninformed brethren, preserve the fraternity's reputation, and cultivate universal benevolence. The charge stresses the importance of preserving ancient landmarks, adhering to established customs, and imitating the example of the 'celebrated artist' (Hiram Abiff) in virtue, honor, and fidelity. It concludes with instructions for the candidate to sign the constitution and by-laws and notes regarding the Master Mason lecture.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0077 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0081 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Closing the Lodge.

This section details the ritualistic closing of a Masonic Lodge, noting its similarity across all three degrees. It outlines a sequence of questions and responses among the Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Secretary, and Junior Deacon, covering topics such as the state of the Lodge, approval of minutes, the process of tyling, and the stations and duties of officers. The ceremony concludes with a prayer and an optional closing song, emphasizing brotherly love and moral conduct.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0081 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0107 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Excellent Master: Ceremony of Completion

This section details the 'Ceremony of Completion' for the Most Excellent Master degree, celebrating the symbolic completion of the Temple. It includes the singing of an 'Excellent Master's Song' which marks the end of labor and the laying of the cope-stone and keystone. The ritual involves placing the keystone into an arch supported by pillars Jachin and Boaz, secured with six gavel raps. Brethren divest themselves of working tools and regalia, hanging them on the arch, signifying the completion of their work. The Ark is brought forward and placed on the altar with a pot of incense. The ceremony culminates in the acceptance and investiture of worthy candidates as Most Excellent Masters, a sign of admiration or astonishment, a prayer for divine presence, and a reading from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4, followed by a ritual involving fire and repeated declarations of God's mercy. The Master then delivers a charge to the candidate, emphasizing the duty to dispense light and truth and the importance of acquiring knowledge.

This section details the completion and closing ceremonies of the Most Excellent Master degree, including specific ritualistic actions, a charge to the newly admitted candidate, and readings from Psalms. The charge emphasizes the candidate's duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons and to diligently acquire knowledge. The closing ritual involves brethren assembling around the altar, kneeling, joining hands, and performing balancing movements, followed by a lecture explaining the significance of the cap stone and the six distinct knocks for admission.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0107 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0108 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Excellent Master Lecture

This section details the lecture for the Most Excellent Master degree, covering the candidate's admission process, the questions asked, and the required answers. It describes the ritualistic progression through the Lodge, the solemn obligation taken, and the specific signs and grips associated with the degree, including their symbolic meanings. The narrative also touches upon the historical context of the degree, linking it to the completion of King Solomon's Temple, the celebration of the cope-stone, and the subsequent death of Hiram Abiff.

This section describes the dedication of King Solomon's Temple after the mourning of Hiram Abiff, including the lodging of the Tabernacle of Moses and the Holy Ark, and Jehovah's divine presence. It outlines the structure of a perfect Lodge, requiring seven members, and introduces the 'Excellent Master' degree as an intermediate step in a sequence leading to the Royal Arch. The text extensively details the symbolism of numbers, particularly three (linked to the ancient Trinity and Church of Rome practices) and six (prominent in the Most Excellent Master degree's ritual actions and also venerated by the Druids). The section concludes with a description of the keystone placement ceremony by the Most Excellent Master and historical evidence of arches and keystones in ancient Ethiopian and Egyptian architecture predating Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0108 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0122 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: First.

This section provides a Q&A overview of the Royal Arch Mason degree, detailing how a Royal Arch Mason is recognized, the constitution and officers of a Chapter, and its historical setting near the ruins of King Solomon's temple. It explains the significance of the four veils (blue, purple, scarlet, white), their symbolic meanings related to friendship, connection to Craft Masonry, fervency, zeal, purity, and rectitude, and their allusion to the four tribes of Israel. The text also describes the placement of guards at the veils to ensure only duly qualified individuals enter the presence of the Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, and Scribe.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0122 -->
