Do not devote all Lodge meetings solely to the conferring of degrees.
Make Lodge meetings more interesting and informative by including topics such as history, landmarks, regulations, symbols, fraternalism, and companionship.
Hold regular meetings with Lodge officers to discuss challenges and priorities, and to secure their input.
Recognize that the Worshipful Master alone cannot do everything and should involve officers in decision-making.
A man must be declared "worthy and well qualified" before being made a Mason, and similarly for aspiring to the office of Worshipful Master.
A Worshipful Master must be moral and upright before God, of good repute, strictly obey the moral law, have served the Lodge well, and be a true example of Masonic spirit.
The Worshipful Master should be a leader of men with respect and confidence to direct Lodge activities.
The most important duty of a Worshipful Master is to preserve the harmony of his Lodge.
The Worshipful Master may, in the exercise of his judgment, exclude any visitor or member from participating in a meeting or appearing in public with the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master is responsible to the Grand Lodge for any abuse of power.
The Worshipful Master must provide the Lodge with direction towards established goals consistent with the Lodge Mission.
The Worshipful Master must be punctual and prepared for all Masonic activities.
The Worshipful Master must exercise firm but kind control over all Lodge activities.
The Worshipful Master must follow the Order of Business (referencing the Blue Lodge Text-Book).
The Worshipful Master must preside over and conduct all Masonic business in an orderly, courteous, and harmonious manner, in strict conformity to the Laws, Regulations, and Edicts of the Grand Lodge.
The Worshipful Master must be well-studied in The Williams Digest of Laws, which includes the Constitution, Rules, and General Laws of the Grand Lodge of Mississippi, Free and Accepted Masons.
The Worshipful Master must ensure proper and effective communication with all Lodge members, family members, Entered Apprentices, Fellowcrafts, and candidates regarding appropriate Lodge activities.
The Worshipful Master must ensure Lodge members do not lack in Masonic education or proficiency in the adopted ritual, planning and announcing schedules for practice and degree work.
The Worshipful Master must develop and implement an annual program and budget plan for the Lodge with assistance from Lodge officers and membership.
The Worshipful Master must ensure all Lodge committees are properly governed and facilitated, appointing and assigning Lodge duties to capable and well-qualified leaders within the lodge.
The Worshipful Master must be in possession or show definite inclination to possess the adopted work and lectures assigned to the Worshipful Master.
The Worshipful Master must enforce an atmosphere for proper Lodge behavior and membership expectations.
Assist the Worshipful Master in the discharge of his duties.
Insure that harmony prevails among the brethren.
Do utmost to reconcile existing differences among brethren.
Report unresolved differences to the Worshipful Master.
Prepare the Lodge program for his upcoming year as Worshipful Master.
Serve as the senior principal officer at all stated communications, special communications, or activities of the Lodge in the absence of the Worshipful Master.
Candidate prays.
First stop, Second stop, Third stop in the ritual.
Room where candidates are prepared.
Ante-room where members enter the lodge.
Hall.
Doors.
Door through which candidates are admitted into the lodge.
Door through which members enter.
Altar.
Officers: Treasurer, Secretary, Senior Deacon, Worshipful Master, Junior Warden, Stewards (17 and 18), Senior Warden, Junior Deacon, Tyler.
All business relative to Masonry is done at a 'regular' meeting, specifically in the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are allowed to be present at business meetings.
Balloting for candidates, receiving petitions, and committee reports are generally done on a 'regular' meeting.
A petition for degrees is received at a 'regular' meeting.
The Master appoints a committee of three to inquire into the applicant's character.
The committee reports good or bad at the next regular meeting.
A ballot is held after the committee's report.
No black balls mean the candidate is elected; one or more black balls mean rejection.
No business is done in an Entered Apprentices' Lodge except to initiate a candidate to the First Degree.
No business is done in a Fellow Crafts' Lodge except to pass a Fellow Craft from the first to the second degree.
A candidate initiated to the First Degree is styled 'entered'.
A candidate who has taken the Second Degree is styled 'passed'.
A candidate who has taken the Third Degree is styled 'raised' to the sublime Degree of a Master Mason.
No one is allowed to be present in any degree of Masonry unless they are of that same degree or higher.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge'.
No other officer wears a hat in a 'Blue Lodge'.
A 'Blue Lodge' is a Lodge of Master Masons where only three degrees are conferred: Entered Apprentice (1st), Fellow Craft (2d), Master Mason (3d).
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons consists of five officers (Worshipful Master, Senior and Junior Wardens, Senior and Junior Deacons), with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
Three Master Masons are the requisite number to constitute a Masters' Lodge, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.
All Lodges meet in one room, alike furnished, for conferring different degrees.
The Tyler's duty is to inform brethren on what degree the Lodge is at work, especially late arrivals.
Brethren arriving late come to the ante-room, where the Tyler is stationed with a sword.
Late brethren inquire of the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is opened, put on an apron, and request entry.
The Tyler gives one rap (•) if the Lodge is opened on the First Degree.
The Tyler gives two raps (• •) if the Lodge is opened on the Second Degree.
The Tyler gives three raps (• • •) if the Lodge is opened on the Third Degree.
The Junior Deacon reports the alarm at the inner door to the Worshipful Master.
Candidate kneels on naked knees.
Candidate places both hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses.
Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel.
All present assemble around the altar.
Candidate repeats his name and the oath after the Worshipful Master.
Candidate detaches hands and kisses the book.
Conductor prompts the candidate to desire 'Further light in Masonry'.
Conductor takes off the hoodwink.
Conductor removes the cable-tow.
All around the altar place their hands in the position of the duegard of a Master Mason.
Worshipful Master gives one rap with his gavel.
Brethren retire to their seats, leaving the Master, conductor, and candidate at the altar.
Production of six strong loops and a buckskin bag stuffed with hair.
Removal of altar, lights, and Second Degree pillars.
Candidate dressed with apron (right-hand corner tucked up) and a yoke with a Senior Warden's jewel.
Friends congratulate the candidate upon his Masonic appearance, asking if he is 'through' to mislead him.
Worshipful Master gives one rap with gavel.
Junior Warden gives one rap, steps to desk, and turns small column down.
Senior Warden steps to stand and turns column up.
Brethren and candidate take seats.
Worshipful Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, and Secretary engage in a deceptive dialogue about closing the Lodge, making the Master Mason sign.
Candidate is called to approach the East.
Conductor (Senior Deacon) leads candidate to the Master's seat in the East.
Conductor takes position behind candidate, holding or concealing a hoodwink.
Worshipful Master questions candidate about his status as a Master Mason, then reveals he is not yet one and must face trials, instructing him to pray for himself.
Candidate kneels, and Conductor ties a hoodwink closely over his eyes.
Candidate prays orally or mentally, signifying completion by saying 'Amen'.
Lodge-room darkened by turning down gaslights or lamps.
Conductor assists candidate to arise and leads him three times around the room, traveling with the sun.
Conductor narrates Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple at high twelve for designs and devotions, then retiring at the south gate.
The Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel, bringing the whole Lodge to their feet.
The Master gives one rap with his gavel, when all take their seats except the candidate, who remains standing before the Master.
The Worshipful Master addresses the candidate, Brother Gabe, in closing the Degree.
Be faithful to your trust.
Enforce obedience to the tenets of the Order by precept and example.
Correct the errors and irregularities of uninformed brethren.
Recommend obedience and submission to inferiors.
Recommend courtesy and affability to equals.
Recommend kindness and condescension to superiors.
Cultivate universal benevolence.
Afford the best example for the conduct of others less informed by your own regular behavior.
Carefully preserve the ancient landmarks of the order.
Support with dignity the character you now bear.
Imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you this evening represent.
Take your seat in this Lodge as a Master Mason after stepping to the Secretary's desk and signing your name to the constitution and by-laws.
The Worshipful Master initiates the closing by asking the Senior Warden if there is anything further before closing the Lodge.
The Senior Warden rises, gives the sign of a Master Mason (if on that degree), and responds.
The Junior Warden makes the same sign and responds when questioned by the Worshipful Master.
The Secretary confirms there is nothing on the desk.
Minutes of the communication are read by the Secretary and approved if no objections, indicated by one rap from the Worshipful Master.
The Junior Deacon rises upon the Worshipful Master's rap and is questioned about the Lodge being duly tyled.
The Junior Deacon makes the sign, confirms the Lodge is tyled, and is instructed to inform the Tyler to tyle accordingly.

The Junior Deacon reports the Lodge is tyled by a brother of the degree within the outer door, whose duty is to keep off cowans and eavesdroppers and allow passage only to the qualified with the Worshipful Master's permission.
The Worshipful Master gives one rap, and the Senior Warden rises to answer questions about becoming a Master Mason, including traveling for wages and relieving distressed Masons.
Optional questions and answers are used by some Lodges regarding traveling from west to east in search of 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) and finding a substitute.
The Worshipful Master gives two raps, and all officers rise to confirm their stations and duties, often referring to the opening ceremony.
The Worshipful Master gives three raps, and all in the Lodge rise, with the Master rising first, as the Senior Warden explains the Master's duty to open and govern the Lodge.
The Worshipful Master orders the Lodge closed until the next communication, instructing the Senior Warden to communicate this to the Junior Warden, who then informs the brethren.
Brethren perform the signs, which are the same as at opening.
The Master gives one rap, responded to by the Senior Warden, then the Junior Warden, and repeated three times each.
The Worshipful Master questions how Masons should meet, act, and part, with responses 'On the Level,' 'On the Plumb,' and 'On the Square.'

The Master removes his hat and recites a closing prayer: 'May the blessing of Heaven rest upon us, and all regular Masons; may brotherly love prevail, and every moral and social virtue cement us. Amen.'
The brethren respond: 'So mote it be.'
Some Lodges sing a closing song, such as 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon.'
Singing of the 'Excellent Master's Song' celebrating the Temple's completion.
Placement of the keystone into an arch supported by two pillars (Jachin and Boaz), secured with six raps of a gavel by the Most Excellent Master.
Brethren moving around the arch and divesting themselves of jewels, aprons, and sashes, hanging them on the arch.
The Ark, carried by four brethren, is placed on the altar, with a pot of incense placed on top of it.
Brethren halting, facing inward to the altar, and beckoning the candidate to join the ceremonies.
Brethren throwing up hands and rolling eyes upward in a 'sign of admiration or astonishment'.
Brothers joining hands as in opening, while the Right Worshipful Master reads Scripture (2 Chron. vii. 1, 4).
The Master (high priest of the Chapter) kneeling and joining hands with the brethren during the Scripture reading.
Brethren repeating 'For he is good, for his mercy endureth forever' six times in concert, bowing their heads low.
A piece of blazing gum-camphor or other combustible matter thrown into the pot of incense on the altar by a brother.
Members balancing six times, rising, and balancing six more times, then disengaging and taking seats.
One of the brethren throws a piece of blazing gum-camphor or other combustible matter into the pot of incense on the altar.
Brethren kneel around the altar, bowing their heads low toward the floor while repeating 'for his mercy endureth forever' six times.
Members balance six times as in opening, rise, and balance six times more.
Brethren assemble around the altar in a circle, kneeling on the right knee, putting their left arms over, and joining hands.
The Master reads verses from the one hundred and thirty-fourth Psalm.
The Master closes the circle as in opening, followed by balancing six times, rising, balancing six times more, disengaging hands, and giving signs from this Degree downward.
The Lodge is closed as in preceding Degrees.
Psalm twenty-third is read at closing.
Admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is gained by six distinct knocks (• • • • • •).
Candidate is asked 'Who comes here?'

Candidate states they are a worthy brother, duly initiated, passed to Fellow Craft, raised to Master Mason, advanced to Mark Master, and regularly passed the chair, now seeking to be received and acknowledged as a Most Excellent Master.
Candidate confirms the act is of their own free will and accord, that they are worthy, well qualified, have made suitable proficiency, and are properly vouched for.
Candidate states they expect to gain privilege 'By the benefit of the pass'.
The pass word is 'Mark Well'.
Candidate is directed to wait for the Right Worshipful Master's answer.
Right Worshipful Master's answer: 'Let the candidate enter'.
Candidate is conducted six times around the Lodge to the Worshipful Senior Warden in the West.
Candidate is conducted to the Right Worshipful Master in the East.
Candidate is conducted to the Worshipful Senior Warden in the West, who teaches the approach to the altar.
Approach to the altar involves six upright Masonic steps, feet forming a square, body erect.
The Most Excellent Master is made 'In due form'.
Due form involves kneeling upon both knees, both hands covering the Holy Bible, square and compasses, body erect.
Candidate takes the solemn oath of a Most Excellent Master.
The duegard sign is given.
The sign of the degree is given.
The sign of admiration is given.
The grip is communicated to a brother.
The name of the grip is 'Rabboni'.
The grip is also called 'The cover grip'.
Solomon dedicated the Temple with solemn prayer and costly sacrifices.
The holy ark was fixed in its proper place between the cherubim.
The grand honors of Masonry involve a thrice-repeated action.
A candidate is prepared with a rope wound six times around their body.
Six distinct knocks are given on the Lodge door, answered by six from within.
The candidate is walked six times around the Lodge, moving with the sun.
More advanced brethren form a procession and march six times around the Lodge, against the course of the sun.
Two pillars, about five feet high, are erected with an arch placed over them.
The Most Excellent Master places the keystone in the arch.
The keystone is driven home with six raps of the gavel.
A pot containing gum camphor or other inflammable matter is used during ceremonies.
Recognition of a Royal Arch Mason is by 'three times three'.
A Chapter of Royal Arch Masons is constituted by nine Regular Royal Arch Masons.
Officers include Most Excellent High Priest, Excellent King, Scribe, Captain of the Host, Principal Sojourner, Royal Arch Captain, and Three Masters of the veils.
Meetings are held in a place representing a Tabernacle, erected near the ruins of King Solomon's temple.
There are four veils: blue, purple, scarlet, and white.
The veils were placed at the outer courts of the tabernacle to serve as a covering and stations for guards.
Guards were stationed to ensure only duly qualified individuals could pass or repass.
The children of Israel made themselves known to guards by words and signs given by God to Moses.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Brotherhood is presented as the 'cornerstone' of the Fraternity, symbolizing its foundational importance and the essential principle upon which the organization is built.

The point within the circle represents an individual brother. The circle symbolizes the boundary line beyond which a Mason should never allow his passions or prejudices to betray him. The two perpendicular parallel lines represent St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist, and also the summer and winter solstices, which symbolize the lines of moral rectitude. The Holy Scriptures resting upon the top indicate their divine origin and inspiration, signifying that they are supported by divinity itself.

Brotherly Love symbolizes the universal brotherhood of mankind, transcending differences in origin, status, and belief. It represents the ideal of unity and mutual support among all people, reflecting a shared divine origin and common planetary home.

Truth is symbolized as a divine attribute and the foundational principle for all virtues, guiding Masonic conduct and fostering integrity within the fraternity.

Prudence symbolizes the rational and judicious approach to life, guiding Masons to act with wisdom and discretion in all circumstances, particularly in safeguarding the integrity of the Craft.

The phrase "acting upon the square" symbolically represents living a life of integrity, fairness, and moral rectitude in dealings with others, aligning with the Masonic principle of upright conduct.

The compasses symbolize the containment and embodiment of the fundamental tenets of Freemasonry: Friendship, Morality, and Brotherly Love. These tenets are presented as foundational principles of the Craft.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.

The instruction to "imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you have this evening represented" directly refers to Hiram Abiff, symbolizing fidelity, integrity, and steadfastness in the face of adversity, even unto death. The Master Mason is expected to embody these virtues in their conduct and adherence to Masonic principles.

The use of black draping for the room, altar, and stations serves as an emblem of mourning. The extinguished altar lights symbolize the absence of life or light, fitting for a ceremony commemorating a deceased brother.

Black drapes covering the room, altar, and stations serve as an emblem of mourning, signifying respect and sorrow for the deceased brother.

The "Third Circuit" symbolizes the journey of life, marked by inevitable suffering, loss, and the ultimate certainty of death. The lamentations represent the human experience of grief and the transient nature of earthly existence ("life is a vapor," "days are passed"). The responses from the Chaplain, particularly the references to a "tree planted by the waters" and "a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens," symbolize enduring faith, divine sustenance, and the promise of immortality and resurrection beyond the grave. The ritual as a whole serves as a memento mori, reminding participants of mortality while simultaneously offering comfort and hope through spiritual belief and the prospect of an afterlife.

Life is presented as 'death’s vestibule' and a 'journey to the grave,' symbolizing the transient nature of earthly existence. The comparison of human life to flowers and grass emphasizes fragility and impermanence. The 'valley of the shadow of death' symbolizes the journey through mortality with divine comfort. The scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the hope of resurrection, transformation from a natural to a spiritual body, and victory over death. The seed analogy in 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the transformation of the body after death into a new, spiritual form. The 'broad mantle of charity' symbolizes Masonic non-judgment and compassion towards the deceased.

The scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job symbolize resurrection, incorruption, transformation from mortal to immortal, and the belief in a Redeemer. The corn of nourishment symbolizes the resurrection of the body, sown in corruption and raised in incorruption, glory, power, and as a spiritual body. The wine of refreshment symbolizes spiritual refreshment, divine grace, blessings, and comfort for those afflicted or distressed. The oil of joy symbolizes comfort, consolation for the bereaved, healing of heart-wounds, the virtue of charity, increased affection, leniency, indulgence, forgiveness, and a joyful passage through death. The lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a Mason's badge, denotes the hope that the departed brother's soul, purified from imperfections, has found a welcome in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

The white glove is an emblem of Fidelity, symbolizing Masonic friendship and brotherly love, and signifies an increased obligation to the living brethren. The sprig of evergreen is an emblem of Masonic Faith in the immortality of the soul, representing the eternal principle in man that survives death and blooms perpetually. It denotes trust that the deceased's immortal spirit has entered a celestial temple and found acceptance with the Supreme Grand Master.

The invocation of Deity symbolizes the spiritual foundation of all Masonic endeavors and the reliance on divine guidance. The Festival of St. John the Evangelist, while a social occasion, is consecrated to duty, representing the ongoing commitment to Masonic principles and the renewal of vows by those taking on leadership. The Marshal's address outlines the symbolic and practical influence of Freemasonry, emphasizing its role as a moral and educational force in society, promoting universal brotherhood and adherence to divine and moral law.

The induction of officers signifies the continuity of leadership, the structured operation of the Lodge, and the fulfillment of the Craft's purposes through chosen individuals.

The Square teaches to regulate actions by rule and line, harmonizing conduct with principles of morality and virtue. The Compasses teach to limit desires, enabling one to rise to eminence by merit, live respected, and die regretted. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pressing forward in virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Holy Writings are presented as an inestimable gift from God, the Great Light in Masonry, guiding to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man.

The Wardens' role as examples of good order and regularity symbolizes the importance of leadership by virtue and adherence to Masonic laws. Their duty to diffuse light and impart knowledge represents the continuous flow of wisdom and instruction within the Craft, ensuring its perpetuation and the enlightenment of its members.

The office of Grand Treasurer represents an important trust within the Fraternity, signifying the high regard the brethren have for the officer's fidelity and discretion. The jewel of office symbolizes this trust and the responsibilities associated with it.

The proclamation symbolizes the public declaration of Masonic principles, including loyalty to country, reverence for the Great Architect, and the practice of benevolence. The handing over of the working tools (Square, Level, Plumb) to the Architect by the Grand Master symbolizes the trust placed in skilled leadership and the importance of proper design, execution, and moral rectitude in all undertakings, particularly in the construction of a symbolic or literal temple. It signifies the transition from planning to execution, guided by Masonic virtues.

The progression of the Senior Warden towards the Oriental Chair symbolizes advancement in leadership and increasing responsibility within the Masonic Lodge. His role as an exponent of harmony reflects the fraternity's ideal of unity and brotherly love.

The Fellow Craft Lodge is symbolically styled 'the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple,' representing a stage of intellectual and moral advancement. The Master Masons' Lodge is symbolically called 'the Sanctum Sanctorum, or, Holy of Holies of King Solomon's Temple,' signifying the most sacred and complete state of Masonic knowledge and authority. The three degrees are masonically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), representing a progression through different levels of understanding and spiritual significance within the Temple.

The act of kneeling on naked knees with hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses signifies humility, reverence, and a solemn commitment to the sacred principles and moral guidance of Freemasonry. The 'third time at the altar of Masonry' represents the progression and increasing depth of commitment through the degrees. The request for 'further light in Masonry' symbolizes the candidate's desire for deeper knowledge, understanding, and spiritual enlightenment within the Craft. The severe penalty for violating the obligation underscores the profound seriousness and binding nature of the vows taken.

The deception regarding the completion of the degree symbolizes the unexpected challenges and deeper truths yet to be revealed in the Masonic journey. The Senior Warden's jewel, worn by the candidate, represents a temporary elevation or a test of his understanding of authority and responsibility. The 'rough and rugged road beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers' symbolizes the moral and spiritual dangers and trials one faces in life, requiring unwavering fidelity and trust. The act of praying for oneself emphasizes self-reliance and direct communion with the Deity. The journey around the darkened lodge, guided by the Conductor, represents a transition into the profound narrative of Hiram Abiff, symbolizing the search for light and knowledge amidst obscurity, and the emulation of a revered Grand Master's devotion and diligence.

The plumb-line symbolizes justice, and the square symbolizes virtue, guiding actions and lives. The square also teaches morality, the level teaches equality, and the plumb teaches rectitude of life. The evergreen blooming at the head of the grave symbolizes the place of interment and virtue's ever-blooming loveliness, designating Free and Accepted Masons. The trowel symbolizes spreading the cement of brotherly love and affection. The compass symbolizes circumscribing words and actions, promoting self-governance. The 'Celestial Lodge above' and 'spiritual temple, that house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens' symbolize the afterlife and divine reward for a virtuous Masonic life.

The instruction to 'imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you this evening represent' directly refers to Hiram Abiff, symbolizing unwavering fidelity, integrity, and adherence to duty even in the face of adversity. The 'ancient landmarks of the order' are symbolic of the foundational, unalterable principles and traditions of Freemasonry, which the Master Mason is charged to preserve.

The ritual of closing the Lodge includes symbolic elements such as the Senior Warden's journey from west to east in search of 'that which was lost,' referring to the Master's word, and finding a substitute. The principles of meeting 'On the Level,' acting 'On the Plumb,' and parting 'On the Square' symbolize equality, uprightness, and rectitude in Masonic conduct. The Worshipful Master's station in the East, like the rising sun, symbolizes his role in governing and instructing the Craft.

The completion of the Temple signifies the culmination of labor and the achievement of a significant spiritual or architectural goal. The cope-stone and keystone represent the final, essential elements that bind the structure together, symbolizing perfection and stability. The divestment of working tools indicates that the physical labor is complete, and the focus shifts to the spiritual and intellectual. The Ark being safely seated on the altar with incense symbolizes the divine presence and acceptance of the completed work. The sign of admiration and the prayer for divine glory emphasize the sacredness of the institution and the aspiration for divine guidance and inspiration. The fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the house signify divine approval and the sacredness of the consecrated space.

The cap stone symbolizes the completion of King Solomon's Temple, upon the dedication ceremonies of which this degree is founded. The six distinct knocks allude to the Sixth Degree of Masonry.

The 'cover grip' symbolizes the duty of Most Excellent Masters to cover the faults and imperfections of brethren with the 'broad mantle of charity and brotherly love', recognizing human frailties. The 'sign of admiration' alludes to the wonder and admiration of ancient brethren upon viewing the interior of King Solomon's magnificent Temple dedicated to the Supreme Being. The word 'Rabboni' signifies 'Good Master' or 'Most Excellent Master', originating from King Solomon's commendation of the master builder at the Temple's completion.

The reiteration of the number three in Masonic ceremonies, particularly the grand honors, is suggested to be in honor and reverence to the ancient Trinity. The number six is highly respected in the Most Excellent Master degree, appearing in various ritualistic actions such as candidate preparation, knocks, circumambulation, and the keystone ceremony, with historical parallels drawn to Druidic veneration. The keystone symbolizes completion, structural integrity, and the essential element of an arch, with its placement being a central act of the Most Excellent Master degree. The cloud of glory filling the Temple signifies Jehovah's divine presence and acceptance.

Blue denotes friendship and is the principal color of a Master Mason. Purple, being a mixture of blue and scarlet, signifies the intimate connection between the Royal Arch degree and ancient Craft Masonry. Scarlet denotes the fervency and zeal that should actuate all Royal Arch Masons and is the peculiar color of this degree. White denotes purity of life and rectitude of conduct, which should govern those seeking admission to the Sanctum Sanctorum or Holy of Holies. The four veils allude to the four tribes of the children of Israel who bore banners through the wilderness: Judah (strength of the Lion), Reuben (intelligence of the Man), Ephraim (patience of the Ox), and Dan (swiftness of the Eagle).
entries.candidate_lesson:
A Master Mason is responsible for maintaining the integrity and honor of the Fraternity through his personal conduct and moral choices. He must embody brotherly love, treating others with respect, offering support without judgment, and always striving to be an honorable example.

A Mason must keep himself circumscribed within the precepts of the Holy Scriptures and the moral rectitude symbolized by the parallel lines. By adhering to these principles, it is impossible for him to materially err. He must never suffer his passions or prejudices to betray him beyond the moral boundary represented by the circle. The principal tenets of Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth are fundamental to the Masonic profession.

The candidate is taught to embrace the principle of Brotherly Love, recognizing all human beings as part of a single family. This lesson encourages mutual aid, support, and protection, and emphasizes the power of Freemasonry to foster genuine friendship and unity across societal divides.

The primary lesson for a candidate is to be good and true, understanding that truth is the foundation of all virtue and should govern their actions and interactions, leading to sincerity and mutual support.

A Mason must cultivate prudence as a fundamental virtue to guide their conduct, make wise decisions, and protect the sacred trust of Freemasonry by never divulging its secrets, signs, tokens, or words, especially in public or mixed company.

The candidate is taught the foundational principles of Masonic conduct, encompassing moral, ethical, civic, and fraternal duties. Key lessons include reverence for God, justice and charity towards others, self-control, loyalty to one's country, discretion in public and private life, and the importance of preserving the integrity and reputation of the Craft through personal conduct and careful selection of new members. The inviolability of Masonic mysteries is also presented as a distinguishing characteristic.

The candidate is taught the three primary tenets of the Institution: Friendship, Morality, and Brotherly Love, which are presented as essential principles to guide their conduct and understanding.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.

As a Master Mason, one is bound by duty, honor, and gratitude to be faithful to their trust, uphold the dignity of their character, and enforce obedience to the Institution's tenets. This includes correcting errors of less informed brethren, preserving the fraternity's reputation through morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, and diligently guarding the ancient landmarks. The Master Mason must remain true to their vows and imitate the fidelity of Hiram Abiff.

The candidate learns about the solemn and specific Masonic customs and regulations concerning the death of a brother, emphasizing the importance of adhering to ancient usage and Grand Lodge decisions regarding funeral services and the structured conduct of a Lodge of Sorrow. It underscores the fraternity's commitment to honoring its deceased members through prescribed rituals.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The Worshipful Master is charged with upholding moral law, being a peaceful and law-abiding citizen, respecting civil authorities, working diligently, living creditably, and acting honorably. They must venerate the Order's rulers, submit to Lodge resolutions, avoid quarrels and excess, be cautious, courteous, and faithful to the Lodge. The Master is to respect true brethren, discountenance imposters, promote the good of society, cultivate social virtues, and propagate Masonic knowledge. They must pay homage to the Grand Master, conform to Grand Lodge edicts, and preserve the Ancient Landmarks. Regular attendance at Grand Lodge committees and communications is required, along with ensuring proper Lodge formation, initiation, and visitor examination. The Master is taught to regulate actions by morality, limit desires, observe duty, pursue virtue, avoid dissimulation, and be guided by divine truth.

The candidate learns that leadership within Masonry requires a profound understanding of its principles, exemplary conduct, and a commitment to teaching and guiding others. It underscores the importance of self-improvement, learning from others' strengths and weaknesses, and being prepared to assume greater responsibilities for the benefit of the fraternity.

The importance of financial integrity, accountability, and trust within the Masonic Fraternity. It teaches that officers are chosen based on their proven fidelity and discretion, and their commitment to the organization's well-being.

The candidate learns about the public-facing virtues of Freemasonry, including loyalty, piety, and universal benevolence. They also learn the importance of skill, capacity, and diligent conduct in leadership roles, as exemplified by the Architect entrusted with the building project. The emphasis on order, harmony, beauty, and strength in construction serves as an allegory for the moral and spiritual building of character.

Candidates are introduced to the fundamental concept of duties and responsibilities that are integral to Masonic membership and conduct.

The qualities and duties of a Worshipful Master serve as an example of the high standards and leadership expected within Freemasonry, implicitly guiding members towards personal and communal responsibility. It highlights the importance of moral conduct, dedication, and adherence to Masonic principles for those who aspire to lead and for all members in their conduct within the Lodge.

A Mason progressing through the chairs, particularly as Senior Warden, must understand the significant increase in leadership responsibilities, the importance of actively fostering harmony, and the necessity of proactive planning for the Lodge's future. Personal conduct must consistently reflect the principles of the fraternity.

Candidates learn the importance of character and integrity through the rigorous inquiry process for petitioners. The progression through the degrees, marked by being 'entered,' 'passed,' and 'raised,' teaches a journey of continuous self-improvement and increasing knowledge. The caution regarding giving the correct sign emphasizes the importance of attentiveness, respect for the degree at which the Lodge is working, and adherence to proper protocol.

The candidate learns the profound and binding nature of the Master Mason's obligation, emphasizing strict adherence to secrecy, fidelity, and mutual aid among brethren. It instills the importance of upholding Masonic laws, supporting the Grand Lodge, and exercising discernment in recognizing true and lawful Masons. The obligation teaches ethical conduct, including protecting a brother's family and property, avoiding harm or fraud, and maintaining moral integrity. It also highlights the continuous pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment within Masonry.

The candidate learns that the Masonic journey is not easily completed and requires profound fidelity and endurance. He is taught to trust in a higher power and to rely on his own spiritual strength, as he must pray for himself in the face of impending trials. The introduction to Hiram Abiff's daily devotions and diligent work instills the importance of consistent dedication, moral rectitude, and reverence for the Deity, setting an example for the candidate's own conduct. The experience also teaches humility, as his presumed status as a Master Mason is challenged, and he is reminded of the 'more valuable secrets' yet to be entrusted.

Candidates are taught to regulate their lives by justice and virtue, cultivate brotherly love, relief, and truth, and learn moral lessons from Masonic working tools. They are encouraged to emulate the integrity and piety of Hiram Abiff, maintain virtuous conduct, and prepare for an afterlife in the Celestial Lodge above through a pure and blameless life and reliance on Divine Providence. The charge instills a sense of mortality while offering hope for eternal bliss and divine approbation.

The candidate is taught the profound responsibilities and moral obligations that come with the Master Mason degree. These include maintaining personal integrity, providing moral leadership within the fraternity, upholding the reputation of Masonry, and diligently preserving its ancient traditions and principles. The lesson emphasizes the importance of setting a virtuous example and demonstrating unwavering fidelity, mirroring the legendary example of Hiram Abiff.

The closing ceremony reinforces the importance of brotherly love, moral and social virtues, and the expectation for every brother to conduct himself as a man and a Mason. It also highlights the duty to support oneself and family, and to contribute to the relief of worthy distressed Master Masons, their widows, and orphans, emphasizing the practical application of Masonic principles in daily life.

The candidate's admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is a recognition of their Masonic abilities and an encouragement to maintain that esteem through good conduct and adherence to rules. A primary duty of a Most Excellent Master is to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, which necessitates an accurate and comprehensive acquaintance with the lectures of all degrees. The candidate is urged to apply double diligence to acquire knowledge and information to properly discharge their duties and preserve the unsullied title of Most Excellent Master.

The candidate's admission is a testament to the brethren's good opinion of their Masonic abilities, which should not be forfeited by misconduct. As a Most Excellent Master, the candidate has a great duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, requiring an accurate acquaintance with the lectures of each degree. Diligence in acquiring knowledge is essential to properly discharge duties and preserve the title of Most Excellent Master.

The candidate learns the importance of upholding their solemn obligation, even under the threat of severe penalty. They are taught the virtues of charity and brotherly love, encouraging them to overlook the imperfections of others. The degree also instills a sense of admiration for the grand achievements of the ancient brethren, particularly the construction and dedication of King Solomon's Temple, and the recognition of excellence in craftsmanship.

Masons are encouraged to examine the meaning and original intention behind the ceremonies and practices, rather than merely performing them from habit. The degree emphasizes the completion of the Temple and the importance of the keystone, suggesting themes of perfection and essential elements in one's Masonic journey.

Candidates are taught the importance of friendship, fervency, zeal, purity of life, and rectitude of conduct. The emphasis on guards and qualifications underscores the necessity of being duly prepared and worthy to advance within the Masonic fraternity and to enter sacred spaces.
tradition_notes: Ancient Brethren dedicated their Lodges to King Solomon, who was considered their first Most Excellent Grand Master.
Modern Lodges are dedicated to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist, who are eminent patrons of Masonry.
The emblem of a point within a circle, embordered by two perpendicular parallel lines and topped by the Holy Scriptures, is represented in every regular and well-governed Lodge.
Lodges are traditionally situated due East and West, following the pattern of King Solomon's Temple.
To be good and true is the first lesson taught in Masonry.
The text alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.
Freemasonry is described as an ancient fraternity, having existed from time immemorial.
The Institution is founded on excellent rules and useful maxims inculcated in Masonic lectures.
The greatest and best of men in all ages have been encouragers and promoters of the art, extending privileges and patronizing assemblies.
The Second Section recites the historical traditions of the Institution.
The text refers to "ancient landmarks of the Institution," emphasizing the importance of preserving long-standing Masonic principles and traditions.
It also mentions "established usages and customs of the fraternity," highlighting adherence to traditional practices.
Ancient Masonic usage dictates that the funeral service at the grave is only performed at the time of burial.
A decision by Grand Master Barkley in 1875, reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge in 1883, established the ruling on the timing of funeral services at the grave.
Decision of Grand Master Barkley in 1875, reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge in 1883, regarding funeral service at the grave.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
Freemasonry has a tradition of invoking a blessing of Deity before any great or important undertaking.
The Festival of St. John the Evangelist is a traditional observance in Freemasonry, consecrated to duty and promoting social enjoyment and brotherly love.
Freemasonry's origins are described as ancient, with its beginnings not recorded on written pages of history.

The practice of inducting officers to preside over a Lodge for a specific term (e.g., twelve months) is a traditional aspect of Masonic governance and ensures the continuous operation and leadership of the Lodge.
The Master must assent to Ancient Charges and Regulations which point out the duty of the Master of a Lodge.
The Master must hold in veneration the original rulers and patrons of the Order of Freemasonry and their regular successors.
The Master must submit to the awards and resolutions of brethren in Lodge convened, consistent with the constitutions of the Order.
The Master must respect genuine and true brethren and discountenance dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
The Master must conform to every edict of the Grand Lodge or General Assembly of Masons that is not subversive of the principles and groundwork of Masonry.
It is admitted that it is not in the power of any man, or body of men, to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
It is admitted that no new Lodge can be formed without permission of the Grand Lodge.
It is admitted that no countenance ought to be given to any irregular Lodge, or to any person clandestinely initiated therein, as being contrary to the ancient charges of the Order.
It is admitted that no person can be regularly made a Freemason in, or admitted a member of, any regular Lodge, without previous notice, and due inquiry into his character.
It is agreed that no visitors shall be received into your Lodge without due examination, and producing proper vouchers of their having been initiated in a regular Lodge.
Masters have supported these regulations in all ages before.
The practice of giving Grand Honors and singing an ode is part of the lodge installation ceremony.
The address to the Senior and Junior Wardens is a traditional part of an installation ceremony, delivered by the Installing Officer.
The investiture with a jewel of office is a traditional practice for Masonic officers.
The selection of officers emphasizes qualities such as fidelity and discretion.

This text describes a traditional Masonic ceremony, likely a cornerstone laying, which involves a public proclamation and the symbolic presentation of working tools to the master builder or architect.
The mention of 'concealed from the eyes of men' suggests the esoteric nature of some Masonic knowledge, even within a public ceremony.
Ritual is a necessary vehicle for conveying the greatest lessons of the fraternity.
Freemasonry encompasses more than just ritual, including history, landmarks, regulations, and symbols.
Fraternalism and companionship are significant aspects of Freemasonry for many members.
It is a Masonic tradition that a man must be declared 'worthy and well qualified' before being made a Mason.
The Grand Lodge of Mississippi operates under 'The Williams Digest of Laws, being the Constitution, Rules and General Laws of the Grand Lodge of Mississippi, Free and Accepted Masons'.
The Senior Warden is traditionally the second principal officer of authority, progressing one step closer to the Oriental Chair.
The progression through the chairs is a traditional path in most Lodges.
All business relative to Masonry is traditionally conducted at a 'regular' meeting, specifically within the Third, or Master Mason Degree.
Only Master Masons are permitted to be present at such business meetings.
Grand Lodges typically make arrangements for the regulation of their subordinate Lodges.
The Master always wears his hat when presiding in a 'Blue Lodge', a privilege not extended to other officers.
A 'Blue Lodge' is defined as a Lodge of Master Masons where only the three Craft degrees (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) are conferred.
A Lodge of Fellow Craft Masons traditionally consists of five officers, with seven generally assisting besides the Tyler.
A Masters' Lodge requires three Master Masons to constitute it by Masonic Law, though generally seven assist besides the Tyler.

The three degrees are symbolically styled by the Craft as the Ground Floor (Entered Apprentice), Middle Chamber (Fellow Craft), and Sanctum Sanctorum (Master Mason), referencing King Solomon's Temple.
The candidate kneels on naked knees and places both hands on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses during the obligation.
The Master assures the candidate that the oath does not conflict with duties to God, neighbor, country, or self.
Brethren assemble around the altar when the Master gives three raps with his gavel during the oath.
The Lodge is erected to Almighty God and dedicated to the holy Sts. John.
The candidate requests 'further light in Masonry' after taking the obligation.
The hoodwink and cable-tow are removed after the obligation.
The duegard of a Master Mason is displayed by those around the altar after the obligation.
A specific, severe penalty is invoked for knowingly violating the Master Mason's obligation.
The custom of dressing the candidate with a rich apron and yoke in some Lodges.
The specific tying of the apron with the right-hand corner tucked up for a Master Mason.
The use of columns by the Wardens to signify refreshment or labor.

The Conductor's narrative introduces Hiram Abiff's daily custom of entering the Sanctum Sanctorum at high twelve for drawing designs and offering devotions, then retiring at the south gate of the Temple. This is explicitly noted as the first time the candidate hears about Hiram Abiff.
Some Masters add a lecture on the emblems, focusing on the solemn thought of death and the promise of eternity through the Bible.
The Master Mason lecture is not generally given by the Master to the candidate on the night of his 'raising' due to its length.
The candidate generally receives the Master Mason lecture from brethren who are well posted in the work.
If time permits, the Master and Senior Warden usually go through the first section of the Master Mason lecture before closing the Lodge.
Some Lodges use specific variations in the Senior Warden's response, such as adding 'in the west' after 'Nothing, Worshipful'.
Some Lodges use specific questions and answers regarding the search for 'that which was lost' (the Master's word) both at opening and closing.
The questions and answers about the Master's word are also occasionally used by some Masters in the lecture.
Some Lodges sing a specific closing song, 'Adieu! a heart-warm, fond adieu,' to the air of 'Bonny Doon'.
The pillars Jachin and Boaz are referenced (see pp. 71 and 83).
The sign of admiration or astonishment is compared to that expressed by the Queen of Sheba upon first viewing Solomon's Temple (see p. 203).
A passage of Scripture from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4 is read by the Right Worshipful Master, describing the fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the Temple after Solomon's prayer.
The Master is identified as the 'high priest of the Chapter'.
The ceremony involves specific readings from Psalms 134 and 23.
The degree is founded upon the ceremonies of the dedication of King Solomon's Temple.
The degree is founded on Masonic tradition described in the ancient Book of Constitutions.
The Temple was finished in seven years and six months, with the cope-stone celebrated by the fraternity with great joy.
The joy was interrupted by the sudden death of Master Hiram Abiff, who was interred near the Temple.
After mourning Hiram Abiff, the tabernacle of Moses and its holy relics were lodged in the Temple.
The grip originated at the completion of the Temple when King Solomon, pleased with the master builder, exclaimed 'Hail, Rabboni'.
A procession was formed, the ark safely seated, the cap stone placed in the principal arch, and the Lodge closed with solemn invocations to Deity.
It is an established doctrine of the Order that while three form a Lodge, five may hold it, seven only can make it perfect.
The 'Excellent Master' degree is noted as being used by 'transatlantic brethren'.
The practice of thrice-repeated actions and the significance of the number three are compared to the Church of Rome's customs, specifically an Ursuline Convent rule of three knocks.

The veneration of the number six by Druids, including their principal ceremonies on the sixth day of the moon and gathering mistletoe in groups of six, is presented as a historical parallel to Masonic practices.
Historical discoveries in Ethiopia and Egypt show arches with keystones predating King Solomon's Temple, providing ancient context for this architectural element.
The Chapter represents a Tabernacle erected by ancient brethren near the ruins of King Solomon's temple.
The three former officers represent ancient brethren who formed the first Most Grand Council at Jerusalem.
The three latter officers represent ancient brethren who brought to light principal secrets buried since the death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff until the erection of the second temple.
The veils allude to the four tribes of the children of Israel who bore banners through the wilderness.
The method of identification for the children of Israel involved words and signs given by God to Moses.
caution_notes: Be not hasty to condemn others
Do not judge a Brother by your own standards
The circle is the boundary line beyond which a Mason is never to suffer his passions or prejudices to betray him.
Hypocrisy and deceit are unknown among us when influenced by the principle of truth.
Masons must be particularly attentive to prudence in all strange and mixed companies.
Never let fall the least sign, token, or word whereby the secrets of Freemasonry might be unlawfully obtained.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance which may impair your faculties or debase the dignity of your profession.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Do not suffer your zeal for the Institution to lead you into argument with those who, through ignorance, may ridicule it.
Be particularly attentive not to recommend a person for initiation unless you are convinced that he will conform to our rules.
No contention should ever exist among members of the society of friends and brothers.
Do not swerve from your duty.
Do not violate your vows.
Do not betray your trust.
Never suffer the ancient landmarks to be infringed.
Never countenance a deviation from the established usages and customs of the fraternity.

Reading the funeral service at the grave of a deceased brother is only warranted at the time of burial; any modern practice otherwise is considered an innovation, as per Grand Master Barkley's decision (1875), reaffirmed by the Grand Lodge (1883).
There is no law nor ancient Masonic usage that warrants reading a funeral service at the grave of a deceased brother, except at the time of burial; modern practice is an innovation.
Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.
Do not be concerned in plots and conspiracies against the government; patiently submit to law and authorities.
Avoid private piques and quarrels, and guard against intemperance and excess.
Discountenance imposters and all dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
It is not in the power of any man or body of men to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
No new Lodge can be formed without Grand Lodge permission; do not countenance irregular Lodges or clandestinely initiated persons.
No person can be regularly made a Freemason or admitted to a regular Lodge without previous notice and due inquiry into character.
No visitors shall be received without due examination and proper vouchers of regular initiation.
The Lodge Charter should never be out of the Master's immediate control and must be duly transmitted to the successor.
Grand Honors are not given during a public installation.
A Masonic Lodge cannot or will not run itself.
No Lodge will succeed if it devotes all its meetings to the conferring of degrees.
A Lodge which depends upon ritualistic work as its sole means of Masonic enjoyment will soon face empty seats or need to stimulate attendance.
The Worshipful Master, alone, cannot do everything that needs to be done.
The Master is responsible to the Grand Lodge for any abuse of power, as in all other cases.
If no planning has been done as Senior Warden, he will not contribute anything to Masonry, and it would be better had he not advanced.

Brethren arriving late must be informed by the Tyler on what degree the Lodge is at work to prevent them from giving the wrong sign to the Worshipful Master, especially if members of a lower degree are present.
The obligation does not interfere with the duty owed to God, neighbor, country, or self.

Secrets of the Master Masons' Degree are not to be revealed to any person except a true and lawful brother of this Degree, or in a regularly constituted Lodge of Master Masons, and only after strict trial, due examination, or lawful information.
Do not aid or be present at the initiation, passing, or raising of a woman, an old man in his dotage, a young man in his nonage, an atheist, a madman, or a fool, knowing them to be such.

Do not sit in a Lodge of clandestine-made Masons, nor converse on Masonry with a clandestine-made Mason, nor one who has been expelled or suspended from a Lodge while under that sentence, knowing them to be such.
Do not cheat, wrong, nor defraud a Master Mason's Lodge, nor a brother of this Degree, knowingly, nor supplant him in any laudable undertakings, but give due and timely notice to ward off danger.
Do not knowingly strike a brother Master Mason or do him personal violence in anger, except in the necessary defense of family or property.
Do not have illegal carnal intercourse with a Master Mason's wife, mother, sister, or daughter, nor suffer the same to be done by others if in power to prevent.
Do not give the Grand Masonic word in any other manner or form than that in which it is received, and then in a low breath.

Do not give the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress except in case of the most imminent danger, in a just and lawful Lodge, or for the benefit of instruction. If seen or heard from a worthy brother in distress, fly to his relief if there is a greater probability of saving his life than losing one's own.

The Worshipful Master explicitly warns the candidate of 'amazing trials and dangers', a 'rough and rugged road to travel, beset with thieves, robbers, and murderers', and the possibility of losing his life, stating it 'will not be the first instance of the kind'.
Guard uninformed brethren against a breach of fidelity.
Let no motive make you swerve from your duty, violate your vows, or betray your trust.
Candidates are cautioned to be careful not to forfeit the esteem of the brethren through misconduct and inattention to the rules of the Lodge.
The candidate is cautioned against forfeiting the brethren's esteem by misconduct and inattention to the rules.
The penalty of the obligation for this degree involves having one's breast torn open, heart torn out, and exposed to rot upon the dung hill, if the secrets are divulged unlawfully.

Guards were stationed to take 'special pains that none pass or repass, except such as were duly qualified,' indicating a strict requirement for proper authorization and qualification to enter sacred spaces or the presence of principal officers.
