entries.title: Masonic Glossary — Master Mason
entries.short_summary: This section provides a glossary of terms relevant to the Master Mason degree, defining words such as Approbation, Brute creation, Cleaves, Dotage, Emulation, Hecatomb, Injunction, Seraph, Speculative, Sublime, and Zeal.
entries.full_summary:
This section provides a glossary of terms relevant to the Master Mason degree, defining words such as Approbation, Brute creation, Cleaves, Dotage, Emulation, Hecatomb, Injunction, Seraph, Speculative, Sublime, and Zeal.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

This section introduces the Fellow Craft candidate to the concepts of Operative and Speculative Masonry, defining each and emphasizing the moral and religious aspects of Speculative Masonry, including the contemplation of creation and homage to Deity. It also details the historical practice of ancient brethren working in both forms and consecrating the seventh day for rest and contemplation. The section concludes with a biblical description of the two pillars of brass, their dimensions, and features like chapiters, network, and pomegranates, as they would appear on the passage to the Middle Chamber.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

The Second Section of the ritual recounts the Institution's historical traditions and presents an exemplary instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity. It emphasizes the importance of spreading brotherly love and affection, which serves as the cement uniting Masons into a sacred society where the only acceptable contention is a noble emulation to excel in work and agreement.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0092 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Sublime Degree of Master Mason

This section introduces the Sublime Degree of Master Mason, marking the entry point to the third degree of Freemasonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0092 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pronunciation Key

This section provides a pronunciation key for various words, many of which are common in Masonic ritual and discourse. It includes rules for indicating accented syllables and short vowel sounds, followed by an alphabetical list of words with their phonetic pronunciations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Beehive

This section describes several Masonic emblems and their moral and philosophical lessons. It begins with the Beehive, symbolizing industry, social dependence, and the importance of contributing to the common stock of knowledge. It then discusses the Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, which emphasizes watchfulness, silence, and circumspection, especially against enemies of Masonry. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart and the All-Seeing Eye are presented as symbols of divine justice and omnipresence, reminding Masons that all thoughts, words, and actions are observed and will be judged. Finally, the Anchor and Ark are explained as emblems of well-grounded hope and a well-spent life, guiding one through life's troubles to a peaceful harbor. The section also briefly mentions the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid and its connection to Pythagoras.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Royal Arch: Exaltation Ceremony

The Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony details the admission process for candidates seeking further light in Masonry. After an alarm at the Chapter door, candidates, who have previously attained the degrees of Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master, confirm their free will, preparation, and proficiency. They present a pass word, RABBONI, before being admitted under a 'Living Arch' formed by companions. This arch involves physical prostration and difficulty in passage. Candidates then kneel at the altar for their seventh obligation, a solemn oath covering secrecy, protection of companions, non-revelation of ineffable characters or the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word (except under specific conditions), avoidance of clandestine chapters, and strict adherence to procedural requirements such as exalting exactly three candidates at a time and requiring nine Royal Arch Masons to open a Chapter.

This section details the extensive obligation of a Royal Arch Mason, covering secrecy, prerequisites for candidates (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master), rules for exaltation (three candidates at a time, nine Masons for opening a Chapter), conduct towards fellow Companions (no speaking evil, apprising of danger, preference in employment, assistance in difficulty, keeping secrets, aiding the indigent), and adherence to constitutional laws and by-laws. The obligation concludes with a severe penalty for violation. Following the obligation, the ritual describes the candidate kissing the book seven times and the Principal Sojourner's address, emphasizing the importance of the Royal Arch secrets and the necessity of trials through 'rough and rugged ways' to test fidelity. The candidates are then led around the Chapter and kneel for a prayer to the 'Supreme and inscrutable Architect of universal Nature'.

This section describes a prayer offered during the Royal Arch Exaltation Ceremony, emphasizing gratitude for human reason, capacity for improvement, and the institution of Freemasonry. It highlights new obligations of virtue and holiness, reverence for God's name, and the diligent search for divine authority in nature. The prayer concludes with a plea for devotion, love, and charity, and for heavenly grace to assist in rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' after the 'first temple of purity and innocence' was destroyed by sin. Following the prayer, the Principal Sojourner leads candidates around the Chapter, reading the first six verses of Exodus chapter 3, which describe Moses encountering the burning bush on Mount Horeb. The candidates are then brought before a representation of the burning bush, and their blindfolds are removed.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0111 -->
practical_elements: Explanation of objects on the passage to the middle chamber.
Description of the two pillars of brass: eighteen cubits high, twelve cubits circumference, five cubit high chapiters, hollow, with network and pomegranates.
Ancient brethren wrought six days and rested on the seventh.
CAPITAL letters indicate accented syllables
a - short a as in the word bat
i - short i as in the word ill or is
e - short e as in the word ethic
(e) - barely audible short e
u - short u as in the word but
Seven distinct knocks are given at the Chapter door.
The Captain of the Host raps nine times, answered by the Principal Sojourner with three times three, then one rap, which the Captain answers with one.
The Principal Sojourner whispers the word RABBONI as a pass.
Candidates are led by a rope upon entry.
Companions form a 'Living Arch' by creating two lines facing each other and locking fingers.
As candidates pass under the Living Arch, companions place knuckles on their necks and backs, kneading them and prostrating candidates on the floor.
Candidates kneel at the altar to receive the obligation.
The obligation is the seventh taken by the candidate in their Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken only in the presence of three Royal Arch Masons (including the speaker), by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltation of candidates must be exactly three at one time.
A Chapter of Royal Arch Masons requires nine Royal Arch Masons to form or open.
Obligation taken in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, candidate making one.
Secrets received by 'three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
Candidate must answer and obey all due signs and summonses within the length of their cable-tow.
Candidate kisses the book seven times.
Candidates are conducted once around the Chapter.
Candidates kneel for a prayer read by the Principal Sojourner.
A prayer is recited during the ceremony.
Candidates are led once around the Chapter by the Principal Sojourner.
The Principal Sojourner reads the first six verses of the third chapter of Exodus.
A representation of the burning bush is placed in a corner of the Chapter.
Candidates are directed to halt in front of the burning bush representation.
Bandages (blindfolds) are removed from the candidates' eyes by the Principal Sojourner.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Terms like 'Seraph' and 'Sublime' carry significant spiritual and moral connotations, representing divine orders and exalted states of being or thought. 'Brute creation' can symbolize the unrefined state of humanity, while 'Dotage' may allude to themes of mortality and the transient nature of physical existence, which are relevant to the Master Mason degree.

Operative Masonry refers to the proper application of architectural rules for structure, figure, strength, beauty, proportion, and correspondence. Speculative Masonry teaches to subdue passions, act upon the square, maintain a good report, secrecy, and charity. It is interwoven with religion, obliging Masons to pay rational homage to Deity, contemplate the glorious works of creation, and adore the divine Creator. The seventh day of rest is consecrated for contemplation of creation and adoration of the Creator.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.

The term 'Sublime Degree' signifies the elevated moral and philosophical lessons conveyed within the Master Mason degree, often associated with the culmination of Craft Masonry's teachings.

The Beehive symbolizes industry, mutual dependence, and the necessity for all rational beings to contribute to society and knowledge, avoiding idleness. The Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, represents the need for vigilance, discretion, and protection of Masonic principles against external threats. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart signifies that divine justice will ultimately prevail, revealing all hidden thoughts and actions. The All-Seeing Eye embodies the omnipresence and omniscience of the Great Creator, who observes the innermost recesses of the human heart and rewards according to one's deeds. The Anchor and Ark are emblems of a steadfast hope and a virtuous life, symbolizing safe passage through life's challenges to a state of eternal peace and rest.

The 'Living Arch' symbolizes the difficult and humbling path to exaltation, requiring perseverance and submission. The physical prostration and kneading represent the trials and purification necessary for advancement. Kneeling for the seventh time at the altar signifies the increasing solemnity and binding nature of the obligations as one progresses through Masonic degrees. The phrase 'he that humbleth himself shall be exalted' directly links the physical act of stooping low under the arch to the spiritual lesson of humility leading to higher understanding. The journey through darkness and crooked paths made straight ('I will bring the blind by a way they know not...') suggests divine guidance and enlightenment provided through the Masonic path.

The 'rough and rugged ways' and 'many trials' symbolize the challenges and tests of fidelity a candidate must undergo to prove their worthiness for the deeper secrets of the Royal Arch Degree. The 'stupendous arch of heaven' mentioned in the prayer symbolizes the divine architectural power of the Supreme Architect of universal Nature.

The prayer symbolizes a deep adoration for divine perfection, gratitude for the gifts of reason and improvement, and a commitment to virtue and holiness. The 'second temple of reformation' represents the spiritual rebuilding of the individual's character after the loss of original purity. The burning bush, where God appeared to Moses, symbolizes divine presence, revelation, and the sacred nature of the encounter, signifying a moment of profound spiritual awakening and instruction for the candidate.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns key vocabulary and concepts that enrich their understanding of the Master Mason degree, its allegories, and the broader philosophical principles of Freemasonry. Understanding these terms is crucial for comprehending the deeper lessons and language of the Craft.

The candidate learns the distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry, understanding that modern Freemasonry is primarily Speculative, focusing on moral and spiritual development. They are taught the importance of self-governance, ethical conduct (acting upon the square, good report, secrecy, charity), and a deep reverence for the Creator and His creation, including the practice of setting aside time for rest and contemplation.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.

The Master Mason degree represents the highest point of Craft Masonry, preparing the candidate for deeper contemplation of life, death, and immortality.

Candidates are taught the importance of industry, social responsibility, and mutual support, emphasizing that they should not be 'drones' but actively contribute to the common good and knowledge. They are instructed to be watchful, guarded in their conduct, and to practice silence and circumspection, particularly when facing those hostile to Masonry. The emblems also impart the lesson of divine justice and accountability, urging Masons to live righteously, knowing that all their actions and thoughts are observed by a higher power. Finally, they are encouraged to cultivate a well-grounded hope and lead a well-spent life, anticipating a peaceful end to their earthly journey.

Candidates learn the importance of humility and perseverance in their Masonic journey, symbolized by the 'Living Arch.' They are taught the paramount importance of secrecy regarding the Royal Arch degree's secrets, including its ineffable characters and the Grand Omnific Word. The obligation reinforces loyalty to the Chapter and fellow companions, prohibiting wronging them or speaking ill of them, and requiring warning of danger. Candidates are instructed on the strict procedural requirements for the Royal Arch degree, including prerequisites, the number of candidates for exaltation, and the quorum for opening a Chapter. The repeated obligations emphasize the cumulative and increasingly binding nature of Masonic commitments, always subordinate to duties to country and Maker.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of fidelity, secrecy, and mutual support within the Royal Arch. They learn the strict procedural requirements for the degree and the extensive moral and fraternal duties expected of a Royal Arch Mason, including charity, assistance, and adherence to Masonic law. The journey through 'rough and rugged ways' emphasizes that true understanding and higher secrets are earned through perseverance and proven commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of gratitude, humility, and adherence to new and stronger obligations of virtue and holiness. They are encouraged to seek divine wisdom in the 'book of nature' and to allow the solemnity of the ceremonies to profoundly impact their lives. The narrative of Moses and the burning bush instructs them in reverence for God's 'great, mighty, and terrible Name,' inspiring devotion, love, and charity, and emphasizing the continuous process of personal reformation and spiritual rebuilding.
tradition_notes: The term 'Hecatomb' refers to an ancient Greek tradition of a public sacrifice of 100 oxen to the gods, typically in thanks for a great discovery, event, or victory.
Freemasonry is considered under two denominations: Operative and Speculative.
Ancient brethren wrought in both Operative and Speculative Masonry.
Modern Masons work in Speculative Masonry only.
Ancient brethren consecrated the seventh day as a day of rest, following God's creation pattern.
The Second Section recites the historical traditions of the Institution.
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is attributed to our ancient friend and brother, the great Pythagoras, who was initiated into several orders during his travels through Asia, Africa, and Europe.
The Royal Arch degree is referred to as the 'august sublime Degree of the Holy Royal Arch'.
Candidates must have received the degrees of Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, and Most Excellent Master as prerequisites.
The Chapter is dedicated to Zerubbabel.
The obligation taken is the seventh in the candidate's Masonic journey.
The Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word is to be spoken by three companions, by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.
Exaltations must involve exactly three candidates at a time.
A minimum of nine Royal Arch Masons are required to form or open a Chapter.
The Royal Arch secrets are considered 'infinitely more importance than any that precede it'.
The obligation is taken 'under a Living Arch, and at low breath'.
The General Grand Royal Arch Chapter of the United States of America and State Grand Chapters are recognized authorities.
The concept of 'cable-tow' is applied to the Royal Arch degree for answering summonses.
The reading from Exodus chapter 3, verses 1-6, is a specific part of the ritual, timed to coincide with the candidates' arrival at the burning bush representation.
The concept of rebuilding a 'second temple of reformation' is a significant allegorical theme within this degree, contrasting with a 'first temple of purity and innocence' lost through sin.
caution_notes: No contention should ever exist among members of the society of friends and brothers.
Masons should be ever watchful and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions, particularly when before the enemies of Masonry.
Justice will sooner or later overtake us, and our hidden thoughts, words, and actions are beheld by the All-Seeing Eye.

I will not reveal the secrets of this Degree to any of an inferior Degree, nor to any being in the known world, except it be to a true and lawful Companion Royal Arch Mason, or within the body of a just and legally constituted Chapter of such and never unto him, or them, whom I shall hear so to be, but to him and them only whom I shall find so to be, after strict trial and due examination, or lawful information given.
I will not wrong this Chapter of Royal Arch Masons, or a companion of this Degree, out of the value of any thing, myself, nor suffer it to be done by others, if in my power to prevent it.
I will not reveal the key to the ineffable characters of this Degree, nor retain it in my possession, but will destroy it whenever it comes to my sight.

I will not speak the Grand Omnific Royal Arch Word, which I shall hereafter receive, in any manner, except in that in which I shall receive it, which will be in the presence of three Companions Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of the number; and then by three times three, under a Living Arch, and at low breath.

I will not be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter, nor converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or with one who has been expelled or suspended, while under that sentence.

I will not assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate to this Degree, who has not received the Degrees of: Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason, Mark Master, Past Master, Most Excellent Master.
I will not be at the exaltation of more nor less than three candidates at one and the same time.
I will not be at the forming or opening of a Chapter of Royal Arch Masons unless there be present nine Royal Arch Masons, myself making one of that number.
I will not speak evil of a Companion Royal Arch Mason, behind his back nor before his face, but will apprise him of all approaching danger, if in my power.
Penalty for violating the obligation: 'skull smote off, and my brains exposed to the scorching rays of the meridian sun'.
Not to be at the exaltation of candidates in a clandestine Chapter.
Not to converse upon the secrets of this Degree with a clandestine-made Mason, or one who has been expelled or suspended.
Not to assist or be present at the exaltation of a candidate who has not received the prerequisite degrees.
Not to strike a Companion Royal Arch Mason so as to draw his blood, in anger.

entries.title: The Bee Hive
entries.short_summary: The Bee Hive is presented as an emblem of industry, teaching the universal virtue of diligence and the importance of actively assisting fellow beings in need when it is within one's power to do so without personal inconvenience.
entries.full_summary: The Bee Hive is presented as an emblem of industry, teaching the universal virtue of diligence and the importance of actively assisting fellow beings in need when it is within one's power to do so without personal inconvenience.
practical_elements: Practice industry in all endeavors.
Do not remain content while others are in want.
Relieve fellow creatures in need when possible without inconvenience.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Bee Hive symbolizes industry and the collective effort of a community. It represents the virtue of diligence and the universal obligation to be productive and helpful to others.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates are taught to be industrious, rational, and intelligent beings who actively contribute to society and provide relief to those in need, reflecting a commitment to universal benevolence.

entries.title: All-seeing Eye
entries.short_summary: The All-seeing Eye represents the omnipresent divine being who governs the cosmos, observes the deepest thoughts of the human heart, and will justly reward individuals based on their merits.
entries.full_summary:
The All-seeing Eye represents the omnipresent divine being who governs the cosmos, observes the deepest thoughts of the human heart, and will justly reward individuals based on their merits.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Beehive

This section describes several Masonic emblems and their moral and philosophical lessons. It begins with the Beehive, symbolizing industry, social dependence, and the importance of contributing to the common stock of knowledge. It then discusses the Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, which emphasizes watchfulness, silence, and circumspection, especially against enemies of Masonry. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart and the All-Seeing Eye are presented as symbols of divine justice and omnipresence, reminding Masons that all thoughts, words, and actions are observed and will be judged. Finally, the Anchor and Ark are explained as emblems of well-grounded hope and a well-spent life, guiding one through life's troubles to a peaceful harbor. The section also briefly mentions the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid and its connection to Pythagoras.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0074 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Scythe

This section describes the Scythe as an emblem of time and mortality, emphasizing its role in cutting the thread of life and leading to eternity. It then introduces other emblems found on the carpet, including the Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Each emblem is linked to the death of the Grand Master and serves as a reminder of the inevitability of death for all, while the acacia symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of resurrection into a celestial Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0074 -->
practical_elements: The emblems discussed (Scythe, Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, Sprig of Acacia) are presented as being 'on the carpet', indicating their use as visual teaching aids within the lodge ritual.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The All-seeing Eye symbolizes the omnipresent and omniscient Great Architect of the Universe, who oversees all creation, from celestial bodies to the innermost thoughts and intentions of humanity. It signifies divine providence, watchfulness, and ultimate justice.

The Beehive symbolizes industry, mutual dependence, and the necessity for all rational beings to contribute to society and knowledge, avoiding idleness. The Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, represents the need for vigilance, discretion, and protection of Masonic principles against external threats. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart signifies that divine justice will ultimately prevail, revealing all hidden thoughts and actions. The All-Seeing Eye embodies the omnipresence and omniscience of the Great Creator, who observes the innermost recesses of the human heart and rewards according to one's deeds. The Anchor and Ark are emblems of a steadfast hope and a virtuous life, symbolizing safe passage through life's challenges to a state of eternal peace and rest.

The Scythe symbolizes time, which inevitably cuts the thread of life, leading to eternity. The Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, and Grave are emblems of mortality, reminding candidates of the certainty of their own death, mirroring the fate of the Grand Master. The Sprig of Acacia, an evergreen, symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul, which never dies, and marks the temporary resting place of the illustrious dead, leading to discovery.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates are taught that their actions and even their secret thoughts are constantly observed by a divine power, and that they will be held accountable and rewarded according to their merits. This encourages moral conduct and integrity.

Candidates are taught the importance of industry, social responsibility, and mutual support, emphasizing that they should not be 'drones' but actively contribute to the common good and knowledge. They are instructed to be watchful, guarded in their conduct, and to practice silence and circumspection, particularly when facing those hostile to Masonry. The emblems also impart the lesson of divine justice and accountability, urging Masons to live righteously, knowing that all their actions and thoughts are observed by a higher power. Finally, they are encouraged to cultivate a well-grounded hope and lead a well-spent life, anticipating a peaceful end to their earthly journey.

Candidates are admonished to be ever mindful of the great change of death, when they will be called from earthly labors to everlasting refreshment in the paradise of God. They are encouraged to live worthily, so that after death and resurrection, they may gain admission into the celestial Lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the Universe.
tradition_notes: The concept emphasizes a divine being whose authority extends over all celestial bodies and whose perception penetrates the deepest parts of human consciousness.
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is attributed to our ancient friend and brother, the great Pythagoras, who was initiated into several orders during his travels through Asia, Africa, and Europe.
The mention of emblems 'on the carpet' refers to the use of tracing boards or floor cloths in Masonic lodges to illustrate symbolic lessons.
The narrative connects the emblems directly to the 'Grand Master's grave' and its 'timely discovery', which is a central element of the Hiramic legend in the Master Mason degree.
caution_notes: Masons should be ever watchful and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions, particularly when before the enemies of Masonry.
Justice will sooner or later overtake us, and our hidden thoughts, words, and actions are beheld by the All-Seeing Eye.

entries.title: The Anchor and Ark
entries.short_summary: The Anchor and Ark are presented as significant emblems within Freemasonry, symbolizing a well-grounded hope and a life well-spent. They represent a divine ark that safely navigates individuals through life's challenges and an anchor that provides a secure, peaceful harbor where one can find rest from worldly troubles.
entries.full_summary:
The Anchor and Ark are presented as significant emblems within Freemasonry, symbolizing a well-grounded hope and a life well-spent. They represent a divine ark that safely navigates individuals through life's challenges and an anchor that provides a secure, peaceful harbor where one can find rest from worldly troubles.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Historical Account.

This section provides a detailed historical account of the murder of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, a pivotal event in the Master Mason degree. It describes a conspiracy by fifteen, then three, Fellow Crafts (Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum) to extort the Master Mason's secrets for wages and travel. Hiram Abiff's daily ritual of offering adorations and drawing designs in the Sanctum Sanctorum is detailed, as are his attempts to escape through the south, west, and east gates, where he was successively accosted and struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge, a square, and a setting-maul, leading to his death. The ruffians buried his body west of Mount Moriah, marking the grave with an acacia. King Solomon's subsequent discovery of Hiram's absence, the confession of the twelve recanting Fellow Crafts, and the organized search for the ruffians are recounted. The ruffians were eventually found near Joppa after their self-imprecations were overheard, apprehended, and executed. The narrative concludes with the discovery of Hiram's putrefied body by the twelve Fellow Crafts, noting the specific gesture of the Master Mason's duegard.

This section details the discovery and raising of Hiram Abiff's body after his murder. It describes the failed attempts to raise the body using the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips due to decomposition, and the successful raising by King Solomon using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw, on the five points of fellowship. The narrative includes the agreement between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, to use the first sigh and word spoken at the grave as a temporary Master's word until the true one is found. The text also describes the three burials of Hiram Abiff and the traditional Masonic monument erected to his memory, detailing its symbolic elements: a weeping virgin, a broken column, an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Father Time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0069 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Beehive

This section describes several Masonic emblems and their moral and philosophical lessons. It begins with the Beehive, symbolizing industry, social dependence, and the importance of contributing to the common stock of knowledge. It then discusses the Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, which emphasizes watchfulness, silence, and circumspection, especially against enemies of Masonry. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart and the All-Seeing Eye are presented as symbols of divine justice and omnipresence, reminding Masons that all thoughts, words, and actions are observed and will be judged. Finally, the Anchor and Ark are explained as emblems of well-grounded hope and a well-spent life, guiding one through life's troubles to a peaceful harbor. The section also briefly mentions the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid and its connection to Pythagoras.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0074 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Scythe

This section describes the Scythe as an emblem of time and mortality, emphasizing its role in cutting the thread of life and leading to eternity. It then introduces other emblems found on the carpet, including the Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Each emblem is linked to the death of the Grand Master and serves as a reminder of the inevitability of death for all, while the acacia symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of resurrection into a celestial Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0074 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0080 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Third Section.

This section, presented in a Q&A format, concludes the Master Mason's Degree by reviewing key aspects of King Solomon's Temple, including its construction duration, structural supports (columns and pillars of Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty), and the workforce involved. It details the constitution and usual meeting places for Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason lodges. The section then enumerates two classes of emblems specific to the Master Mason Degree, such as the Pot of Incense, Beehive, All-seeing Eye, Anchor and Ark, Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid, Hour-Glass, Scythe, Three Steps, Setting-Maul, Spade, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Finally, it addresses the lost word of a Master Mason, its substitute 'Mah-Hah-Bone' given on the five points of fellowship, and notes that the original word was later found and used in the Royal Arch Degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0080 -->
practical_elements: The Master Mason candidate represents Grand Master Hiram Abiff during the exemplification of the degree.
Hiram Abiff's daily practice involved entering the unfinished Sanctum Sanctorum to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The three ruffians positioned themselves at the south, west, and east gates of the inner courts of the Temple.
Hiram Abiff was struck with a twenty-four-inch gauge across his throat, a square across his breast, and a setting-maul on his forehead.
The ruffians buried Hiram's body in the rubbish of the Temple until low-twelve, then moved it to a grave dug due east and west, six feet perpendicular, west of Mount Moriah.
An acacia was planted at the head of Hiram's grave to conceal it and mark the place.
The twelve recanting Fellow Crafts appeared before King Solomon clothed in white gloves and aprons as a token of their innocence.
Search parties were divided into groups of three, traveling east, west, north, and south.
The Master places his hands in the form of a duegard of a Master Mason, alluding to the manner in which Hiram's body was found.
Attempted raising of the body using the Entered Apprentice grip
Attempted raising of the body using the Fellow Craft grip
Successful raising of the body using the strong grip of a Master Mason, or lion's paw
Raising on the five points of fellowship
Explanation of the several signs and tokens of the Master Mason Degree, commencing with the first and ending with the grips (referencing Figs. 5, 6, 7, 16, 17 and Note L, Appendix)
The emblems discussed (Scythe, Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, Sprig of Acacia) are presented as being 'on the carpet', indicating their use as visual teaching aids within the lodge ritual.
King Solomon's Temple was built in seven years, during which it did not rain in the daytime to avoid obstructing workmen.
The Temple was supported by 1453 columns and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
An Entered Apprentices' Lodge is constituted by seven members: one Master and six Entered Apprentices, meeting on the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
A Fellow Crafts' Lodge is constituted by five members: two Masters and three Fellow Crafts, meeting in the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
A Masters' Lodge is constituted by three Master Masons, meeting in the Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies, of King Solomon's Temple.
The substitute word 'Mah-Hah-Bone' is given on the five points of fellowship and in low breath.
The missing word was found after 470 years and is used in the Royal Arch Degree.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Anchor and Ark symbolize a well-grounded Hope and a well-spent life. The Ark is emblematical of a divine vessel that safely carries individuals over the 'tempestuous sea of troubles,' while the Anchor represents a secure mooring in a 'peaceful harbor' where the 'wicked cease from troubling and the weary shall find rest.'

The narrative exemplifies virtue, fortitude, and integrity through Hiram Abiff's unwavering refusal to betray his trust and the secrets of a Master Mason, even unto death. The acacia planted at the grave symbolizes remembrance and potentially immortality. The entire account serves as a profound allegory for fidelity, the consequences of treachery, and the eventual triumph of justice.

The beautiful virgin weeping over the broken column symbolizes the unfinished state of the Temple and the untimely death of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The open book before her signifies that his virtues are perpetually recorded. The sprig of acacia in her right hand represents the divinity of the body. The urn in her left hand indicates that his ashes were safely deposited near the Sanctum Sanctorum. Time, unfolding the ringlets of her hair, denotes that time, patience, and perseverance accomplish all things. The failure of the Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft grips to raise the body, and the success of the Master Mason grip, symbolizes the progression of knowledge and the ultimate power of the Master's degree.

The Beehive symbolizes industry, mutual dependence, and the necessity for all rational beings to contribute to society and knowledge, avoiding idleness. The Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, represents the need for vigilance, discretion, and protection of Masonic principles against external threats. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart signifies that divine justice will ultimately prevail, revealing all hidden thoughts and actions. The All-Seeing Eye embodies the omnipresence and omniscience of the Great Creator, who observes the innermost recesses of the human heart and rewards according to one's deeds. The Anchor and Ark are emblems of a steadfast hope and a virtuous life, symbolizing safe passage through life's challenges to a state of eternal peace and rest.

The Scythe symbolizes time, which inevitably cuts the thread of life, leading to eternity. The Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, and Grave are emblems of mortality, reminding candidates of the certainty of their own death, mirroring the fate of the Grand Master. The Sprig of Acacia, an evergreen, symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul, which never dies, and marks the temporary resting place of the illustrious dead, leading to discovery.

The three grand columns or pillars supporting the Temple are named Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty. Wisdom represents King Solomon, who conceived the Temple; Strength represents Hiram, King of Tyre, who supported the undertaking; and Beauty represents Hiram Abiff, whose craftsmanship adorned the Temple. The emblems of the Master Mason Degree, divided into two classes, serve as symbolic lessons, though their specific explanations are noted as monitorial. The Sprig of Acacia is a significant emblem. The lost word and its substitute, Mah-Hah-Bone, symbolize a profound truth or secret that was lost and later replaced, with the original word eventually being recovered in a higher degree.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The emblems of the Anchor and Ark teach the importance of cultivating a strong, unwavering hope and living a virtuous, well-spent life. This path allows one to navigate life's difficulties with divine guidance and ultimately achieve a state of peace and rest.

The candidate is taught the paramount importance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity, as demonstrated by Grand Master Hiram Abiff's ultimate sacrifice. It underscores the solemnity of Masonic obligations and the sacredness of its secrets, emphasizing that these principles must be upheld at all costs. The narrative also conveys lessons on the inevitable consequences of betrayal and the pursuit of justice.

The candidate learns about the profound loss of the Master's word and the temporary substitute established. The narrative emphasizes the importance of the Master Mason's strong grip and the five points of fellowship in restoring what was lost. The monument's symbolism teaches lessons of virtue, remembrance, the transient nature of life, and the enduring power of patience and perseverance.

Candidates are taught the importance of industry, social responsibility, and mutual support, emphasizing that they should not be 'drones' but actively contribute to the common good and knowledge. They are instructed to be watchful, guarded in their conduct, and to practice silence and circumspection, particularly when facing those hostile to Masonry. The emblems also impart the lesson of divine justice and accountability, urging Masons to live righteously, knowing that all their actions and thoughts are observed by a higher power. Finally, they are encouraged to cultivate a well-grounded hope and lead a well-spent life, anticipating a peaceful end to their earthly journey.

Candidates are admonished to be ever mindful of the great change of death, when they will be called from earthly labors to everlasting refreshment in the paradise of God. They are encouraged to live worthily, so that after death and resurrection, they may gain admission into the celestial Lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the Universe.

The candidate learns about the historical and structural grandeur of King Solomon's Temple, emphasizing the collaborative effort and diverse skills required for its construction. The pillars of Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty teach the necessity of these virtues in any great undertaking. The constitution and meeting places of the different lodges reinforce the hierarchical structure and progression within Masonry. The emblems provide moral and philosophical instruction, while the narrative of the lost word and its substitute underscores themes of loss, perseverance, and the eventual recovery of truth, encouraging continued pursuit of Masonic knowledge.
tradition_notes: It was Hiram Abiff's usual practice to enter the unfinished 'Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies,' at high twelve to offer adorations and draw designs on the trestle-board.
The ruffians, Jubela, Jubelo, and Jubelum, were believed to be brothers and men from Tyre.
The Master Mason's secrets were traditionally believed to allow travel in foreign countries and the reception of Master's wages.
Hiram Abiff's body was buried 'in a grave dug due east and west, six feet, perpendicular, at the head of which they planted an acacia'.
The twelve Fellow Crafts who recanted their murderous designs appeared before King Solomon 'clothed in white gloves and aprons, in token of their innocence'.
The specific imprecations uttered by the ruffians (e.g., 'Oh! that my throat had been cut across') are part of the traditional narrative.
The 'duegard of a Master Mason' gesture is explicitly linked to the discovery of Hiram Abiff's body.

It was agreed between King Solomon and Hiram, King of Tyre, that the first sigh given at the grave and the first word spoken after the body was raised would be used as the Master's word until the right one is found.
Masonic tradition informs us of a monument erected to Hiram Abiff's memory, consisting of a beautiful virgin weeping over a broken column, with an open book, a sprig of acacia, an urn, and Time.
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is attributed to our ancient friend and brother, the great Pythagoras, who was initiated into several orders during his travels through Asia, Africa, and Europe.
The mention of emblems 'on the carpet' refers to the use of tracing boards or floor cloths in Masonic lodges to illustrate symbolic lessons.
The narrative connects the emblems directly to the 'Grand Master's grave' and its 'timely discovery', which is a central element of the Hiramic legend in the Master Mason degree.
It is a tradition that during the seven years of the Temple's construction, it did not rain in the daytime.
The word 'Mah-Hah-Bone' is presented as the substitute for the lost word in the Master Mason's Degree.
The original lost word was reportedly found after 470 years and is used in the Royal Arch Degree.
caution_notes: The text describes a 'horrid conspiracy' and 'murderous designs' by Fellow Crafts, serving as a strong caution against treachery and the violation of Masonic trust.
The execution of the ruffians 'according to the several imprecations upon their own heads' highlights the severe and just consequences for such heinous crimes within the Masonic tradition.
Masons should be ever watchful and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions, particularly when before the enemies of Masonry.
Justice will sooner or later overtake us, and our hidden thoughts, words, and actions are beheld by the All-Seeing Eye.

entries.title: The Forty-seventh
entries.short_summary: This section introduces 'The Forty-seventh', likely referring to the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid, a significant emblem in Freemasonry.
entries.full_summary:
This section introduces 'The Forty-seventh', likely referring to the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid, a significant emblem in Freemasonry.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0047 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Lecture: Middle Chamber Work

This section details the Fellow Craft's journey to the Middle Chamber, beginning with the encounter of the two pillars, Boaz and Jachin, at the entrance of King Solomon's Temple. It describes their names, symbolic meanings (strength and establishment), dimensions, composition (molten brass), and adornments, including chapiters, net-work, lily-work, pomegranates (denoting unity, peace, and plenty), and globes (representing the universality of Masonry). The narrative then proceeds to the winding stairs, explaining the symbolism of its three, five, and seven steps, which allude to Masonic supports, human life stages, degrees in Masonry, orders of architecture, human senses (emphasizing hearing, seeing, feeling for recognition), and the seven liberal arts and sciences, respectively. The section concludes with the candidate reaching the outer door of the Middle Chamber, tyled by the Junior Warden, and the method of gaining admission.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0047 -->
practical_elements: Passage through a long porch or alley.
Encountering two large columns or pillars, Boaz (left) and Jachin (right).
Pillars' initial dimensions: 35 cubits height, 12 cubits circumference, 4 cubits diameter.
Chapiters on pillars were 5 cubits in height.
Pillars adorned with wreaths of net-work, lily-work, and pomegranates.
Pillars adorned with two large globes or balls.
Entire height of pillars was 40 cubits.
Pillars contained maps and charts of celestial and terrestrial bodies.
Pillars composed of molten or cast brass.
Pillars cast by Hiram Abiff on the banks of the river Jordan, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zaredatha.
Pillars cast hollow with a thickness of four inches or a hand's breadth.
Ascending a long, winding staircase or flight of winding stairs, consisting of three, five, and seven steps.
Reaching the outer door of the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
The outer door was partly open but closely tyled by the Junior Warden in the south.
Gaining admission by the pass and token of the pass of a Fellow Craft.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid (Pythagorean Theorem) symbolizes the demonstration of divine truth and the pursuit of knowledge through geometry and the liberal arts. It represents the stability and foundation of the Craft, emphasizing the importance of science and reason.

Boaz denotes strength and Jachin denotes establishment; collectively, they allude to the Scripture: "In strength shall this house be established." Net-work symbolizes union due to its connection. Lily-work symbolizes peace due to its whiteness and purity. Pomegranates symbolize plenty due to the exuberance of their seed. The globes on the pillars denote the universality of Masonry, implying a Mason's charity should be equally extensive. The hollow nature of the pillars symbolizes their ability to withstand inundations or conflagrations and their role in containing the archives of Masonry. The three steps of the winding stairs allude to the three principal supports in Masonry (wisdom, strength, beauty), the three stages in human life (youth, manhood, age), and the three degrees in Masonry (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason). The five steps allude to the five orders in architecture (Tuscan, Doric, Ionic, Corinthian, Composite) and the five human senses (hearing, seeing, feeling, smelling, tasting). The seven steps allude to various septenary concepts, but more especially the seven liberal arts and sciences (grammar, rhetoric, logic, arithmetic, geometry, music, astronomy), signifying the high estimation of the number seven among Masons.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid teaches the candidate the importance of geometry as a foundation for knowledge and the pursuit of truth, encouraging the study of the liberal arts and sciences to build a well-rounded character and understanding.

Masons should strive for charity as extensive as the universality of Masonry. The three principal supports of Masonry (wisdom, strength, beauty) are fundamental to the craft. The three stages of human life (youth, manhood, age) remind Masons of their journey and development. The three degrees of Masonry represent a progressive path of knowledge. The five human senses, especially hearing, seeing, and feeling, are essential for Masonic recognition and communication, allowing Masons to know each other in both light and darkness. The seven liberal arts and sciences are crucial for intellectual development and understanding, emphasizing the importance of education within Masonry.
tradition_notes: The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is a prominent emblem of a Past Master, often displayed on their jewel.
It is closely associated with the Fellow Craft degree's emphasis on geometry and the liberal arts and sciences.
The pillars Boaz and Jachin are named, and their individual and collective scriptural allusions are provided.
Hiram Abiff is identified as the Grand Master who cast the pillars and other holy vessels.
The specific location for casting the pillars is given as the banks of the river Jordan, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zaredatha.
The hollow nature of the pillars is explained as a means to withstand inundations or conflagrations and to contain the archives of Masonry.
The number seven has always been held in high estimation among Masons for various reasons, including its connection to the liberal arts and sciences.

entries.title: The Hour Glass
entries.short_summary: The Hour Glass is presented as a Masonic emblem symbolizing the swift and imperceptible passage of human life. It illustrates how quickly life draws to a close, comparing man's existence to the rapid flow of sand and the transient nature of worldly achievements, ultimately leading to a return to the earth.
entries.full_summary:
The Hour Glass is presented as a Masonic emblem symbolizing the swift and imperceptible passage of human life. It illustrates how quickly life draws to a close, comparing man's existence to the rapid flow of sand and the transient nature of worldly achievements, ultimately leading to a return to the earth.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0073 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Hour-glass

The hour-glass is presented as a powerful emblem of human life, illustrating its swift and imperceptible passage. It highlights how quickly our lives draw to a close, comparing the rapid depletion of sand to the transient nature of man's existence, from the tender leaves of hope and blossoming honors to the inevitable fall like autumn leaves.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0073 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0075 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Spade

This section introduces the spade as an emblem, leading into a Master Mason's lecture on the solemn thought of death and the promise of eternity. It reflects on the inevitability of death, the transient nature of life, and the Masonic principle of looking forward to greater light beyond the grave, sustained by faith and hope as revealed in the Bible. The lecture concludes with the Worshipful Master giving three raps, bringing the Lodge to its feet.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0075 -->
practical_elements: The Worshipful Master gives three raps with his gavel.
The whole Lodge comes to their feet in response to the raps.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Hour Glass symbolizes the brevity and fleeting nature of human life, demonstrating how quickly time passes and how rapidly our lives approach their end. It serves as a powerful reminder of mortality, likening man's life cycle from hope and blossoming honors to an inevitable fall, much like autumn leaves returning to the earth.

The hour-glass symbolizes the swift, imperceptible, and transient nature of human life. It serves as a reminder that life, much like the sand running through the glass, quickly passes, and that man's journey from hope and prosperity to its inevitable end is brief and fleeting, ultimately returning to the earth.

The spade, though not explicitly detailed in its physical symbolism here, serves as an emblem that prompts contemplation on mortality and the transition from earthly life to an "endless eternity." The lecture uses the imagery of sinking into a "fathomless abyss" of death, contrasted with the hope and joy offered by the Bible, which points beyond the grave to eternal scenes. It symbolizes the ultimate fate of man and the Masonic aspiration for immortality.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is taught to reflect on the transient nature of human existence and the certainty of mortality. This emblem encourages contemplation on the swift passage of time and the importance of considering one's life and aspirations in the context of its inevitable end.

The candidate is taught to reflect on the brevity and preciousness of human life. The hour-glass encourages contemplation on mortality, the rapid passage of time, and the importance of making the most of one's existence before it, like the sand, is completely exhausted.

The candidate is taught to confront the solemn reality of death and the transient nature of human existence. Despite the certainty of mortality, the lesson emphasizes the Masonic principle of looking "forward for greater light" and finding hope, joy, and sustenance in faith, particularly through the teachings of the Bible, which promises an eternity beyond the grave. It encourages a perspective that transcends the physical end of life.
tradition_notes: The Hour Glass is recognized as a traditional Masonic emblem.

The lecture on the spade and its associated themes of death and eternity is noted as an addition made by "Some Masters," suggesting it may not be universally included in all rituals but is a recognized traditional elaboration.

entries.title: Second Circuit
entries.short_summary: This section describes the 'Second Circuit' of a Masonic ritual, featuring a series of pronouncements from various officers (Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon) on the brevity, toil, and sorrow inherent in mortal life, culminating in the Chaplain's message of hope and steadfastness for those who trust in the Lord. The ritual concludes with the brethren halting, facing inward, and repeating the honors.
entries.full_summary:
This section describes the 'Second Circuit' of a Masonic ritual, featuring a series of pronouncements from various officers (Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon) on the brevity, toil, and sorrow inherent in mortal life, culminating in the Chaplain's message of hope and steadfastness for those who trust in the Lord. The ritual concludes with the brethren halting, facing inward, and repeating the honors.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mason.

This section details the duegard and sign of a Master Mason, which alludes to the penalty of the obligation, and the grand hailing sign of distress. It describes the physical actions for each sign, including the accompanying words for the grand hailing sign of distress, "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?" This sign is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff. The text also outlines the specific gavel raps used by the Master and Wardens to open a Lodge on the First, Second, and Third Degrees, concluding with the Master reciting a passage of Scripture (Psalm 133) about brethren dwelling in unity, followed by the brethren's response.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0009 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Degree: Closing Notes

This section concludes the Fellow Craft Degree, offering detailed explanations and historical context through footnotes. It explores the historical assertion of St. John's connection to the Essenes as a secret society conveying moral truths through symbols. The text describes a ritualistic gesture involving a rake across the breast, emphasizing the penalty of the obligation and symbolizing fortitude against adversity and the importance of fidelity to secrets. It introduces the Jewish Cabala's rule of Transposition, applying it to Hebrew words like ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) to signify moral and physical light. A comprehensive explanation of 'Shibboleth' is provided, detailing its dual meanings (ear of grain, stream of water) and its historical use by the Gileadites to identify Ephraimites based on speech peculiarities. Finally, it outlines the Fellow Craft Lecture, covering the passing ceremony, the distinction between operative and speculative Masonry, the study of liberal arts and sciences, and the legend of wages paid in the Middle Chamber, guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0049 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0053 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Master Mason Reception and Perambulation

This section details the formal reception of a candidate seeking the Master Mason degree at the inner door of the Lodge. It describes the sequence of knocks, the Senior Deacon's inquiry, and the Junior Deacon's affirmations regarding the candidate's free will, qualifications, preparation, proficiency in previous degrees, and proper vouching by a brother.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0053 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0087 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Mark Master: the Rejected Keystone

This section details a ritualistic sequence within the Mark Master degree where a candidate presents a keystone for inspection by the Junior, Senior, and Master Overseers. Despite its unique appearance, the keystone is repeatedly rejected for not being 'square work' and lacking a craftsman's mark. The Overseers ultimately agree to cast it aside as rubbish. The scene concludes with the workmen becoming impatient for their wages, leading to a song and a procession to receive payment.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0087 -->
practical_elements: Master delivers a pronouncement on life's brevity and calamities.
Senior Warden delivers a pronouncement on life as a fleeting vapor.
Junior Warden delivers a pronouncement on life as toil, sigh, sob, and breath.
Senior Deacon delivers a pronouncement on life falling like a leaf, bringing grief and sorrow.
Junior Deacon delivers a pronouncement on the heavy griefs of mortal life, decay, disappointment, and death.
Chaplain delivers a message of hope and steadfastness through trust in the Lord.
Brethren halt and face inward.
Honors are repeated as before.
Duegard of a Master: (Fig. 5) is mentioned as a visual representation.
Sign of a Master Mason: Draw the right hand (thumb in) across the stomach as low as the vest, then drop the hand suddenly (Fig. 6).
Grand Hailing Sign of Distress: Raise the hands as represented in Fig. 7 and drop them with spirit, repeated three times.
Words accompanying the Grand Hailing Sign of Distress (in night/dark): "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?"

Gavel raps for opening a Third Degree Lodge: Master gives one rap, Senior Warden one, Junior Warden one. Master gives one rap (second time), responded to by Wardens. Master gives one rap (third time), responded to by Wardens.
Gavel raps for opening a Second Degree Lodge: Two raps only.
Gavel raps for opening a First Degree Lodge: One rap each, first by Master, then Senior Warden, lastly Junior Warden.

Master removes hat and recites Psalm 133: "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! It is like the precious ointment upon the head, that ran down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard; that went down to the skirts of his garments; as the dew of Hermon, and as the dew that descended upon the mountains of Zion: for there the Lord commanded the blessing, even life forever more." Amen!
Brethren respond: "Amen! So mote it be!"
Gives candidate a rake across his breast with the hand to draw attention to the penalty.
Workmen's wages were paid daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly in their respective Lodges.
Wages were paid in the Middle Chamber when the Temple was nearly completed.
The foot of the winding staircase to the Middle Chamber was guarded by the Junior Warden, and the summit by the Senior Warden of a Fellow Crafts' Lodge.
The Junior Deacon leads the candidate by the left arm to the inner door of the Lodge.
The Junior Deacon gives three loud, distinct knocks at the inner door.
The Senior Deacon, stationed at the inner door to the right of the Senior Warden, rises and makes the sign of a Master Mason to the Worshipful Master upon hearing the knocks.
The Worshipful Master instructs the Senior Deacon to attend to the alarm and ascertain its cause.
The Senior Deacon gives three knocks, which are responded to by one knock from outside.
The Senior Deacon answers with one rap and opens the door.

A dialogue ensues between the Senior Deacon and Junior Deacon, confirming the candidate's identity (Brother Gabe), his progression through Entered Apprentice and Fellow Craft degrees, and his desire to be raised to the sublime Degree of a Master Mason.
The Senior Deacon asks if the candidate's request is of his own free-will and accord, to which the candidate affirms.
The Senior Deacon questions the Junior Deacon about the candidate's worthiness, qualification, preparation, proficiency in preceding degrees, and vouching.
The Junior Deacon affirms all inquiries, stating the candidate is worthy, well qualified, duly and truly prepared, has made suitable proficiency, and is properly vouched for by a brother.
Returning the block to the Senior Deacon after inspection.
Workmen moving six paces to position the candidate.
Junior Deacon instructing the candidate on how to make the alarm and present work.
Candidate presenting the keystone for inspection.
Junior Overseer applying a square to the keystone.
Junior Overseer pointing to letters on the keystone.
Candidate stating 'It is not' regarding the mark.
Passing on to the Senior Overseer at the west gate for further inspection.
Repeating the inspection scene at the Senior Overseer's station.
Proceeding to the Master Overseer at the east gate.
Senior Deacon presenting his block or stone to the Master Overseer.
Master Overseer applying his square to the Senior Deacon's work.
Master Overseer applying his square to the candidate's keystone.
Master Overseer stamping on the floor four times to summon other Overseers.
Master and Senior Overseers taking up the keystone.
Swinging the keystone four times back and forth.
Junior Overseer catching the keystone over the left shoulder of the Master Overseer (imitating the 'heave-over' sign).
Throwing the keystone aside as rubbish.
Brethren shuffling around the room, leaving their seats.
R. W. M. giving one rap with his gavel to restore order.
Lodge rising to their feet and singing a song.
Members forming two by two (candidate behind) and marching around the Lodge, singing.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The sequence of pronouncements from the officers symbolizes the universal human experience of life's transient nature, its inherent difficulties, and the inevitability of death. The Chaplain's response introduces the symbolic importance of faith and trust in a higher power as a source of enduring strength and resilience amidst life's challenges, likening it to a tree firmly rooted by water, unaffected by adversity.

The sign of a Master Mason alludes to the penalty of the obligation of a Master Mason. The grand hailing sign of distress, accompanied by the words "O Lord my God! is there no help for the widow's son?", is given by the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, symbolizing a plea for aid in dire circumstances and a connection to the central legend of the degree. The scripture passage (Psalm 133) emphasizes the importance of unity among brethren.

Secret societies like the Essenes conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures. The breast, as the abode of fortitude, teaches the candidate to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity with patience rather than betraying secrets. Hebrew words ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) are symbolically interpreted to mean fortifying oneself with higher sciences and preparing to receive moral light. Shibboleth signifies both an ear of grain and a stream of water, with its symbolic interpretation increasing interest as one advances through the 'field of corn by the river-side'. The payment of wages without fear or scruple symbolizes the employers' entitlement to unlimited confidence.

The rigorous questioning and affirmations at the inner door symbolize the thorough vetting process and the candidate's demonstrated readiness and worthiness to advance to the highest degree in Craft Masonry. The pursuit of 'further light' represents the candidate's aspiration for deeper knowledge and understanding within the Craft.

The rejected keystone, despite its 'singular form and beauty,' symbolizes work that does not conform to established Masonic standards or lacks proper identification. Its rejection and casting aside as rubbish emphasize the importance of precision, adherence to rules, and accountability for one's craft. The subsequent impatience for wages and the song about the 'Sabbath' highlight the completion of labor and the expectation of just reward, contrasting with the fate of the non-conforming work.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is taught to contemplate the fragility and brevity of mortal existence, acknowledging the sorrows and disappointments that accompany life. This contemplation is balanced by the lesson that steadfast hope and trust in the divine can provide inner strength, stability, and spiritual fruitfulness, enabling one to persevere through life's trials.

The candidate learns the specific signs and words associated with the Master Mason degree, particularly the solemnity of the obligation and the importance of recognizing and responding to a brother's distress. The ritual of opening the Lodge and the recitation of Psalm 133 reinforce the core Masonic value of unity and brotherly love, reminding members of the blessings that come from harmonious fellowship.

Candidates are taught to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity, distress, pain, or disappointment with patience. This fortitude prevents the bitter stings of remorse that result from betraying secrets entrusted under solemn obligation. The neophyte should prepare to receive moral light through higher sciences. Candidates are directed to an attentive study of the liberal arts and sciences. The legend of wages paid without fear or scruple teaches the importance of unlimited confidence in employers.

The candidate learns that advancement in Freemasonry is not automatic but requires personal free will, proven worthiness, proper preparation, and demonstrated proficiency in preceding degrees. It emphasizes the importance of merit, the structured progression within the Craft, and the fraternal support of being vouched for by a brother.

The candidate learns the critical importance of presenting work that is 'good work, true work, and square work,' adhering strictly to the craft's authorized standards and bearing the proper mark of a craftsman. It teaches about the consequences of attempting to pass off unauthorized or improperly marked work, and the necessity of personal integrity and accountability in all endeavors.
tradition_notes: The repetition of 'honors' suggests a specific traditional gesture or salute performed at certain points in the ritual.
The grand hailing sign of distress is specifically linked to the Master at the grave of Grand Master Hiram Abiff.
The specific sequence of gavel raps by the Master and Wardens is a traditional method for opening Lodges on different degrees.
The recitation of Psalm 133 and the response "Amen! So mote it be!" are traditional elements of the Lodge opening ceremony.
Calmet asserts that St. John was an Essene, a secret society of his day that conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures, which may be termed Freemasonry under another name.
The Jewish Cabala includes a rule called Transposition, used to find meaning in anagrammatically formed words.
Brother Rosenberg, a Jewish Mason, used Transposition to interpret ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) in Hebrew, relating them to receiving higher sciences and moral light.
SHIBBOLETH in Hebrew has two significations: an ear of grain and a stream of water.
The historical account of the Ephraimites and Gileadites illustrates how the word 'Shibboleth' was used as a test, exploiting a peculiarity in the Ephraimites' speech organs.

The Fellow Crafts' Degree Lecture's first section recapitulates the ceremonies of passing a candidate, and the second section discusses the ancient division into operative and speculative Masons and the liberal arts and sciences.

According to Masonic legends, workmen's wages were paid in their respective Lodges, and later in the Middle Chamber, which was accessible by a winding staircase guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.
The text references 'Note J, Appendix' regarding the knocking sequence, suggesting a traditional or specific procedural detail that would be elaborated upon in the appendix.
The ritual involves a specific sequence of inspection by three Overseers (Junior, Senior, Master) at different 'gates' (stations).
The 'heave-over' sign is a specific ritualistic gesture associated with casting aside the keystone.
The timing of the disturbance and the call for wages is traditionally set at 'the sixth hour of the sixth day of the week,' signifying the end of the work week.
The singing of a hymn and a procession for wages are traditional elements concluding this part of the ritual.
caution_notes: A rake across the breast is given to draw the candidate's attention to the penalty.
Betrayal of secrets entrusted on the faith of a solemn obligation will inevitably result in the bitter stings of remorse.

The Master Overseer's stern look and questioning ('Why do you bring another man's work to impose upon the Overseers?') serve as a caution against deception, presenting work that is not one's own, or attempting to circumvent established inspection processes.

entries.title: Charge to the Grand Orator
entries.short_summary: The Grand Orator is appointed and invested with the jewel of their office. Their primary duty is to deliver orations as requested by the Grand Master. This role requires the Orator to be deeply conversant with the tenets and teachings of the Institution, inspiring them to continuously seek knowledge and understanding to effectively enlighten the Craft.
entries.full_summary:
The Grand Orator is appointed and invested with the jewel of their office. Their primary duty is to deliver orations as requested by the Grand Master. This role requires the Orator to be deeply conversant with the tenets and teachings of the Institution, inspiring them to continuously seek knowledge and understanding to effectively enlighten the Craft.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Beehive

This section describes several Masonic emblems and their moral and philosophical lessons. It begins with the Beehive, symbolizing industry, social dependence, and the importance of contributing to the common stock of knowledge. It then discusses the Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, which emphasizes watchfulness, silence, and circumspection, especially against enemies of Masonry. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart and the All-Seeing Eye are presented as symbols of divine justice and omnipresence, reminding Masons that all thoughts, words, and actions are observed and will be judged. Finally, the Anchor and Ark are explained as emblems of well-grounded hope and a well-spent life, guiding one through life's troubles to a peaceful harbor. The section also briefly mentions the Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid and its connection to Pythagoras.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0071 -->
practical_elements: Appointment as Grand Orator
Investment with the jewel of office
Delivering orations upon request by the Grand Master
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The jewel of office symbolizes the authority and responsibility bestowed upon the Grand Orator. The act of delivering orations represents the communication and dissemination of Masonic knowledge and principles throughout the fraternity.

The Beehive symbolizes industry, mutual dependence, and the necessity for all rational beings to contribute to society and knowledge, avoiding idleness. The Book of Constitutions, guarded by the Tyler's Sword, represents the need for vigilance, discretion, and protection of Masonic principles against external threats. The Sword Pointing to a Naked Heart signifies that divine justice will ultimately prevail, revealing all hidden thoughts and actions. The All-Seeing Eye embodies the omnipresence and omniscience of the Great Creator, who observes the innermost recesses of the human heart and rewards according to one's deeds. The Anchor and Ark are emblems of a steadfast hope and a virtuous life, symbolizing safe passage through life's challenges to a state of eternal peace and rest.
entries.candidate_lesson:
While not directly for a candidate, this charge emphasizes the importance of continuous learning, deep understanding of Masonic tenets, and the duty to share knowledge to enlighten others within the Craft. It highlights that leadership roles require significant intellectual and moral commitment.

Candidates are taught the importance of industry, social responsibility, and mutual support, emphasizing that they should not be 'drones' but actively contribute to the common good and knowledge. They are instructed to be watchful, guarded in their conduct, and to practice silence and circumspection, particularly when facing those hostile to Masonry. The emblems also impart the lesson of divine justice and accountability, urging Masons to live righteously, knowing that all their actions and thoughts are observed by a higher power. Finally, they are encouraged to cultivate a well-grounded hope and lead a well-spent life, anticipating a peaceful end to their earthly journey.
tradition_notes: The appointment and investment of a Grand Orator with a specific jewel is a traditional practice within Grand Lodges.
The role of delivering orations on Masonic subjects is a long-standing tradition for this office.
The Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid is attributed to our ancient friend and brother, the great Pythagoras, who was initiated into several orders during his travels through Asia, Africa, and Europe.
caution_notes: Masons should be ever watchful and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions, particularly when before the enemies of Masonry.
Justice will sooner or later overtake us, and our hidden thoughts, words, and actions are beheld by the All-Seeing Eye.

entries.title: Viii. the Crowning Landmark
entries.short_summary: This section defines Masonic Landmarks as the fundamental and immutable principles that constitute the 'Great Body of Masonry'. It emphasizes that these Landmarks cannot be innovated upon, altered, added to, taken away from, repealed, modified, or changed in any way.
entries.full_summary: This section defines Masonic Landmarks as the fundamental and immutable principles that constitute the 'Great Body of Masonry'. It emphasizes that these Landmarks cannot be innovated upon, altered, added to, taken away from, repealed, modified, or changed in any way.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates learn that the foundational principles and laws of Freemasonry, known as Landmarks, are sacred and unchangeable. This instills a deep respect for the ancient traditions and the enduring structure of the fraternity, emphasizing fidelity to its core tenets.
tradition_notes: The text describes the traditional understanding of Masonic Landmarks as the unchangeable foundation of the fraternity, forming its 'Great Body'.
caution_notes: No innovation can be made to the Landmarks.
No alteration, either by adding to or taking away, can be allowed.
Landmarks cannot be repealed, cannot be modified, cannot be changed.
