entries.title: Hiram Abiff: Legend, Murder, Burial, and Discovery
entries.short_summary: This section details the ritualistic instruction for a candidate to approach the East, the symbolic 'place of further light in Masonry'. The Worshipful Master directs the Senior Deacon to have the Senior Warden teach the candidate how to perform three specific upright, regular steps to position themselves erect at the altar before the Master.
entries.full_summary: This section details the ritualistic instruction for a candidate to approach the East, the symbolic 'place of further light in Masonry'. The Worshipful Master directs the Senior Deacon to have the Senior Warden teach the candidate how to perform three specific upright, regular steps to position themselves erect at the altar before the Master.
practical_elements: The Worshipful Master orders the Senior Deacon to reconduct the candidate to the Senior Warden.
The Senior Deacon reports the Worshipful Master's orders to the Senior Warden.
The Senior Warden instructs the candidate to face the East (towards the Master).

The Senior Warden teaches the candidate to take three specific steps: 1) step off with the left foot, bringing the heel of the right foot into the hollow of the left; 2) step off with the right foot, bringing the heel of the left foot into the hollow of the right; 3) step off with the left foot, bringing both heels together.
The candidate is to maintain an erect body at the altar before the Worshipful Master in the East.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The act of approaching the East by three upright, regular steps symbolizes the candidate's progression towards greater Masonic knowledge and enlightenment, as the East is considered the 'place of further light'. The upright posture and regular steps represent rectitude, discipline, and a deliberate, structured advancement in Masonic principles.
entries.candidate_lesson: The candidate learns the precise ritualistic method of approaching the East, which signifies their journey towards understanding and their commitment to the established forms and traditions of Freemasonry. It emphasizes the importance of order, precision, and a respectful demeanor in the pursuit of Masonic light.

entries.title: The Trowel
entries.short_summary: This section details the procedure for calling the Lodge from labor to refreshment during an initiation ceremony. The Worshipful Master instructs the Junior Warden to declare 'High twelve' and call the craft to refreshment for a specified period. The Junior Warden then erects his column, gives three raps with his gavel, and announces the refreshment. It also describes the contrasting positions of the Junior and Senior Wardens' columns, indicating whether the Lodge is at labor or refreshment. During this time, the candidate is prepared in the ante-room, and the Lodge is made ready for the subsequent part of the ceremony, including the preparation of a large canvas.
entries.full_summary: This section details the procedure for calling the Lodge from labor to refreshment during an initiation ceremony. The Worshipful Master instructs the Junior Warden to declare 'High twelve' and call the craft to refreshment for a specified period. The Junior Warden then erects his column, gives three raps with his gavel, and announces the refreshment. It also describes the contrasting positions of the Junior and Senior Wardens' columns, indicating whether the Lodge is at labor or refreshment. During this time, the candidate is prepared in the ante-room, and the Lodge is made ready for the subsequent part of the ceremony, including the preparation of a large canvas.
practical_elements: Conductor and candidate exit the Lodge.
Worshipful Master gives three gavel sounds (• • •).
Worshipful Master asks Junior Warden the hour.
Junior Warden responds 'High twelve'.
Worshipful Master orders Junior Warden to erect his column and call the craft from labor to refreshment for thirty or fifteen minutes.
Junior Warden takes an eighteen-inch wooden column from his desk and sets it upright at his right hand.
Junior Warden gives three raps (• • •) with his gavel.
Junior Warden announces, 'Brethren, you are accordingly at refreshment.'
Junior Warden's column is upright when at refreshment, and horizontal when the Lodge is opened (at labor).
Senior Warden's column is horizontal when the Junior Warden's is up, and upright when the Lodge is opened (at labor).
Candidate is prepared in the ante-room.
Lodge is prepared for the remaining portion of the initiation ceremony, including a canvas seven feet long and six feet wide.
tradition_notes: The practice of calling the craft from labor to refreshment at 'High twelve' is a traditional Masonic procedure.
The use of columns by the Junior and Senior Wardens to signify the Lodge's state (labor or refreshment) is a traditional visual cue.

entries.title: Hiram Legend: Historical Recapitulation
entries.short_summary: This section describes two significant gestures within the Master Mason degree: the 'Hand to back' and 'Cheek to cheek, or mouth to ear.' Each gesture is accompanied by its symbolic meaning, emphasizing mutual assistance, character vindication, and brotherly counsel for reformation and protection from danger. The Master then instructs the candidate to proceed to the East to receive an historical account of the degree.
entries.full_summary: This section describes two significant gestures within the Master Mason degree: the 'Hand to back' and 'Cheek to cheek, or mouth to ear.' Each gesture is accompanied by its symbolic meaning, emphasizing mutual assistance, character vindication, and brotherly counsel for reformation and protection from danger. The Master then instructs the candidate to proceed to the East to receive an historical account of the degree.
practical_elements: The 'Hand to back' gesture is performed.
The 'Cheek to cheek, or mouth to ear' gesture is performed.
All brethren take their seats except the Master and candidate.
The Master instructs the candidate to repair to the East.
The Master takes his seat in the East and requests the candidate to stand before him.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The 'Hand to back' symbolizes a readiness to assist and save a fallen brother, and to vindicate his character both in his presence and absence. The 'Cheek to cheek, or mouth to ear' symbolizes the duty to caution and offer good counsel to an erring brother, to remind him of his errors in a friendly manner, aid his reformation, and provide timely notice to help him avoid approaching danger.
entries.candidate_lesson: The candidate is taught the importance of active brotherly love, which includes providing support, defending a brother's reputation, offering constructive criticism for personal improvement, and warning a brother of impending harm. These lessons emphasize loyalty, discretion, and mutual responsibility within the fraternity.

entries.title: The Scythe
entries.short_summary: This section describes the Scythe as an emblem of time and mortality, emphasizing its role in cutting the thread of life and leading to eternity. It then introduces other emblems found on the carpet, including the Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Each emblem is linked to the death of the Grand Master and serves as a reminder of the inevitability of death for all, while the acacia symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of resurrection into a celestial Lodge.
entries.full_summary: This section describes the Scythe as an emblem of time and mortality, emphasizing its role in cutting the thread of life and leading to eternity. It then introduces other emblems found on the carpet, including the Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Each emblem is linked to the death of the Grand Master and serves as a reminder of the inevitability of death for all, while the acacia symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of resurrection into a celestial Lodge.
practical_elements: The emblems discussed (Scythe, Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, Grave, Sprig of Acacia) are presented as being 'on the carpet', indicating their use as visual teaching aids within the lodge ritual.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Scythe symbolizes time, which inevitably cuts the thread of life, leading to eternity. The Spade, Setting-maul, Coffin, and Grave are emblems of mortality, reminding candidates of the certainty of their own death, mirroring the fate of the Grand Master. The Sprig of Acacia, an evergreen, symbolizes faith in the immortality of the soul, which never dies, and marks the temporary resting place of the illustrious dead, leading to discovery.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates are admonished to be ever mindful of the great change of death, when they will be called from earthly labors to everlasting refreshment in the paradise of God. They are encouraged to live worthily, so that after death and resurrection, they may gain admission into the celestial Lodge above, presided over by the Supreme Architect of the Universe.
tradition_notes: The mention of emblems 'on the carpet' refers to the use of tracing boards or floor cloths in Masonic lodges to illustrate symbolic lessons.
The narrative connects the emblems directly to the 'Grand Master's grave' and its 'timely discovery', which is a central element of the Hiramic legend in the Master Mason degree.

entries.title: Proclamation
entries.short_summary: This section details a ritual journey, beginning with the Proclamation of Cyrus calling for the rebuilding of the house of the Lord at Jerusalem. Candidates, referred to as Companions, express willingness to go but lack a password. The Captain of the Host provides the password "I AM THAT I AM," derived from Exodus, for them to identify themselves to brethren. The Companions then undertake a symbolic journey, passing three times under a "Living Arch" and over a "rugged road" made of various obstacles, symbolizing the difficulties faced by those who traveled from Babylon to Jerusalem. During this journey, the Principal Sojourner instructs them to stoop low, emphasizing humility, and leads them in prayers from Psalms cxii, cxlii, and cxliii. The journey concludes with their arrival at the ruins of the old Temple, near the outer veil of the Tabernacle, where an alarm is made by the Principal Sojourner to admit them.
entries.full_summary: This section details a ritual journey, beginning with the Proclamation of Cyrus calling for the rebuilding of the house of the Lord at Jerusalem. Candidates, referred to as Companions, express willingness to go but lack a password. The Captain of the Host provides the password "I AM THAT I AM," derived from Exodus, for them to identify themselves to brethren. The Companions then undertake a symbolic journey, passing three times under a "Living Arch" and over a "rugged road" made of various obstacles, symbolizing the difficulties faced by those who traveled from Babylon to Jerusalem. During this journey, the Principal Sojourner instructs them to stoop low, emphasizing humility, and leads them in prayers from Psalms cxii, cxlii, and cxliii. The journey concludes with their arrival at the ruins of the old Temple, near the outer veil of the Tabernacle, where an alarm is made by the Principal Sojourner to admit them.
practical_elements: Reading of Cyrus's Proclamation.
Dialogue between Captain of the Host and Principal Sojourner regarding the journey to Jerusalem and the need for a password.
Reading of Exodus 3:13-14 to reveal the password "I AM THAT I AM."
Candidates (Companions) passing under a "Living Arch" three times.
Candidates stumbling over a "rugged road" made of blocks, logs, old chairs, and benches.
Companions forming the Living Arch pressing harder on candidates each time.
Principal Sojourner instructing candidates to stoop low and kneel for prayer.
Reading of Psalm cxii, cxlii, and cxliii by the Principal Sojourner.
Arrival at the outer veil of the Tabernacle.
Veils being pushed apart and then closed.
Officers (except Principal Sojourner) taking their seats.
Principal Sojourner making an alarm by stamping nine times on the floor.
Master from the First Veil appearing.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Proclamation of Cyrus symbolizes a divine call to rebuild and return to a sacred purpose. The journey from Babylon to Jerusalem represents a pilgrimage from a state of captivity or spiritual exile to a place of enlightenment and divine presence, often fraught with difficulties. The password "I AM THAT I AM" signifies the ineffable and eternal nature of God, serving as a sacred means of identification and a reminder of divine authority. The "Living Arch" symbolizes the support and unity of the fraternity, while the act of stooping low represents humility and the principle that "he that humbleth himself shall be exalted." The "rugged road" symbolizes the trials, tribulations, and arduous path that individuals, and particularly Masons, must often traverse in their pursuit of knowledge, virtue, and spiritual progress. The repeated prayers from the Psalms emphasize reliance on divine guidance and protection during challenging journeys. The arrival at the outer veil of the Tabernacle signifies nearing a sacred space and a new stage of initiation or understanding.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates are taught the importance of responding to a divine call, even when the path is uncertain or difficult. They learn that humility is essential for spiritual exaltation and that the journey towards enlightenment often involves overcoming significant obstacles. The use of a sacred password reinforces the concept of divine identity and the need for proper identification within the fraternity. The prayers emphasize the necessity of faith, supplication, and trust in God's protection and guidance when facing challenges. The experience teaches perseverance, the value of communal support (Living Arch), and the understanding that many great individuals have trodden similar difficult paths.
tradition_notes: The ritual incorporates the historical figure of Cyrus the Great and his proclamation allowing the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple, as recorded in the Bible (Ezra 1:1-4).
The password "I AM THAT I AM" is directly taken from the biblical account of Moses' encounter with God at the burning bush (Exodus 3:13-14).
The journey from Babylon to Jerusalem is a central theme in Royal Arch Masonry, symbolizing the return from captivity and the rebuilding of the Temple.
The ritual uses specific Psalms (cxii, cxlii, cxliii) for prayers, reflecting a tradition of incorporating scriptural texts into Masonic ceremonies.
The "Living Arch" and "rugged road" are traditional ritualistic elements designed to convey symbolic lessons through physical experience.
